# 1. Test setup

## 1.1 Purpose of testing

Anti-AI Writing Kit was built through repeated manual tests and revisions. This larger test asks whether those methods still reduce observable AI-writing patterns across varied direct-writing tasks. Humanizer, Stop Slop, and a no-skill condition provide reference points.

The test is not designed to show that our skill is definitely in a leading position. Two stages cannot establish that. The result is evidence about the tested versions, prompts, and model. It also shows where the kit still needs work.

## 1.2 Skills compared

As of July 19, 2026, Humanizer and Stop Slop were the two most-starred English-language GitHub skills we found in this area. Star counts are dated snapshots, not quality scores.

### 1.2.1 Humanizer

[Humanizer](https://github.com/blader/humanizer) is a portable skill created by [Siqi Chen](https://github.com/blader). It draws on Wikipedia's Signs of AI writing guide and checks content, language, style, and communication patterns. The repository had 29,865 GitHub stars when checked on July 19, 2026. We tested version 2.8.2.

### 1.2.2 Stop Slop

[Stop Slop](https://github.com/hardikpandya/stop-slop) was created by [Hardik Pandya](https://github.com/hardikpandya). It targets predictable phrases, structures, rhythms, and sentence habits associated with machine-written prose. The repository had 14,072 GitHub stars when checked on July 19, 2026. We tested commit `8da1f030185bdfe8471220585162991eaeb970e9`.

### 1.2.3 Anti-AI Writing Kit

[Anti-AI Writing Kit](https://github.com/evelynyaxueke/anti-ai-writing-kit) was created by [Evelyn Ke](https://github.com/evelynyaxueke). It is a customizable skill for drafting and editing prose without common AI-writing habits. It uses load receipts, mechanical scans, and a final review of meaning, support, scope, and repetition. We tested the frozen V5 snapshot at commit `eb02ce2c86b1c4d4a83a9f8065e7022c68559bd5`.

## 1.3 Test at a glance

| Item | Test setup |
|---|---|
| Generation model | `gpt-5.6-sol` |
| Generation reasoning | Medium |
| Evaluation model | `gpt-5.6-sol` |
| Evaluation reasoning | Medium |
| Writing task | English direct writing |
| Stages | 2 |
| Prompts | 16 total, 8 per stage |
| Repetitions | 4 per prompt under each condition |
| Conditions | Anti-AI Writing Kit, Stop Slop, Humanizer, and no skill |
| Accepted generations | 256 total, 64 per condition |
| New generations in this re-evaluation | None |

# 2. Results

## 2.1 Combined result

The result uses all 48 operational patterns documented by at least one tested skill. Lower is better.

| Condition | Words in flagged sentences or lines | Total words | AI-smell rate |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Anti-AI Writing Kit | 437 | 37,143 | 1.18% |
| Stop Slop | 791 | 37,515 | 2.11% |
| Humanizer | 828 | 37,451 | 2.21% |
| No skill | 1,640 | 38,715 | 4.24% |

AI-smell rate is the share of output words located in complete sentences or Markdown lines containing a detected pattern. It is not an authorship estimate. It is also not the percentage of individually bad words.

## 2.2 Interpretation

- All three skills reduced the rate from the no-skill baseline of 4.24%: Humanizer recorded 2.21%, Stop Slop 2.11%, and Anti-AI Writing Kit 1.18%. This happened with a current OpenAI model in the test environment, whose no-skill output was already relatively clean.

- Anti-AI Writing Kit had the lowest observed combined rate at 1.18% across all 48 patterns. That is the result of this test, not proof of a general or permanent position.

- The gaps among the three skills were small in absolute percentage points. We ran only two stages. More prompts, repetitions, models, or future skill versions could narrow, widen, or reverse those gaps.

- A single conspicuous AI-writing pattern can affect how a whole piece reads even when the percentage-point gap is small. This test measured pattern frequency, not reader perception, so it cannot quantify that effect. All outputs used `gpt-5.6-sol` at the medium setting. Older models may produce different rates or wider gaps, but this test did not compare model generations.

- This test does not support a zero-rate guarantee. Anti-AI Writing Kit still produced flagged sentences. The practical aim is to reduce their frequency and improve the chance of receiving a clean draft.

- These skills can change. Maintainers may update them, and users may customize them. Later tests should freeze new snapshots and treat the results as a new comparison.

The combined tables contain Stage 1 and Stage 2. We stopped after those two stages for this report.

## 2.3 Stage results

### 2.3.1 Stage 1

| Condition | Words in flagged sentences or lines | Total words | AI-smell rate |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Anti-AI Writing Kit | 145 | 20,253 | 0.72% |
| Stop Slop | 387 | 20,497 | 1.89% |
| Humanizer | 509 | 20,645 | 2.47% |
| No skill | 1,018 | 21,503 | 4.73% |

### 2.3.2 Stage 2

| Condition | Words in flagged sentences or lines | Total words | AI-smell rate |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Anti-AI Writing Kit | 292 | 16,890 | 1.73% |
| Stop Slop | 404 | 17,018 | 2.37% |
| Humanizer | 319 | 16,806 | 1.90% |
| No skill | 622 | 17,212 | 3.61% |

# 3. Methodology

## 3.1 Test design

This comparison covers direct writing only. Editing tasks and general writing-quality scores were excluded. Each stage used eight prompts, four repetitions, and four conditions. Each condition therefore produced 32 outputs per stage and 64 across both stages.

Every generation attempt used a new temporary working directory and temporary `CODEX_HOME`. The baseline contained no installed skill. Each skill condition contained one frozen snapshot. No live or user-level skill was changed. The first technically valid output was preserved.

| Condition | Frozen source | Version or commit | Snapshot checksum |
|---|---|---|---|
| Humanizer | [GitHub](https://github.com/blader/humanizer) | 2.8.2, 1b48564898e999219882660237fde01bf4843a0f | `6a3cf5d083c0db88d1870b2a11ea00c358faeca6d47c28b70f7b4ab249007b93` |
| Stop Slop | [GitHub](https://github.com/hardikpandya/stop-slop) | 8da1f030185bdfe8471220585162991eaeb970e9 | `2b160d464bcf0254cae5b09daac4578339cc83203fc5225d63545891a39c7ba5` |
| Anti-AI Writing Kit | Frozen project snapshot | V5 test snapshot, eb02ce2c86b1c4d4a83a9f8065e7022c68559bd5 | `af77a09d25cc34b16a3f7e0639c2ba44f620eb97fcb7311c7f0887a152093996` |

## 3.2 Pattern codebook

The codebook began with the documented bad-AI-writing rules in the three frozen skills. Related rules were merged into operational patterns. Each pattern has an inclusion rule, an exclusion rule, examples, and source references. The evaluator had to identify an exact excerpt and explain why it met the rule. It could not flag general writing quality, factual validity, prompt following, or a stylistic preference unless a codebook pattern applied.

The public result contains 48 patterns supported by at least one skill. It uses one word-rate metric throughout the report.

<details><summary>Operational pattern list</summary>

| ID | Pattern | Source snapshots |
|---|---|---|
| P001 | Empty hype or significance inflation | C1, C2, C3 |
| P002 | Empty stock AI or corporate vocabulary | C1, C2, C3 |
| P003 | Vague or unnamed authority | C1, C2, C3 |
| P004 | Throat-clearing, signposting, or reveal framing | C1, C2, C3 |
| P005 | Chatbot or process residue | C1, C2, C3 |
| P006 | False contrast or negative parallelism | C1, C2, C3 |
| P007 | Forced three-item packaging | C1, C2, C3 |
| P008 | Actor-hiding passive or subjectless construction | C1, C2, C3 |
| P009 | Authored em dash | C1, C2, C3 |
| P010 | Excessive or content-free hedging | C1, C2, C3 |
| P011 | Manufactured staccato drama | C1, C2, C3 |
| P012 | Mechanically uniform rhythm | C1, C2, C3 |
| P013 | Manufactured quotable, slogan, or aphorism | C1, C2, C3 |
| P014 | Vague declarative | C1, C2, C3 |
| P015 | Notability or prestige padding | C1 |
| P016 | Participial fake depth or gerund motion | C1, C3 |
| P017 | Promotional praise cluster | C1, C3 |
| P018 | Formulaic challenge or future section | C1, C3 |
| P019 | Bloated copula substitute | C1, C3 |
| P020 | Synonym cycling | C1, C3 |
| P021 | False range | C1, C3 |
| P022 | Decorative or template formatting | C1, C3 |
| P023 | Generic positive conclusion or dead closing | C1, C3 |
| P024 | Cutoff, access, or generic disclaimer residue | C1, C3 |
| P025 | Sycophantic praise | C1, C3 |
| P026 | Rhetorical question used as an immediate reveal | C1, C2, C3 |
| P027 | Anthropomorphic abstraction or false agency | C2, C3 |
| P028 | Invented crowd or sweeping absolute | C2, C3 |
| P029 | Filler transition | C1, C2, C3 |
| P030 | Stock or stacked analogy | C2, C3 |
| P031 | Fragmented header or generic scaffolding | C1, C3 |
| P032 | Repeated recap, conclusion, or recommendation | C3 |
| P033 | Generic technology change or prestige label | C3 |
| P034 | Generic outcome abstraction | C3 |
| P035 | Reader coaching or repeated caution | C2, C3 |
| P036 | Formulaic two-item setup or open-ended list | C3 |
| P037 | Punctuation theatrics | C3 |
| P038 | Verbose filler phrase | C1, C2, C3 |
| P039 | Additional stock AI vocabulary | C1, C2, C3 |
| P040 | Assistant offer residue | C1, C3 |
| P041 | Reassurance hedge fragment | C2 |
| P042 | Uniform layout rhythm or punchline endings | C2, C3 |
| P043 | Engagement command or permission line | C2, C3 |
| P044 | Stock speculative gap-filling | C1 |
| P045 | Diff-anchored writing | C1 |
| P046 | Narrator-from-a-distance construction | C2 |
| P047 | False balance or irrelevant concession | C3 |
| P048 | Performative intimacy or borrowed emotion | C2, C3 |

</details>

## 3.3 Blind evaluation

The 256 existing outputs received opaque evaluation IDs. Evaluators saw the exact prompt, complete output, and evaluator instructions. They did not see the skill, condition, run ID, repetition, or loading record. Eight balanced batches contained 32 outputs each. Each batch had equal hidden representation from every condition and prompt.

All 256 evaluations were accepted on the first scoring attempt. Their records were validated and checksum-locked before labels were restored. The frozen evaluator used `gpt-5.6-sol` at the medium setting.

## 3.4 AI-smell rate

For each detected issue, the exact excerpt was expanded to the complete sentence or Markdown line it touched. If several issues touched the same unit, that unit counted once. The numerator is the number of unique lexical words in flagged units. The denominator is every lexical word in the output set.

A lexical word contains Unicode letters or numbers and may contain one internal straight or curly apostrophe. Sentence boundaries were produced with `Intl.Segmenter` for English. A long sentence contributes all of its words when a qualifying pattern appears inside it. This makes the rate direct to read, but it also gives long sentences more weight.

## 3.5 Evaluation audit and corrections

A post-evaluation audit checked record integrity, excerpt boundaries, source overlap, exclusions, and arithmetic. The locked blind evaluation was preserved unchanged. Corrections were written to a separate adjudication file and then applied mechanically.

The audit removed nine P005 flags because they were normal previews inside real emails or newsletters, which the rule excludes. It removed four P008 flags where the omitted actor was conventional or irrelevant. It also removed two P032 flags where the second sentence added a distinct fact. Missing high-confidence P009, P026, and P035 occurrences were added. Excerpts that overlapped or crossed sentence boundaries were repaired without changing the underlying flagged words.

## 3.6 Technical failures and preservation

Stage 1 accepted 88 outputs on attempt one, 8 on attempt two, 8 on attempt three, and 24 on attempt four after model-service transport failures. Stage 2 accepted all 128 outputs on attempt one. No output was regenerated because of its content or evaluation.

The first evaluation freeze had an incorrect source-artifact path. All four workers failed during preflight, before any model call or score. That aborted freeze was preserved. The corrected freeze then produced 256 first-attempt evaluations. Nonfatal model-list refresh warnings appeared in 31 Stage 1 evaluation attempts, but each returned a valid score.

## 3.7 Limits

- The test used one generation model at one setting.

- It used one model evaluator, followed by rule and integrity audits. It did not use an independent human panel or measure inter-rater agreement.

- The 16 prompts were designed for this test. They were not sampled randomly from all writing tasks.

- The codebook was derived from the three tested skills. It is not an independent scientific taxonomy and may align more closely with the most detailed skill.

- The test covers English direct drafting, not editing, other languages, factual quality, usefulness, or human preference.

- Whole-sentence and whole-line counting gives more weight to long units and includes ordinary context words around a detected pattern.

- Rates are descriptive. They do not establish universal performance or statistical superiority.

# 4. Skill loading and final-check audit

The complete Anti-AI Writing Kit file and active-rules receipt appeared in all 64 kit runs. The loading mechanism therefore worked in every tested run.

The delivery check was not fully reliable. A final-check receipt appeared in 63 of 64 runs. The exact last checked candidate matched the delivered output in 60 runs. One Stage 1 output had no final-check receipt. Three Stage 2 outputs changed after their last recorded check. Two changes repaired damaged text; one added a factual comparison clause.

The benchmark kept the first valid delivered output in every case. These four exceptions remain part of the results and identify a delivery-control issue for future versions.

# Appendix A: Exact prompts

Each prompt below is the exact text sent for that condition. The same text was used for repetitions R1 through R4. Showing each condition version once avoids printing four identical copies.

## A.1 Stage 1 prompts

<details><summary>Stage 1 P1, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `e87ff17736dcaf03d88e9a38dddb1d129e05552cff21cf95e0755ef6098b715f`

````text
Write a LinkedIn post of 110 to 140 words in the first person for Mara, founder of a four-person remote design studio.

Purpose: share the result of a six-week experiment with the studio's weekly status meeting.

Facts:
- Before the experiment, all four people joined a 45-minute video call every Monday.
- During the experiment, each person instead posted a written update answering four questions: What shipped? What is stuck? What decision is needed? What happens next?
- The team posted 22 of 24 updates on time.
- They held a 15-minute call when an update revealed a decision that could not wait. This happened in four of the six weeks.
- The studio recorded two missed handoffs during the experiment, the same number as in the preceding six weeks.
- The team will continue the practice for three more months, then review it again.

The audience is people who lead small remote teams. Do not add a title or hashtags. Make no claims beyond the supplied facts. Return only the finished post.

````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 1 P1, Humanizer (R1 to R4)</summary>

````text
- The team posted 22 of 24 updates on time.
````

<details><summary>Stage 1 P2, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `1f1d7dbe821b4b51c793eaae081bf6adb8d3fb1ac9ac39ce0b3c32864dbb6d47`

````text
Write a 700 to 850-word section for a reported magazine feature about Marlow's 2025 library cooling-center pilot. Begin with the exact heading "After Closing Time" and do not add other headings.

The section should explain what happened, what the available data can and cannot establish, and what the city approved for the following summer.

Facts:
- Three neighborhood libraries extended their usual closing time from 6 p.m. to 9 p.m. on 12 days when the forecast high reached at least 38°C.
- Door counters recorded 2,840 entries on those days, compared with 1,760 entries on the same calendar dates in 2024.
- The counters measure entries, not unique visitors. Someone who left and returned was counted twice.
- Of the 2,840 entries, 1,079 occurred after 6 p.m.
- The pilot cost $18,600: $11,200 for staff overtime, $4,900 for security, and $2,500 for bottled water and bus vouchers.
- Visitors used 212 bus vouchers. Sixty-one percent were redeemed at the Riverside branch.
- A voluntary survey received 146 responses. Respondents could select more than one reason for visiting. Eighty-nine said their home was uncomfortably hot, 37 said they had no working air conditioner, and 52 came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device.
- Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings. North Hill never used more than 60 percent of its seats.
- No medical emergency was reported at any participating branch.
- Citywide emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. The two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group.
- The council voted to repeat the pilot at Riverside and Central only, triggered by heat alerts with 24 hours' notice. It also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both branches.

Use only these facts. You may draw cautious inferences, but do not invent scenes, people, quotations, motives, or additional figures. Write for a general reader. Return only the finished section.

````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 1 P3, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `5d7d7b767c36cf690384a64ec168aa20acccf0960d5a791af10636ecd2720254`

````text
Write 190 to 240 words of landing-page copy for Cutline, a browser-based client-review tool for freelance video editors and production studios with two to eight people.

Include, in this order:
1. A hero headline, a subhead, and a primary call-to-action label.
2. Three feature blocks, each with a heading and one or two sentences.
3. A "How it works" section with three numbered steps.
4. One pricing line.
5. A closing call-to-action heading and button label.

Product facts:
- A team uploads an MP4 file of up to 5 GB and shares a browser link.
- Reviewers do not need an account.
- Reviewers can attach comments to exact timecodes.
- A reviewer can mark each uploaded version "approved" or "changes requested."
- Every upload is treated as a separate version.
- Reminder emails are sent manually by the project owner.
- Cutline costs $24 per active project per month and allows unlimited reviewers.
- After a project is closed, its files remain available for 30 days and are then deleted.
- Cutline does not edit video and does not provide transcripts, watermarking, integrations, or a mobile app.

The audience currently collects feedback through email and chat. The brand voice is composed, practical, and direct. Do not invent testimonials, customer numbers, time-saved estimates, security claims, or additional capabilities. Return only the finished page copy.

````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 1 P4, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `52373c7fd001db033353ef213e9ed2fbe4518a81a41cc6a6edbca91a56796ab4`

````text
Write a 750 to 850 word article for the staff newsletter of North Coast University. Use only the facts below. The article should explain what the university knows about delays in its equipment-purchase process, what remains unknown, and what will happen next. Keep the tone direct and professional. Give the article a specific title. Do not add a preface, notes, sources section, or explanation of your process. Return only the finished article.

Facts:

- In March 2026, the university sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members. It received 412 responses.
- Of those 412 respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months.
- In the same survey, 176 respondents said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the previous six months.
- The survey report contains aggregate totals only. It does not cross-tabulate approval waits and workarounds, so it does not show how many people reported both.
- The survey did not ask respondents why they used a workaround.
- Procurement system logs cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026.
- The median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. The 90th percentile was 11.6 business days.
- Requests that required an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. Requests without that review had a median of 3.4 business days.
- The logs identify review type and elapsed time but do not identify the reasons for individual delays.
- In January and February 2026, Procurement had six approval officers. In March it had eight.
- Median approval time was 4.8 business days in February and 3.6 business days in March.
- The university also introduced a revised request form on March 1. Because staffing and the form changed at the same time, the records do not isolate the effect of either change.
- On April 22, the Administrative Council approved a twelve-week pilot in which purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will use a shorter approval route.
- The published council decision does not state why members chose this pilot or which evidence they relied on.
- The pilot starts May 18. Procurement will publish median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks.
- The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies.

````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 1 P5, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `0b301b8cd087ad999f3cabd85b2d9bd019a173ed557dbed7c0166a5e3054e7da`

````text
Write a 750 to 850 word decision memo from the operations director of Harborline Foods to the executive team. Use only the record below. Recommend whether to retire the manual exception queue now, extend it, or run a limited transition. Preserve supported relationships in the evidence, but do not invent relationships that the record does not establish. Use the headings Decision, Evidence, Risks, and Next 30 Days in that order. Do not include an executive summary or a concluding recap. Return only the finished memo.

Record:

- The manual exception queue handles wholesale orders rejected by the automated credit screen.
- From January through April 2026, 1,840 orders entered the manual queue.
- A timestamp audit found that 1,126 of those orders spent at least 24 hours waiting before an analyst opened them.
- The audit traced 804 of the waits of at least 24 hours to a routing rule that sent orders back to the bottom of the queue whenever a sales representative attached a new document. This relationship was established by matching the routing events to the affected order timestamps.
- The other long waits had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not estimate the contribution of each cause.
- During the same four months, Customer Support logged 296 complaints that mentioned slow wholesale-order review.
- Support records are stored by customer account, while the queue extract used for this memo contains order IDs with customer fields removed. No linkage was performed between complaints and queued orders.
- Forty-three wholesale accounts did not renew during the same period. The renewal report gives no reason for nonrenewal and was not linked to either complaints or queue records.
- A two-week shadow test processed 210 new exceptions through both the current queue and a proposed rules-based route.
- The proposed route produced the same approve-or-decline result as the manual queue for 198 orders.
- Twelve orders produced different results. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records.
- Compliance reviewed those twelve cases and said all twelve still require analyst judgment until the rule set is revised.
- For the other 198 orders, median time to a decision was 3.1 hours under the proposed route and 18.7 hours under the manual queue.
- The shadow test assigned incoming exceptions to the two routes at the same time and used the same submitted documents, so the comparison supports attributing the measured time difference for those 198 orders to the routing method during the test.
- The proposed route has not been tested during a month-end volume spike.
- The chief executive asked for a change to be ready by June 15. The request does not state the reason for that date.
- Engineering can disable the document-attachment reset rule in five business days.
- Engineering can place overseas registrations and corrected tax records into a protected manual lane in eight business days.
- A full retirement of the manual queue would take at least six weeks and would require a revised compliance rule set.
- Operations has capacity to audit every decision produced by the proposed route for 30 days, up to 350 orders per week.

````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 1 P6, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `0d5524705812ea95fc09295bae277aedd281d3feb73939662e9ada3ff732e029`

````text
Write a 700 to 800 word internal article using only the material below. The exact title must be “What the Relay Trial Changed”. Use exactly these headings, in this order: Setup, Result, What We Can Claim, Next Test. Preserve both quotations exactly, including punctuation and names. The article may draw the supported conclusion in the material and must not flatten it into mere correlation. Do not add a separate introduction before Setup or a conclusion after Next Test. Return only the finished article.

Material:

- Lumen Freight tested a new handoff protocol, called Relay, for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海.
- The trial ran for eight weeks, from February 2 through March 29, 2026.
- It included 48 dispatch teams. Before assignment, teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. Within each group, a random draw assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing handoff process.
- Relay teams used a four-field handoff: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued using free-form notes.
- Both groups kept the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule during the trial.
- The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads that departed more than 20 minutes late.
- Before the trial, the Relay group’s late-departure rate was 14.8 percent and the control group’s rate was 14.6 percent.
- During the trial, the Relay group’s rate was 9.7 percent and the control group’s rate was 14.1 percent.
- The pre-registered analysis compared the change in the Relay group with the change in the control group. It estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.
- The random assignment, stable operating rules, and pre-registered comparison support attributing that measured reduction to Relay for the teams and period studied.
- The trial did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch.
- Three Relay teams missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of handoffs. Their late-departure rate still remained in the analysis.
- Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.”
- Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”
- In a post-trial questionnaire, 31 of 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond. The questionnaire was not administered to control teams.
- On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record says the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test.
- The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch, will add an optional exception note, and will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload.

````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 1 P6, Humanizer (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `cac352816a18df74377e05f3103fbdfb1ebd74035390d7b5aeb9cb4a161a059f`

````text
Use the humanizer skill.

Write a 700 to 800 word internal article using only the material below. The exact title must be “What the Relay Trial Changed”. Use exactly these headings, in this order: Setup, Result, What We Can Claim, Next Test. Preserve both quotations exactly, including punctuation and names. The article may draw the supported conclusion in the material and must not flatten it into mere correlation. Do not add a separate introduction before Setup or a conclusion after Next Test. Return only the finished article.

Material:

- Lumen Freight tested a new handoff protocol, called Relay, for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海.
- The trial ran for eight weeks, from February 2 through March 29, 2026.
- It included 48 dispatch teams. Before assignment, teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. Within each group, a random draw assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing handoff process.
- Relay teams used a four-field handoff: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued using free-form notes.
- Both groups kept the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule during the trial.
- The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads that departed more than 20 minutes late.
- Before the trial, the Relay group’s late-departure rate was 14.8 percent and the control group’s rate was 14.6 percent.
- During the trial, the Relay group’s rate was 9.7 percent and the control group’s rate was 14.1 percent.
- The pre-registered analysis compared the change in the Relay group with the change in the control group. It estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.
- The random assignment, stable operating rules, and pre-registered comparison support attributing that measured reduction to Relay for the teams and period studied.
- The trial did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch.
- Three Relay teams missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of handoffs. Their late-departure rate still remained in the analysis.
- Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.”
- Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”
- In a post-trial questionnaire, 31 of 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond. The questionnaire was not administered to control teams.
- On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record says the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test.
- The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch, will add an optional exception note, and will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload.

````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 1 P7, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `ecd76e290673d0fe53d85764994c78c965a0daae46f063d4470a2132f61dfc59`

````text
Write a 750 to 850 word internal article for Riverbend Transit dispatch staff. Use only the record below. Recommend whether Riverbend should deploy QuickTurn citywide now, extend it in a limited test, or stop using it. Explain the evidence, the limits of the record, and the next action in continuous prose. Give the article a specific title, but do not use body headings, bullet points, an executive summary, or a concluding recap. Return only the finished article.

Record:

- QuickTurn is a five-field handoff used when an incoming dispatcher takes responsibility for an unresolved route assignment.
- Riverbend tested QuickTurn for seven weeks at 36 dispatch desks in two depots.
- Before assignment, desks were grouped by depot and by their average late-start rate during the prior six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the existing free-form handoff.
- Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours remained the same for both groups during the trial.
- The primary outcome was the share of scheduled routes that started more than 15 minutes late.
- Before the trial, the late-start rate was 13.2 percent for desks later assigned to QuickTurn and 13.0 percent for control desks.
- During the trial, the QuickTurn rate was 8.4 percent and the control rate was 12.8 percent.
- The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that QuickTurn reduced the late-start rate by 4.6 percentage points during the trial, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 percentage points.
- The random assignment, stable operating rules, and pre-registered comparison support attributing the measured reduction to QuickTurn for the desks, depots, and trial period studied.
- Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs. The late-start outcomes for those four desks remained in the analysis.
- The record does not state whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis.
- The trial measured late starts and corrected assignment errors. It did not measure driver satisfaction or overtime cost.
- A voluntary post-trial survey received responses from 260 dispatch employees across the two depots.
- The survey allowed respondents to select more than one answer. In the aggregate results, 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation.
- The survey report has no respondent-level cross-tabulation, so it does not show how many selected both answers.
- The survey was not linked to dispatch-desk assignment, so it does not compare QuickTurn and control staff.
- Operations director Mara Velez requested a citywide deployment by September 1. The request gives no reason for that date.
- Engineering estimates twelve business days of configuration after final requirements are signed.
- Final requirements have not been signed, and the record gives no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout.
- A limited extension could keep QuickTurn at the 18 assigned desks for six more weeks.
- The extension plan would add an optional exception note and measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload.
- Riverbend has capacity to audit every QuickTurn handoff during a six-week extension.

````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 1 P8, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `d8fcbdf7be4da995c970af60e778dbf6cda77a3a84b6b16c75bd1085000aa948`

````text
Write a 700 to 800 word internal decision article for Meridian Care’s scheduling operations team using only the record below. The exact title must be “What CallPath Justifies Now”. Use exactly these headings, in this order: Completed Trial, Claim Limits, Next Test, Decision. Explain what the completed trial supports, keep the supplied measurement and scope boundaries intact, and recommend whether Meridian Care should continue CallPath only at the trial desks while running the next test, deploy it across the network now, or stop using it. Do not add an executive summary, a separate introduction, or a conclusion after Decision. Return only the finished article.

Record:

- Meridian Care tested CallPath, a five-field appointment-handoff protocol, at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka.
- The trial ran for six weeks, from May 4 through June 14, 2026.
- Before assignment, the desks were grouped by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the previous six weeks.
- A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 desks to the existing free-form handoff.
- CallPath required these fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code.
- Both groups kept the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours during the trial.
- The primary outcome was the share of callbacks not completed within four hours.
- Before the trial, that rate was 13.7 percent among desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent among control desks.
- During the trial, the CallPath group’s rate was 8.9 percent and the control group’s rate was 13.1 percent.
- The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that CallPath reduced the rate of callbacks not completed within four hours by 4.4 percentage points during the six-week trial.
- The 95 percent confidence interval was 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.
- The random assignment, stable operating rules, and pre-registered comparison support attributing the measured reduction to CallPath for the desks, sites, and trial period studied.
- Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs.
- The callback outcomes from those four desks remained in the analysis.
- The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis.
- The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors.
- The trial explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes.
- The record says nothing about whether scheduler-reported workload was measured.
- A voluntary questionnaire went only to staff assigned to CallPath.
- Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer.
- Six staff assigned to CallPath did not respond.
- Control staff did not receive the questionnaire.
- Meridian Care has kept CallPath at the 18 trial desks while deciding what to do next.
- The proposed next test will run for six weeks at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial.
- Before assignment, those desks will be grouped by site and baseline callback-completion rate.
- A random draw within each group will assign 24 desks to CallPath and 24 desks to the existing workflow.
- Both groups will use the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.
- The primary outcome will be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours.
- Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload will be secondary outcomes.
- The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on callback completion during the six-week test.
- Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change during the next test.
- The record does not say that this audit can identify missing status changes or establish compliance with the workflow.
- Scheduling Operations must decide whether to run the next test while continuing CallPath only at the 18 trial desks, deploy CallPath across the network now, or stop using it.
````

</details>

````text
- The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that CallPath reduced the rate of callbacks not completed within four hours by 4.4 percentage points during the six-week trial.
- The 95 percent confidence interval was 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.
- The random assignment, stable operating rules, and pre-registered comparison support attributing the measured reduction to CallPath for the desks, sites, and trial period studied.
- Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs.
- The callback outcomes from those four desks remained in the analysis.
- The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis.
- The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors.
- The trial explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes.
- The record says nothing about whether scheduler-reported workload was measured.
- A voluntary questionnaire went only to staff assigned to CallPath.
- Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer.
- Six staff assigned to CallPath did not respond.
- Control staff did not receive the questionnaire.
- Meridian Care has kept CallPath at the 18 trial desks while deciding what to do next.
- The proposed next test will run for six weeks at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial.
- Before assignment, those desks will be grouped by site and baseline callback-completion rate.
- A random draw within each group will assign 24 desks to CallPath and 24 desks to the existing workflow.
- Both groups will use the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.
- The primary outcome will be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours.
- Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload will be secondary outcomes.
- The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on callback completion during the six-week test.
- Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change during the next test.
- The record does not say that this audit can identify missing status changes or establish compliance with the workflow.
- Scheduling Operations must decide whether to run the next test while continuing CallPath only at the 18 trial desks, deploy CallPath across the network now, or stop using it.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 1 P8, Anti-AI Writing Kit (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `f12e05a48088a0649378127c0d70c49f0904aea9e889ebe94d863336f94e3c3d`

````text
Use the anti-ai-writing-kit skill.

Write a 700 to 800 word internal decision article for Meridian Care’s scheduling operations team using only the record below. The exact title must be “What CallPath Justifies Now”. Use exactly these headings, in this order: Completed Trial, Claim Limits, Next Test, Decision. Explain what the completed trial supports, keep the supplied measurement and scope boundaries intact, and recommend whether Meridian Care should continue CallPath only at the trial desks while running the next test, deploy it across the network now, or stop using it. Do not add an executive summary, a separate introduction, or a conclusion after Decision. Return only the finished article.

Record:

- Meridian Care tested CallPath, a five-field appointment-handoff protocol, at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka.
- The trial ran for six weeks, from May 4 through June 14, 2026.
- Before assignment, the desks were grouped by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the previous six weeks.
- A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 desks to the existing free-form handoff.
- CallPath required these fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code.
- Both groups kept the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours during the trial.
- The primary outcome was the share of callbacks not completed within four hours.
- Before the trial, that rate was 13.7 percent among desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent among control desks.
- During the trial, the CallPath group’s rate was 8.9 percent and the control group’s rate was 13.1 percent.
- The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that CallPath reduced the rate of callbacks not completed within four hours by 4.4 percentage points during the six-week trial.
- The 95 percent confidence interval was 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.
- The random assignment, stable operating rules, and pre-registered comparison support attributing the measured reduction to CallPath for the desks, sites, and trial period studied.
- Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs.
- The callback outcomes from those four desks remained in the analysis.
- The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis.
- The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors.
- The trial explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes.
- The record says nothing about whether scheduler-reported workload was measured.
- A voluntary questionnaire went only to staff assigned to CallPath.
- Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer.
- Six staff assigned to CallPath did not respond.
- Control staff did not receive the questionnaire.
- Meridian Care has kept CallPath at the 18 trial desks while deciding what to do next.
- The proposed next test will run for six weeks at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial.
- Before assignment, those desks will be grouped by site and baseline callback-completion rate.
- A random draw within each group will assign 24 desks to CallPath and 24 desks to the existing workflow.
- Both groups will use the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.
- The primary outcome will be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours.
- Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload will be secondary outcomes.
- The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on callback completion during the six-week test.
- Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change during the next test.
- The record does not say that this audit can identify missing status changes or establish compliance with the workflow.
- Scheduling Operations must decide whether to run the next test while continuing CallPath only at the 18 trial desks, deploy CallPath across the network now, or stop using it.
````

</details>

## A.2 Stage 2 prompts

<details><summary>Stage 2 P1, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `66525bba02603214e1dc16f5024d519da20418067b5e7c01f072431d59cb46b4`

````text
Write a first-person social post of 130 to 160 words for Leena, who owns a neighborhood bookstore in Bristol.

The post should tell readers what happened when the shop tested a weekly quiet hour and what Leena decided to do next.

Facts:

- For eight Wednesdays in May and June, the shop turned off its music from 5 p.m. to 6 p.m.
- The shop remained open as usual. Staff still answered questions and used the till.
- Leena tried the quiet hour after three customers separately asked whether the music could be lowered while they browsed.
- Across the eight quiet hours, the shop recorded 143 purchases. Across the same hour on the previous eight Wednesdays, it recorded 129 purchases.
- The two periods included different book releases and different weather. The shop did not count visitors who left without buying.
- Fourteen people left written comments during the test. Eleven asked the shop to keep the quiet hour, two preferred music, and one asked for a quiet morning instead.
- One staff member found the hour restful. Another said the silence made closing tasks feel awkward.
- Leena will keep the quiet hour on Wednesdays through September and ask staff and customers again before deciding whether to continue.

The audience includes regular customers and other small shop owners. Do not add a title, hashtags, emojis, or a request for comments. Do not claim that the quiet hour caused sales to rise. Return only the finished post.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 2 P2, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `f94ab5dbb5a61be223a5fe9bd07696d11339a19ae377bf470d1d3e484c5dcd3a`

````text
Write a professional email of 240 to 300 words from Priya Shah, laboratory manager at Westbridge Materials Lab, to the 17 researchers who use Freezer B.

The email must include a subject line. It should explain what happened, give recipients clear instructions, and state what is still uncertain.

Facts:

- At 6:42 a.m. on Monday, July 13, Freezer B triggered a temperature alarm after rising from minus 80°C to minus 61°C.
- The backup generator was working. Facilities found a failed door seal at 7:25 a.m.
- Staff moved 86 labeled sample boxes to Freezers D and E between 7:40 a.m. and 8:18 a.m.
- The inventory lists the destination freezer and shelf for every moved box.
- No box was discarded.
- Temperature probes show that the air inside Freezer B remained above minus 70°C for 54 minutes. The probes do not show the temperature inside individual sample tubes.
- The lab's existing protocol requires each principal investigator to decide whether exposure affects the planned use of their samples.
- Researchers must check the shared inventory by noon on Tuesday, July 14, and report any missing or incorrectly placed box to Priya.
- Principal investigators must record their sample-use decision in the incident sheet by 5 p.m. on Thursday, July 16.
- Facilities expects the replacement seal to arrive Wednesday morning. That delivery time has not been confirmed.
- Freezer B must remain empty until it holds minus 80°C for 12 continuous hours after repair.
- Priya will send another email when the freezer is cleared for use.

Do not say that the samples are safe, damaged, or unaffected. Do not add apologies, praise for the team, or details not supplied above. Return only the finished email.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 2 P3, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `323655dd868221c45aca5800806dd7ed2a156e7167ac52ebeb61d9a8a9146c0b`

````text
Write 280 to 340 words of landing-page copy for Stackbox, a reusable moving-crate rental service operating in Manchester and Salford.

Use this order:

1. Headline, short subhead, and button label.
2. A short section explaining what the customer receives.
3. A section titled "Delivery and collection".
4. A section titled "Prices".
5. A short section explaining the limits of the service.
6. A final button label.

Service facts:

- Customers rent lidded plastic crates instead of buying cardboard boxes.
- A standard home pack contains 25 crates, 25 labels, and one wheeled dolly.
- Crates measure 60 by 40 by 32 centimetres and hold up to 25 kilograms each.
- Stackbox delivers to the old address and collects from the new address.
- Customers choose a two-hour delivery window and a two-hour collection window.
- The standard rental period is 14 days.
- A standard home pack costs £89, including delivery and collection within Manchester or Salford.
- Each additional week costs £24.
- Extra crates cost £2.50 each for the original rental period.
- Bookings can be changed without charge until 4 p.m. two days before delivery.
- Stackbox does not provide packing, removal, storage, or cleaning services.
- Crates cannot be taken outside mainland Great Britain.
- Customers are charged £18 for each crate that is missing or returned with structural damage. Ordinary marks are not charged.
- Delivery is available Tuesday through Saturday. Same-day delivery is not available.

The audience is people planning a local house move. The voice should be practical and calm. Do not invent customer quotations, environmental savings, time savings, service areas, guarantees, or features. Return only the finished page copy.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 2 P4, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `cde34f93f1b20cdb44d073507dc3174fdcf4207656a1b67ed533c832ec6db320`

````text
Write a reported article of 750 to 850 words for a regional newspaper about Bellhaven Community College's evening childcare pilot. Give the article a specific title and use no body headings.

The article should explain who used the pilot, what participants said, what the records do and do not establish, and what the college will do next.

Record:

- Bellhaven ran the pilot for ten teaching weeks in spring 2026 at its East Campus.
- The childcare room was open Monday through Thursday from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m.
- It accepted children aged three through ten whose parent or guardian was attending an evening class on campus.
- The room had 24 places per evening. Families paid £3 per child per evening.
- Eighty-seven students registered at least one child. Sixty-two used the service on four or more evenings.
- Attendance records show 1,146 child-visits during the pilot. The records count each evening visit, not unique children.
- The room reached its 24-place limit on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one registered family could not book a place.
- The college did not record how many families decided not to request a place after seeing that an evening was full.
- Forty-nine participating students completed a voluntary survey. Forty-one said the service made it easier to attend class. Respondents could select more than one answer.
- Course records show that 74 percent of registered users completed all their spring evening courses. The completion rate was 68 percent among all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course.
- Students chose whether to use childcare, and the two groups may differ in ways the records do not measure. The figures do not establish that the pilot caused the difference in course completion.
- The pilot cost £42,300. Fees collected from families totaled £3,438.
- The student-services budget paid the remaining cost.
- No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded.
- Parent Sofia Mendes said, “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”
- Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”
- The college approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus with 32 places per evening.
- The second pilot will keep the same hours and fee. It will add a waiting list and record requests made after an evening is full.
- The college has not approved the service at its other two campuses.

Preserve both quotations exactly. Do not invent scenes, interviews, motives, demographic details, or causal claims. Return only the finished article.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 2 P4, Humanizer (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `ac44ee36d48cf3a5a9bf6bce79f1982e76054cc2b8fea2d8dc21cf77e3c758cb`

````text

- It accepted children aged three through ten whose parent or guardian was attending an evening class on campus.
- The room had 24 places per evening. Families paid £3 per child per evening.
- Eighty-seven students registered at least one child. Sixty-two used the service on four or more evenings.
- Attendance records show 1,146 child-visits during the pilot. The records count each evening visit, not unique children.
- The room reached its 24-place limit on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one registered family could not book a place.
- The college did not record how many families decided not to request a place after seeing that an evening was full.
- Forty-nine participating students completed a voluntary survey. Forty-one said the service made it easier to attend class. Respondents could select more than one answer.
- Course records show that 74 percent of registered users completed all their spring evening courses. The completion rate was 68 percent among all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course.
- Students chose whether to use childcare, and the two groups may differ in ways the records do not measure. The figures do not establish that the pilot caused the difference in course completion.
- The pilot cost £42,300. Fees collected from families totaled £3,438.
- The student-services budget paid the remaining cost.
- No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded.
- Parent Sofia Mendes said, “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”
- Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”
- The college approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus with 32 places per evening.
- The second pilot will keep the same hours and fee. It will add a waiting list and record requests made after an evening is full.
- The college has not approved the service at its other two campuses.

Preserve both quotations exactly. Do not invent scenes, interviews, motives, demographic details, or causal claims. Return only the finished article.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 2 P5, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `ab4ddf331d8b93416b72b7e6e94bacb70450b94033cb5c00308b1a5d0960b71e`

````text
Write a decision memo of 600 to 700 words from Rowan Blake, visitor operations manager at Kestrel Museum, to the museum director.

Use exactly these headings in this order: Recommendation, Pilot Record, Operational Risks, August Plan. Recommend whether the museum should replace all staffed audio-guide desks with self-service lockers, abandon the lockers, or run a limited transition. Do not add an executive summary or a closing section.

Record:

- The museum currently issues audio guides from two staffed desks in the entrance hall.
- During a 21-day June pilot, one desk remained staffed and the other was replaced with 30 self-service lockers.
- Visitors unlocked a device by scanning the barcode on their admission ticket.
- The pilot covered 6,420 admissions. Staffed-desk records show 1,106 audio-guide issues. Locker logs show 984 successful issues.
- The two issue totals came from separate service points. The museum did not assign visitors between them, so the totals do not support a controlled comparison of demand.
- Median transaction time was 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the staffed desk, based on timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point.
- Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. Forty-eight involved unreadable ticket screens, 39 involved a locker that did not open on the first attempt, and 30 involved visitors asking how to change the device language.
- These categories were recorded as one primary reason per intervention.
- Eleven devices were returned to the wrong locker. Staff located all eleven before closing on the same day.
- Two devices were missing at closing during the pilot. One was returned the next morning. The other was not recovered.
- During the 21 days before the pilot, one device issued from the staffed desks was not recovered. Visitor volume in that earlier period was 5,870 admissions.
- The pilot schedule moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours each day.
- The museum did not measure whether the additional gallery coverage changed visitor questions, rule compliance, or incident rates.
- Accessibility staff observed that the locker screen could not be used independently by three visitors with low vision during the pilot.
- The supplier says an audio-navigation update is planned for September but has not provided a release date or test version.
- The museum's busiest month is August. Daily admissions in August 2025 averaged 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average.
- The locker system has not been tested at August visitor volume.
- The supplier can add ten lockers by July 27 for £2,800.
- Visitor Operations can keep one staffed desk open and assign one employee to the locker area throughout August.
- A full move to lockers would remove both staffed desks. The record contains no approved staffing plan for that option.

Use only this record. Do not assign causes or outcomes that the record does not establish. Return only the finished memo.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 2 P6, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `5cb06230c2d3e024389f7d1e05a4f9fcbf4ae090009b4abf72531f452cf39cd6`

````text
Write a 600 to 700 word email newsletter for customers of Alder Energy explaining its new evening electricity rate. Give the newsletter a specific subject line and title. Use at most three body headings.

The newsletter should help a household understand when the rate applies, how the first bill will be calculated, and what choices are available.

Facts:

- The new rate applies from October 1, 2026, to residential customers with a working smart meter on the Home Flex plan.
- Electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. on weekdays will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour.
- Electricity used at all other times will cost 21 pence per kilowatt-hour.
- Weekends and public holidays use the 21-pence rate all day.
- The time band is based on when electricity is used, not when an appliance is started or when the bill is issued.
- Standing charges do not change.
- The first bill after October 1 may contain usage from before and after the change. Usage before October 1 will be charged under the customer's previous tariff.
- A household that uses 6 kilowatt-hours between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity. The same 6 kilowatt-hours outside that period would cost £1.26. These examples exclude the standing charge.
- Customers can view half-hour usage in the Alder Energy app or download it as a CSV file.
- The app data normally appears by noon the following day. It can take up to 72 hours when meter data is delayed.
- Customers may leave Home Flex without an exit fee.
- Customers who switch plans before September 20 will have the new plan applied by October 1. Later requests may take up to ten working days.
- Alder Energy will not remotely control customer appliances.
- The company has not estimated how much an individual household will save or spend under the new rate.
- Customers without a working smart meter will remain on their existing tariff until a meter is installed or repaired.

Do not claim that the new rate will lower a customer's bill, change energy use, or help the electricity grid. Do not add lifestyle advice beyond what is needed to explain the customer's options. Return only the finished newsletter.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 2 P7, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `9f1c805737e528f8b5d97869733f9a39a576235cecaf4b2ced7c6fd06cbca8b2`

````text
Write a 520 to 620 word spoken script for a five-minute video by Nico, who runs a small ceramics studio and teaches weekend classes.

The script should sound natural when read aloud. Nico is explaining why he stopped offering a “make a mug in one afternoon” class and what will replace it. Do not add stage directions, shot notes, a title, or a request to like, subscribe, or comment.

Facts Nico can state:

- The class ran on 14 Saturdays between January and April 2026.
- A total of 112 people attended. Each class had eight places and every place was booked.
- Participants shaped a mug in the first two hours. Studio staff handled drying, bisque firing, glazing, and the final firing after participants left.
- The advertised collection time was three weeks after class.
- Fifty-eight mugs were ready within three weeks. Forty-one were ready during week four. Thirteen took five weeks or longer.
- Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. Those participants were offered a replacement made by Nico or a place in another class.
- The booking page said that cracking was possible, but the three-week collection time appeared in the headline.
- Staff spent an average of 46 minutes finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each participant's mug after class.
- Nico had estimated 25 minutes per mug when setting the £48 class price.
- The studio did not track whether delays affected later bookings or shop sales.
- Nico received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. The emails came from 18 participants.
- Beginning in June, the studio will offer a two-session mug class for £72. The sessions will be two weeks apart.
- Participants will shape the mug in session one and glaze it in session two.
- Collection will be scheduled two weeks after the second session.
- The new format has not yet been tested.
- Nico will run four classes before deciding whether to keep the format.

Nico should accept responsibility for the original promise without turning the script into a dramatic confession or a sales pitch. Make no claims beyond the supplied facts. Return only the finished script.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 2 P7, Humanizer (R1 to R4)</summary>

````text
- The advertised collection time was three weeks after class.
- Fifty-eight mugs were ready within three weeks. Forty-one were ready during week four. Thirteen took five weeks or longer.
- Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. Those participants were offered a replacement made by Nico or a place in another class.
- The booking page said that cracking was possible, but the three-week collection time appeared in the headline.
- Staff spent an average of 46 minutes finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each participant's mug after class.
- Nico had estimated 25 minutes per mug when setting the £48 class price.
- The studio did not track whether delays affected later bookings or shop sales.
- Nico received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. The emails came from 18 participants.
- Beginning in June, the studio will offer a two-session mug class for £72. The sessions will be two weeks apart.
- Participants will shape the mug in session one and glaze it in session two.
- Collection will be scheduled two weeks after the second session.
- The new format has not yet been tested.
- Nico will run four classes before deciding whether to keep the format.

Nico should accept responsibility for the original promise without turning the script into a dramatic confession or a sales pitch. Make no claims beyond the supplied facts. Return only the finished script.
````

</details>

<details><summary>Stage 2 P8, No skill (R1 to R4)</summary>

Prompt SHA-256: `440f3075e274b0cef6083cb15a98fb8a1f42771a181f3b661af67f7c89c6d464`

````text
Write an opinion essay of 850 to 950 words for a publication read by university instructors and academic administrators.

Argue for or against this position: A university should require instructors to state permitted AI use on each assignment rather than relying only on one institution-wide policy.

Build the argument from the supplied record. Give the essay a specific title. Use continuous prose with no body headings or bullet points. You may make reasoned judgments, but do not cite outside research or invent facts.

Record:

- Calder University adopted an institution-wide student AI policy in August 2025.
- The policy allows instructors to permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI for assessed work.
- It requires students to disclose permitted AI use but does not prescribe wording for individual assignments.
- In February 2026, the university reviewed 240 assignment briefs from 60 undergraduate modules.
- Ninety-four briefs explicitly stated whether and how students could use generative AI.
- Fifty-seven referred only to the institution-wide policy.
- Eighty-nine did not mention AI.
- A brief that did not mention AI may still have been accompanied by verbal instructions. The review examined written briefs only.
- The student union surveyed 1,860 students in March 2026 and received 623 responses.
- Of those respondents, 381 said they had been unsure about permitted AI use on at least one assignment during the academic year.
- The survey did not identify the assignment briefs seen by each respondent and cannot connect uncertainty to any of the three brief categories.
- In the same survey, 207 respondents said rules differed between two assignments in the same module. The survey did not ask whether those differences were intentional.
- The Academic Integrity Office recorded 73 AI-related case referrals from September 2025 through March 2026.
- Twenty-one referrals were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation.
- A dismissal does not establish that the student used AI or that clearer wording would have prevented the referral.
- The remaining referrals had several outcomes: 18 findings of a violation, 14 findings of no violation, and 20 cases still open on April 1.
- The university's engineering school added a required “AI use for this assignment” box to its brief template in January 2026.
- A February audit found that 46 of 50 sampled engineering briefs contained a completed box.
- The audit checked whether the box was completed. It did not assess whether the wording was clear or whether students followed it.
- The history department voted against a required box. Its chair wrote that some assignments need a paragraph of explanation and others need no AI-specific instruction beyond the university policy.
- The student union supports an assignment-level statement with three options: prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure.
- The teaching committee will vote in May on whether every assessed assignment must contain an AI-use statement. The proposal allows instructors to add explanation after the statement.

Address the strongest practical objection to the position you take. Keep the limits of the evidence clear without turning the essay into a neutral report. Return only the finished essay.
````

</details>

# Appendix B: Complete outputs and output-level evidence

Every accepted output appears below. The detected issues reflect the audited evaluation records. Each issue includes the operational pattern, exact excerpt, and explanation.

## B.1 Stage 1 outputs

<details><summary>Stage 1 P1 R1, No skill</summary>

- Run ID: `P1_R1_C0`

- Accepted attempt: 1

- Prompt SHA-256: `e87ff17736dcaf03d88e9a38dddb1d129e05552cff21cf95e0755ef6098b715f`

- Output SHA-256: `adea44a817c1b55d437eb92e5c2bfce7e96690c0e6f57786e6a2c6eeae758b73`

- Total words: 133

- Flagged words: 18 (13.53%)

Detected issues:

- P009, Authored em dash: The sentence uses an authored em dash as punctuation in newly written prose, and it is not part of quoted material, a title, a proper name, code, or discussion of the mark.

````text
We also recorded two missed handoffs during the experiment—the same number as in the preceding six weeks.
````

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P1_R1_C0 -->
````text
For six weeks, my four-person remote design studio replaced our Monday status meeting with written updates.

Before the experiment, all four of us joined a 45-minute video call every Monday. Instead, each person answered four questions: What shipped? What is stuck? What decision is needed? What happens next?

We posted 22 of 24 updates on time.

When an update revealed a decision that couldn’t wait, we held a 15-minute call. That happened in four of the six weeks.

We also recorded two missed handoffs during the experiment—the same number as in the preceding six weeks.

For our team, these results are enough to keep testing the practice. We’ll continue using written updates for three more months, with short calls when a decision can’t wait, and then review it again.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P1_R1_C0 -->

</details>

````text
Six weeks ago, I started an experiment with our four-person remote design studio. Instead of putting everyone on a 45-minute video call every Monday, each of us posted a written update answering four questions: What shipped? What is stuck? What decision is needed? What happens next?

We posted 22 of 24 updates on time. When an update revealed a decision that could not wait, we held a 15-minute call. That happened in four of the six weeks.

We also recorded two missed handoffs, the same number as in the six weeks before the experiment.

I’m not calling the experiment a sweeping success. The numbers are the numbers. We’ll continue the practice for three more months, then review it again. For now, our weekly status meeting stays written.
````

````text
Before the experiment, all four of us joined a 45-minute video call every Monday. Instead, each person answered four questions: What shipped? What is stuck? What decision is needed? What happens next?

We posted 22 of 24 updates on time. When an update surfaced a decision that couldn’t wait, we held a 15-minute call. That happened in four of the six weeks.

We also recorded two missed handoffs during the experiment—the same number as in the preceding six weeks.

For now, we’re continuing the practice for three more months. Then we’ll review it again. For anyone leading a small remote team, that’s our result so far: fewer scheduled meeting minutes, with the same number of missed handoffs.
````

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P2_R1_C0 -->
````text
After Closing Time

Marlow’s first experiment with keeping libraries open as cooling centers produced a clear result before it produced a clear verdict: people used the extra hours. During the summer of 2025, three neighborhood libraries extended their usual closing time from 6 p.m. to 9 p.m. on 12 days when the forecast high reached at least 38°C. Door counters recorded 2,840 entries across those days, up from 1,760 on the same calendar dates in 2024. More than a third of the 2025 traffic—1,079 entries—came after 6 p.m., when the buildings ordinarily would have been closed.

Those figures show substantial use, but they do not reveal exactly how many people came through the doors. The counters measured entries rather than unique visitors, so anyone who left a library and later returned was counted again. The comparison with 2024 also has limits. It covers the same dates, but only the 2025 libraries offered the three additional evening hours. The higher count is therefore consistent with the longer schedule and demand for a cool public place, but it cannot establish how much of the increase resulted from extreme heat, the extension itself, or repeat entries.

A voluntary survey offered a partial picture of what brought visitors in. It received 146 responses, a small share of the recorded traffic, and respondents could choose more than one reason. Eighty-nine said their homes were uncomfortably hot, while 37 reported having no working air conditioner. Fifty-two said they came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. The overlap matters: the answers suggest that relief from heat and access to everyday services were not necessarily separate needs. They do not, however, describe everyone who entered. Because the survey was voluntary, its results cannot be assumed to represent all visitors, and the multiple-choice format means its categories cannot simply be added together.

Use was also uneven among the participating branches. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, evidence that demand there sometimes surpassed the space available. North Hill, by contrast, never used more than 60 percent of its seats. The transportation assistance distributed through the pilot followed a similar pattern. Visitors used 212 bus vouchers, and 61 percent of those vouchers were redeemed at Riverside. Together, the seating and voucher figures point to a particularly strong concentration of use at that branch, though the available data do not explain why Riverside drew more demand or whether transportation, location, household conditions, or some combination accounted for the difference.

The pilot cost $18,600. Staff overtime was the largest expense, at $11,200, followed by $4,900 for security. The remaining $2,500 paid for bottled water and bus vouchers. These totals establish what the city spent to operate the extended hours and provide support, but they do not by themselves measure the program’s value. There is no count of unique users, no estimate of how long visitors stayed, and no comparison with other possible ways to provide cooling access. Nor does the voucher total show how many people used them, since the figures describe vouchers redeemed rather than individual riders.

On the most immediate measure of safety inside the libraries, the 12-day pilot passed without a reported crisis: no medical emergency was reported at any of the three participating branches. A broader citywide number was encouraging but much harder to interpret. Emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. The pilot might appear to fit within that decline, but the data cannot show that the libraries caused it. The two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group—a similar population without access to the program—against which to judge the result. The decline could reflect weather differences or other factors not captured by the pilot data.

What Marlow can say with confidence is narrower. The libraries recorded more entries than on the same dates a year earlier; 1,079 entries occurred during the added hours; some survey respondents lacked adequate cooling at home; and Riverside reached capacity repeatedly. The city can also say that the program operated without a reported medical emergency. It cannot say how many distinct residents were served, whether the cooling centers reduced heat illness, or whether the year-to-year increase in traffic was entirely attributable to the pilot.

For the following summer, the council chose a more targeted version rather than an identical repeat. It approved renewed cooling-center hours at Riverside and Central, leaving North Hill out of the next round. The extensions will be triggered by heat alerts and announced with 24 hours’ notice. The council also authorized more charging strips and more bus vouchers at both participating branches. The decision preserves the central idea of the pilot—turning familiar public buildings into evening refuges during dangerous heat—while concentrating resources at two locations and expanding two forms of assistance visitors used.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P2_R1_C0 -->

</details>
