<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P2_R1_C1 -->
````text
After Closing Time

For 12 days in 2025, three neighborhood libraries in Marlow stayed open three hours later than usual. The extension applied when the forecast high reached at least 38°C, moving closing time from 6 p.m. to 9 p.m. The idea was simple: keep public space available into the evening, when the branches would ordinarily be locked. The records show that people used those extra hours. They do not, on their own, show who benefited most or whether the pilot prevented anyone from becoming ill.

Door counters recorded 2,840 entries across the three libraries on those 12 days. On the same calendar dates in 2024, they recorded 1,760. Of the 2025 total, 1,079 entries came after 6 p.m., during the hours created by the pilot. Those figures give the city a clear measure of traffic through the doors, but only traffic. The counters tracked entries, not individual visitors. A person who stepped out and came back was counted again. The numbers therefore cannot establish that 2,840 different people visited, or even how many unique visitors there were.

The comparison with 2024 is suggestive, though less conclusive than it may first appear. Entries rose on the dates in question, and almost all of the numerical difference between the two annual totals came after the normal closing hour. Still, the figures do not isolate the effect of the extended schedule from every other difference between the two years. They also cannot say what visitors would have done if the libraries had closed at 6. What they establish is narrower: the branches recorded substantial use after their usual closing time on the days covered by the pilot.

A voluntary survey provides a closer, if incomplete, look at why some people came. It drew 146 responses, a small share of the entries recorded, and respondents could choose more than one reason. Eighty-nine said their home was uncomfortably hot. Thirty-seven reported having no working air conditioner. Fifty-two said they came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. Those answers show that heat, connectivity and access to power overlapped for at least some respondents. Because participation was voluntary and entries were not unique people, the survey cannot be treated as a profile of everyone who used the libraries.

Demand was also uneven. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings. North Hill, by contrast, never filled more than 60 percent of its seats. That split matters for interpreting the combined attendance count: a busy pilot overall did not mean that every branch faced the same pressure. The bus-voucher figures point in the same direction. Visitors used 212 vouchers, and 61 percent were redeemed at Riverside. The available data do not explain why Riverside drew more demand, but they gave the city a practical reason to distinguish among locations when deciding what to do next.

The 12-day pilot cost $18,600. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security for $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers for $2,500. These expenses describe what it took to operate the longer hours under the pilot. They do not produce a cost per person helped, since the city does not know the number of unique visitors or how many visits would have happened during ordinary hours anyway. Nor can the voucher count show how many people needed transportation but did not receive it.

No participating branch reported a medical emergency. Citywide emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. That decline may sound like evidence that the cooling centers improved public health, but the study design cannot support that conclusion. The summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group. A citywide change also cannot be attributed to a program operating at three libraries on 12 days. The pilot may have offered relief, but neither the entry counters nor the emergency-room total can tell whether it prevented a medical crisis.

For the following summer, the council chose a smaller footprint and a more targeted trigger. It approved another pilot at Riverside and Central, leaving North Hill out. Instead of using the forecast threshold described for 2025, the new plan will open the branches in response to heat alerts, with 24 hours' notice. The council also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both participating branches.

The decision follows the clearest patterns in the limited record. People used the libraries after 6 p.m.; many survey respondents described hot homes, failed air conditioning or a need for Wi-Fi and power; and Riverside repeatedly ran out of seats. At the same time, the council did not treat a drop in heat-related emergency-room visits as proof of success. The next summer's two-branch pilot will repeat the service where the council approved it, with added capacity for charging and transportation, while leaving the larger question of health impact unanswered.
````

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P2_R1_C2 -->
````text
After Closing Time

Marlow’s library cooling-center pilot began with a narrow change to the clock. On 12 days in 2025, when the forecast high reached at least 38°C, three neighborhood libraries stayed open until 9 p.m. instead of closing at 6. The extra hours gave residents another place to spend three evening hours. The city offered bottled water and bus vouchers, treating transportation and basic supplies as part of access to the cooling centers.

Door counters registered 2,840 entries across those 12 days. On the same calendar dates in 2024, the libraries recorded 1,760. The year-to-year rise shows that more trips through the doors occurred on the pilot days. It does not show how many individuals used the libraries. The counters logged entries, so a visitor who stepped out and returned appeared twice. They cannot reveal why someone came, how long anyone stayed, or whether the later closing time caused the full increase. The city had no comparison group of similar branches operating on their regular schedules.

The timing offers firmer evidence that residents used the extended hours. Of the 2,840 entries, 1,079 came after 6 p.m., when the branches would have closed under their usual schedules. Those counts include repeat entries, but they place more than a thousand door crossings inside the new three-hour window. They establish use of the service, though they cannot establish how many people depended on it or what those visits prevented.

A voluntary survey adds context, with limits of its own. The city received 146 responses, from people who chose to answer. Respondents could name more than one reason for visiting. Eighty-nine said their homes felt uncomfortably hot, and 37 reported having no working air conditioner. Fifty-two came in part to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. A cooling-center visit could meet several needs at once. The survey documents the circumstances of those respondents, but it cannot describe every visitor or supply percentages for unique users because the city did not count unique users.

Demand varied by branch. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, evidence that the branch faced crowding during part of the pilot. North Hill never filled more than 60 percent of its seats. Those results argue against treating the three locations as interchangeable. They do not explain the difference. The available facts do not show whether transportation, neighborhood conditions, branch location, or another factor produced the uneven use.

Bus-voucher records point in the same direction without settling the question. Visitors used 212 vouchers, and Riverside accounted for 61 percent of redemptions. That concentration may reflect greater transportation needs among Riverside visitors, heavier demand at the branch, or both. The records cannot distinguish among those possibilities. They show that visitors used the vouchers, with most redemptions occurring at the branch that exceeded capacity.

The city spent $18,600 on the 12-day program. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security for $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers for $2,500. Those figures provide a clear accounting of the pilot’s direct expenses. They do not measure benefits such as comfort, access to charging, or time away from an uncomfortably hot home. Nor do they show whether another design could have served the same visitors at a lower cost. With no count of unique visitors, a cost-per-person calculation would rest on an unknown denominator.

No participating branch reported a medical emergency. That describes what occurred inside the libraries, but it does not prove that the pilot prevented heat illness. Across Marlow, emergency-room visits for heat illness fell 9 percent from the previous summer. The two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group. The decline could reflect the weather difference, the cooling centers, other unmeasured factors, or some combination. The data cannot assign the change to three libraries or connect any citywide emergency-room visit to a person’s use of a branch.

For the following summer, the council chose a smaller repeat instead of continuing at all three branches. It voted to operate the pilot at Riverside and Central, leaving out North Hill. Heat alerts, with 24 hours’ notice, will trigger the extended service. The council approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both branches.

The decision concentrates resources at two sites while responding to two uses documented during the pilot: transportation help and access to power. The 2025 record supports a measured conclusion. Residents used the added hours, Riverside strained against its seating limit, and survey respondents reported hot homes and practical needs. Whether the pilot reduced illness across Marlow remains unanswered.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P2_R1_C2 -->

````text
After Closing Time

On 12 days in the summer of 2025, Marlow kept three neighborhood libraries open until 9 p.m., three hours past their usual 6 p.m. closing time. The extension took effect when the forecast high reached at least 38°C. Across those days, door counters at the participating branches registered 2,840 entries. On the same calendar dates in 2024, they had registered 1,760. The rise is a measure of increased traffic through the doors, though it is not a count of how many people used the libraries.

The counters recorded entries, not unique visitors. A person who stepped outside and came back was counted twice, so the 2,840 entries could include repeat visits during a day. The year-to-year comparison also combines the regular daytime hours with the added evening hours. It says the buildings received more entries on the selected dates in 2025 than on those dates in 2024. It cannot establish how much of that increase came from the later closing time, and it cannot turn the entry total into a visitor total.

The clearest measure of use during the extension is the timing of the counts. Of the 2,840 entries in 2025, 1,079 occurred after 6 p.m., when the libraries ordinarily would have been closed. Those entries establish that people came through the doors during the hours created by the pilot. They do not establish whether any of those entries replaced visits that otherwise would have occurred before 6 p.m. The pilot supplied access in the evening; the counters describe use of that access, not the net change in library use caused by it.

Marlow spent $18,600 on the 12-day pilot. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security for $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers for $2,500. Visitors used 212 bus vouchers, with 61 percent redeemed at the Riverside branch. That concentration is consistent with heavier voucher use at Riverside, but the figures do not identify why the branch accounted for most redemptions or how many voucher users would otherwise have been unable to reach a cooling center. Nor can the spending be converted into a cost per visitor, because the door counters did not identify individual visitors.

A voluntary survey added a partial view of why some people came. It received 146 responses, and respondents could choose more than one reason. Eighty-nine said their home was uncomfortably hot. Thirty-seven said they had no working air conditioner. Fifty-two said they came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. The responses do not say how much those groups overlapped: some of the 37 without a working air conditioner may also be among the 89 who called home uncomfortable, and either group may include people who also wanted Wi-Fi or power. The counts therefore cannot divide respondents into separate groups. Because participation was voluntary and covered only 146 responses, the survey describes the people who answered, not every person counted at the doors.

Use was uneven across the branches. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings. North Hill never used more than 60 percent of its seats. The contrast indicates that the pilot encountered crowding at one branch while another retained seats, but it does not explain the difference or establish that demand would follow the same pattern under different alert dates. Riverside also accounted for 61 percent of redeemed bus vouchers, although the data do not establish a connection between voucher redemptions and the evenings when seating exceeded capacity.

No medical emergency was reported at any participating branch. Across Marlow, emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. That citywide decline cannot be credited to the library pilot from the available evidence. The two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group. The emergency-room figure covers the city, while the pilot involved three libraries on 12 forecast-triggered days. It records what happened in the same broad period, not what would have happened without the extended hours.

For the following summer, the council voted to repeat the pilot at Riverside and Central only. North Hill will not participate. The extension will be triggered by heat alerts, with 24 hours’ notice, rather than by the 2025 rule of a forecast high reaching at least 38°C. The council also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both branches, carrying two services used during the first pilot into the next round while concentrating the program at two locations.
````

````text
After Closing Time

Marlow’s 2025 library cooling-center pilot kept three neighborhood branches open three hours beyond their usual 6 p.m. closing time, until 9 p.m., on 12 days when the forecast high reached at least 38°C. Across those days, door counters recorded 2,840 entries, compared with 1,760 on the same calendar dates in 2024. Of the 2025 total, 1,079 entries occurred after 6 p.m., suggesting that the extended hours met substantial demand at a time when the buildings would ordinarily have been closed.

The comparison is striking but narrower than it may first appear. The increase between the two years was almost identical to the number of entries recorded during the added evening hours. That is consistent with the pilot drawing people into the libraries after 6 p.m. while daytime traffic remained broadly similar to the previous year. It does not, however, show how many individual residents used the cooling centers. Door counters measure entries, not unique visitors: anyone who left a branch and later returned was counted again. The totals therefore describe movement through the doors, not a head count of people served.

A small voluntary survey offers some insight into why visitors came, though it cannot establish the experience of everyone who entered. The survey received 146 responses, and respondents could choose more than one reason. Eighty-nine said their homes were uncomfortably hot, while 37 reported having no working air conditioner. Fifty-two said they came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. Those answers show that heat relief overlapped with ordinary needs for connectivity and power. Because participation was voluntary and represented only a fraction of the recorded entries, the results cannot be treated as a representative profile of all visitors.

Use also varied sharply by branch. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, evidence that demand there sometimes surpassed the space available. North Hill, by contrast, never used more than 60 percent of its seats. The bus-voucher figures point to the same uneven pattern, although they do not explain it: visitors used 212 vouchers, and 61 percent were redeemed at Riverside. The available data cannot determine whether Riverside’s heavier use reflected greater neighborhood need, easier access, stronger awareness of the program or some other factor. It does show that a uniform schedule across all three locations did not produce uniform demand.

The pilot cost $18,600. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security for $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers for $2,500. Those figures establish what the city spent to provide the extended hours and related support. They do not yield a reliable cost per person, because the city did not count unique visitors. Dividing the total cost by door entries would produce a cost per entry, including repeat entries, rather than a cost for each resident served.

No medical emergency was reported at any participating branch. That is an important operational result, but it is not evidence that the pilot prevented medical crises elsewhere. Citywide emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. The city had no comparison group, and the two summers differed in weather severity. As a result, the decline cannot be attributed to the library program. It could reflect differences in the heat, the effects of other circumstances, or some combination that the pilot data were not designed to separate.

The same limitations apply to broader claims about public health. The figures show that people used the branches, especially during the added hours, and that some survey respondents lacked comfortable or adequately cooled homes. They also show crowding at Riverside and lighter use at North Hill. They cannot show how long visitors stayed, how many were repeat users, whether they would otherwise have remained in dangerous heat, or whether access to a branch changed anyone’s medical outcome.

For the following summer, the council chose a more targeted version of the program. It voted to repeat the pilot at Riverside and Central only, leaving North Hill out. Extended operations would be triggered by heat alerts, with 24 hours’ notice, rather than by the previous threshold of a forecast high of at least 38°C. The council also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both participating branches. The decision preserved the basic idea of keeping libraries available during dangerous heat while concentrating the next round at two locations and adding resources tied to needs visible in the pilot: transportation, device charging and, at Riverside in particular, heavy evening demand.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P2_R2_C0 -->

````text
After Closing Time

On 12 days in 2025, when Marlow's forecast high reached at least 38°C, three neighborhood libraries stayed open three hours later than usual. Instead of closing at 6 p.m., they kept their doors open until 9. The cooling-center pilot was narrow in duration and simple in design: use public buildings already spread across the city to offer a place to sit through the hottest part of the day and into the evening.

The door counters registered 2,840 entries across the three branches on those 12 days. On the same calendar dates in 2024, they had recorded 1,760. That is a substantial rise in traffic, but it is not a count of people. The counters recorded every entry, so a visitor who stepped out and came back appeared twice. The totals cannot say how many individual residents used the libraries, how long they stayed, or whether the extended hours caused the full increase.

One figure gets closer to measuring the pilot itself. Of the 2,840 entries in 2025, 1,079 came after 6 p.m., when the libraries ordinarily would have been closed. Those entries show that the additional hours were used. They still do not reveal how many distinct people came in, or what those visitors would have done had the branches closed at the usual time. The comparison with 2024 is useful context, not a controlled test.

Demand was also uneven. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, while North Hill never filled more than 60 percent of its seats. The bus-voucher numbers point in the same direction. Visitors used 212 vouchers, and 61 percent of them were redeemed at Riverside. Together, those figures suggest that Riverside faced the greatest pressure of the three branches. They cannot explain why the branches drew different levels of use, since the pilot collected no evidence that would isolate a cause.

The voluntary survey adds detail, though it represents only the 146 people who chose to respond. Respondents could select more than one reason for visiting. Eighty-nine said their homes were uncomfortably hot, and 37 said they had no working air conditioner. Fifty-two came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. The overlap matters: these categories describe reasons, not separate groups of visitors. The results show that heat drove many respondents to the libraries and that some also needed connectivity or power. They do not establish the motives of everyone counted at the doors.

The pilot cost $18,600. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security for $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers for $2,500. No medical emergency was reported at any participating branch. That is reassuring as an account of what happened inside the libraries, but it does not show that the pilot prevented emergencies. There is no record here of what would have occurred without the later closing time.

Citywide emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than during the previous summer. That number may look like evidence of a broader health benefit, but the available data cannot support that conclusion. The two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group. Changes in emergency-room visits could reflect the weather or other factors that the pilot data did not measure. Nor can a citywide figure identify an effect from three branches open late on 12 days.

The clearest finding is modest but useful: residents made substantial use of hours that had not previously been available, especially at Riverside. The survey responses are consistent with the idea that the libraries provided relief to people dealing with hot homes, limited air conditioning, and practical needs such as Wi-Fi and device charging. Yet a tally of entries, a small voluntary survey, and an uncontrolled comparison between summers cannot answer the larger question of how much the program improved health.

For the following summer, the council chose a smaller repeat rather than a citywide expansion. It approved the pilot at Riverside and Central only, with openings triggered by heat alerts and 24 hours' notice. It also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both branches. The decision concentrated resources at Riverside, where capacity had been exceeded, while dropping North Hill, where seat use remained comparatively low. It preserved the part of the 2025 experiment the records support most clearly: on dangerously hot days, people did come through the library doors after closing time.
````

````text
After Closing Time

For 12 days in 2025, Marlow kept three neighborhood libraries open through the hottest part of the evening. Each date qualified because the forecast high reached at least 38°C. The branches extended their usual 6 p.m. closing time to 9 p.m., giving residents three extra hours indoors as daytime heat lingered. Door counters recorded 2,840 entries across those dates. On the same calendar dates in 2024, the libraries recorded 1,760.

The increase of 1,080 entries offers the clearest sign that use rose during the pilot. It does not provide a head count. The door counters logged each entry, so a visitor who left and came back added two to the total. The city therefore cannot say how many individuals used the branches, or how many visitors accounted for the year-to-year increase. The comparison also covers the full day, including hours the libraries would have operated without the pilot. Differences between the two summers could have affected daytime traffic as well.

The available figures include no hour-by-hour breakdown for 2024. They cannot show whether some visitors shifted trips from afternoon to evening, and they say nothing about how long anyone stayed. A person who spent three hours inside and one who stepped out after a few minutes each contributed one entry on arrival.

The evening count narrows the picture. Of the 2,840 entries in 2025, 1,079 came after 6 p.m., during hours that the three branches added for the cooling-center program. Those entries show substantial use of the extension. They still do not equal 1,079 people, and the available figures do not show how many evening visitors would have gone somewhere else had the libraries closed at 6.

A voluntary survey supplies a limited view of why visitors came. Among 146 respondents, 89 said their home was uncomfortably hot, while 37 reported having no working air conditioner. Fifty-two came in part to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. Respondents could choose more than one reason, so those groups overlap. The answers connect library use to heat, home cooling, and access to power or internet service. Yet 146 responses represent a small share of the recorded entries, and a voluntary survey cannot establish whether the respondents resembled all visitors.

Use varied by branch. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, a sign that demand there outstripped the available chairs at peak periods. North Hill used no more than 60 percent of its seats. Bus-voucher use showed a similar concentration: visitors used 212 vouchers, and 61 percent were redeemed at Riverside. The figures support a case for tailoring the next round by location. They do not explain why Riverside drew more demand, or whether transportation, neighborhood conditions, branch amenities, or repeat visits produced the difference.

Marlow spent $18,600 on the 12-day pilot. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security cost $4,900, and the city spent $2,500 on bottled water and bus vouchers. The available records do not divide those costs by branch or separate water spending from voucher spending. A cost per unique visitor would mislead because the counters did not track unique visitors.

No participating branch reported a medical emergency. That result describes what happened inside the three libraries; it cannot show how many emergencies the extended hours prevented. Across Marlow, emergency-room visits for heat illness fell 9 percent from the previous summer. The city had no comparison group, and the two summers differed in weather severity. Researchers would need a sound comparison to separate any effect of the library hours from weather and other influences. The citywide decline, on its own, cannot establish that the pilot reduced heat illness.

For the following summer, the council chose a smaller version of the program. It approved another round at Riverside and Central, leaving North Hill out. Heat alerts, with 24 hours’ notice, will trigger the extended service. The council approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both participating branches. The plan carries forward the pilot’s clearest findings: residents used the added evening hours, Riverside faced capacity pressure, and many survey respondents sought relief from hot homes or needed a place to connect and charge devices. Measuring unique visitors and creating a stronger comparison would help answer the larger question of whether the program improves health across the city.
````

````text
After Closing Time

In 2025, Marlow kept three neighborhood libraries open later when extreme heat was forecast. Their usual 6 p.m. closing time moved to 9 p.m. on 12 days when the forecast high reached at least 38°C. On each qualifying day, residents had three additional evening hours to enter the buildings. The threshold made the extension a limited measure, activated on selected days rather than maintained throughout the summer. It also tied the schedule to anticipated heat: the forecast, not a temperature recorded after the fact, determined when the doors stayed open.

Door counters logged 2,840 entries across the three branches on the 12 pilot days. On the same calendar dates in 2024, they had logged 1,760. The comparison points to heavier use during the pilot, but it does not translate directly into a count of people. The devices counted entries, not unique visitors. A person who stepped out and came back registered twice. Nor does the year-to-year comparison isolate the effect of later closing; the dates came from different summers. The most direct measure of activity during the extension is the 1,079 entries recorded after 6 p.m., when the libraries ordinarily would have been closed. That figure still counts movements through the doors, not individual residents helped.

Use was uneven among the branches. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, while North Hill never used more than 60 percent of its seats. Riverside therefore faced crowding on several nights as North Hill retained unused seating even at its busiest. The figures establish a difference in demand between those two locations during the pilot. They do not explain why the branches were used so differently, and they provide no basis for assuming that extra seats at North Hill could have served people who went to Riverside.

The pilot cost $18,600. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security for $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers for $2,500. The available figures give an overall cost for operating the extension across all three branches. They are not enough to calculate a separate cost per entry for each site. Visitors used 212 bus vouchers, and 61 percent were redeemed at Riverside. That concentration is consistent with Riverside’s capacity pressure.

A voluntary survey offered a closer look at why some visitors came. It received 146 responses. Respondents could select more than one reason, and the answers reflect needs beyond relief from heat. Eighty-nine respondents said their homes were uncomfortably hot. Thirty-seven said they had no working air conditioner. Fifty-two said they came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. The categories may overlap, so they cannot be added to produce a total number of visitors with separate needs.

The survey describes the people who chose to answer, not everyone counted at the doors. Because the counters did not identify unique visitors, the 146 responses cannot be converted into a response rate using the entry total. Voluntary participation also means the answers cannot establish how common uncomfortable homes, broken air conditioning, or the need for connectivity were among all visitors. What they do establish is narrower: each count reports a reason selected by respondents, with no requirement that anyone choose only one.

No medical emergency was reported at any participating branch. Citywide emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. Because the two summers differed in weather severity and the city had no comparison group, that decline cannot be attributed to the library pilot. Weather differences or other factors could account for some or all of the change. The branch reports cover emergencies at the participating libraries, while the emergency-room figure covers heat illness across the city.

For the following summer, the council voted to repeat the pilot at Riverside and Central; North Hill will not participate. The extension will be triggered by heat alerts, with 24 hours’ notice, rather than by the pilot’s forecast threshold. The new plan retains Riverside, where the seating limit had been exceeded, and removes North Hill, where use never rose above 60 percent of seats. Those facts describe the change but do not establish the council’s motive for selecting the two branches. The council also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both participating branches.
````

````text
After Closing Time

Marlow’s 2025 library cooling-center pilot was modest in scale: three neighborhood branches, 12 exceptionally hot days and three additional hours of access each evening. On days when the forecast high reached at least 38°C, the participating libraries postponed their usual 6 p.m. closing time until 9 p.m. The experiment offered residents an established public place to remain indoors during the early evening, when the libraries otherwise would have been closed.

Door counters registered 2,840 entries across the three branches on those 12 days. On the same calendar dates in 2024, they recorded 1,760. That is a substantial increase in traffic, but the comparison has limits. The counters measured entries, not individual people: anyone who stepped out and returned was counted again. The figures therefore cannot reveal how many distinct residents used the libraries, how long they stayed or whether the extended hours caused all of the year-to-year increase.

The timing of the entries provides clearer evidence that residents used the added hours. Of the 2,840 entries in 2025, 1,079 occurred after 6 p.m.—a period when the buildings normally would not have been open. Those counts do not show whether each visitor came primarily to escape the heat, and they still include possible repeat entries. But they establish that the evening extension was not merely available; it was used.

A voluntary survey offers a partial view of why people came. It received 146 responses, a small share relative to the entry count and not necessarily representative of everyone who visited. Respondents could choose more than one reason. Eighty-nine said their homes were uncomfortably hot, while 37 reported having no working air conditioner. Fifty-two said they came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. The overlapping answers suggest that the branches served more than one function at once: relief from hot homes could coincide with the need for internet access or electricity. Because the survey was voluntary and did not count unique visitors, however, it cannot establish how common any reason was among all users.

Demand also differed by location. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, indicating that at least one branch sometimes had more demand than it could accommodate. North Hill, by contrast, never used more than 60 percent of its seats. That contrast matters for judging the pilot’s reach. The aggregate entry total shows overall activity but can obscure uneven use among branches. It also helps explain why a repeat program need not reproduce the first pilot exactly at every location.

Transportation support showed a similar concentration. Visitors used 212 bus vouchers, and 61 percent of those vouchers were redeemed at Riverside. The pilot’s figures do not say why Riverside accounted for most redemptions, or whether vouchers enabled trips that otherwise would not have occurred. They do show that the transportation assistance was used and that its use was weighted toward the same branch that exceeded seating capacity.

The 12-day program cost $18,600. Staff overtime was the largest expense, at $11,200, followed by $4,900 for security. Bottled water and bus vouchers together cost $2,500. Those amounts describe what the city spent to keep three libraries open later and provide the associated support. They do not, on their own, calculate a cost per person: the door count is not a count of people, and the available facts do not identify how many unique residents benefited.

No medical emergency was reported at any participating branch. That is reassuring as an account of what occurred inside the pilot sites, but it does not prove that the extended hours prevented heat illness. Citywide emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than during the previous summer. Yet the two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group. The decline therefore cannot be attributed to the libraries. Without a comparable set of residents or locations that lacked the program—and without equivalent weather conditions—the data cannot separate any effect of the pilot from broader differences between the two summers.

What the evidence supports is narrower but still useful. Residents made substantial use of the branches on the designated days, more than a thousand entries occurred during the newly available evening hours, and survey respondents frequently identified uncomfortable homes among their reasons for coming. Riverside faced capacity pressure, while North Hill retained ample space. Bus-voucher use, like crowding, was concentrated at Riverside. What the evidence does not support is a precise count of people served, a representative account of all their reasons, or a claim that the pilot reduced medical harm citywide.

For the following summer, the council approved a more targeted version. The pilot will return at Riverside and Central, but not North Hill. Instead of being tied to the forecast-high threshold used in 2025, the extension will be triggered by heat alerts with 24 hours’ notice. The council also approved additional charging strips and bus vouchers at both participating branches. The decision preserves the late-hours program while narrowing its footprint and expanding two forms of practical assistance that visitors used during the first summer.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P2_R3_C0 -->

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P2_R3_C2 -->
````text
After Closing Time

For 12 days in the summer of 2025, three neighborhood libraries in Marlow stayed open three hours past their usual 6 p.m. closing time. The city activated the pilot when the forecast high reached at least 38°C, keeping the branches open as cooling centers until 9 p.m. Door counters logged 2,840 entries across those days. On the same calendar dates in 2024, they had logged 1,760. The increase offers the clearest sign that more people came through the doors during the pilot, while the timing shows that the added hours mattered: 1,079 entries occurred after 6 p.m., when the buildings would have closed.

Those numbers require care. The counters recorded each entry, not each person. A visitor who stepped out and came back added two entries, and the data do not show how often that happened. The comparison with 2024 covers the same dates, not a matched set of days with the same temperatures or heat alerts. It shows a rise in door traffic during the pilot period. It cannot tell the city how many individual residents used the cooling centers, how many came because of the heat, or how much of the increase resulted from the longer hours rather than other differences between the two summers.

A voluntary survey adds some detail, though its 146 responses represent a small, self-selected portion of the recorded entries. Respondents could choose more than one reason for visiting. Eighty-nine said their homes were uncomfortably hot, and 37 said they had no working air conditioner. Fifty-two came in part to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. The answers show that heat, connectivity and access to power overlapped for some respondents. They do not establish the circumstances of everyone who entered, and the survey design does not support estimates of how common each need was among all users.

Use varied by branch. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, evidence that demand surpassed the available seating at peak times. North Hill never filled more than 60 percent of its seats. The gap suggests that location mattered, though the pilot data do not explain why. Bus-voucher use points in the same direction. Visitors used 212 vouchers, and they redeemed 61 percent of them at Riverside. The figures do not show whether transportation access drove Riverside’s higher demand or reflected another pattern of branch use.

The city spent $18,600 on the 12-day pilot. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security for $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers for $2,500. Those costs describe what Marlow paid to operate the extra hours at three branches. They do not provide a cost per visitor, because the counters cannot identify unique visitors. Nor do they isolate the cost of each service within the combined water-and-voucher category. The spending record can guide planning for another summer, but it cannot answer whether the program delivered more benefit per dollar than another heat response would have.

No participating branch had a reported medical emergency. Citywide, emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. The two facts may sound encouraging, but they do not demonstrate that the library pilot reduced heat illness. The summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group. Citywide emergency-room data cannot identify what happened to people who used the libraries, or what would have happened had the branches closed at 6 p.m. The pilot may have given visitors relief from hot homes and access to water, transportation, Wi-Fi or power. The available evidence measures use and reported reasons, not health outcomes.

For the following summer, the council chose a smaller version of the program. It approved another pilot at Riverside and Central, leaving North Hill out. Heat alerts will trigger the extended service, with 24 hours’ notice. The council approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both branches. Those choices track two findings from 2025: Riverside faced crowding, while many survey respondents cited device charging or Wi-Fi and visitors used the transportation assistance.

The new plan does not settle every question raised by the first summer. Reducing the program from three branches to two concentrates resources where the council chose to continue, but the 2025 data cannot show how residents who might have used North Hill will fare. A heat-alert trigger with one day’s notice changes the activation rule from the forecast threshold used in 2025. Marlow will enter the next summer with evidence that residents used the late hours, with the strongest signs of demand at Riverside, and with no sound basis for claiming that the pilot caused the citywide decline in heat-related emergency-room visits.
````

````text
After Closing Time

On 12 days in 2025, three neighborhood libraries in Marlow stayed open until 9 p.m., three hours beyond their usual 6 p.m. closing. The extension applied when the forecast high reached at least 38°C. Door counters at the branches registered 2,840 entries on those days, compared with 1,760 entries on the same calendar dates in 2024.

A door counter records a passage through the entrance, not the identity of the person making it. Someone who left a branch and returned was counted twice. The 2025 and 2024 totals therefore measure traffic rather than unique visitors, and they provide no way to separate first arrivals from repeat entries.

The year-to-year comparison has another limit. It matches calendar dates, but the two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group. The totals establish that the counters recorded more entries on the 2025 pilot days than on the corresponding dates in 2024. They cannot isolate the longer hours as the cause of the difference because the comparison does not hold conditions constant.

Use of the added hours is easier to see. Among the 2,840 entries in 2025, 1,079 occurred after 6 p.m., when the participating branches would ordinarily have closed. That count records people passing through the doors during the extension. It still measures entries rather than individuals, and it says nothing about the length of a visit. Even so, it captures activity that could not have occurred inside those libraries under their usual closing schedule.

The branches did not face the same pressure. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings. North Hill never used more than 60 percent of its seats. One site therefore ran beyond its stated seating limit on several evenings while another retained substantial room throughout the pilot. The figures identify uneven use of seating across the branches. They do not say how many people were turned away at Riverside, or whether anyone who found North Hill less crowded had first tried another location.

The 12-day pilot cost $18,600. Staff overtime, at $11,200, was the largest expense. Security cost $4,900, while bottled water and bus vouchers cost $2,500. The last category combines two forms of aid, so the line items do not assign separate costs to water and vouchers. Visitors used 212 bus vouchers, and 61 percent were redeemed at Riverside. Redemption records establish that the transportation aid was used and that most redemptions occurred at one branch. They do not reveal whether a voucher made a particular library visit possible or why use was concentrated at Riverside.

A voluntary survey supplied a closer, though partial, view of why people came. It received 146 responses, and respondents could select more than one reason. Eighty-nine said their homes were uncomfortably hot; 37 said they had no working air conditioner; and 52 came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. Those counts may overlap and cannot be combined into separate groups. The survey also cannot stand in for everyone counted at the doors, because responding was optional and entries were not unique visitors. Its answers describe the people who chose to participate: home heat, lack of working air conditioning, and access to connectivity or power all appeared among their stated reasons.

No medical emergency was reported at any participating branch. Across Marlow, emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. The citywide decline cannot be attributed to the library pilot: weather severity differed between the two summers, the city had no comparison group, and a citywide count cannot separate any effect of three libraries extending their hours on 12 days. The branch reports describe what happened at those sites, but they do not count illnesses prevented inside or outside the libraries.

For the following summer, the council chose a smaller footprint and a different trigger. It approved another round at Riverside and Central, leaving North Hill out. That choice retains Riverside, which exceeded capacity on four evenings, and drops North Hill, where seat use never rose above 60 percent. The available facts do not state that the seating figures caused the council’s decision. Instead of using the 2025 threshold of a forecast high of at least 38°C, the two branches will open late when heat alerts are issued, with 24 hours’ notice. The council also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both branches.
````

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P2_R4_C0 -->
````text
After Closing Time

Marlow’s 2025 library cooling-center pilot was modest in scale: three neighborhood libraries, 12 very hot days and three extra hours of operation each evening. On days when the forecast high reached at least 38°C, the participating branches moved their usual closing time from 6 p.m. to 9 p.m., giving residents an indoor place to remain after the libraries ordinarily would have shut their doors.

The door counters recorded 2,840 entries across those 12 days. On the same calendar dates in 2024, they recorded 1,760. That difference—1,080 additional entries—suggests substantially heavier use during the pilot, although the counters cannot show exactly how many people visited. They measure entries, not unique visitors, so someone who went out and later returned was counted twice. Nor does the year-to-year comparison isolate the effect of the longer hours from other possible differences between the two summers.

The timing of the entries nevertheless provides direct evidence that residents used the extension itself. Of the 2,840 entries recorded on pilot days, 1,079 occurred after 6 p.m., when the buildings normally would have been closed. Those evening entries accounted for more than a third of the total. The number does not reveal how long visitors stayed, whether they came more than once or what they would have done if the libraries had closed at the regular time. It does establish that the additional hours were not merely available; they were used.

A voluntary survey offers a limited view of why. It received 146 responses, a small portion of the recorded entries and not necessarily a representative sample of everyone who came through the doors. Respondents could also select more than one reason, so the answers overlap and cannot be treated as separate groups. Still, the responses indicate that relief from hot homes was a prominent concern. Eighty-nine respondents said their home was uncomfortably hot, and 37 said they had no working air conditioner. Fifty-two said they came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device, showing that the libraries’ ordinary services remained part of their appeal during the heat.

Demand was not evenly distributed. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, while North Hill never used more than 60 percent of its seats. Bus-voucher use showed a similar concentration: visitors used 212 vouchers, and 61 percent were redeemed at Riverside. These figures point to stronger demand at Riverside than at North Hill, but they do not explain the difference. The available data do not establish whether it resulted from neighborhood need, location, transportation access, awareness of the program or some other factor.

The pilot cost $18,600. Staff overtime was the largest expense, at $11,200. Security cost $4,900, while bottled water and bus vouchers accounted for the remaining $2,500. No medical emergency was reported at any participating branch. That is an important operational result, but it should not be mistaken for proof that the program prevented heat illness. An absence of reported emergencies inside the libraries does not reveal what happened to visitors before they arrived, after they left or elsewhere in the city.

Citywide emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. The decline is encouraging, but the pilot cannot be credited for it on the evidence available. The two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group—a similar population that did not have access to the extended library hours. Without one, it is impossible to separate any effect of the pilot from the influence of weather or other citywide conditions. The program reached people during dangerous heat, but the data do not establish that it caused the reduction in emergency-room visits.

For the following summer, the council chose to continue the experiment in a narrower form. It voted to repeat the pilot at Riverside and Central, leaving out North Hill, where seat use had remained below 60 percent. The new version will be triggered by heat alerts and operate with 24 hours’ notice, rather than being described solely by a forecast-high threshold. The council also approved additional charging strips and bus vouchers at both participating branches.

The decision reflects what the first summer’s numbers support most clearly. Residents made use of the later hours, especially at Riverside; some survey respondents were seeking relief from uncomfortably hot homes or lacked working air conditioning; and many also needed connectivity or power. The figures are less capable of answering the larger public-health question. Marlow can say that the libraries drew evening users and that one branch sometimes ran out of seats. It cannot yet say how many distinct people the pilot served, how much heat exposure it prevented or whether it reduced illness across the city.
````

````text
After Closing Time

In 2025, Marlow tried a limited answer to a basic problem: where people could go when extreme heat outlasted the hours of a public building. On 12 days when the forecast high reached at least 38°C, three neighborhood libraries stayed open until 9 p.m. instead of closing at 6. The extra three hours turned Riverside, Central and North Hill into evening cooling centers, though the pilot's records say much more about use of the buildings than about who used them or why.

Door counters registered 2,840 entries across the three libraries on those 12 days. On the same calendar dates in 2024, they registered 1,760. That is a substantial increase in traffic, but the unit matters. The machines counted entries, not individual visitors. A person who stepped outside and came back appeared twice in the total. The comparison also covers matching dates, not necessarily matching heat. It shows that more entries were recorded during the pilot days than on those dates a year earlier. It cannot, by itself, tell the city how many people came, or how much of the increase resulted from the later closing time rather than differences between the two summers.

The timing offers a clearer measure of demand for the extended hours. Of the 2,840 entries, 1,079 came after 6 p.m., when the libraries ordinarily would have been closed. In practical terms, more than one-third of all recorded entries occurred during the three-hour extension. Those entries establish that the added hours were used. They still do not equal 1,079 separate people, and the counters do not reveal whether visitors stayed for ten minutes or all evening.

The city spent $18,600 on the pilot. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security for $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers for the remaining $2,500. The spending record shows what it took to operate the extensions, but not a cost per visitor, since the number of visitors is unknown. Visitors used 212 bus vouchers, 61 percent of them at Riverside. That pattern may point to a greater transportation need around Riverside, heavier use of that branch, or both. The figures supplied for the pilot do not separate those possibilities.

A voluntary survey adds some detail, with the usual limits of a self-selected sample. It drew 146 responses, a small number beside the entry count, which itself was not a count of people. Respondents could choose more than one reason for coming. Eighty-nine said their homes were uncomfortably hot, and 37 said they had no working air conditioner. Fifty-two said they came in part to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. Those answers show that cooling, connectivity and access to power overlapped for at least some respondents. They do not establish the circumstances of everyone who entered, nor can the categories be added together to estimate a total.

Use also varied sharply by branch. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings. North Hill, by contrast, never filled more than 60 percent of its seats. The difference undercuts any simple citywide conclusion about how much library space was needed. At Riverside, capacity was insufficient on four evenings. At North Hill, a sizable share remained unused throughout the pilot. The available figures do not explain why.

No participating library reported a medical emergency. That is reassuring as an account of what occurred inside the branches, but it is not evidence that the pilot prevented heat illness. Citywide emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. The city had no comparison group, and the two summers differed in weather severity. Without a comparable group or comparable exposure to heat, the decline cannot be assigned to the library program.

For the following summer, the council chose a narrower repeat rather than a three-branch expansion. It approved the pilot for Riverside and Central, leaving out North Hill, with openings triggered by heat alerts and 24 hours' notice. It also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both participating branches. The decision follows the clearest distinctions in the 2025 record: substantial use after normal closing time, crowding at Riverside, demand for charging and Wi-Fi among survey respondents, and concentrated voucher use at Riverside. It does not turn the pilot into proof of a health effect. What Marlow learned with confidence was more modest: people used the extra hours, but they did not use every branch in the same way.
````

````text
After Closing Time

Marlow’s 2025 cooling-center pilot changed one element of the city’s heat response: how long three neighborhood libraries kept their doors open. On 12 days when the forecast high reached at least 38°C, the branches extended their usual 6 p.m. closing time to 9 p.m. The trial gave residents three extra indoor hours and allowed the city to compare demand across the participating branches.

Door counters registered 2,840 entries across the 12 pilot days. The same calendar dates in 2024 produced 1,760 entries. The increase was substantial, and the timing offers a clue about its source: 1,079 of the 2025 entries came after 6 p.m., during hours the pilot added. The increase from the previous year and the after-hours count differed by a single entry.

Those totals establish that residents used the extended hours. They leave the number of people unknown. The counters tracked each entry through a door, so a person who left for a meal or an errand and returned counted twice. The city cannot say how many individual residents came in or how long they stayed. The calendar-date comparison also covers two different schedules, since the 2025 branches remained open three hours longer. The available figures include no branch-by-branch or hourly comparison with 2024.

Demand varied by location. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings, showing repeated crowding there. North Hill never used more than 60 percent of its seats. The contrast supports keeping Riverside in the program and raises questions about continued spending at North Hill, though the figures do not explain why residents favored one branch over another. They also provide no comparable capacity figure for Central.

The city spent $18,600. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security cost $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers cost $2,500. Visitors used 212 bus vouchers, with 61 percent of redemptions at Riverside. That concentration fits Riverside’s crowding, though voucher use and seat counts measure different behavior. A voucher could help someone reach or leave a branch; a seat count captures occupancy at one point. Neither measure supplies a full visitor count.

A voluntary survey added a narrower view of why some visitors came. The city collected 146 responses, a small number beside the recorded entries, with repeat entries making the relationship between the two figures uncertain. Respondents could select more than one reason. Eighty-nine said their homes were too hot for comfort, and 37 said they lacked a working air conditioner. Fifty-two included Wi-Fi access or device charging among their reasons for visiting.

The responses show that heat drove many surveyed visits and that the libraries served people who needed internet access or power. They cannot establish the same pattern among everyone who entered. Participation was voluntary, and the available facts do not describe how the city recruited respondents. Since respondents could mark several reasons, the categories overlap. The survey cannot divide visitors into separate groups or determine which need brought each person through the door.

The city recorded no medical emergency at any participating branch. Citywide emergency-room visits for heat illness fell 9 percent from the previous summer. That decline cannot measure the pilot’s effect. The two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group. Factors outside the three libraries could have influenced emergency-room visits across Marlow. The pilot supports a more limited conclusion: residents used the added hours, Riverside faced the clearest capacity pressure, and many survey respondents came because their homes were hot or lacked working air conditioning.

For the following summer, the council voted to repeat the pilot at Riverside and Central, leaving North Hill out. Heat alerts will trigger the extended hours with 24 hours’ notice. The council also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both participating branches. The decision concentrates the program at two locations while expanding services that visitors cited or used. Another summer of entry counts, occupancy figures, voucher records, and surveys could give the city a clearer picture of whom the extra hours reach.
````

````text
After Closing Time

On 12 days in 2025, when Marlow’s forecast high reached at least 38°C, three neighborhood libraries kept their doors open until 9 p.m. instead of closing at 6. The extra three hours turned ordinary branches into cooling centers during the hottest forecast days covered by the pilot. The same schedule applied at all three branches on each of the 12 qualifying days. Across those dates, door counters registered 2,840 entries. The same calendar dates in 2024 produced 1,760 entries, a difference of 1,080.

The year-over-year counts establish that more entries were recorded on the pilot dates than on the corresponding dates a year earlier. They do not establish how many individual people used the libraries, or how much of the increase came from the later closing time. The counters recorded each entry, not each visitor. Anyone who stepped outside and returned appeared twice in the total. The available figures also do not separate first visits from repeat visits across the 12 days. They describe traffic through the doors, not the size of the population the program reached.

Of the 2,840 entries, 1,079 came after 6 p.m., when the libraries ordinarily would have been closed. Because the branches added those hours for the pilot, the count documents use made possible by the extension. It remains an entry count rather than a tally of distinct visitors. It cannot say how long people stayed, whether they would have gone elsewhere without the pilot, or whether the evening schedule caused the higher full-day count.

A voluntary survey provided a partial account of why people came. It received 146 responses. Eighty-nine respondents said their homes were uncomfortably hot, and 37 said they had no working air conditioner. Fifty-two came partly to use Wi-Fi or charge a device. Respondents could choose more than one reason, so those groups may overlap and cannot be added together. Heat, internet access and charging were reasons among the people who answered. The 146 voluntary responses cannot be treated as a profile of every user, and the entry total cannot supply a denominator for a response rate among unique visitors.

Use was uneven across the three branches. Riverside exceeded its 72-seat capacity on four evenings. North Hill, by contrast, never used more than 60 percent of its seats. The figures indicate that the pressure was not uniform, although they do not explain the difference between the branches. Riverside’s capacity figure shows that demand for seats surpassed supply on those four evenings, but not by how much. Bus-voucher use also tilted toward Riverside. Visitors used 212 vouchers during the pilot, and 61 percent were redeemed at that branch. Capacity and voucher figures both point to heavier demand at Riverside on at least some measures, while leaving open how many people accounted for that activity.

The 12-day pilot cost $18,600. Staff overtime accounted for $11,200, security for $4,900, and bottled water and bus vouchers for $2,500. Staff overtime was the largest of the three stated expense categories. Those figures put a price on extending the hours and supplying the participating branches. They cannot provide a cost per unique visitor from the available entry counts. Dividing the total by entries would instead produce a cost per recorded trip through a door, with return trips counted again. The spending breakdown also leaves bottled water and bus vouchers combined in a single category, so it cannot assign a separate cost to each.

No medical emergency was reported at any participating branch during the 12 pilot days. Citywide, emergency-room visits for heat illness were 9 percent lower than in the previous summer. Neither fact demonstrates that the library pilot prevented illness. The emergency-room figure covered the whole city, the two summers differed in weather severity, and the city had no comparison group. A citywide change cannot be assigned to three libraries from these data.

For the following summer, the council approved a smaller repeat of the program. Riverside and Central will participate; North Hill will not. The new version will be triggered by heat alerts, with 24 hours’ notice, rather than the 2025 rule based on a forecast high of at least 38°C. The council also approved more charging strips and bus vouchers at both participating branches.
````

````text
# Video feedback, right on the frame

Cutline gives freelance editors and small production studios one clear place to collect client notes, manage versions, and get final approval—without chasing feedback across email and chat.

**Start a project**

## Timecoded comments

Reviewers leave comments at exact timecodes, so editors know precisely which moment each note refers to. No copied timestamps, scattered messages, or guesswork.

## Clear decisions on every version

Each upload is treated as a separate version. Reviewers can mark it **Approved** or **Changes requested**, giving your team a clear decision before the next edit.

## Simple browser review

Upload an MP4 up to 5 GB and share a browser link. Reviewers can watch, comment, and respond without creating an account, and every project supports unlimited reviewers.

## How it works

1. **Upload your cut.** Add an MP4 file to the project as a new, separate version.

2. **Share the review link.** Clients open it in their browser—no account required—and attach feedback to exact timecodes. When needed, the project owner can send reminder emails manually.

3. **Get a decision.** Reviewers mark the version Approved or Changes requested, keeping the next step unambiguous.

**$24 per active project per month, with unlimited reviewers. After you close a project, its files remain available for 30 days and are then deleted.**

## Move every cut toward a clear decision

Keep review focused on the video, the version, and the work that comes next.

**Start a project**
````

````text
# Client feedback, pinned to the right frame

Cutline gives freelance editors and small production teams one place to collect precise comments, track each version, and get a clear decision. Upload your MP4, send a browser link, and keep the review out of email threads and chat.

**Send a review link**

## Comments at the exact timecode

Reviewers click the moment they mean and leave a comment there. You get specific notes tied to the footage, without matching vague messages to a scene.

## A decision on every version

Cutline treats each upload as a separate version. Each reviewer marks it “approved” or “changes requested,” so your team can see where that cut stands.

## Open to every reviewer

Clients open the link in their browser and review without an account. Invite as many reviewers as the project needs, then send reminder emails when you choose.

## How it works

1. **Upload the cut**  
   Add an MP4 file up to 5 GB. Cutline creates a separate version for every upload.

2. **Share the link**  
   Send the browser link to clients or collaborators. They can comment at exact timecodes without signing up.

3. **Collect the decision**  
   Reviewers choose “approved” or “changes requested” for that version. When you close the project, Cutline keeps its files available for 30 days, then deletes them.

**$24 per active project per month, with unlimited reviewers.**

## Bring your next review into Cutline

**Start a project**
````

````text
# Keep video feedback tied to the frame
````

Detected issues:

- P032, Repeated recap, conclusion, or recommendation: This concluding sentence restates the article’s opening distinction between what the evidence shows and what it cannot explain without adding a new fact or condition.

````text
The evidence now available gives the university a clearer description of the issue, but not a complete explanation.
````

- P032, Repeated recap, conclusion, or recommendation: This sentence recaps findings already stated and supported in the body without adding a new inference or action.

````text
Survey respondents reported extended waits and workarounds, system records documented the distribution of approval times, and requests involving information-security review took longer at the median.
````

- P032, Repeated recap, conclusion, or recommendation: This sentence reissues limitations already explained individually in the body without introducing a new evidentiary constraint.

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P4_R1_C0 -->
````text
# Equipment-Purchase Delays: What We Know and What Comes Next

North Coast University has gathered survey responses and procurement records to better understand delays in the equipment-purchase process. The available information shows that many survey respondents experienced at least one approval wait of more than five business days, while system logs show substantial differences in processing time depending on whether an information-security review was required. The evidence also has important limits: it does not explain individual delays, establish why staff used purchasing workarounds, or isolate the effects of recent process changes.

In March 2026, the university sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members and received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. Another 176 said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the same period.

These totals show that extended waits and informal purchasing methods were reported by substantial numbers of respondents. They do not show how common either experience was among all university staff. Because participation was voluntary and 412 of the 1,180 invited staff members responded, the results describe the respondents rather than the entire group that received the survey.

The survey also cannot establish a connection between approval delays and workarounds. Its report contains aggregate totals only and does not cross-tabulate responses about waiting times with responses about personal credit cards or other informal methods. As a result, the university does not know how many respondents reported both an approval wait of more than five business days and a workaround. The survey did not ask why respondents used workarounds, so it cannot show whether delays influenced those decisions.

Procurement system logs provide a broader view of formal equipment requests. The logs cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. The 90th-percentile time was 11.6 business days, meaning that while half of requests were approved within 4.2 business days, the slowest portion of requests took considerably longer.

Information-security review was associated with a marked difference in approval time. Requests requiring that review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. Requests that did not require it had a median of 3.4 business days. The logs identify each request’s review type and elapsed time, but they do not record the reasons for individual delays. They therefore show where longer processing times were concentrated, but not what caused a particular request to take longer.

The university has already made two changes affecting the process. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February 2026 and eight in March. A revised request form was also introduced on March 1. Median approval time declined from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March.

That decline is measurable, but its cause remains unknown. Staffing increased at the same time the revised form took effect, so the records do not isolate the effect of either change. The available data cannot determine how much of the March improvement, if any, resulted from the additional approval officers, the revised form, or a combination of the two.

A further change will be tested through a twelve-week pilot approved by the Administrative Council on April 22. During the pilot, purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will use a shorter approval route. The published council decision does not state why members selected this pilot or identify the evidence on which they relied.

The pilot starts May 18. Every two weeks, Procurement will publish the median and 90th-percentile approval times for requests eligible for the shorter route. These measures will show both the typical processing time and the experience of requests taking longer than most. The regular reporting will allow staff to follow approval times during the twelve-week period.

The pilot applies only to purchases below the stated threshold that do not require an information-security review. It does not change the route for requests that require such a review, the category that had the longer median approval time in the earlier system logs.

Staff should continue to follow university purchasing policy while these changes are tested. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. A personal credit card or another informal workaround does not, by itself, create a right to reimbursement.

The evidence now available gives the university a clearer description of the issue, but not a complete explanation. Survey respondents reported extended waits and workarounds, system records documented the distribution of approval times, and requests involving information-security review took longer at the median. What remains unknown includes the reasons for individual delays, the reasons staff used workarounds, the overlap between the two survey groups, and the separate effects of March’s staffing and form changes. The next concrete step is the twelve-week pilot and its biweekly reporting.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P4_R1_C0 -->

</details>
