Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P4_R1_C1 -->
````text
# Equipment purchase delays: what the records show and what comes next

North Coast University has two main sources of information about recent equipment purchase delays: a staff survey and procurement system logs. Together, they show that many survey respondents encountered waits longer than five business days, while systemwide approval times varied considerably. They also show that requests involving information security took longer than other requests. The records do not, however, explain every delay or establish why some staff used workarounds.

In March 2026, the university sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members. It received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment purchase approval during the previous six months. Another 176 said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the same period.

Those figures describe the experiences reported by the people who answered the survey. They do not establish how common either experience was among all 1,180 staff members invited to take part, or among university staff as a whole. The survey was voluntary, and 768 of the people invited did not respond.

The survey also leaves two important questions unanswered. Its report contains aggregate totals only and does not cross-tabulate approval waits with workarounds. The university therefore does not know how many respondents reported both a wait of more than five days and an informal purchasing method. Nor can it conclude that delays caused staff to use workarounds. The survey did not ask respondents why they had done so.

The procurement system logs provide a broader and different view. They cover all 2,940 equipment purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time between submission and final approval was 4.2 business days. In other words, half were approved in 4.2 business days or less, and half took at least that long. The 90th-percentile time was 11.6 business days, meaning nine in ten requests were approved within 11.6 business days and one in ten took longer.

Review type was associated with a substantial difference. Requests requiring an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. The median for requests without that review was 3.4 business days. The logs identify the type of review and the elapsed time for each request, but they do not record the reasons for individual delays. The figures show where longer waits were concentrated, not what happened in any particular case.

More recent monthly figures show a change, but they do not identify its cause. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February and eight in March. Median approval time fell from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March. The university also introduced a revised request form on March 1. Because the staffing increase and the new form occurred at the same time, existing records cannot separate their effects. They also cannot establish that either change, by itself, produced the lower March median.

The next step is a twelve-week pilot approved by the Administrative Council on April 22. Beginning May 18, purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will follow a shorter approval route. The published council decision does not state why council members selected this pilot or what evidence they relied on. It would therefore go beyond the available record to attribute a particular rationale to the council.

During the pilot, Procurement will publish median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks. Those measures will show both the typical approval time and the experience of requests toward the slower end of the distribution. Regular publication will allow staff to see whether approval times for eligible purchases change as the pilot proceeds.

The pilot is limited. It does not apply to purchases of $1,500 or more, and it does not apply when an information-security review is required. The published measures will report elapsed times, but the facts now available do not say that they will identify the reasons behind individual slow requests. Results will need to be read within those boundaries.

University purchasing policy remains in effect while this work continues. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. Staff should not assume that a delay, or participation in the new pilot, creates a new exception for personal credit cards or other informal methods.

What the university knows is specific: delays affected many survey respondents, approval times varied across the full set of logged requests, and requests involving information security generally took longer. What remains unknown is equally important. Current records do not connect individual waits to workarounds, explain why workarounds were used, identify the reason for each delay, or isolate the effect of March's staffing and form changes. The pilot and its fortnightly reporting will add new timing data for a defined group of lower-value purchases.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P4_R1_C1 -->

</details>

````text
# Equipment-Purchase Delays: What We Know and What Comes Next

North Coast University has two sets of records on equipment-purchase approvals: a staff survey conducted in March 2026 and procurement system logs covering requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Together, they show that some staff encountered long waits and that requests involving information-security review took more time. The records do not establish why individual requests stalled or why staff used workarounds.

The university sent the voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members and received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for approval of at least one equipment purchase during the previous six months. Another 176 said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during that period.

Those figures describe the respondents’ reported experiences. The survey report provides aggregate totals and does not cross-tabulate the answers about approval waits and workarounds. As a result, the university does not know how many respondents reported both. The survey also did not ask why anyone used a workaround, so the responses do not show whether approval time, an urgent need, confusion about the process, or another reason prompted a purchase outside the standard route.

The procurement logs provide a broader measure of approval times. They include all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. Half of the requests took no more than 4.2 business days, while half took at least that long. The 90th-percentile time was 11.6 business days, meaning 90 percent reached final approval within 11.6 business days and 10 percent took longer.

Information-security review accounts for a large difference in the recorded times. Requests requiring that review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. Requests that did not require it had a median of 3.4 business days. The logs identify each request’s review type and elapsed time, but they do not record the reason for an individual delay. The university can measure the gap between the two groups from these records, but it cannot use them to determine which step held up a specific request.

Records from February and March show a drop in the monthly median after two changes. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February, then eight in March. The university also introduced a revised request form on March 1. Median approval time fell from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March.

The timing prevents the university from assigning that improvement to one change. Procurement added two officers in the same month that staff began using the revised form. The records do not isolate the effect of added staff from the effect of the form, and they do not show how much either change contributed to the lower March median.

The next change will begin in May. On April 22, the Administrative Council approved a twelve-week pilot for purchases under $1,500 that do not require information-security review. Those purchases will follow a shorter approval route starting May 18. The published council decision does not state why council members selected this pilot or identify the evidence they used.

Procurement will report results during the pilot rather than waiting until its end. Every two weeks, it will publish the median and 90th-percentile approval times for requests eligible for the shorter route. The median will show the midpoint of approval times. The 90th percentile will show how long most eligible requests take and will make longer waits visible in each reporting period. These measures match the two system-wide measures already available for the September-through-February requests.

The pilot focuses on a defined group: purchases below $1,500 that require no information-security review. Its published results will therefore describe eligible requests, not equipment purchases outside that group. The facts provided do not specify what decision the university will make after the twelve weeks or what threshold it will use to judge the pilot.

Staff should continue to follow university purchasing policy while the pilot runs. The shorter route will apply only to eligible requests from May 18. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. A personal credit card or other informal workaround does not create a new exception.

The university now has evidence of reported waits, documented approval-time patterns, and a measurable difference associated with information-security review. It also has clear limits on what those records can answer. The biweekly pilot reports will add current results for eligible purchases and allow staff to track both typical approval time and the slower end of the process over the twelve-week period.
````

</details>

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P4_R1_C3 -->
````text
Equipment purchase delays: What the records tell us

In March 2026, North Coast University sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members. It received 412 responses. The survey offers a direct account from those respondents about approval waits and informal purchasing practices during the previous six months. Its findings apply to the people who responded; the university cannot use those aggregate totals to count experiences among staff members who did not participate.

Of the 412 respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval. This is a count of people who recalled at least one such wait, rather than a count of delayed requests. It does not indicate how many requests each person submitted, how long every request took, or what held up any particular approval.

In the same survey, 176 respondents said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase. The report does not cross-tabulate this count with the 238 who reported an approval wait, so the number reporting both remains unknown. The survey did not ask why respondents used a workaround, leaving no basis for attributing those purchases to approval delays.

Procurement system logs answer a different set of questions. They cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026 and measure time at the request level. Unlike the voluntary survey, the logs include every equipment-purchase request submitted during their stated six-month period. The median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. That figure means half the requests were approved in 4.2 business days or less, while half took 4.2 days or more. The 90th-percentile approval time was 11.6 business days, so 90 percent were approved within that period and 10 percent took longer.

The logs also show a difference by review type. Requests that required an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days, compared with 3.4 business days for requests without that review. The records identify the review type and elapsed time for each request. They give no reasons for individual delays, including which part of an information-security review may have taken extra time. The figures establish a difference between the two categories of requests, not the cause of that difference.

The survey and logs should be read according to their separate units. The survey counts respondents who reported at least one experience during six months. The logs count requests and calculate approval times for the 2,940 submissions from September through February. The survey's five-day threshold and the logs' median describe different things: at least one reported personal experience and the midpoint across requests. The 238 responses cannot be converted into a share of logged requests, while the logged median cannot tell the university how many staff members encountered a delay.

The university has more recent monthly results as well. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February 2026 and eight in March. A revised request form also took effect on March 1. Median approval time declined from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March. The March figure falls outside the September-through-February log period described above. Both changes occurred while the median was falling, so the records cannot isolate the effect of the staffing increase or the revised form. Assigning the decline to either change would go beyond the available evidence.

On April 22, the Administrative Council approved a twelve-week pilot for a shorter approval route. It applies to purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review. Purchases of $1,500 or more and purchases requiring an information-security review are outside the approved pilot route. The published council decision gives neither the members' reason for choosing the pilot nor the evidence they relied on. As a result, the public record does not tie the decision to a particular survey result, log measure, staffing change, or feature of the revised form.

The pilot starts May 18. Procurement will publish median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks. These reports will provide recurring measures of the middle of the approval-time distribution and its slower end for purchases using the shorter route. They can document approval times during the pilot, but the two published measures alone cannot explain individual delays or prove that the pilot caused any change.

Staff must continue to follow the university's purchasing policy during the pilot. The survey's reports of personal cards and other workarounds do not alter the reimbursement rule. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies.
````

````text
# Equipment-Purchase Delays: What We Know and What Comes Next

North Coast University is reviewing delays in its equipment-purchase process using information from a staff survey and procurement system records. Together, these sources show that some staff members have experienced waits longer than five business days, that approval times vary substantially, and that requests requiring information-security review take longer than other requests. The available evidence does not, however, establish why individual requests were delayed or why some staff members used purchasing workarounds.

In March 2026, the university sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members and received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. Another 176 respondents said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the same period.

Those survey figures require careful interpretation. Because the report contains aggregate totals only, it does not cross-tabulate approval waits and workarounds. The university therefore does not know how many respondents reported both a wait of more than five business days and the use of a workaround. The survey also did not ask why respondents used a personal credit card or another informal process. It cannot show whether approval delays influenced those decisions or whether staff members used workarounds for other reasons.

Procurement system logs provide a broader record of processing times. The logs cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. The 90th-percentile approval time was 11.6 business days. In other words, the records show both a typical approval time below five business days and a group of requests that took considerably longer.

Information-security review is associated with a clear difference in processing time. Requests requiring that review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days, compared with 3.4 business days for requests that did not require it. The system logs identify the type of review and the elapsed approval time, but they do not record the reasons for delays in individual cases. As a result, the university can identify where longer processing times are concentrated, but it cannot use the logs to explain why a particular request took longer than another.

Recent records also show a reduction in the overall median approval time. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February 2026 and eight in March. The median approval time was 4.8 business days in February and 3.6 business days in March. A revised request form was introduced on March 1, at the same time as the staffing change. Because both changes occurred together, the records do not isolate the effect of either one. The March improvement cannot be attributed specifically to the additional officers, the revised form, or any particular combination of the two.

The next step is a twelve-week pilot approved by the Administrative Council on April 22. Beginning May 18, purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will use a shorter approval route. The published council decision does not explain why members selected this pilot or identify the evidence on which they relied. The university therefore cannot draw conclusions from the published decision about the council’s reasoning.

During the pilot, Procurement will publish median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks. Those two measures will provide different views of performance: the median will show the midpoint of approval times, while the 90th percentile will help show the experience of requests taking longer than most. Regular publication will make it possible to track approval times for purchases using the shorter route throughout the twelve-week period.

The current evidence supports several firm conclusions. Some survey respondents experienced approval waits longer than five business days, and some used personal funds or other informal workarounds. Systemwide records show a median approval time of 4.2 business days for requests submitted from September through February, with much longer times at the 90th percentile. They also show substantially longer median times for requests requiring information-security review. What remains unknown are the reasons for individual delays, the reasons staff members used workarounds, the overlap between those two survey groups, and the separate effects of the March staffing and form changes.

Staff should continue to follow university purchasing policy while the pilot proceeds. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. Procurement’s biweekly reporting will provide the next evidence on whether approval times for eligible purchases change under the shorter route.
````

````text
# What we know about equipment-purchase delays

North Coast University has two main sources of information about equipment-purchase approvals: a voluntary staff survey and procurement system logs. Together, they show that many survey respondents encountered waits or used informal purchasing methods, while the full set of system records shows wide variation in approval times. The records also point to longer processing when an information-security review is required. They do not, however, explain every delay or establish why staff used workarounds.

In March 2026, the university sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members and received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval in the previous six months. Another 176 said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during that period.

Those figures describe the people who chose to respond. The survey results are aggregate totals, and the report does not cross-tabulate answers about approval waits with answers about workarounds. As a result, the university does not know how many of the 176 respondents who reported a workaround were also among the 238 who reported a wait of more than five business days. The survey also did not ask why anyone used a workaround. It therefore cannot show whether a delay led to a particular purchase outside the standard process.

The procurement logs provide a broader view of approval times. They cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. This means half of the requests were approved within 4.2 business days and half took longer. The 90th-percentile time was 11.6 business days, meaning 90 percent were approved within that period and 10 percent took longer.

Review type is associated with a substantial difference in timing. Requests that required an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. For requests that did not require that review, the median was 3.4 business days. The logs identify the type of review and the elapsed time for each request, but they do not record the reasons for individual delays. The figures show where longer waits were concentrated, not what caused each request to take the time it did.

More recent monthly records show that median approval time fell from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March. Two changes occurred around the same time. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February, then eight in March. The university also introduced a revised request form on March 1. Because staffing and the form changed together, the available records cannot isolate the effect of either one. The lower March median may be related to one change, both changes, or other factors that the records do not identify.

A further change will begin in May. On April 22, the Administrative Council approved a twelve-week pilot for purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review. Eligible purchases will use a shorter approval route. The pilot starts May 18.

The published council decision does not state why members selected this pilot or which evidence they relied on. That leaves an important part of the decision-making record unknown. The pilot's published measures will provide new information about processing times for the eligible requests, but they will not by themselves answer every outstanding question about past delays, staff workarounds, or the reasons behind the council's choice.

During the twelve weeks, Procurement will publish the median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks. Reporting both measures matters because the median describes the middle request, while the 90th percentile shows how long requests near the slower end of the process are taking. The repeated reports will make it possible to track whether approval times for eligible requests change during the pilot.

Staff should continue to use the university's purchasing process while this work proceeds. The survey confirms that some respondents used personal cards or other informal methods, but it does not change reimbursement rules. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies.

The next step is therefore a defined, time-limited test with regular public reporting. Its results should add evidence about the shorter route for a specific group of purchases. Until those results are available, the university can say that approval times vary, information-security reviews take longer at the median, and March's overall median was lower than February's. It cannot yet assign causes to individual delays, link reported workarounds to waits, separate the effects of added staff and the revised form, or explain the council's reasoning beyond the published decision.
````

````text
# Equipment-Purchase Delays: Current Findings and the Twelve-Week Pilot

North Coast University has two sets of records on equipment-purchase approvals: a voluntary staff survey conducted in March 2026 and procurement system logs covering requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Together, these records show that many survey respondents encountered waits longer than five business days and that information-security reviews took more time than other reviews. The records cannot explain why individual requests stalled, why staff used informal purchasing methods, or which March change reduced the median approval time.

The university sent the March survey to 1,180 staff members and received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. In a separate survey item, 176 respondents said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during that period.

Those totals need careful reading. The survey report includes aggregate totals and no cross-tabulation of the two answers. The university therefore does not know how many of the 176 respondents who reported a workaround also belonged to the group of 238 who reported an approval wait. The survey also omitted a question about the reasons staff used workarounds. It cannot establish whether an approval delay prompted any specific purchase outside the standard process.

The response count creates another boundary. The 412 responses describe the experiences reported by those respondents. The survey received no response from 768 of the 1,180 staff members invited to take part. The reported totals do not describe the purchasing experience of every staff member who received the survey.

The procurement system logs provide a broader record of submitted requests. They cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests entered from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. The 90th percentile was 11.6 business days, marking the upper end of approval times for most requests.

Review type accounts for a clear difference in those records. Requests requiring an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. Requests without that review had a median of 3.4 business days. The logs identify the review type and elapsed time for each request. They do not record the reason for an individual delay, so the university cannot use them to determine why a particular request took longer than the median or crossed the 90th-percentile mark.

March records show a lower median after two changes. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February 2026 and eight in March. The university also introduced a revised request form on March 1. Median approval time fell from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March.

The records do not separate the effect of the two added officers from the effect of the revised form. Both changes occurred during the same period. The lower March median may reflect one change, both changes, or other factors that the available records do not identify. The university cannot assign the improvement to either staffing or the form from these figures.

The next step begins May 18. On April 22, the Administrative Council approved a twelve-week pilot for purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review. Eligible purchases will use a shorter approval route during the pilot. The published council decision does not explain why council members selected this approach or identify the evidence they used.

Procurement will track the pilot through regular reporting. Every two weeks, it will publish the median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests. The median will show the midpoint of approval times, while the 90th percentile will show the upper range experienced by a smaller share of requests. Publishing both measures will show the typical wait and the longer waits near the upper end of the distribution during the twelve-week period.

The pilot has a defined scope. It applies to purchases below $1,500 that do not need an information-security review. The longer median recorded for requests requiring that review remains outside the shorter route. The facts available for the pilot do not describe a separate change for those requests.

Staff should continue to follow university purchasing policy while the pilot runs. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. The survey’s 176 reports of personal-card use or another workaround do not change that rule.

The university knows the size and timing of the delays recorded in the procurement system, the difference associated with information-security review, and the experiences reported by survey respondents. It still lacks evidence about individual causes, the connection between delays and workarounds, and the separate effects of the March staffing and form changes. The twelve-week pilot and its two-week reporting schedule will add current measurements for a narrow category of lower-cost requests.
````

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P4_R2_C3 -->
````text
Equipment purchase delays: What we know and what comes next

In March 2026, North Coast University sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members. The university received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. This count applies to respondents who reported at least one such wait. Because the survey was voluntary, it does not establish how often the same experience occurred across everyone invited to participate.

The same survey found that 176 respondents had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the previous six months. That figure must be read separately from the approval-wait figure. The survey report provides aggregate totals and does not cross-tabulate the two answers, so the university does not know how many respondents reported both a wait and a workaround. The two counts cannot be added together because an unknown number of respondents may appear in both.

The survey did not ask why respondents used a workaround. It cannot tell us whether a delayed approval prompted any particular purchase outside the standard process.

The survey and the procurement logs also count different things. The survey counts staff members who reported at least one experience during the six-month period covered by its questions. The logs count requests and summarize the time each request spent between submission and final approval. The 238 survey responses cannot be treated as a percentage of logged requests, and the system-wide median cannot be treated as a description of every respondent’s experience.

Procurement system logs provide a broader record of equipment requests. The logs cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days, while the 90th-percentile time was 11.6 business days. These measures describe the center and the upper end of recorded approval times without assigning a reason to any request.

Review type is one clear difference recorded in the logs. Requests that required an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. The median for requests without that review was 3.4 business days. This comparison identifies an association between review type and elapsed time in the recorded requests. It does not explain why an individual request took the time it did, because the logs identify review type and elapsed time but not reasons for individual delays.

Recent records show a change in both staffing and processing time. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February 2026, then eight in March. Median approval time was 4.8 business days in February and 3.6 business days in March. Those figures document a shorter median in March, but they do not establish that the two additional officers caused the reduction.

A revised request form also took effect on March 1. Because Procurement added approval officers and introduced the form at the same time, the records do not isolate the effect of staffing, the form, or a particular combination of the two on the lower March median.

The next formal step is a twelve-week pilot approved by the Administrative Council on April 22. Under the pilot, purchases below $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will follow a shorter approval route. The pilot begins May 18. Requests at or above $1,500, as well as requests requiring an information-security review, are outside the stated eligibility criteria.

The published council decision does not say why members selected this pilot or identify the evidence on which they relied. The university should not infer from the decision alone that the survey, the system logs, the staffing change, or the revised form led the council to approve the shorter route. The available record states what the council approved, but not its rationale.

During the pilot, Procurement will publish the median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks. Those figures will describe approval times for requests using the shorter route. The facts available do not state whether the published updates will give reasons for individual delays, and no method is specified for attributing any change in times to the pilot.

The reimbursement rule remains in force while the pilot and its reporting proceed. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. Staff should continue to use the approved purchasing process and should not assume that a delay, the pilot, or participation in the March survey creates a new reimbursement exception.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P4_R2_C3 -->

</details>

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P4_R3_C0 -->
````text
# Equipment-Purchase Delays: What We Know and What Comes Next

North Coast University is taking steps to address delays in its equipment-purchase process, while recognizing that the available information does not answer every question about why delays occur or how staff respond to them. A staff survey and procurement system logs provide two different views of the process. Together, they show that some employees have experienced approval waits longer than five business days, that longer waits are associated with requests requiring information-security review, and that approval times improved in March. They do not establish the causes of individual delays or the effect of recent process changes.

In March 2026, the university sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members. It received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. Another 176 respondents said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the same period.

These figures describe the experiences reported by survey respondents, but they have important limits. Because the survey was voluntary and 412 of the 1,180 invited staff members responded, it does not provide information about the purchasing experiences of those who did not participate. The survey report also contains aggregate totals only. It does not cross-tabulate approval waits and workarounds, so the university does not know how many respondents reported both a wait of more than five business days and the use of an informal workaround.

The survey also did not ask why respondents used personal credit cards or other workarounds. As a result, the university cannot determine from the survey whether those actions were related to approval times, another part of the purchasing process, or other circumstances. The reported waits and workarounds are both matters of concern, but the available survey results do not establish a connection between them.

Procurement system logs provide a broader and more complete record of approval times. The logs cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. The 90th-percentile approval time was 11.6 business days. In other words, the midpoint of recorded approval times was 4.2 business days, while requests near the slower end of the distribution took substantially longer.

The records show a clear difference based on whether a request required an information-security review. Requests requiring that review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. Requests that did not require it had a median of 3.4 business days. This identifies review type as an important distinction in the timing data. However, the logs identify only the type of review and the elapsed time. They do not identify the reasons for delays in individual cases, so they cannot show which specific steps or circumstances accounted for a particular request’s approval time.

More recent monthly figures show that the median approval time declined from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March. Two changes occurred during this period. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February and eight in March. The university also introduced a revised request form on March 1.

Because the staffing increase and revised form took effect at the same time, the records do not isolate the effect of either change. The March improvement therefore cannot be attributed solely to the additional approval officers or solely to the new form. The figures show that median approval time decreased, but they do not establish why it decreased or whether the improvement will continue.

The next formal step is a twelve-week pilot approved by the Administrative Council on April 22. Under the pilot, purchases below $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will follow a shorter approval route. The pilot will begin May 18.

The published council decision does not state why council members selected this pilot or which evidence they relied on. It therefore does not resolve questions about how the survey findings, system-log data, staffing change, revised form, or other considerations influenced the decision. What is known is the pilot’s scope, duration, start date, and reporting plan.

During the pilot, Procurement will publish median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks. These measures will provide regular information about both the midpoint of approval times and the experience of requests taking longer than most. The reporting will allow staff and university leaders to observe timing during the twelve-week period, although the published data will need to be assessed before conclusions can be drawn about the shorter route.

University purchasing policy remains in effect throughout the pilot. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. Staff should continue to use the approved purchasing process rather than personal credit cards or informal workarounds unless an established exception covers the purchase.

The university currently has evidence that approval experiences vary and that information-security review is associated with longer median times. It also has evidence of a lower overall median in March. It does not yet know the causes of individual delays, why survey respondents used workarounds, how often waits and workarounds overlapped, or which March change contributed to the improvement. The pilot and its biweekly reporting are the next opportunity to add clearer, current evidence about approval performance.
````

# Equipment purchase delays: what the data show and what comes next

North Coast University has two main sources of information about equipment-purchase delays: a voluntary staff survey conducted in March 2026 and procurement system records. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. Another 176 said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the same period.

Those figures require care. Because participation was voluntary, the results describe the 412 people who responded, not all 1,180 staff members who received the survey or the university workforce as a whole. The survey report also provides only aggregate totals. It does not cross-tabulate the answers about approval waits and workarounds, so the university does not know how many respondents reported both experiences.

The survey did not ask why anyone used a workaround. It therefore cannot establish whether staff used personal cards or other informal methods because of approval delays, for convenience, in response to an urgent need, or for another reason. The two survey totals should not be treated as evidence of a direct connection. They show that both experiences occurred among respondents during the same six-month period, but nothing more specific.

Procurement system logs provide a broader record of approval times. They cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted between September 2025 and February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. The 90th-percentile time was 11.6 business days. In other words, half of the requests were approved within 4.2 business days, while approval took 11.6 business days or less for 90 percent of requests.

Information-security review is associated with a substantial difference in processing time. Requests requiring that review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. The median for requests that did not require it was 3.4 business days. The logs identify the review type and total elapsed time, but they do not record the reasons for delays in individual cases. They cannot show whether a particular request waited because information was missing, a reviewer was unavailable, a question required follow-up, or some other issue arose.

More recent figures show a change in both staffing and approval time. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February 2026, then eight in March. Median approval time fell from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March. The university also introduced a revised request form on March 1.

Because the staffing increase and form revision happened at the same time, the records cannot isolate the effect of either change. The lower March median may reflect one change, both changes, or other factors not identified in the available records. The university can say that approval times decreased as staffing and the form changed, but it cannot assign the decrease to one cause.

The next step is a twelve-week pilot approved by the Administrative Council on April 22. Beginning May 18, purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will follow a shorter approval route. The published council decision does not state why members selected this pilot or identify the evidence they relied on. The university therefore cannot draw conclusions about the council's reasoning from the published decision.

During the pilot, Procurement will publish median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks. Those measures will show both the typical approval time and the point within which 90 percent of eligible requests are completed. The regular reports will allow staff to follow performance during the twelve weeks, although the stated reporting plan does not include reasons for individual delays.

Current purchasing rules remain in effect while the pilot proceeds. Staff should continue to use approved purchasing channels. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. The pilot changes the approval route for qualifying requests; it does not create a new reimbursement exception for personal credit cards or other informal workarounds.

The university introduced a revised request form on March 1. During the same period, the monthly median approval time fell from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March.

The timing prevents the records from separating the effects of the two changes. They do not show how much of the March reduction came from the two additional officers, how much came from the revised form, or whether both contributed. The lower March median records an improvement in processing time, but it does not identify its cause.

The next change will begin May 18. On April 22, the Administrative Council approved a twelve-week pilot for purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review. During the pilot, those purchases will follow a shorter approval route.

The published council decision does not state why council members selected this pilot or which evidence they relied on. Staff should not read a rationale into the decision that the published record does not contain. The pilot’s scope is specific: the shorter route applies to purchases below the stated threshold only when they do not require information-security review.

Procurement will report results during the twelve weeks. Every two weeks, it will publish the median and 90th-percentile approval times for requests eligible for the pilot. Those two measures will let staff track both the typical approval time and the slower end of processing for eligible purchases as the pilot proceeds.

The pilot does not change the university’s reimbursement policy. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. Staff should continue to use approved purchasing routes rather than a personal credit card or another informal workaround, except where an existing exception permits a different course.

The survey, procurement records, and pilot reports answer different questions. The survey records what 412 respondents experienced and did. The procurement data measure elapsed approval times across submitted requests and compare review types. The coming reports will show approval times for purchases using the pilot route. None of these sources, on its own, supplies the missing reasons behind individual delays or past workarounds. The biweekly figures will provide the next evidence about how eligible requests move through the shorter route.
````

````text
Equipment-purchase delays: what we know and what comes next

In March 2026, North Coast University sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members and received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. Because participation was voluntary, the results describe the staff members who answered rather than the entire group invited to take part.

The survey also found that 176 respondents had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the same six-month period. This figure records whether respondents used a workaround at least once. It does not give the number or value of purchases made that way.

The report contains aggregate totals only and does not cross-tabulate approval waits and workarounds. As a result, the university cannot calculate how many respondents reported both experiences from the published totals.

A separate gap concerns motive. The survey did not ask respondents why they used a personal card or another informal workaround. It cannot tell the university whether an approval wait influenced that choice.

Procurement system logs provide a different view of the process. They cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. Half were approved within that time and half took longer. The 90th-percentile time was 11.6 business days, meaning 90 percent were approved in 11.6 business days or less and 10 percent took longer.

The median describes the middle of the full set of requests, while the 90th percentile gives information about requests toward the slower end. These measures describe request times across the six-month period; they do not count how many individual staff members submitted those requests.

Review type is associated with a substantial timing difference. Requests that required an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. The median for requests without that review was 3.4 business days. On the recorded measure, the median for the first category was 5.7 business days longer.

The logs identify review type and elapsed time, but they do not identify the reasons for individual delays. A request can be placed in one of the two review categories and timed from submission to approval, without an explanation of why it took that long.

The survey and log figures measure different things. The survey asked whether a person had experienced at least one wait longer than five business days during six months. The logs calculate timing across requests, and one person could have submitted more than one request. A median of 4.2 business days can therefore coexist with 238 survey respondents reporting at least one wait longer than five days. The records provided do not connect a respondent’s survey answer to a specific request in the system.

Recent monthly figures show that approval times fell after February. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February 2026 and eight in March. Median approval time declined from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March. The figures establish the staffing levels and the change in median time during those months.

A revised request form took effect on March 1, when staffing also increased. Because both changes occurred at the same time, the records do not isolate the contribution of either one.

On April 22, the Administrative Council approved a twelve-week pilot. Purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will use a shorter approval route during the pilot. Purchases at or above that amount and those requiring an information-security review are outside the stated eligibility rules for the shorter route.

The published council decision does not state why members chose this pilot or which evidence they relied on. The public record therefore provides no basis for attributing the choice to the survey, the logs, the staffing increase, the revised form, or a combination of those sources and changes.

The pilot starts May 18. Every two weeks, Procurement will publish the median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests. The median will report the midpoint of those approval times, while the 90th percentile will indicate how long the slower requests are taking. Staff will receive both figures regularly during the twelve-week pilot rather than only after it ends.

The pilot changes the approval route for eligible requests, not the university’s reimbursement rule. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. A delay or eligibility for the shorter route does not create a new exception, so staff should continue to use the approved purchasing process.
````

````text
# Equipment-Purchase Delays: What We Know and What Comes Next

North Coast University is examining delays in its equipment-purchase process using two sources of information: a voluntary staff survey and procurement system logs. Together, these sources show that some staff have experienced approval waits and that requests involving information-security review take longer than other requests. They also show recent improvement in the overall median approval time. However, the available records do not explain every delay, establish why staff used purchasing workarounds, or determine which recent process change produced the improvement.

In March 2026, the university sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members and received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. In addition, 176 respondents said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during that period.

Those figures identify concerns among respondents, but the survey has important limits. Because participation was voluntary and 412 of the 1,180 invited staff members responded, its results describe the respondents rather than all staff members. The report also contains aggregate totals only. It does not cross-tabulate approval waits and workarounds, so the university does not know how many respondents reported both experiences. The survey did not ask why respondents used a workaround. It therefore cannot show whether a workaround was connected to an approval delay or arose for another reason.

Procurement system logs provide a broader view of approval times. The logs cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. The 90th-percentile approval time was 11.6 business days. In other words, the midpoint of recorded approval times was 4.2 business days, while requests near the slower end of the distribution took substantially longer.

Review type is associated with a clear difference in timing. Requests requiring an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. Requests that did not require that review had a median of 3.4 business days. The logs identify both the review type and the elapsed approval time, but they do not record the reasons for individual delays. As a result, the university can identify where longer approval times are concentrated, but it cannot use the current logs to explain why a particular request took longer than another.

More recent monthly figures show that the median approval time declined from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March. Two changes occurred during the same period. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February, then eight in March. The university also introduced a revised request form on March 1.

Because staffing and the request form changed at the same time, the records do not isolate the effect of either change. The March result shows that approvals were faster at the median than they had been in February. It does not establish whether the additional approval officers, the revised form, or some combination of the two caused that change.

The next formal step is a twelve-week pilot approved by the Administrative Council on April 22. Under the pilot, purchases below $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will use a shorter approval route. The pilot will begin May 18.

The published council decision does not state why council members selected this pilot or identify the evidence on which they relied. That means the decision itself does not resolve the unanswered questions in the survey and system records. It does, however, establish a defined process change for a specific category of purchases.

During the pilot, Procurement will publish median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks. Reporting both measures will show the typical approval time as well as the experience of requests toward the slower end. The twelve-week period and regular reporting will provide a record of how approval times for eligible purchases develop under the shorter route.

The university’s purchasing policy remains in effect throughout the pilot. Purchases made outside policy will not be reimbursed unless an existing exception applies. Staff should therefore continue to use approved purchasing procedures rather than personal credit cards or informal workarounds when no existing exception applies.

The evidence available now supports several conclusions: approval experiences vary; information-security review is associated with longer median approval times; some survey respondents have used informal workarounds; and the overall monthly median improved in March. It does not show why individual requests were delayed, why respondents used workarounds, how often the same person experienced both, or which March change reduced the median. The pilot’s published results will add new timing data for eligible requests, while those limits should remain clear in any assessment of the purchasing process.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P4_R4_C1 -->
````text
# What we know about equipment-purchase delays

North Coast University now has two sets of records on equipment-purchase approvals: a voluntary staff survey and procurement system logs. Together, they show that many survey respondents encountered waits of more than five business days, while approval times varied substantially across all requests. They also show a longer median time for requests requiring information-security review. The records do not, however, explain why individual requests were delayed or why staff used informal purchasing workarounds.

The university sent the survey to 1,180 staff members in March 2026 and received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. Another 176 said they had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during that period.

Those totals need to be read carefully. The survey was voluntary, and its findings describe the 412 people who responded. The report contains aggregate totals only. It does not cross-tabulate approval waits with workarounds, so the university does not know how many respondents reported both experiences. The survey also did not ask why anyone used a workaround. It therefore cannot establish that approval delays led staff to make purchases outside the usual process.

The procurement logs offer a broader view of approval times. They cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. In other words, half were approved in 4.2 business days or less and half took longer. The 90th-percentile time was 11.6 business days, meaning 90 percent were approved within that period and 10 percent took longer.

Information-security review is associated with a clear difference in the records. Requests that required such a review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. Requests that did not require one had a median of 3.4 business days. That comparison shows where longer processing times were concentrated, but it does not explain the reason for any individual delay. The logs record review type and elapsed time, not what happened within a particular request.

More recent figures show that approval times fell after February. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February, then eight in March. The median approval time was 4.8 business days in February and 3.6 business days in March. A revised request form also took effect on March 1. Because the staffing increase and form revision happened at the same time, the records cannot separate their effects. They do not show how much, if at all, either change contributed to the lower March median.

The next step is a twelve-week pilot approved by the Administrative Council on April 22. Beginning May 18, purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review will use a shorter approval route. These criteria limit the pilot to lower-cost requests that can proceed without that additional review. Requests outside those criteria will not use the shorter route.

The published council decision does not say why members selected this pilot or which evidence they considered. It would therefore be inaccurate to present the survey, the system logs, the staffing change, or the revised form as the stated basis for the council's action. The public record establishes what the council approved, but not its reasoning.

Procurement will report results while the pilot is under way. Every two weeks, it will publish the median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests. The median will show the midpoint of processing times, while the 90th percentile will show how long most eligible requests took, including those near the slower end. Publishing both measures will show whether typical waits and longer waits change during the pilot.

One policy point remains unchanged. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. Staff should not assume that a delayed request, participation in the survey, or the start of the pilot creates a new reimbursement exception.

The evidence so far gives the university a measured account of the problem, not a complete explanation. It documents reported waits and workarounds, the distribution of approval times, the longer median for requests requiring information-security review, and a March decline in the overall median. It does not connect individual workarounds to delays, identify the causes of specific waits, isolate the effect of recent operational changes, or explain the council's choice. The pilot's biweekly figures will add new information about eligible requests starting May 18.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P4_R4_C1 -->

</details>

````text
# Equipment Purchase Delays: What We Know and What Comes Next

North Coast University has two sets of information about equipment-purchase delays: a voluntary staff survey from March 2026 and procurement system records. The survey describes the experiences of 412 respondents. The system records measure approval times for every equipment-purchase request submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Each source answers some questions, but neither identifies the cause of each delay.

In March, the university sent the purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members and received 412 responses. Among those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. Another survey question found that 176 respondents had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the same period.

Those figures require care. Because staff chose whether to participate, the results describe the 412 respondents rather than all 1,180 staff members who received the survey. The university cannot use the survey totals to determine how common either experience was across the full group.

The survey leaves two other questions unanswered. The report gives aggregate totals and does not cross-tabulate approval waits with workarounds. As a result, the university does not know how many respondents reported both a wait of more than five business days and an informal purchasing method. The survey did not ask why anyone used a workaround, so it cannot establish whether approval delays prompted those purchases.

Procurement records provide a broader measure of processing times. The system contains all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. Across those requests, the median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days. Half of the requests took less time and half took more. The 90th-percentile time was 11.6 business days, meaning 90 percent reached final approval within 11.6 business days and 10 percent took longer.

Review type had a clear association with elapsed time. Requests that required an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. Requests without that review had a median of 3.4 business days. The records identify each review type and the elapsed approval time. They do not record why an individual request took as long as it did. The figures show where longer processing times occurred, but they do not identify a reason for any specific delay.

The university changed both staffing and the request process in March. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February and eight in March. The university introduced a revised request form on March 1. Median approval time fell from 4.8 business days in February to 3.6 business days in March.

The available records do not tell the university how much of that decline came from adding two approval officers, how much came from revising the form, or whether other factors played a part. Because both changes took effect during the same period, the university cannot isolate the effect of either one.

The Administrative Council approved a twelve-week pilot on April 22. Starting May 18, purchases under $1,500 will use a shorter approval route when they do not require an information-security review. A request must meet both conditions to qualify. Requests that require an information-security review will remain outside the shorter route, regardless of their value. The published council decision does not explain why council members selected this pilot or identify the evidence they used.

Procurement will track the pilot through scheduled reporting. Every two weeks, it will publish the median and 90th-percentile approval times for requests eligible for the shorter route. Those measures will show how long eligible requests take during the pilot, including the experience of requests near the slower end of the distribution. The announced reporting does not include reasons for individual delays.

Staff should continue to follow university purchasing policy while the pilot runs. The figures on informal workarounds do not change the reimbursement rules. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies.

The university knows that many survey respondents encountered waits longer than five business days, that some respondents used informal workarounds, and that requests involving information-security review took longer at the median than requests without that review. It knows approval times improved in March after staffing and the form changed. It does not know why respondents used workarounds, why individual requests stalled, which March change affected approval times, or why the council chose the pilot. The biweekly reports will add current performance data for eligible purchases over the twelve-week test.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P4_R4_C2 -->

</details>

````text
Equipment-purchase delays: What the records show and what comes next

In March 2026, North Coast University sent a voluntary purchasing survey to 1,180 staff members and received 412 responses. Of those respondents, 238 said they had waited more than five business days for at least one equipment-purchase approval during the previous six months. The result describes the experiences of the people who answered the survey. Since 768 invited staff members did not respond, it does not provide an equipment-purchase history for everyone invited to participate. The available count records respondents with at least one such wait, rather than the total number of delayed approvals.

The same survey found that 176 respondents had used a personal credit card or another informal workaround for at least one work purchase during the previous six months. Because the report contains aggregate totals and does not cross-tabulate approval waits with workarounds, the university does not know how many people reported both. It also cannot establish whether respondents used a workaround because of an approval delay or for another reason, since the survey did not ask why they did so. Both survey figures describe the 412 respondents, not all 1,180 staff members invited to participate.

Procurement system logs provide a different view of the process. They cover all 2,940 equipment-purchase requests submitted from September 2025 through February 2026. The median time from submission to final approval was 4.2 business days, and the 90th percentile was 11.6 business days. Half of the requests reached final approval within 4.2 business days, while 90 percent did so within 11.6 business days. The median describes the midpoint of the full set, and the 90th percentile provides a view of requests toward the longer end of the distribution. These figures cover requests rather than survey respondents and should not be treated as a count of individual staff experiences.

Approval times differed by review type. Requests that required an information-security review had a median approval time of 9.1 business days. Requests without that review had a median of 3.4 business days. The logs identify review type and elapsed time, but not the reasons for individual delays. The two medians describe the recorded groups; the available fields do not assign a cause to the difference or explain why a specific request took longer than another.

The university has more recent figures for February and March, when two parts of the purchasing process changed. Procurement had six approval officers in January and February 2026, then eight in March. Median approval time was 4.8 business days in February and 3.6 business days in March. The university also introduced a revised request form on March 1. Because staffing and the form changed at the same time, the records do not isolate the effect of either change. The decline in the monthly median cannot be assigned separately to the additional officers or the revised form.

On April 22, the Administrative Council approved a twelve-week pilot for purchases under $1,500 that do not require an information-security review. Eligible purchases will use a shorter approval route. The published council decision gives neither the reasons members chose this pilot nor the evidence they relied on, leaving the basis for their choice unknown in the published record.

The pilot starts May 18. Procurement will publish median and 90th-percentile approval times for eligible requests every two weeks. This schedule will give staff regular figures for both the midpoint and the longer end of processing times among requests using the shorter route during the twelve weeks. Successive reports will make it possible to observe whether those two measures change during the pilot. The announced figures will not, by themselves, identify reasons for individual delays or isolate the effect of the shorter route without a comparison designed for that purpose.

The information scheduled for publication does not cover four unresolved issues: the overlap between survey respondents who reported waits and workarounds; respondents’ reasons for using workarounds; the reasons for individual delays in the procurement logs; and the separate effects of the March staffing increase and request-form revision. Answering those questions would require information beyond the median and 90th-percentile times in the planned reports.

The pilot changes the route for a defined group of purchases, but it does not suspend the university’s purchasing rules. The university will not reimburse purchases made outside policy unless an existing exception applies. Staff should continue to follow the approved process and use an existing exception only when it applies. The next scheduled step is the May 18 pilot start, followed by Procurement’s approval-time reports every two weeks for twelve weeks.
````

````text
# Decision

I recommend a limited transition from the manual exception queue to the proposed rules-based route, with protected analyst review for overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records. We should also disable the document-attachment reset rule immediately. We should not fully retire the manual queue now, because the proposed rules produced different outcomes in twelve shadow-test cases that Compliance says still require analyst judgment, and a compliant full retirement would take at least six weeks.

This approach can be ready by June 15. Engineering can disable the reset rule in five business days and establish the protected manual lane in eight business days. All other eligible exceptions should enter the rules-based route, subject to Operations auditing every resulting decision for the first 30 days. The manual capability should remain available during this period for the protected categories, audit escalations, and operational fallback.

The transition should be treated as a controlled production test, not as evidence that complete automation is ready. At the end of 30 days, we should decide whether to expand the rules-based route, extend the transition, or pause it based on decision consistency, compliance findings, processing times, and performance under higher volumes.

# Evidence

The current process has a material waiting-time problem. From January through April 2026, 1,840 wholesale orders rejected by the automated credit screen entered the manual queue. Of those, 1,126 waited at least 24 hours before an analyst opened them. The timestamp audit traced 804 of those waits to the routing rule that returns an order to the bottom of the queue whenever a sales representative attaches a new document. Because routing events were matched to affected order timestamps, the record supports attributing those 804 waits to the reset rule.

The remaining waits of at least 24 hours had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not quantify each cause, so we cannot rank their contributions or claim that changing the reset rule will eliminate all long waits.

The two-week shadow test provides direct support for a limited transition. It processed 210 new exceptions through both the current queue and the proposed route at the same time, using the same submitted documents. For 198 orders, the two routes reached the same approve-or-decline result. Median decision time for those orders was 3.1 hours under the proposed route and 18.7 hours under the manual queue. The simultaneous assignment and common documents support attributing this measured difference, for those 198 test orders, to the routing method during the test.

The remaining twelve orders produced different results: nine involved overseas corporate registrations and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all twelve and determined that they continue to require analyst judgment until the rules are revised. A protected manual lane therefore addresses every divergent category observed in the test without delaying transition for the other cases.

# Risks

The proposed route has not been tested during a month-end volume spike. Its production performance, reliability, and decision consistency under peak demand are therefore unknown. Retaining manual fallback and monitoring throughput are necessary until the route has operated through higher-volume conditions.

The shadow test is also limited in size and duration. Agreement on 198 of 210 orders is encouraging, but it does not establish that other exception types will never require judgment. Thirty-day auditing may identify additional categories that need manual handling or rule changes.

Customer Support logged 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review during the same four months, but those complaints cannot be linked to the queue extract because Support records use customer accounts while the extract contains anonymized order IDs. We cannot claim that the queued orders caused those complaints. Likewise, 43 wholesale accounts did not renew, but the renewal report provides no reasons and was not linked to complaints or queue records. The record does not support attributing nonrenewals to review delays or either routing method.

Finally, the chief executive’s June 15 date has no stated rationale. We can meet it with the limited transition, but the date itself is not evidence for full retirement.

# Next 30 Days

Within five business days, Engineering will disable the document-attachment reset rule. Operations will verify through timestamp sampling that newly attached documents no longer return affected orders to the bottom of the queue.

Within eight business days, Engineering will create the protected manual lane for overseas registrations and recently corrected tax records. Operations and Compliance will validate the classification criteria before production use and confirm that every protected order reaches an analyst.

By June 15, we will activate the proposed route for all other new exceptions. For 30 days, Operations will audit every decision it produces, within the stated capacity of up to 350 orders per week. Audits will compare the routed decision with analyst review and record processing time, reversals, category, compliance concerns, and any fallback use.

We will monitor month-end performance separately, including incoming volume, time to decision, backlog, and system failures. Any new category with disputed outcomes or a compliance concern will move to manual review pending investigation. At day 30, Operations, Compliance, and Engineering will review the audit results and determine whether the evidence supports broader retirement, continued limited operation, or revision of the rules.
````
