````text
## Decision

I recommend a limited transition, not immediate retirement or a general extension of the current manual exception queue. We should disable the document-attachment reset rule, move overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records into a protected manual lane, and send the remaining eligible exceptions through the proposed rules-based route. The manual queue should stay available during the transition for the protected cases and any orders that fall outside the revised routing design.

This choice addresses a documented source of delay without getting ahead of the compliance work. It can also be ready by June 15: Engineering estimates five business days to remove the reset rule and eight business days to create the protected lane. Full retirement would take at least six weeks and requires a revised compliance rule set, so it cannot meet the requested date on the present record. The reason for June 15 was not provided, and this recommendation does not assume one.

## Evidence

Between January and April, 1,840 wholesale orders entered the manual queue after rejection by the automated credit screen. Of those, 1,126 waited at least 24 hours before an analyst opened them. The audit traced 804 of those long waits to the document-attachment reset rule by matching routing events with order timestamps. When a sales representative added a document, the rule returned the order to the bottom of the queue. That is a supported cause of delay and a direct target for correction.

The remaining waits of at least 24 hours had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not measure how much each cause contributed. Removing the reset rule should therefore reduce a known source of delay, but we should not claim that it will eliminate long waits.

The shadow test gives a sound basis for a controlled move beyond that rule change. It sent 210 new exceptions through the current queue and proposed route at the same time, using the same documents. For 198 orders, both routes reached the same approve-or-decline result. Median decision time for those orders was 3.1 hours under the proposed route and 18.7 hours under the manual queue. Because assignment timing and submitted documents were held constant, the test supports attributing that measured difference to routing method during the test.

The routes disagreed on 12 orders. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all 12 and determined that they still need analyst judgment until the rules are revised. A protected manual lane follows that finding.

Customer Support also recorded 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review, and 43 wholesale accounts did not renew in the same four months. Those figures provide context, but they cannot be tied to the queue delays. Complaint records use customer accounts, the queue extract contains de-identified order IDs, and no linkage was performed. The renewal report states no reasons and was not linked to either data set.

## Risks

The proposed route has not been tested during a month-end volume spike. Its test results may not hold at higher volume, so the transition needs an operating limit and a fallback to manual handling.

The 12 disagreements show that the current rules do not cover every exception safely. If the protected-lane criteria are applied incorrectly, cases that require judgment could enter the automated route. We also do not know whether other uncommon case types will produce disagreements because the shadow test covered only 210 orders.

A faster route may reduce review time, but the record does not establish that it will reduce complaints or improve renewals. We should not use either outcome as proof of success without linking the relevant records. Finally, disabling the reset rule will address 804 audited waits, but weekends, tax-record gaps, and capacity may continue to produce delays.

## Next 30 Days

Engineering should begin both changes immediately: disable the attachment reset within five business days and deploy the protected manual lane within eight. Operations and Compliance should approve the lane criteria before activation, with overseas registrations and corrected tax records explicitly routed to analysts.

Once those controls are live, Operations should place eligible exceptions on the proposed route, capped at 350 orders per week so every routed decision can be audited for 30 days. The audit should compare the proposed decision with analyst review and record decision time, case type, document changes, and any routing failure. Any disagreement should go to Compliance before release, and recurring patterns should trigger a pause for the affected case type.

During the first month-end spike, Operations should track incoming volume, time to decision, backlog, and manual-lane capacity separately. If volume exceeds the audit cap, excess orders should remain in manual handling rather than pass through without review.

At day 30, Operations should report agreement rates, turnaround times, protected-lane volumes, audit findings, and month-end performance. We should consider full retirement only after Compliance revises the rule set and the transition data show that the proposed route remains reliable under peak conditions.
````

# Decision

I recommend a limited transition. By June 15, Engineering should disable the document-attachment reset rule, start the proposed rules-based route for exceptions outside the two identified case types, and direct overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records to a protected manual lane. Operations should audit each rules-based decision for 30 days, with routed volume capped at our audit capacity of 350 orders per week.

This approach addresses a proven source of delay and uses the proposed route where the shadow test supports it. It also keeps analyst judgment for the twelve cases that produced conflicting results and gives us a controlled way to test performance during a month-end volume spike. We should defer full retirement. Engineering needs at least six weeks for that step, and Compliance requires a revised rule set first.

# Evidence

From January through April 2026, 1,840 wholesale orders entered the manual exception queue after the automated credit screen rejected them. Analysts did not open 1,126 of those orders for at least 24 hours. The timestamp audit matched routing events to order timestamps and traced 804 of those waits to the rule that returns an order to the bottom of the queue whenever a sales representative attaches a new document. Engineering can disable that rule in five business days.

The audit recorded weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity among the causes of the remaining long waits. It did not estimate how much each cause contributed. We therefore have support for removing the reset rule, but we cannot assign the rest of the delay to any one cause or claim that the proposed route will eliminate it.

The two-week shadow test gives us a basis for a controlled routing change. The test sent 210 new exceptions through both routes at the same time, using the same submitted documents. Both routes reached the same approve-or-decline result for 198 orders. For those 198, the proposed route produced a median decision time of 3.1 hours, compared with 18.7 hours in the manual queue. Because the test held timing and documents constant, we can attribute that measured difference during the test to the routing method.

The routes differed on twelve orders. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all twelve and requires analyst judgment for those case types until the team revises the rules. Engineering can place them in a protected manual lane in eight business days.

Customer Support logged 296 complaints that mentioned slow wholesale-order review during the same four months. We cannot connect those complaints to the 1,840 queued orders because Support stores records by customer account and the queue extract removed customer fields. No one performed a linkage. Forty-three wholesale accounts also failed to renew during the period, but the renewal report gives no reasons and no one linked it to complaints or queue records. We cannot claim that queue delays caused complaints or nonrenewals.

# Risks

The proposed route has not faced a month-end volume spike. Decision time, agreement with analysts, or both could change under that load. The 30-day audit should include the next month-end period and track route volume, median decision time, agreement, case type, and any reversals that auditors require.

The shadow test included 210 orders and found twelve disagreements concentrated in two known case types. A protected lane contains that known compliance risk, but other case patterns may surface as volume grows. Auditors need authority to stop rules-based processing for a case type if they find a disagreement that calls for analyst judgment.

Audit capacity sets another boundary. Operations can review each proposed-route decision for 30 days up to 350 orders per week. We should keep weekly routed volume within that limit and send excess exceptions to the manual queue. This constraint preserves full review during the transition.

Disabling the reset rule will prevent document attachments from sending orders to the bottom, but the record does not show how the change will affect the other recorded causes of long waits. We should measure those causes rather than assume the rule change resolves them.

# Next 30 Days

Engineering should disable the attachment reset rule within five business days and create the protected manual lane within eight. Operations and Compliance should define the two protected case types in routing instructions before launch and test that documents reach the correct lane.

On launch, Operations should route eligible exceptions through the proposed method, audit each decision, and cap volume at 350 per week. Analysts should record agreement, overrides, case type, submission time, decision time, and the reason for each override. Operations should report results each week and separate protected-lane performance from rules-based performance.

During the month-end spike, Operations should compare the same measures with the shadow-test baseline while avoiding claims beyond the observed 30-day period. Compliance should use disagreement cases to revise the rule set. At day 30, the executive team should decide whether to expand the route, extend the transition, or begin the six-week retirement work based on decision agreement, compliance findings, audit capacity, and month-end performance.

````text
# Decision

I recommend a limited transition from the manual exception queue to the proposed rules-based route. We should disable the document-attachment reset rule, send overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records to a protected manual lane, and use the proposed route for all other wholesale credit exceptions. For the first 30 days, Operations should audit every automated decision within its stated capacity of 350 orders per week.

We should not retire the manual queue now. Compliance has determined that the two identified exception types still require analyst judgment until the rules are revised, and full retirement would require at least six weeks plus a revised compliance rule set. Simply extending the current queue is also unwarranted. The timestamp audit identified a correctable routing defect behind most waits of at least 24 hours, while the shadow test showed substantially faster decisions under the proposed route for cases in which both routes reached the same result.

This transition narrows the manual queue to cases that currently need judgment while allowing lower-risk exceptions to move through a faster route. It can also be implemented through changes Engineering estimates at five and eight business days, making it the available option most compatible with the chief executive’s request for a change to be ready by June 15. The record does not explain the reason for that date.

# Evidence

From January through April 2026, 1,840 wholesale orders rejected by the automated credit screen entered the manual exception queue. Of those, 1,126 waited at least 24 hours before an analyst opened them. The audit traced 804 of those long waits to the rule that returned an order to the bottom of the queue whenever a sales representative attached a new document. Matching routing events with affected order timestamps established that relationship. Engineering can disable the reset rule in five business days.

The remaining long waits had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not estimate how much each cause contributed. Disabling the reset rule should therefore address the 804 waits attributed to it, but the record does not support a forecast for the remaining waits.

The two-week shadow test provides the strongest evidence for using the proposed route selectively. It processed 210 new exceptions through both routes at the same time using the same submitted documents. The two routes produced the same approve-or-decline result for 198 orders, or about 94 percent of the test volume. For those orders, median decision time was 3.1 hours under the proposed route and 18.7 hours under the manual queue. Because assignment was simultaneous and documents were identical, the test supports attributing that measured difference to the routing method during the test.

The routes differed on 12 orders: nine involving overseas corporate registrations and three involving recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all 12 and determined that they still require analyst judgment until the rule set is revised. Engineering can place both categories into a protected manual lane in eight business days.

Customer Support recorded 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review during the same four months, and 43 wholesale accounts did not renew. These figures indicate contemporaneous customer concerns and nonrenewals, but they cannot be attributed to the queue. Complaint records were organized by customer account, the queue extract contained anonymized order IDs, and no linkage was performed. The renewal report gave no reasons and was linked to neither complaints nor queue records.

# Risks

The proposed route has not been tested during a month-end volume spike. Its observed speed and agreement rate may not hold under heavier demand. A limited transition therefore requires active monitoring rather than assuming the two-week results will generalize.

The 12 divergent cases show that full automation would create an immediate compliance risk if judgment-dependent categories were not reliably isolated. The protected lane reduces that risk, but its classification and routing must be verified before activation.

Residual delay will remain in the manual lane and possibly elsewhere. Weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity were recorded as causes, but their individual effects are unknown. We should not promise that the transition will eliminate long waits or that it will change complaint or renewal outcomes.

The audit ceiling is also a constraint. Operations can review every proposed-route decision for 30 days only up to 350 orders per week. If volume exceeds that level, we will need to limit intake to the proposed route or establish a risk-based audit plan before exceeding capacity.

# Next 30 Days

During the first five business days, Engineering should disable the document-attachment reset rule. Operations should verify that newly attached documents no longer change an order’s queue position and track waits of at least 24 hours separately for the protected manual lane and the proposed route.

Within eight business days, Engineering should activate the protected lane for overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records. Compliance should validate the routing criteria before live use, and analysts should retain responsibility for every case entering that lane.

Once those controls are verified, Operations should begin the limited transition and audit every proposed-route decision for 30 days, subject to the 350-orders-per-week ceiling. The daily review should record route assignment, decision, elapsed time, document completeness, and any analyst disagreement. Month-end performance should be reviewed separately.

At day 30, Operations, Compliance, and Engineering should assess decision agreement, median and long-wait performance, protected-lane accuracy, audit findings, and month-end behavior. Any recommendation for broader retirement should wait for that evidence and a revised compliance rule set.
````

## Decision

I recommend a limited transition from the manual exception queue to the proposed rules-based route. We should not retire the manual queue now. Starting by June 15, standard exceptions should move through the proposed route, while overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records go to a protected manual lane. Operations should audit every decision from the proposed route for 30 days, subject to the stated capacity of 350 orders per week.

This approach addresses a documented source of delay without automating cases that Compliance says still require analyst judgment. It also gives us operating evidence before we consider full retirement. A complete shutdown would take at least six weeks and depends on a revised compliance rule set, so it cannot provide a sound change by June 15. The record does not explain why that date was chosen, but the limited transition can be ready within it.

Engineering should also disable the document-attachment reset rule. That change can be completed in five business days and should proceed whether or not the new route launches on schedule.

## Evidence

The manual queue is producing long waits. From January through April 2026, it received 1,840 wholesale orders rejected by the automated credit screen. Of those, 1,126 waited at least 24 hours before an analyst opened them. That is more than half of all orders entering the queue.

The audit established a specific cause for 804 of those long waits. When a sales representative attached a new document, a routing rule sent the order back to the bottom of the queue. Auditors matched routing events to affected order timestamps, so the relationship is supported by the record. Engineering can disable that rule in five business days.

The remaining waits of at least 24 hours had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not estimate how much each cause contributed. We therefore should not assume that removing the reset rule will eliminate the other delays.

The shadow test provides a reasonable basis for moving standard exceptions to the proposed route. During two weeks, 210 new exceptions went through both routes at the same time using the same submitted documents. The routes reached the same approve-or-decline result for 198 orders. For those orders, median decision time was 3.1 hours under the proposed route and 18.7 hours under the manual queue. Because the test controlled assignment timing and documents, it supports attributing that measured difference to the routing method during the test.

The twelve disagreements define the present limit of automation. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all twelve and said they still require analyst judgment until the rules are revised. Engineering can direct both categories into a protected manual lane within eight business days.

Customer Support recorded 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review during the same four months. We cannot say which, if any, arose from orders in the queue because support records use customer accounts and the queue extract contains anonymized order IDs. Likewise, 43 wholesale accounts did not renew, but the renewal report gives no reasons and was not linked to complaints or queue records. These figures show concurrent customer concerns and nonrenewals, not that queue delays caused either one.

## Risks

The strongest operational risk is applying the two-week result too broadly. The proposed route has not been tested during a month-end volume spike. Its 3.1-hour median may not hold under heavier volume, and the test does not establish performance outside the 198 matching cases.

A second risk is classification. Overseas registrations and corrected tax records must reliably reach the protected lane. If they do not, the proposed route could issue decisions in cases that Compliance has reserved for analysts.

The manual lane will also retain some delays. Weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity remain recorded causes, though their individual effects are unknown. Disabling the reset rule addresses 804 audited waits, but it is not evidence that every long wait will disappear.

Finally, the 30-day audit capacity has a ceiling of 350 orders per week. If transition volume exceeds that level, Operations cannot audit every automated decision under the current staffing plan.

## Next 30 Days

Within five business days, Engineering will disable the document-attachment reset rule. Operations will verify that new attachments no longer change an order’s queue position and will review affected timestamps for any recurrence.

Within eight business days, Engineering will create the protected manual lane for overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records. Compliance and Operations will test that both categories are captured before standard exceptions move to the proposed route.

By June 15, we will begin the limited transition if those checks pass. For 30 days, Operations will audit every proposed-route decision, up to 350 orders per week. The audit will record the proposed decision, final audited decision, processing time, case category, and any routing error. We will report volumes above the audit ceiling rather than allowing unaudited decisions to proceed.

During the same period, we will include a month-end volume spike if one occurs and track median decision time separately for the proposed route and protected manual lane. After 30 days, Operations and Compliance will decide whether the evidence supports broader use, rule revision, or continued manual handling. Full retirement will remain off the table until Compliance revises the rule set and the proposed route performs acceptably under the audited transition.

The test covered two weeks and did not include a month-end volume spike. We do not know whether the proposed route will sustain its measured speed or decision agreement under peak volume.

Other, unrecognized exception patterns may produce disagreements. Auditing each decision gives Operations a way to identify and route those patterns. The limit of 350 orders per week constrains that control. If intake exceeds the limit, Operations should send the excess to manual review.

Disabling the reset rule addresses the cause linked to 804 long waits, but it will not resolve waits associated with weekends, incomplete tax records, or analyst capacity. We cannot forecast the delays from those causes.

## Next 30 Days

Engineering should disable the reset rule within five business days. By business day eight, Engineering should activate the protected manual lane for overseas registrations and corrected tax records and direct other incoming exceptions to the proposed route.

Operations should audit each proposed-route decision for 30 days, subject to the limit of 350 orders per week. Operations should record the route result, analyst result, decision time, exception type, document-attachment events, and any return to manual review. Compliance should review each disagreement and decide whether it identifies another protected case type or a needed rule revision.

Operations should report each week to the executive team on volume, median decision time, agreement with analyst audit, disagreements by case type, protected-lane volume, and orders waiting at least 24 hours. Reports should keep complaint and renewal counts separate unless analysts create a valid link to individual queued orders.

At day 30, I will recommend whether to expand, revise, or stop the transition based on audited agreement, protected-lane demand, residual waits, and peak-volume performance if the period includes a spike. We should defer full retirement until Compliance approves a revised rule set and the route performs within agreed controls.
````

Of those long waits, 804 were traced to a routing rule that returned an order to the bottom of the queue whenever a sales representative attached a new document. Matching routing events to affected order timestamps established that relationship.

The other long waits had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not estimate how much each cause contributed. The methods reached the same approve-or-decline result for 198 orders. For those orders, median decision time was 3.1 hours under the proposed route and 18.7 hours under the manual queue.

Simultaneous assignment with the same documents supports attributing the measured time difference for those 198 orders during the test to routing method.

Twelve orders had different results. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all twelve and said they require analyst judgment until the rule set is revised.

## Risks

Month-end volume remains untested. The timing comparison covers the 198 matching cases under the two-week test conditions. A spike may produce different timing, capacity, or routing results.

The protected lane retains manual handling for the twelve-case categories identified by Compliance, but the record does not establish how often those categories will appear in future volume. Weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity could continue to produce long waits; the audit gives no basis for ranking their contributions.

## Next 30 Days

Engineering should disable the document-attachment reset rule within five business days. Operations will track queue-entry and analyst-open timestamps after the change and review recorded routing events for waits of at least 24 hours. Within eight business days, Engineering should place overseas registrations and corrected tax records in the protected manual lane. Compliance should validate that both categories reach an analyst before eligible exceptions begin using the proposed route.

Once the lane is active, Operations should run the proposed route for eligible exceptions for 30 days and audit every decision, subject to the 350-order weekly ceiling. Each audit will compare the proposed route’s approve-or-decline result with analyst review and record decision time. Any disagreement should go to Compliance before the order leaves exception review.

Operations will report weekly volume, median decision time, disagreements, protected-lane volume, and the number of decisions not audited if volume exceeds the ceiling. Complaint and renewal data should remain separate from route results unless a valid linkage is performed.

````text
# Decision

I recommend a limited transition from the manual exception queue to the proposed rules-based route. We should disable the document-attachment reset rule, route eligible exceptions through the proposed method, and place cases involving overseas corporate registrations or recently corrected tax records into a protected manual lane. For the first 30 days, Operations should audit every decision from the proposed route, subject to the available capacity of 350 orders per week.

We should not retire the manual queue now. Compliance has determined that two identifiable case types still require analyst judgment until the rule set is revised, and full retirement would take at least six weeks. We also should not simply extend the current queue unchanged. The record establishes a significant, correctable source of delay in the existing process, while the shadow test demonstrates substantially faster decisions for cases on which the two methods agreed.

This transition provides a change that can be readied on the requested timeline using work Engineering can complete in five to eight business days. The record does not explain why the chief executive selected June 15, so that date should be treated as a delivery requirement, not as evidence of customer, financial, or compliance impact.

# Evidence

From January through April 2026, 1,840 wholesale orders rejected by the automated credit screen entered the manual exception queue. A timestamp audit found that 1,126 orders, or approximately 61 percent, waited at least 24 hours before an analyst opened them. The audit traced 804 of those long waits to the routing rule that moved an order to the bottom of the queue whenever a sales representative attached a new document. That relationship was established by matching routing events with affected order timestamps.

The remaining waits of at least 24 hours had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. Because the audit did not estimate the contribution of each cause, we should not assume that changing the reset rule will eliminate all long waits. It directly addresses the 804 waits attributed to that rule, while other delays may persist.

The two-week shadow test provides the strongest evidence for a controlled transition. It processed 210 new exceptions through both the current queue and the proposed rules-based route. The routes received each exception at the same time and used the same submitted documents. For 198 orders, they produced the same approve-or-decline result. Among those orders, median decision time was 3.1 hours under the proposed route and 18.7 hours under the manual queue. Because the test controlled assignment timing and documents, it supports attributing the measured difference for those 198 cases to the routing method during the test.

The other 12 orders produced different outcomes. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all 12 and determined that they require analyst judgment until the rule set is revised. This finding supports a protected manual lane for those categories, but it does not establish that all other possible exception types are suitable for permanent automation.

Customer Support recorded 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review during the same four months. However, the support system stores records by customer account, while the queue extract contains order IDs with customer fields removed. Because no linkage was performed, we cannot say which complaints concerned queued orders or whether particular waits caused complaints.

Similarly, 43 wholesale accounts did not renew during the period, but the renewal report gives no reasons and was not linked to complaints or queue records. The record therefore does not establish that queue delays, review complaints, or any other identified factor caused those nonrenewals.

# Risks

The principal operational risk is that the proposed route has not been tested during a month-end volume spike. Its observed speed and agreement rate may not hold under higher load. A second risk is decision quality outside the tested mix of 210 orders. The shadow test identified two categories requiring judgment, but its size and duration do not prove that these are the only categories needing manual review.

Retaining a protected manual lane also leaves some exposure to delay from weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The available evidence identifies these causes but does not quantify them. Disabling the reset rule may materially improve queue behavior without resolving those other constraints.

There is also a risk of overstating the business case. Complaint and nonrenewal counts provide relevant context, but neither dataset was linked to queued orders. We should monitor these measures without presenting the transition as a proven remedy for complaints or account losses.

# Next 30 Days

Engineering should first disable the document-attachment reset rule within five business days. It should then implement, within eight business days, a protected manual lane for overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records. Compliance should confirm the lane definitions and approve the operating procedure before live routing begins.

Once the limited transition starts, eligible exceptions should enter the proposed route, while protected cases continue to receive analyst judgment. Operations should audit every proposed-route decision for 30 days, up to the stated capacity of 350 orders per week. Each audit should record the automated result, the reviewer’s result, decision time, exception type, document status, and whether the order arrived during a volume spike.

Operations should report weekly on order counts, agreement rates, median decision times, manual-lane volumes, audit findings, and any newly observed categories requiring judgment. Month-end performance should be reported separately because it has not yet been tested.

At day 30, Compliance, Engineering, and Operations should review the audit evidence. Full retirement should be considered only after the rule set is revised, protected cases are addressed, and performance under month-end volume is known.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P5_R3_C0 -->

</details>

````text
## Decision

I recommend a limited transition. We should not retire the manual exception queue now, but we should stop sending every rejected wholesale order through it.

Engineering should disable the document-attachment reset rule and establish a protected manual lane for overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records. All other exceptions should use the proposed rules-based route for 30 days, with Operations auditing every decision. If weekly volume exceeds our audit capacity of 350 orders, the excess should remain in the manual queue.

This approach addresses a documented source of delay, uses the faster route where the shadow test found matching decisions, and keeps analyst judgment where Compliance requires it. It can also be ready within the chief executive's June 15 target: Engineering needs five business days to remove the reset rule and eight business days to create the protected lane.

Full retirement is not ready. It would take at least six weeks and depends on a revised compliance rule set. Extending the current queue without changes would leave a known routing problem in place.

## Evidence

From January through April, 1,840 wholesale orders entered the manual queue. Analysts did not open 1,126 of them for at least 24 hours. The audit linked 804 of those long waits to the document-attachment reset rule by matching routing events with order timestamps. When a sales representative added a document, the rule returned the order to the bottom of the queue.

The remaining waits of at least 24 hours had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not determine how much each cause contributed. Disabling the reset rule should therefore remove one established source of delay, but it will not eliminate every long wait.

The two-week shadow test provides a reasonable basis for moving part of the workload. Both routes received the same 210 exceptions at the same time and used the same documents. They reached the same approve-or-decline result on 198 orders. For those orders, the proposed route had a median decision time of 3.1 hours, compared with 18.7 hours in the manual queue. Because the assignments were concurrent and the documents were identical, the test supports attributing that 15.6-hour difference to the routing method during the test.

The routes disagreed on 12 orders. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all 12 and determined that they still need analyst judgment until the rules change. A protected manual lane follows that finding directly.

Customer Support also recorded 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review during the four-month period. Those complaints show that customers were reporting delays, but we cannot tie them to specific queued orders because the two datasets use different identifiers and no linkage was performed. Likewise, 43 wholesale accounts did not renew, but the renewal report gives no reasons and was not linked to the complaint or queue records. We should not claim that queue delays caused either the complaints or the nonrenewals.

## Risks

The proposed route has not been tested during a month-end volume spike. The shadow test covered 210 orders over two weeks, so it does not establish how the route will perform under higher volume. The 30-day transition needs to capture and separately review month-end performance before we consider full retirement.

The proposed route also produced different decisions in 12 of 210 cases. Protecting the two identified case types addresses the disagreements observed in the test, but the record does not establish that no other exception type could require judgment in a larger sample. Auditing every proposed-route decision for 30 days limits that exposure and gives Compliance evidence for revising the rule set.

The manual lane will still face some sources of delay. Weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity appeared among the recorded causes of long waits, though their individual contributions are unknown. The protected lane should therefore be monitored for aging rather than treated as inherently fast.

Finally, June 15 is a readiness target, not evidence that full retirement should occur by then. The request gives no reason for the date. We can meet it with the limited transition without taking the unsupported risk of eliminating analyst review.

## Next 30 Days

Within five business days, Engineering should disable the document-attachment reset rule. Operations should confirm through timestamp checks that adding a document no longer changes an order's queue position.

Within eight business days, Engineering should place overseas registrations and recently corrected tax records into the protected manual lane. Operations and Compliance should verify that analysts review every order in those categories.

Once both changes are active, route all other new exceptions through the proposed method for 30 days. Operations will audit every decision, up to 350 orders per week. The audit should record the proposed result, the auditor's result, decision time, exception type, and any case sent back for analyst judgment. Volume above the weekly audit limit should stay in the manual queue.

At the end of each week, Operations should report volumes, matching and differing decisions, median decision times, orders waiting at least 24 hours, and protected-lane aging. Any disagreement should go to Compliance for review.

Before full retirement, the executive team should require results from a month-end spike, completion of the revised compliance rule set, and evidence from the 30-day audit that the proposed route remains reliable outside the two protected categories.
````

## Decision

I recommend a limited transition from the manual exception queue to the proposed rules-based route. We should send standard exceptions through the proposed route, place overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records in a protected manual lane, and audit each rules-based decision for 30 days. We should also disable the document-attachment reset rule throughout the manual workflow.

We should not retire the manual queue now. Compliance says the two identified case types require analyst judgment until the rule set changes, and full retirement would require a revised rule set and at least six weeks. We also lack results from a month-end volume spike. Extending the current queue without changing its routing would preserve a known source of delay and forgo the time improvement measured in the shadow test.

This approach offers a change with an eight-business-day implementation estimate while keeping analysts responsible for cases the rules cannot handle. The chief executive requested readiness by June 15. The record gives no reason for that date, so we should treat it as a delivery target, not as grounds to relax compliance controls.

## Evidence

From January through April 2026, 1,840 wholesale orders entered the manual queue after the automated credit screen rejected them. Analysts did not open 1,126 for at least 24 hours. The timestamp audit linked 804 of those waits to the document-attachment reset rule: when a sales representative attached a new document, the rule returned the order to the bottom of the queue. Investigators established this link by matching routing events with order timestamps. Engineering can remove that rule within five business days.

The other long waits had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not determine how much each cause contributed. Disabling the reset rule addresses the mechanism tied to 804 waits, but the record does not support a forecast for all long waits or total review time after the change.

The two-week shadow test provides the basis for moving standard exceptions. The test sent 210 new exceptions through both routes at the same time, using the same documents. Both routes reached the same approve-or-decline result for 198 orders. For those orders, the proposed route reduced median decision time from 18.7 hours to 3.1 hours, a difference of 15.6 hours. The test design supports attributing that measured difference for those 198 orders during the test to the routing method.

The routes disagreed on 12 orders. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all 12 and requires analyst judgment for those cases until the team revises the rules. A protected lane keeps those categories with analysts while allowing other exceptions to use the proposed route.

Customer Support logged 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review during the same period. We cannot determine how many concerned queued orders because Support stores records by customer account, the queue extract contains deidentified order IDs, and no one linked the datasets. The 43 wholesale accounts that did not renew require the same restraint. The renewal report provides no reasons, and no one linked those accounts to complaints or queue records. The figures document concurrent concerns; they do not establish that queue delays caused complaints or nonrenewals.

## Risks

The proposed route has not faced a month-end volume spike. Its speed and result agreement could change under higher load or a different case mix. The shadow test covered 210 orders across two weeks, so its 198 matching decisions do not establish future performance for every standard exception.

The protected lane depends on correct classification. A classification error could send a case that needs judgment through the proposed route. Operations must keep analyst review available for uncertain cases and track transfers between lanes.

Operations can audit up to 350 orders per week. If intake exceeds that limit, we should cap rules-based routing at the volume Operations can audit and send the excess to the manual lane.

## Next 30 Days

Engineering should disable the document-attachment reset rule within five business days. Within eight business days, it should create the protected lane, including a manual fallback for uncertain classifications. Operations and Compliance should verify the lane criteria before release.

After verification, Operations should begin the limited transition and audit every proposed-route decision for 30 days, up to 350 orders per week. The audit log should capture route, decision, decision time, audit result, lane transfers, and classification errors. Operations should separate month-end performance from other periods in its weekly reports so the team can assess the untested load condition.

Compliance should review each disagreement and revise the rule set using observed cases. At day 30, the executive team should consider full retirement only if audits show acceptable result agreement, confirm that the protected lane captures the identified categories, and demonstrate month-end performance within operating and audit capacity. If those conditions fail, we should retain the protected lane and adjust the rules before expanding the proposed route.

# Decision

I recommend a limited transition from the current manual exception queue to the proposed rules-based route. We should not retire the manual queue now, because the shadow test identified two case types—overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records—that Compliance says still require analyst judgment until the rules are revised. We also should not extend the current queue unchanged, because the record establishes a significant routing defect and shows a substantial time advantage for the proposed route on cases where both methods reached the same decision.

Under the transition, most wholesale orders rejected by the automated credit screen would enter the proposed rules-based route. Overseas registrations and corrected tax records would enter a protected manual lane. Engineering should also disable the rule that resets an order to the bottom of the queue when a sales representative attaches a new document. Operations would audit every decision produced by the proposed route for 30 days, subject to the available capacity of 350 orders per week.

This approach can produce a meaningful change without assuming that the shadow test supports full automation. It also provides a change responsive to the chief executive’s June 15 request. The record does not explain the reason for that date, so the transition should be designed around operational and compliance readiness rather than an inferred business event.

# Evidence

From January through April 2026, 1,840 wholesale orders entered the manual exception queue. Of those, 1,126—about 61 percent—waited at least 24 hours before an analyst opened them. The audit traced 804 of these long waits to the document-attachment reset rule by matching routing events with affected order timestamps. That evidence supports treating the reset rule as a direct contributor to those 804 waits and makes disabling it an immediate, evidence-based intervention.

The remaining long waits had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not quantify the contribution of each. We therefore should not assume that changing the routing rule, or introducing the proposed route, will eliminate every delay.

The shadow test offers stronger support for a controlled transition. During two weeks, 210 new exceptions were processed through both methods using the same documents, with assignment occurring at the same time. For 198 orders, the proposed route and manual queue produced the same approve-or-decline result. Among those orders, median decision time was 3.1 hours under the proposed route and 18.7 hours under the manual queue. Because the test controlled assignment timing and submitted documents, the measured difference for those 198 orders can be attributed to the routing method during the test.

The other 12 orders produced different results: nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all 12 and determined that each still requires analyst judgment pending revision of the rule set. This finding supports isolating those categories in a manual lane; it does not support routing them automatically or retiring manual review altogether.

Customer Support logged 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review during the four-month period, but the support and queue records were not linked. We cannot determine whether those complaints concerned the audited orders or whether particular queue waits produced particular complaints. Similarly, 43 wholesale accounts did not renew, but the renewal report provides no reasons and was not linked to complaints or queue records. The record therefore does not establish that queue delays caused complaints or nonrenewals.

# Risks

The largest operational risk is scaling from a two-week, 210-order shadow test to normal production. The proposed route has not been tested during a month-end volume spike, so its speed and stability under peak conditions remain unknown. The observed median time improvement should not be projected unchanged to higher volumes without further evidence.

There is also a decision-quality risk. Agreement on 198 orders shows consistency between the routes in the test, not that either result was independently correct. The 12 disagreements demonstrate that the current rule set does not adequately handle at least two identifiable categories. New exception patterns could emerge after launch.

The manual lane may continue to experience delays from weekends, incomplete tax records, or analyst capacity. Disabling the reset rule addresses the 804 waits traced to that rule, but the record does not support estimating how much it will reduce total waiting time. Finally, the audit limit of 350 proposed-route orders per week could be exceeded, requiring intake controls or continued use of the manual queue.

# Next 30 Days

In the first five business days, Engineering should disable the document-attachment reset rule and Operations should confirm through timestamp checks that new attachments no longer move orders to the bottom of the queue.

Within eight business days, Engineering should establish the protected manual lane for overseas registrations and corrected tax records. Compliance should provide clear identification criteria so that those cases are consistently diverted before automated handling.

Once both controls are in place, Operations should begin the limited transition and audit every proposed-route decision for 30 days, up to 350 orders weekly. The audit should record routing category, decision, processing time, any later correction, and any case requiring analyst intervention. Results should be reviewed weekly with Engineering and Compliance.

During the period, we should include a month-end volume spike if one occurs, monitor whether audit demand approaches capacity, and compare decision times separately for the proposed route and protected manual lane. Full retirement should remain off the table until Compliance revises the rule set, the disagreement categories are retested, peak-volume performance is observed, and the 30-day audit provides sufficient evidence on decision quality and operating capacity.
````

## Decision

I recommend a limited transition from the manual exception queue to the proposed rules-based route. We should not retire the manual queue now. Instead, Engineering should disable the document-attachment reset rule, move overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records into a protected manual lane, and route all other exceptions through the proposed process for 30 days. Operations should audit every automated decision during that period, subject to the stated capacity of 350 orders per week.

This approach addresses a verified source of delay and uses the faster route where the shadow test supports doing so. It also keeps analysts responsible for the case types that Compliance says still require judgment. Full retirement is not ready: it would take at least six weeks, requires a revised compliance rule set, and has no test evidence from a month-end volume spike.

The limited transition can be ready before the chief executive's June 15 date based on Engineering's five-day and eight-day estimates. The record does not state why June 15 was selected, so we should treat it as a delivery date, not as evidence that a full retirement is necessary by then.

## Evidence

From January through April 2026, 1,840 wholesale orders rejected by the automated credit screen entered the manual exception queue. Of those, 1,126 waited at least 24 hours before an analyst opened them. That is a substantial queue delay, although the audit did not determine how those waits affected customer behavior or order outcomes.

The audit did establish one direct cause. It traced 804 of the waits of at least 24 hours to a routing rule that moved an order back to the bottom of the queue whenever a sales representative attached a new document. Investigators matched routing events to affected order timestamps. Engineering can disable that rule in five business days, giving us a specific and relatively quick correction to a documented problem.

The remaining long waits had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not estimate how much each cause contributed. We therefore should not assume that changing the routing rule will eliminate every long wait.

Customer Support recorded 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review during the same four months. Those complaints show that customers were reporting delays, but the records cannot establish that the manual queue caused any particular complaint. Support data is organized by customer account, while the queue extract contains order IDs and has no customer fields. No linkage was performed. The same limitation applies to the 43 wholesale accounts that did not renew. The renewal report provides no reasons, and it was not linked to either complaints or queue records. We should not attribute those nonrenewals to review delays.

The two-week shadow test gives stronger evidence for the proposed route. It processed 210 new exceptions through both routes at the same time using the same documents. For 198 orders, the proposed route reached the same approve-or-decline result as the manual queue. Median decision time for those orders was 3.1 hours under the proposed route, compared with 18.7 hours under the manual queue. Because the routes received the exceptions simultaneously and used identical submissions, the test supports attributing that measured difference to the routing method during the test.

Twelve results differed. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all twelve and determined that they still require analyst judgment until the rules are revised. Engineering can place both case types into a protected manual lane within eight business days.

## Risks

The largest operational risk is treating a short shadow test as proof that the proposed route will perform equally well under all conditions. It has not been tested during a month-end volume spike. Processing time, audit workload, or result consistency could change at higher volumes.

There is also a compliance risk if overseas registrations or corrected tax records escape the protected lane. All twelve differing decisions came from those two categories, and Compliance has explicitly retained analyst judgment for them. The transition controls must identify and route those cases reliably.

A 30-day audit will require sustained Operations attention. Capacity is capped at 350 orders per week. If transition volume exceeds that limit, Operations cannot audit every proposed-route decision under the stated staffing capacity. The scope would then need to remain within the audit limit rather than continuing without the promised review.

Finally, faster routing should not be presented as a proven answer to complaints or nonrenewals. The record supports a time improvement for 198 tested orders. It does not connect queue delays to individual complaints, account retention, or revenue.

## Next 30 Days

Engineering should disable the document-attachment reset rule within five business days. By business day eight, it should also establish the protected manual lane for overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records. Operations and Compliance should verify that both categories enter that lane before the proposed route handles other exceptions.

Once those controls are confirmed, begin the 30-day limited transition. Route eligible exceptions through the proposed method and have Operations audit every resulting decision, up to 350 orders per week. Record decision agreement, time to decision, routing errors, case type, and any instances requiring analyst intervention.

Include any month-end volume spike that falls within the 30-day window and report its results separately. At the end of the period, Compliance should review the audit findings and the twelve previously identified exception patterns. The executive team can then decide whether to expand the proposed route, extend the limited transition, or begin the six-week retirement process after the compliance rules are revised.
````

Auditors established the link by matching routing events with order timestamps. Disabling the rule addresses a documented cause. Auditors did not estimate how much delay the change will remove because weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity also caused long waits.

The two-week shadow test gives us grounds to route most tested exceptions through the proposed route. The twelve differing results set the current limit of automation. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved tax records with recent corrections. Compliance reviewed the twelve and requires analyst judgment until the team revises the rules. A protected lane follows that direction while the other tested cases use the faster route.

We should examine customer signals, but we cannot make a causal claim. Customer Support logged 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review during the same period. Support stores records by account, while the queue extract contains order IDs with customer fields removed. No one linked the sources. Forty-three wholesale accounts did not renew, but the renewal report lists no reasons, and no one linked those accounts to complaints or queued orders. We cannot claim that queue delays caused the complaints or nonrenewals.

# Risks

The shadow test did not cover a month-end volume spike. A 210-order, two-week test cannot establish performance during that condition or across a longer period. We can use the 198 matching results to support a controlled release, but they do not prove that future eligible orders will match manual decisions.

Operations can audit each proposed-route decision for 30 days while volume remains at or below 350 orders per week. Higher volume would exceed the stated capacity unless Operations diverts the excess to manual review.

The protected lane will retain manual work and its delays. Disabling the attachment reset should remove one documented delay mechanism, but it will not address weekends, incomplete tax records, or analyst capacity. Auditors provided no estimate of the wait time that those causes produce.

# Next 30 Days

Engineering should disable the reset rule and build the protected lane. Before release, Operations and Compliance should verify that the route sends both named case types to that lane. Engineering should confirm how the two work estimates combine and whether the transition can meet June 15.

We should cap intake at 350 orders per week and divert excess orders to manual review so Operations can audit each rules-based decision. Operations should compare each result with analyst review for the full 30 days.

Operations should track route volume, median decision time, waits of at least 24 hours, and differences between rules-based and analyst decisions. The team should report results by route and case type. We should include a month-end spike in the 30-day observation window if the calendar permits. If the window misses month-end, Harborline should retain the manual lane until we test the proposed route during a spike.

Compliance should review each difference and decide whether rule changes can resolve it. On day 30, the executive team should consider full retirement if Compliance approves the revised rule set, auditors find consistent decisions, and we see acceptable performance during the month-end test. Until those conditions hold, Harborline should keep the protected manual lane.

## Decision

I recommend a limited transition rather than retiring or extending the manual exception queue in its current form. We should disable the document-attachment reset rule, place overseas corporate registrations and recently corrected tax records in a protected manual lane, and send the remaining wholesale credit exceptions through the proposed rules-based route. Full retirement would take at least six weeks and requires a revised compliance rule set. Extending the present queue would leave a known routing problem in place and forgo the measured time reduction seen in the test.

## Evidence

The manual queue receives wholesale orders rejected by the automated credit screen. From January through April 2026, 1,840 orders entered it. A timestamp audit found that analysts did not open 1,126 orders until they had waited at least 24 hours. For 804 of those waits, the audit matched routing events to order timestamps and traced the delay to a rule that returned an order to the bottom of the queue whenever a sales representative attached a new document.

The remaining long waits had several recorded causes, including weekends, incomplete tax records, and analyst capacity. The audit did not estimate each cause's contribution. Disabling the reset rule addresses 804 traced waits, not every source of delay in the manual process. Engineering can make that change in five business days.

The two-week shadow test sent 210 new exceptions through the current queue and proposed route at the same time, using the same submitted documents. The routes reached the same approve-or-decline result for 198 orders. Their median decision times were 3.1 hours through the proposed route and 18.7 hours through the manual queue. The simultaneous assignment and identical documents support attributing the measured time difference for those 198 orders to the routing method during the test.

Twelve orders produced different results. Nine involved overseas corporate registrations, and three involved recently corrected tax records. Compliance reviewed all 12 and said each still requires analyst judgment until the rule set is revised. Engineering can direct these categories to a protected manual lane in eight business days.

Other records describe conditions during the same four months but cannot be tied to queue outcomes. Customer Support logged 296 complaints mentioning slow wholesale-order review. Support stores records by customer account, while the queue extract has order IDs with customer fields removed, and no linkage was performed. Forty-three wholesale accounts did not renew; the renewal report provides no reason and was not linked to complaints or queue records. The record supports no attribution of complaints or nonrenewals to particular queued orders or queue delays.

## Risks

The proposed route has not been tested during a month-end volume spike, so its test timing and results may not carry over to that condition. The 30-day audit is feasible up to 350 proposed-route orders per week. Above that ceiling, current Operations capacity cannot audit every automated decision.

The protected lane contains categories identified by the shadow test. The record does not establish that they are the only cases in which rules and analysts could differ. The audit must look for decision differences and emerging case types across work sent through the proposed route. Overseas registrations and corrected tax records must remain with analysts while Compliance and Engineering revise the rules.

The chief executive asked for a change to be ready by June 15 but did not state the reason for that date. The five- and eight-business-day engineering estimates support preparing the two controls without waiting for the six-week full-retirement project. The record gives no start date from which to establish completion by June 15.

## Next 30 Days

Engineering should begin the five-business-day reset-rule change and the eight-business-day protected-lane change. Operations and Compliance should verify that overseas registrations and corrected tax records enter the manual lane before proposed-route decisions begin.

Once the controls are active, Operations should run the limited transition for 30 days and audit every proposed-route decision up to 350 orders per week. The audit should record decision agreement, decision time, case type, and any order returned for analyst judgment. Month-end volume should remain in the trial to measure route performance during the spike absent from the shadow test.

Operations should report weekly volumes against the audit ceiling and stop adding proposed-route orders if full review would exceed capacity. Compliance should use differing and newly escalated cases to revise the rule set. At day 30, the executive team should choose whether to expand the proposed route, continue the limited transition, or plan full retirement based on audited decisions, month-end timing, capacity experience, and the status of the compliance revision.

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海, over eight weeks from February 2 through March 29, 2026. The trial included 48 dispatch teams. Before assignment, the teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group then assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 teams to the existing handoff process.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued using free-form notes. Throughout the trial, both groups operated under the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule. Holding those conditions stable helped isolate the effect of changing the handoff protocol.

The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late. The analysis plan was registered in advance and specified a comparison between the change in the Relay group and the change in the control group. Before the trial, the groups were closely aligned: the late-departure rate was 14.8 percent for Relay teams and 14.6 percent for control teams.

## Result

During the trial, the Relay group’s late-departure rate fell to 9.7 percent, while the control group’s rate was 14.1 percent. The pre-registered comparison of those changes estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial. The 95 percent confidence interval ranged from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

The result includes all Relay teams as assigned, including three teams that missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. Their late-departure rates remained in the analysis. This means the estimate reflects the protocol’s performance across the assigned teams, rather than only among teams that completed every field consistently.

Operational feedback also indicated why the structured handoff may have helped. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 identified a practical limitation: “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

In a post-trial questionnaire, 31 of the 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond. This feedback is useful for understanding acceptance among Relay users, but the questionnaire was not administered to control teams and therefore does not provide a controlled comparison of dispatcher preferences.

## What We Can Claim

For the teams and period studied, the evidence supports attributing the measured reduction in late departures to Relay. This is more than a correlation between Relay use and improved performance. Teams were assigned by random draw within groups defined by site and prior late-departure performance, the operating rules remained stable, and the analysis followed a pre-registered comparison. Together, those features support the conclusion that Relay caused a 4.6 percentage-point reduction in the late-departure rate during the trial, subject to the reported confidence interval.

The claim should remain bounded to what the trial examined. Relay was tested with overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海 for eight weeks. The trial did not measure daytime dispatch, driver satisfaction, fuel use, or damage claims. It therefore cannot establish an effect on any of those outcomes or show that the same reduction will occur in other settings or periods.

The questionnaire likewise supports a limited statement: most responding Relay dispatchers wanted to retain the protocol. It does not establish that Relay dispatchers preferred their process more than control dispatchers preferred free-form notes, because control teams were not surveyed.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record states that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test. This decision preserves the tested protocol where it produced a measured operational benefit while extending the evidence base.

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. It will add an optional exception note, directly addressing the need for more room during situations such as weather exceptions without removing the four-field structure. The test will continue measuring late departures and will add fuel use and dispatcher-reported workload. Those measures will show whether Relay’s demonstrated overnight effect extends to daytime dispatch and whether the revised protocol has effects beyond departure timing.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P6_R1_C0 -->

</details>

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P6_R1_C1 -->
````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

For eight weeks, from February 2 through March 29, 2026, Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The trial included 48 teams.

Before assignment, the teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate over the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group then assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing handoff process. This design made the comparison less vulnerable to differences between the two sites or to one group entering the trial with a better recent record.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams kept using free-form notes. Staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and the overnight shift schedule stayed the same for both groups. The primary outcome was also set in advance: the share of scheduled loads that departed more than 20 minutes late.

The groups started from nearly the same position. During the eight weeks before the trial, the late-departure rate was 14.8 percent for teams later assigned to Relay and 14.6 percent for teams assigned to the control group.

## Result

During the trial, the Relay teams’ late-departure rate fell to 9.7 percent. The control teams’ rate was 14.1 percent. The pre-registered analysis did not rely only on that trial-period gap. It compared the change in the Relay group with the change in the control group.

That analysis estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the trial. The 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points. In other words, the measured effect was large enough that the full confidence interval remained on the side of fewer late departures.

The result also kept imperfect implementation in view. Three Relay teams left at least one required field blank in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. Their late-departure results remained in the analysis. The estimate therefore reflects Relay as it was used across all 24 assigned teams, including those that did not follow the format consistently.

The practical reason the fields may have helped is easy to recognize in the handoffs themselves. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 identified a limitation as well: “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

## What We Can Claim

For these 48 overnight dispatch teams, over these eight weeks, Relay caused a reduction in late departures. That is stronger than saying Relay was merely associated with improvement. Teams were assigned at random within groups defined by site and prior performance, operating rules stayed stable, and the analysis was registered before the results were known. Together, those features support attributing the estimated 4.6 percentage-point reduction to Relay.

The claim still has boundaries. The trial did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch. It cannot tell us whether Relay affected any of those outcomes, and it does not establish that the same reduction will appear on other shifts, at other sites, or over a longer period.

The post-trial questionnaire offers a useful but narrower signal about acceptance. Of 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay, 31 wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond, and control teams did not receive the questionnaire. Those answers describe the views of responding Relay dispatchers; they do not provide a controlled estimate of Relay’s effect on satisfaction.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record states that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test. This was a decision based on the trial’s causal result, within the limits of the population, period, and outcome studied.

The next test will move to daytime dispatch for six weeks. It will retain the four-field structure and add an optional exception note, directly addressing the need 王磊 described for unusual conditions such as weather. It will measure late departures again, while adding fuel use and dispatcher-reported workload.

That test will answer questions the overnight trial could not. It will show whether Relay works in daytime operations, whether an exception note makes the format more usable without weakening its discipline, and whether the protocol changes fuel use or reported workload. Until those results arrive, the sound position is specific: Relay reduced late departures in the overnight trial, and the next test will examine how far that finding travels.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P6_R1_C1 -->

</details>
