````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The trial ran for eight weeks, from February 2 through March 29, 2026, and included 48 teams.

Before assignment, teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing handoff process.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams kept using free-form notes. Both groups followed the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule.

The trial defined its primary outcome as the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late. Before the trial, Relay teams had a 14.8 percent late-departure rate. Control teams had a 14.6 percent rate. The pre-registered analysis accounted for that starting difference by comparing the change in one group with the change in the other.

## Result

During the trial, Relay teams recorded a 9.7 percent late-departure rate, down 5.1 percentage points from their pre-trial rate. Control teams recorded 14.1 percent, down 0.5 percentage points. The pre-registered comparison estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week period. The 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

The handoff fields addressed a source of confusion. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 identified a limit in the format: “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

Compliance varied. Three Relay teams omitted at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. The analysis retained their late-departure results, so the estimate did not depend on removing teams that used Relay with less consistency.

A post-trial questionnaire provided a view of dispatcher preference, though its design limits comparison. Of 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay, 31 wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond, and Lumen Freight did not administer the questionnaire to control teams. The responses show support among many Relay respondents. They do not show whether Relay dispatchers preferred their process more than control dispatchers preferred free-form notes.

## What We Can Claim

Lumen Freight can attribute the measured 4.6 percentage-point reduction in late departures to Relay for the overnight teams and eight-week period studied. Random assignment created the basis for a causal comparison. Grouping teams by site and prior late-departure rate before the draw accounted for two factors tied to performance. Stable staffing rules, software, escalation policy, and schedules reduced the chance that another operating change produced the gap. The pre-registered comparison set the analysis before the trial produced its results.

The confidence interval defines the uncertainty around the estimated effect. The analysis estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points. The evidence supports a reduction during this trial without establishing one exact effect for other teams, shifts, or periods.

The trial provides no result for driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch because it did not measure those outcomes. The questionnaire cannot establish a comparative preference effect because only Relay dispatchers received it. Those boundaries do not turn the late-departure result into a correlation. They define the outcomes and setting covered by the supported causal conclusion.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. Its decision record states that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test. Continued use keeps the protocol in the overnight teams that produced the evidence while Lumen Freight examines a new setting.

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. It will add an optional exception note, responding to the need 王磊 described for weather exceptions. The test will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload.

The daytime test will examine whether Relay reduces late departures in a setting outside overnight dispatch. It will also supply evidence on fuel use and reported workload, two outcomes the first trial did not cover. The optional note gives exception cases more room while preserving the four-field handoff that Lumen Freight tested in the overnight teams.
````

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The eight-week trial ran from February 2 through March 29, 2026, and included 48 teams. Its purpose was specific: test whether a structured handoff could reduce the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late. That share was the trial’s primary outcome.

Before assignment, the teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate over the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing process. This design made the comparison less vulnerable to differences between the two sites or to a chance concentration of teams with worse recent performance in one arm.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued using free-form notes. Staffing rules stayed the same for both groups. So did the dispatch software, the process for raising urgent issues, and the overnight shift schedule. The protocol was therefore the assigned operating difference under study, while the other named rules and systems remained fixed.

The groups began at almost the same late-departure rate. During the eight weeks before the trial, the Relay group averaged 14.8 percent, compared with 14.6 percent for the control group. The analysis plan was registered in advance and called for comparing each group’s change, rather than relying only on their rates during the trial.

## Result

During the trial, the Relay group’s late-departure rate fell to 9.7 percent. The control group’s rate was 14.1 percent. In change terms, Relay teams improved by 5.1 percentage points, while control teams improved by 0.5 percentage points. The pre-registered comparison estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the trial. Its 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

The result remained based on the assigned Relay group even when use of the form was imperfect. Three Relay teams omitted at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs, and their late-departure rates stayed in the analysis. That choice avoided turning the main result into a comparison limited to teams that followed the protocol more consistently.

Comments from the teams give concrete examples of how the format worked and where it felt constrained. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.” These accounts describe operational experiences; they were not the primary outcome.

In a post-trial questionnaire, 31 of the 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond. Because control teams did not receive the questionnaire, those responses describe acceptance among Relay respondents and do not provide a comparative estimate of preference.

## What We Can Claim

For the 48 overnight teams in Montréal and 上海 from February 2 through March 29, the trial supports a causal claim: Relay reduced the late-departure rate by an estimated 4.6 percentage points. Random assignment within site-and-baseline groups, unchanged operating rules, and the pre-registered change comparison support attributing the measured reduction to Relay for those teams and that period. Treating it as mere correlation would discard the central protection created by random assignment.

The estimate has defined boundaries. The confidence interval puts the trial’s supported reduction between 2.1 and 7.0 percentage points at the 95 percent level. The trial did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch, so it supplies no result for those outcomes or that shift. The questionnaire cannot fill those gaps, and it does not establish how control-team dispatchers would have answered.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record gives a direct reason: the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test.

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. It will add an optional exception note, addressing the need 王磊 identified for weather cases without expanding every routine handoff. It will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload.
````

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The eight-week trial ran from February 2 through March 29, 2026, and included 48 dispatch teams.

Before assignment, the teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. Within each group, a random draw assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 teams to the existing handoff process. This design helped ensure that comparisons accounted for both operating location and recent performance.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued using free-form notes. During the trial, both groups operated under the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule. The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late.

The groups began from nearly identical baselines. Before the trial, the late-departure rate was 14.8 percent among teams later assigned to Relay and 14.6 percent among teams assigned to the control process. The analysis plan was pre-registered and compared the Relay group’s change with the control group’s change, rather than relying only on a comparison of rates observed during the trial.

## Result

During the trial, the Relay teams’ late-departure rate fell to 9.7 percent. The control teams’ rate was 14.1 percent. The pre-registered analysis estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial. The 95 percent confidence interval ranged from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

That result remained inclusive of imperfect implementation. Three Relay teams missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs, and their late-departure rates remained in the analysis. The estimate therefore reflects the protocol as it was actually used across all 24 assigned teams, rather than a subset limited to teams with stronger adherence.

The operating experience also identified why parts of the structure may have mattered and where it could be extended. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

In a post-trial questionnaire, 31 of the 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond. Because the questionnaire was not administered to control teams, it describes views within the Relay group but does not provide a controlled comparison of dispatcher preference.

## What We Can Claim

For the teams and period studied, the measured reduction in late departures can be attributed to Relay. This is not merely an observed correlation. Teams were randomly assigned within groups defined by site and prior late-departure performance; the operating rules that might otherwise affect outcomes remained stable; and the change relative to the control group was evaluated using a pre-registered comparison. Together, those features support the causal conclusion that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by an estimated 4.6 percentage points during the trial.

The claim should remain bounded to what was tested. The evidence concerns 48 overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海 over eight weeks. It does not establish the effect of Relay in daytime dispatch, in other locations, or over a longer period. The trial also did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch. It therefore provides no basis for claiming that Relay improved those outcomes.

The questionnaire should be interpreted with similar care. Most responding Relay dispatchers wanted to retain the protocol, but four did not respond, and there was no control-team questionnaire. That feedback can inform continued use and design changes, but it does not establish a comparative effect on satisfaction or workload.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record states that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test.

The next test will move into daytime dispatch and run for six weeks. It will retain Relay’s structured handoff while adding an optional exception note, directly addressing the reported need for more room when normal operating conditions do not apply. It will again measure late departures and will expand the outcomes to include fuel use and dispatcher-reported workload.

This next stage will test whether Relay’s effect extends beyond the overnight teams and period already studied. It will also produce evidence on two outcomes the first trial could not address, while evaluating a specific protocol refinement suggested by frontline use.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P6_R2_C0 -->

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The trial ran for eight weeks, from February 2 through March 29, 2026, and included 48 teams.

Before assignment, the teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing process. This design helped ensure that differences in site or recent performance did not determine which teams received Relay.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued using free-form notes. Both groups operated under the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule throughout the trial. The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads that departed more than 20 minutes late.

## Result

Before the trial, late-departure rates were nearly identical. The rate was 14.8 percent among teams later assigned to Relay and 14.6 percent among control teams. During the trial, the Relay group’s rate fell to 9.7 percent, while the control group’s rate was 14.1 percent.

The pre-registered analysis compared the change in the Relay group with the change in the control group. It estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the trial. The 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

The result remained intact even though use of the protocol was not perfect. Three Relay teams omitted at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. Their late-departure results stayed in the analysis rather than being removed for noncompliance.

The four fields also changed how some dispatchers handled responsibility at handoff. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 identified a limit in the format: “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

## What We Can Claim

For the teams and period studied, the evidence supports attributing the measured reduction in late departures to Relay. This is more than an observed association. Teams were assigned by random draw within groups defined before the trial, other operating rules remained stable, and the analysis was registered in advance. Together, those features provide a sound basis for a causal conclusion about Relay’s effect during the eight-week overnight trial.

The claim still has boundaries. The trial covered overnight dispatch in Montréal and 上海, not daytime operations or other sites. It measured late departures, but it did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch. The result therefore does not establish an effect on any of those outcomes. It also does not show whether the reduction would persist over a longer period.

The post-trial questionnaire offers useful context but answers a different question. Of the 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay, 31 said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond, and control teams did not receive the questionnaire. Those responses describe views among most of the Relay dispatchers who replied. They do not provide a controlled comparison of dispatcher preference, nor do they explain the measured operational effect.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record states that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test.

That test will extend the work into daytime dispatch for six weeks. It will retain the four-field structure while adding an optional exception note, addressing the need 王磊 described for unusual conditions such as weather. The test will again measure late departures and will add fuel use and dispatcher-reported workload.

This next step tests questions the first trial could not answer: whether Relay works under daytime conditions, whether the optional note accommodates exceptions without weakening the handoff, and whether the protocol affects fuel use or reported workload. It builds on a measured causal result while keeping the new claims tied to new evidence.
````

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海, for eight weeks from February 2 through March 29, 2026. The company included 48 dispatch teams. Before assignment, Lumen Freight grouped them by site and by each team’s average late-departure rate during the prior eight weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing process.

Relay replaced free-form notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams kept using free-form notes. Both groups followed the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule during the trial. The protocol was the operating difference the test introduced.

The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads that departed more than 20 minutes late. Lumen Freight registered its analysis before examining the trial results. The analysis compared the change in the Relay teams with the change in the control teams, accounting for the small difference between their starting rates.

## Result

Before the trial, Relay teams had a 14.8 percent late-departure rate, while control teams had a 14.6 percent rate. During the trial, the Relay rate fell to 9.7 percent. The control rate edged down to 14.1 percent.

The pre-registered comparison estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week test. Analysts calculated a 95 percent confidence interval from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points. Even the low end represents a reduction in the primary outcome for the teams and period studied.

The trial kept three Relay teams in the analysis despite incomplete use. Each of those teams missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of handoffs. Their inclusion means the estimate reflects Relay as teams used it during the test, including those lapses.

Staff comments show how the format worked during a shift. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

## What We Can Claim

Lumen Freight can claim that Relay caused the measured reduction in late departures for these 48 overnight teams in Montréal and 上海 during the eight weeks studied. The company assigned teams through a random draw after grouping them by site and prior late-departure rate. Both groups operated under the same staffing rules, software, escalation policy, and shift schedule. Lumen Freight also committed to the change comparison before examining the results. Together, those features support attributing the 4.6-point reduction to Relay.

The claim has firm boundaries. Relay reduced the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late by an estimated 4.6 percentage points in this trial, with uncertainty represented by the 2.1-to-7.0-point confidence interval. The random assignment makes this a causal finding for the teams and period studied, rather than a correlation between Relay use and performance.

Lumen Freight has no basis for claims about driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch because the company did not measure those outcomes or that shift. The post-trial questionnaire answers a separate question. Among 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay, 31 wanted to keep it, and four Relay dispatchers did not respond. Control teams received no questionnaire. Those responses describe acceptance among Relay respondents; they cannot establish a comparative effect on preference or workload.

The incomplete handoffs set another boundary. The estimate includes the three teams that missed required fields in more than 15 percent of handoffs, but Lumen Freight reported no separate effect for complete and incomplete handoffs. Relay worked under the level of adherence observed in the trial. These results do not quantify how much each field contributed.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The group wrote in its decision record that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test. That choice extends use for the original Relay teams; it does not extend the completed trial’s causal claim beyond its overnight setting and eight-week period.

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. It will add an optional exception note, addressing the need 王磊 identified for weather exceptions, and will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload. The operations group can then examine whether Relay reduces late departures in daytime operations and measure two outcomes the first trial left unmeasured.
````

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The eight-week trial ran from February 2 through March 29, 2026, and included 48 teams.

Before assignment, the teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. Within each group, a random draw assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing handoff process. Relay teams used four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued writing free-form notes.

The trial changed the handoff format while holding the surrounding operation steady. Both groups kept the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule. The primary outcome was also set in advance: the share of scheduled loads that departed more than 20 minutes late.

The comparison tested whether replacing free-form notes with a short, structured record changed late departures under otherwise stable operating rules for these teams.

## Result

The groups entered the trial with nearly identical late-departure rates. During the prior eight weeks, the rate was 14.8 percent for teams later assigned to Relay and 14.6 percent for teams later assigned to the control group.

During the trial, the Relay group’s rate fell to 9.7 percent. The control group’s rate was 14.1 percent. The pre-registered analysis compared the change in the Relay group with the change in the control group. It estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial. The 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

Implementation was imperfect, and the analysis retained that imperfection. Three Relay teams missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. Their late-departure rates remained in the analysis rather than being removed for incomplete use of the protocol.

The four fields also produced concrete feedback about how the format worked. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

A post-trial questionnaire went only to dispatchers assigned to Relay. Of the 44 who responded, 31 said they wanted to keep the protocol; four Relay dispatchers did not respond. Because control teams were not surveyed, the questionnaire records acceptance among responding Relay dispatchers, not a comparative preference between the two handoff processes.

## What We Can Claim

For the teams and period studied, Lumen Freight can attribute the measured 4.6-percentage-point reduction in late departures to Relay. The estimate is causal within that scope. Teams were assigned by random draw within groups defined by site and prior late-departure rate, operating rules stayed stable, and the analysis used the comparison registered before results were known.

The confidence interval places the estimated reduction between 2.1 and 7.0 percentage points at the 95 percent level. It does not extend the finding beyond the trial’s scope. Relay was tested in overnight dispatch in Montréal and 上海 for eight weeks. The trial did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch, so it supports no claim about Relay’s effects on those outcomes or settings.

The questionnaire has a different evidentiary role. It indicates that 31 responding Relay dispatchers wanted to continue, while four assigned dispatchers supplied no response. Without responses from control teams, it cannot establish that dispatchers preferred Relay over free-form notes. The field-missing data describe actual use during the trial; they do not erase the causal estimate because those three teams remained in the analysis.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record states that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during that next test. Continued use therefore follows the result already established for the overnight trial, within the same 24 teams, rather than a claim that every operational question has been answered.

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch and will add an optional exception note. It will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload. This will provide daytime measurements, add evidence on fuel use that the first trial did not measure, and record dispatcher-reported workload during the six-week test.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P6_R2_C3 -->

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The eight-week trial ran from February 2 through March 29, 2026, and included 48 dispatch teams.

Before assignment, the teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. Within each group, a random draw assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 teams to the existing handoff process. This design helped ensure that the comparison accounted for both location and recent performance.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued to use free-form notes. Both groups operated under the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule throughout the trial.

The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late. The analysis plan was pre-registered and specified a comparison between the change in the Relay group and the change in the control group.

## Result

Before the trial, the late-departure rates were closely aligned: 14.8 percent for the Relay group and 14.6 percent for the control group. During the trial, the Relay group’s rate fell to 9.7 percent, while the control group’s rate was 14.1 percent.

The pre-registered analysis estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial. The 95 percent confidence interval ranged from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points. The interval therefore supports a reduction while also showing the uncertainty around its precise size.

The operational mechanism described by team members was consistent with the structure of the protocol. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 identified both the value and a limitation of the format: “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

Adherence was not perfect. Three Relay teams missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. Their late-departure results remained in the analysis rather than being removed.

## What We Can Claim

This trial supports a causal claim for the teams, setting, and period studied: Relay reduced late departures. That conclusion does not rest on a simple correlation between Relay use and better performance. Teams were randomly assigned within groups defined by site and prior late-departure rates, operating rules remained stable across both groups, and the analysis compared changes according to a pre-registered plan. Together, those features support attributing the measured 4.6-percentage-point reduction to Relay.

The scope of that claim matters. The evidence covers the 48 overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海 during the eight weeks from February 2 through March 29, 2026. The trial did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch. It therefore cannot establish Relay’s effect on those outcomes or in daytime operations.

The post-trial questionnaire offers separate information about acceptance, but it is not comparative evidence of Relay’s effect. Of 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay, 31 said they wanted to keep it, while four Relay dispatchers did not respond. Control teams did not receive the questionnaire. The responses show that a majority of responding Relay dispatchers favored continued use, but they do not show whether Relay dispatchers were more satisfied than control dispatchers.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record states that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test. That decision follows the demonstrated result without extending the evidence beyond what the trial measured.

The next test will address two important boundaries of the first trial. It will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch, where Relay has not yet been tested, and it will add an optional exception note in response to the need identified for weather exceptions. The four-field structure will therefore be supplemented with room for cases that do not fit a normal night.

The next test will again measure late departures and will add fuel use and dispatcher-reported workload. Those outcomes will show whether Relay’s effect extends to daytime dispatch and whether the revised protocol has consequences beyond departure timing.
````

<details><summary>Stage 1 P6 R3, Humanizer</summary>

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight ran an eight-week trial of Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The trial began February 2 and ended March 29, 2026. It covered 48 teams, split evenly between Relay and the existing process.

Before the random draw, Lumen grouped teams by site and by their average late-departure rate over the previous eight weeks. Within each group, 24 teams were assigned to Relay and 24 to the control condition. This balanced the two sites and teams’ recent operating records before the test began.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams kept using free-form notes. Both groups retained the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule.

The primary outcome was set in advance: the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late. The Relay teams entered the trial with a late-departure rate of 14.8 percent, compared with 14.6 percent for control teams. The pre-registered method compared how much each group changed during the trial.

## Result

During the eight weeks, the late-departure rate among Relay teams fell to 9.7 percent. The control rate moved to 14.1 percent. Under the pre-registered comparison, Relay reduced the late-departure rate by an estimated 4.6 percentage points. The 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

That comparison accounts for the change in the control group instead of treating the Relay group’s before-and-after decline as sufficient evidence. Because teams were randomly assigned within groups defined by site and prior performance, the measured difference is not merely an association between teams that used Relay and teams that improved.

The analysis also kept imperfect users in the Relay group. Three Relay teams omitted at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs, and their late-departure results remained included. The estimate therefore covers all 24 assigned teams, including those with incomplete handoffs.

Comments from dispatchers add context, though they are not substitutes for the measured outcome. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

Of 44 dispatchers assigned to Relay who answered the post-trial questionnaire, 31 wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond, and control teams did not receive the questionnaire. The responses describe sentiment among Relay respondents; they do not establish a comparative effect on preference.

## What We Can Claim

For the 48 overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海, from February 2 through March 29, Relay caused a measured reduction of 4.6 percentage points in the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late. Random assignment supports that causal claim. Stable staffing rules, software, escalation policy, and shift schedule reduce concern that another operating change produced the difference. The pre-registered comparison adds support because the analysis was specified before the outcome was known.

The estimate was 4.6 percentage points, while the 95 percent confidence interval was 2.1 to 7.0 points. The evidence supports a reduction during this trial, but it does not fix the size of the effect at one value for every future use.

The trial did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch. It gives no basis for saying Relay improved any of them. The Relay-only questionnaire likewise cannot show that dispatchers preferred Relay over the control process.

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record said the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test. That decision follows the trial evidence while leaving the unanswered questions for further study.

## Next Test

The next test will move Relay into daytime dispatch for six weeks. It will add an optional exception note, accommodating the need 王磊 described for weather exceptions.

The test will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload. It extends the evidence into outcomes and a setting the overnight trial did not cover. Until those results arrive, the supported causal conclusion remains specific: Relay reduced late departures for the overnight teams and trial period in Montréal and 上海.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P6_R3_C1 -->

</details>

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海, for eight weeks from February 2 through March 29, 2026. Forty-eight teams took part.

Lumen Freight grouped the teams by site and by each team’s average late-departure rate over the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing handoff process. That design balanced the assignment on location and recent performance before the trial began.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. The control teams kept using free-form notes. Both groups followed the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule. The protocol was the operating difference under test.

The trial’s primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads that departed more than 20 minutes late. Before the trial, Relay teams had a 14.8 percent late-departure rate. Control teams had a 14.6 percent rate.

## Result

During the trial, the Relay group’s late-departure rate fell to 9.7 percent. The control group’s rate moved to 14.1 percent. Relay teams improved by 5.1 percentage points from their pre-trial rate, while control teams improved by 0.5 percentage points.

The pre-registered analysis compared those two changes. It estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial. The 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

Teams did not have to execute Relay without error to remain part of that estimate. Three Relay teams omitted at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs, and the analysis kept their late-departure results. The estimate therefore reflects the protocol as the 24 assigned teams used it, including those lapses.

Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Her account identifies one way the format could prevent a missed task: the handoff gave one person ownership of the next call.

## What We Can Claim

The trial supports a causal claim for the teams and period studied: Relay reduced late departures by an estimated 4.6 percentage points. The basis goes beyond rates that happened to move together. Lumen Freight assigned teams by random draw within groups defined before the trial, held the other operating rules constant, and used a comparison registered before results came in. The confidence interval places the estimated reduction between 2.1 and 7.0 percentage points.

The claim has firm boundaries. The trial covered overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海 from February 2 through March 29. It did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch. The results cannot establish Relay’s effect on those outcomes or settings.

The post-trial questionnaire answers a separate question about acceptance. Among 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay, 31 wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond, and Lumen Freight did not give the questionnaire to control teams. Those responses describe views within the Relay group; they do not provide a randomized comparison of dispatcher preference.

Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.” That experience points to a specific revision without changing the four-field core.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while it prepared another test. Its decision record cites the measured reduction in late departures as the reason for continued use during the next test. Continued use follows the trial result while keeping the causal claim tied to the completed eight-week study.

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. It will add an optional exception note, addressing the gap 王磊 described for weather exceptions. It will measure late departures again and add fuel use and dispatcher-reported workload.

That test extends the evidence in two directions the first trial left open. Daytime dispatch will test Relay in a new operating setting, and the added measures will show whether the protocol affects fuel use or reported workload. The next results can also show how teams use the optional note while retaining the named owner, next action, deadline, and unresolved-load fields that defined Relay in the overnight trial.
````

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight ran an eight-week trial of Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The trial began on February 2 and ended on March 29, 2026. It included 48 teams, split evenly between Relay and the existing handoff process.

Assignment happened only after the teams had been grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group then assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the control group. This approach made the comparison within each site and accounted for the teams’ recent late-departure experience before assignment.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued to use free-form notes. Staffing rules, dispatch software, the escalation policy, and the overnight shift schedule stayed the same for both groups throughout the trial.

The primary outcome measured the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late. Before the trial, that rate was 14.8 percent among teams later assigned to Relay and 14.6 percent among teams assigned to the control process. The pre-registered analysis was designed to compare how the rate changed in the Relay group with how it changed in the control group. The control teams provided a concurrent benchmark under the same overnight operating rules, rather than relying on the Relay group’s before-and-after change alone.

## Result

During the trial, the late-departure rate for Relay teams fell to 9.7 percent. The control group’s rate was 14.1 percent. Against their respective baselines, the Relay rate dropped 5.1 percentage points and the control rate dropped 0.5 point. The pre-registered comparison estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight weeks. Its 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

Three Relay teams missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. Their late-departure rate remained in the analysis, so the estimate did not remove those teams’ results because their use of the four-field format was incomplete.

Comments from Relay staff describe how the format worked in practice. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

A post-trial questionnaire drew responses from 44 dispatchers assigned to Relay; 31 said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond. Because control teams did not receive the questionnaire, those responses describe acceptance among responding Relay dispatchers and do not provide a comparison with staff using free-form notes.

## What We Can Claim

Relay caused the measured reduction for the 48 overnight teams in Montréal and 上海 during the trial period. Random assignment within the site and prior-performance groups, stable operating rules across both trial arms, and the pre-registered comparison of changes support that attribution.

The estimate also has a defined size and uncertainty. The trial’s best estimate is a 4.6 percentage-point reduction, while the 95 percent confidence interval places the estimated reduction between 2.1 and 7.0 percentage points. This range captures uncertainty around the magnitude of Relay’s effect during the trial.

The trial did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch. It therefore provides no result for those outcomes or for Relay’s effect during daytime operations. The questionnaire adds information about the preferences of the 44 Relay dispatchers who responded, but its lack of a control questionnaire prevents a between-group preference comparison.

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. Its decision record states that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during that next test. Continued use followed the demonstrated result for the trial teams, while the new test addresses a different dispatch period and adds other measurements.

## Next Test

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. Relay will gain an optional exception note, an addition consistent with 王磊’s comment about weather exceptions. The test will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload in the daytime setting.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P6_R4_C0 -->
````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The trial ran for eight weeks, from February 2 through March 29, 2026, and included 48 dispatch teams.

Before assignment, the teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. Within each group, a random draw assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 teams to the existing handoff process. This design created a comparison between teams operating in the same sites and starting from similar recent performance.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued using free-form notes. Both groups otherwise worked under the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule throughout the trial. The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late.

The groups entered the trial with nearly identical late-departure rates. During the previous eight weeks, the rate was 14.8 percent among teams later assigned to Relay and 14.6 percent among teams assigned to the control process. The analysis plan was registered before the results were known. It compared the change in the Relay group with the change in the control group, rather than relying only on the difference observed during the trial.

## Result

During the trial, the Relay teams’ late-departure rate fell to 9.7 percent. The control teams’ rate was 14.1 percent. Under the pre-registered comparison, Relay reduced the late-departure rate by an estimated 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial. The 95 percent confidence interval ranged from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

The result includes all 24 teams assigned to Relay, including the three teams that missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. Their late-departure rates remained in the analysis. The estimate therefore did not depend on removing teams with weaker adherence to the four-field process.

Comments from dispatchers illustrate how the protocol operated, although they are not substitutes for the measured outcome. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

A post-trial questionnaire provided additional information about Relay dispatchers’ preferences. Of the 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay, 31 said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond. Because the questionnaire was not administered to control teams, it does not provide a controlled comparison of preferences between the two processes.

## What We Can Claim

The trial supports a causal conclusion for the teams, operations, and period studied: Relay reduced late departures during the eight-week overnight-dispatch trial. That conclusion does not rest on a simple association. Teams were randomly assigned within groups defined by site and prior late-departure performance, operating rules remained stable across the two groups, and the comparison was pre-registered. Together, those features support attributing the measured 4.6-percentage-point reduction to Relay.

The scope of that claim matters. The trial covered overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海 from February 2 through March 29, 2026. It did not test daytime dispatch, and it did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or any outcome other than late departures among the operational measures listed. The results therefore establish neither benefits nor harms in those unmeasured areas.

The questionnaire also should be kept separate from the causal operational finding. It shows that 31 of 44 responding Relay dispatchers wanted to keep the protocol, but it cannot establish how Relay affected dispatcher preferences relative to free-form notes. The late-departure result, by contrast, came from the randomized comparison and included teams regardless of imperfect compliance.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. The decision record states that the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test. Continued use is therefore grounded in the trial’s measured causal result for those teams, while the additional test will examine whether the protocol’s value extends to a different dispatch setting and a broader set of outcomes.

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. It will retain Relay while adding an optional exception note, addressing the reported need for more room during weather exceptions without changing the four core fields described in the first trial. The test will measure late departures again and will add fuel use and dispatcher-reported workload. Those measurements will extend the evidence beyond the overnight setting and begin answering questions that the first trial did not address.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P6_R4_C0 -->

</details>

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海. The trial ran from February 2 through March 29, 2026, and included 48 teams.

Before assignment, the teams were grouped by site and by their average late-departure rate over the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group then assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing handoff process. Relay teams recorded four fields at handoff: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams kept using free-form notes.

Other operating conditions stayed the same. Both groups followed the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule. The primary outcome was also set in advance: the share of scheduled loads that left more than 20 minutes late.

The two groups entered the trial with nearly identical records. Relay teams had a late-departure rate of 14.8 percent during the previous eight weeks, compared with 14.6 percent for control teams. The pre-registered method compared how much each group changed during the trial.

## Result

During the trial, the Relay group’s late-departure rate fell to 9.7 percent. The control group’s rate moved to 14.1 percent. The pre-registered comparison of those changes estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week period. The 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

The implementation was not flawless. Three Relay teams omitted at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. Their late-departure results remained in the analysis, so the estimate did not exclude teams with weaker adherence.

Comments from the teams help explain what the four-field format changed in practice. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 identified a limit as well: “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

A post-trial questionnaire provides a separate view of how Relay was received. Of the 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay, 31 said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond. Control teams were not given the questionnaire, so these responses describe sentiment among Relay dispatchers; they do not establish that Relay was preferred over the existing process in a comparative survey.

## What We Can Claim

For the teams and period studied, the measured reduction in late departures can be attributed to Relay. That is stronger than saying Relay was merely associated with better results. Teams were randomly assigned within groups defined by site and prior late-departure performance. The operating rules remained stable, and the analysis had been registered before the results were known. Together, those features support a causal conclusion about the 4.6 percentage point reduction during this eight-week overnight trial.

The boundaries matter. The trial covered overnight dispatch in Montréal and 上海, not daytime work or other sites. It measured late departures, but it did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch. The results therefore do not show whether Relay affected any of those outcomes. Nor does the dispatcher questionnaire support a comparison with the control process, since control teams were not surveyed.

The confidence interval also gives the estimate an appropriate range. The analysis supports a reduction, with plausible values from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points at the stated 95 percent confidence level. The point estimate is 4.6 percentage points, not a promise that every team or later period will see exactly that result.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. Its decision record says the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test. That decision follows the evidence from the overnight trial without treating unanswered questions as settled.

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. It will add an optional exception note, directly addressing the need 王磊 described for weather exceptions while retaining the four required fields used in the first trial. It will again measure late departures and will add fuel use and dispatcher-reported workload.

This second test extends the work into a setting the first trial did not cover and collects two outcomes the first trial did not measure. Its results will show whether Relay’s effect on late departures carries into daytime dispatch, and whether the protocol affects fuel use or reported workload under those conditions. Until those results are available, the supported claim remains specific: Relay reduced late departures for the 48 overnight teams studied from February 2 through March 29, 2026.
````

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海, for eight weeks from February 2 through March 29, 2026. Forty-eight teams took part. Lumen Freight grouped the teams by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing handoff process.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams kept free-form notes. Both groups used the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule through the trial. Those controls helped isolate the effect of the handoff format.

The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads that left more than 20 minutes late. Before the trial, Relay teams had a late-departure rate of 14.8 percent. Control teams had a rate of 14.6 percent. The pre-trial difference between the groups was 0.2 percentage points.

Lumen Freight pre-registered an analysis comparing the change in the Relay group with the change in the control group. That choice mattered because a before-and-after result for Relay teams alone could have captured changes that affected both groups during the same period.

## Result

During the trial, Relay teams recorded a late-departure rate of 9.7 percent, down 5.1 percentage points from their pre-trial rate. Control teams recorded 14.1 percent, down 0.5 percentage points. The pre-registered comparison estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial. The 95 percent confidence interval ran from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

The protocol also changed what dispatchers put into a handoff. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 said, “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

Three Relay teams missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of handoffs. Lumen Freight kept their late-departure results in the analysis, so the estimate did not depend on removing teams with weaker adherence.

In a post-trial questionnaire, 31 of 44 responding Relay dispatchers said they wanted to keep the protocol. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond. Lumen Freight did not administer the questionnaire to control teams, so those responses describe acceptance among respondents rather than a comparison between Relay and control staff.

## What We Can Claim

The trial supports a causal claim for the teams, setting, and period studied: Relay reduced the share of scheduled loads departing more than 20 minutes late by an estimated 4.6 percentage points. Random assignment made the Relay and control groups comparable within site and prior late-departure bands. Stable operating rules held staffing, software, escalation, and shift schedules constant. The pre-registered comparison measured the difference between each group’s change. Together, those features support attributing the measured reduction to Relay rather than treating the result as a mere association.

The confidence interval places the estimated reduction between 2.1 and 7.0 percentage points at the 95 percent confidence level. It describes uncertainty around the measured effect; it does not extend the finding to operations that the trial did not study.

The claim covers overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海 during the eight weeks from February 2 through March 29. The trial did not measure daytime dispatch, driver satisfaction, fuel use, or damage claims. It therefore supports no claim about Relay’s effect on those outcomes or settings. The questionnaire provides a separate signal about preference among responding Relay dispatchers, but it cannot establish whether Relay caused greater staff acceptance because the control teams received no questionnaire and four Relay dispatchers did not respond.

## Next Test

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. Its decision record says the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during the next test. Continued use keeps the proven trial configuration in place for those teams while Lumen Freight tests a broader application.

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. It will add an optional exception note, addressing the gap 王磊 identified for weather exceptions without removing the four-field structure tested overnight. Lumen Freight will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload.

That design extends the evidence in three defined ways. It moves Relay into a dispatch period the first trial did not cover, tests a revised format, and adds two outcomes. The results can show whether Relay’s effect on late departures carries into daytime dispatch and whether the optional note works alongside the core fields. Fuel-use and workload measurements will give the operations group evidence that the first trial could not provide.
````

````text
# What the Relay Trial Changed

## Setup

Lumen Freight tested Relay, a new handoff protocol for overnight dispatch teams in Montréal and 上海, for eight weeks from February 2 through March 29, 2026. The trial included 48 teams. Before assignment, Lumen grouped the teams by site and by their average late-departure rate during the previous eight weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 24 teams to Relay and 24 to the existing handoff process.

Relay replaced free-form handoff notes with four required fields: unresolved load, named owner, next action, and deadline. Control teams continued using free-form notes. During the trial, both groups operated under the same staffing rules, dispatch software, escalation policy, and overnight shift schedule. Holding those conditions steady reduced the chance that an operating-policy change, rather than the handoff protocol, explained a difference between the groups.

The primary outcome was the share of scheduled loads that departed more than 20 minutes late. The analysis plan was registered before results were available. It specified a comparison between the change in the Relay group and the change in the control group, rather than a simple comparison of their trial-period rates.

## Result

Before the trial, late departures accounted for 14.8 percent of scheduled loads in the Relay group and 14.6 percent in the control group. During the trial, the Relay rate fell to 9.7 percent, while the control rate was 14.1 percent. The pre-registered comparison estimated that Relay reduced the late-departure rate by 4.6 percentage points during the eight-week trial, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 2.1 to 7.0 percentage points.

Execution was not perfect. Three Relay teams missed at least one required field in more than 15 percent of their handoffs. Their late-departure rates remained in the analysis, so those teams were not excluded because of the missing fields.

The handoff fields also changed how some dispatchers handled responsibility. Team lead Anaïs Mercier said, “The named owner stopped two people from assuming the other had called the dock.” Dispatcher 王磊 identified a limit in the fixed format: “Four fields were enough on a normal night, but weather exceptions needed room for one more note.”

## What We Can Claim

Teams were randomly assigned within groups defined by site and prior late-departure rate, the operating rules stayed the same across trial arms, and the analysis followed the pre-registered comparison of changes. Given that design, Relay caused the measured 4.6-percentage-point reduction in late departures for the 48 overnight teams in Montréal and 上海 from February 2 through March 29.

The claim stops at the trial’s boundaries. The result does not establish Relay’s effect outside the participating overnight teams or beyond the eight-week period. The trial did not measure driver satisfaction, fuel use, damage claims, or daytime dispatch, so it supports no conclusion about those outcomes or that operating context.

The post-trial questionnaire provides a separate view of acceptance, with narrower limits. Of 44 responding dispatchers assigned to Relay, 31 said they wanted to keep it. Four Relay dispatchers did not respond, and control teams did not receive the questionnaire. Those responses describe preferences among the Relay respondents; they do not provide a randomized comparison of dispatcher preference.

On April 6, the operations group kept Relay in the 24 trial teams while preparing another test. According to the decision record, the measured reduction in late departures justified continued use during that next test. Continued use applies the result in the same teams while Lumen prepares to examine Relay in a different shift.

## Next Test

The next test will run for six weeks in daytime dispatch. It will add an optional exception note to Relay. That change gives dispatchers room for cases like the weather exceptions 王磊 described while leaving the four named handoff fields in place.

The test will measure late departures, fuel use, and dispatcher-reported workload. It will collect evidence about Relay in daytime dispatch, which the overnight trial did not measure. Fuel use was also outside the earlier trial’s measures. The stated plan identifies the new context, protocol change, duration, and outcomes, but does not include an assignment method or comparison design. Those details will govern whether the six-week results can support a causal claim, as the first trial did, or only describe what happened while Relay was in use.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P6_R4_C3 -->

</details>

````text
# QuickTurn Shows Promise, but Citywide Deployment Should Wait

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn in a limited six-week test at the 18 desks already assigned to use it, rather than deploy it citywide now or stop using it. The seven-week trial produced credible evidence that the five-field handoff reduced serious route delays under the conditions studied. At the same time, the record leaves important questions about implementation consistency, operating costs, employee workload, and the feasibility of a September 1 rollout. A tightly controlled extension would preserve the demonstrated benefit while filling several of those gaps before Riverbend commits every dispatch desk to the process.

The strongest evidence comes from the trial’s design and primary outcome. Riverbend grouped 36 desks by depot and prior late-start performance, then randomly assigned 18 desks within those groups to QuickTurn and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Before the trial, the two groups were closely matched: desks later assigned to QuickTurn had a 13.2 percent late-start rate, compared with 13.0 percent among control desks. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. Those safeguards reduce the likelihood that baseline differences or simultaneous operating changes explain what happened during the test.

During the trial, 8.4 percent of routes handled by QuickTurn desks started more than 15 minutes late, while the control rate was 12.8 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6-percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 points. Because the interval remains above zero, the result supports a real reduction rather than merely a chance fluctuation in this sample. Random assignment, stable operating conditions, and a pre-registered analysis make this substantially stronger evidence than an informal before-and-after comparison. For the two depots, 36 desks, and seven-week period studied, QuickTurn appears to have improved the primary outcome.

That conclusion should not be stretched into a citywide guarantee. Only two depots participated, and the record provides no evidence about other depots’ workflows, staffing patterns, or readiness. It also does not state whether every assigned desk other than the four desks with frequent missing fields remained in the analysis. Although the late-start outcomes for those four desks were retained, uncertainty about retention elsewhere limits confidence in how completely the reported comparison reflects the original assignment. The trial measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but the record supplies no corrected-error results. It did not measure overtime cost or driver satisfaction, leaving Riverbend unable to judge whether fewer late starts came with financial or workforce tradeoffs.

Field completion also deserves attention before expansion. Four of the 18 QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs. Their outcomes remained in the analysis, which is useful because it avoids presenting results only from perfect users. However, the omissions show that adopting a five-field form does not ensure that every handoff contains all five fields. Citywide deployment would multiply that implementation risk without evidence about why fields were skipped, whether omissions affected performance, or what level of training and auditing would be required. The proposed extension is especially valuable because Riverbend can audit every QuickTurn handoff for six weeks, creating a complete view of adherence rather than relying on spot checks.

The voluntary employee survey does not resolve these questions. Among 260 respondents across the two depots, 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Those responses suggest that employees perceived possible operational advantages, but respondents could select more than one answer, and the report lacks respondent-level cross-tabulation. Riverbend therefore cannot determine how many people selected both benefits or translate the two totals into distinct shares of respondents. More importantly, responses were not linked to desk assignment, so the survey cannot compare QuickTurn users with control staff. It should be treated as descriptive feedback from the depots, not as evidence that QuickTurn caused better employee experiences.

A September 1 citywide launch is not supported by the available implementation record. Engineering needs twelve business days after final requirements are signed, but the requirements remain unsigned. There is no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout. Mara Velez’s request supplies no operational reason for the deadline. Committing to that date would therefore combine an incomplete evidence base with an undefined delivery schedule.

Riverbend should authorize the six-week extension at the existing 18 desks, add the optional exception note, and use its audit capacity to review every handoff. The extension should retain late starts as the central outcome while collecting the planned measures of corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload. Riverbend should also document desk retention and field completion explicitly so the next decision rests on a fully traceable analysis. Final requirements and a realistic configuration, training, and depot rollout sequence should be settled only after those results show whether QuickTurn’s reduction in late starts persists without unacceptable cost or workload effects.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P7_R1_C0 -->

</details>

````text
# QuickTurn has earned a longer test, not a citywide launch

Riverbend should keep QuickTurn at the 18 assigned desks for the proposed six-week extension, then decide whether to expand it. The seven-week trial gives us good reason to believe the five-field handoff reduced serious route delays in the two depots studied. It does not yet tell us enough about cost, staff workload, consistent use, or performance outside those depots to justify a citywide deployment. Stopping now would discard a promising tool. Expanding now would run ahead of the evidence.

The trial result is substantial. Before the trial, 13.2 percent of scheduled routes handled by desks later assigned to QuickTurn started more than 15 minutes late. The comparable rate for control desks was 13.0 percent. During the trial, the QuickTurn rate fell to 8.4 percent, while the control rate was 12.8 percent. The pre-registered comparison of those changes estimated a 4.6 percentage point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval of 1.9 to 7.2 points. Even the low end of that interval would matter in daily operations.

The study design makes that finding more persuasive than a simple before-and-after comparison. Riverbend grouped desks by depot and prior late-start rate, then used a random draw within each group to assign 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. Those controls reduce the chance that the difference came from easier routes, a software change, or different operating rules. For the desks, depots, and seven weeks studied, the record supports treating QuickTurn as the cause of the measured reduction.

There are still gaps that matter before citywide use. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their late-start results remained in the analysis, which is useful because it prevents those weaker users from simply disappearing from the outcome. But the record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis. If any desks were excluded after assignment, we would need to know which ones and why before judging the result as complete. The trial also measured corrected assignment errors but provides no result for that measure, so no claim about QuickTurn’s effect on those errors is supported here.

The employee survey cannot fill those gaps. It drew 260 voluntary responses from the two depots, with 118 respondents selecting faster assignment and 74 selecting clearer escalation. Respondents could choose more than one answer, and the report lacks respondent-level cross-tabulation, so we do not know how many selected both. More important, responses were not linked to desk assignment. The survey therefore cannot show that QuickTurn staff saw assignments as faster or escalation as clearer than control staff did. It records opinions across the depots, not a comparison between the two handoff methods.

The record is also silent on two consequences that could change the decision: overtime cost and driver satisfaction. A handoff that reduces late starts may still add dispatcher effort or shift work into overtime. The trial did not measure either possibility. It did not measure driver satisfaction, so we should not claim that drivers preferred the change or benefited beyond the recorded start times. Citywide deployment would also reach depots and desks that were not part of the experiment. The current result may carry over, but this trial alone cannot establish that it will.

The September 1 request is not a workable deployment plan. Engineering needs twelve business days of configuration after final requirements are signed, yet the requirements remain unsigned. The record gives no signing date, start date, sequencing plan, training estimate, or depot rollout estimate. It also gives no operational reason for September 1. Treating that date as fixed would force planning around a deadline that has neither a stated purpose nor the preparation needed to meet it responsibly.

Riverbend should approve the six-week extension at the same 18 desks, add the optional exception note, and audit every QuickTurn handoff, using the capacity already available. The extension should track late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload as planned. The audit should record missing fields and confirm that every assigned desk remains in the analysis. Final requirements can be refined from those findings, but citywide configuration and rollout should wait. At the end of six weeks, Riverbend will have a stronger basis for deciding whether the reduction in late starts persists, whether staff can use the handoff reliably, and whether its operational benefits justify its costs.
````
