````text
# QuickTurn Has Earned a Six-Week Extension, Not a Citywide Launch

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn at the 18 assigned desks for six weeks before deciding on a citywide deployment. The seven-week trial produced credible evidence that the five-field handoff reduced late route starts in the two depots studied. It left open questions about implementation, staff workload, overtime cost, and the completeness of the analysis. A limited extension can answer several of those questions while preserving the conditions needed to judge the handoff on its performance.

The trial design gives us reason to take the late-start result seriously. Riverbend grouped 36 dispatch desks by depot and prior late-start rate, then used a random draw within each group to assign 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Both groups kept the same staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours. Their late-start rates before the trial were close: 13.2 percent among desks entering QuickTurn and 13.0 percent among control desks. During the trial, the QuickTurn rate fell to 8.4 percent while the control rate reached 12.8 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 points.

Random assignment and stable operating rules strengthen the case that QuickTurn caused the measured reduction during the trial. The confidence interval excludes zero, and the size of the estimated reduction matters for an outcome as central as routes starting more than 15 minutes late. The result applies most securely to the 36 desks, two depots, and seven-week period in the test. The record gives us no basis for assuming the same result across every depot, operating condition, or longer period.

Handoff compliance also needs attention. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs. Their late-start outcomes remained in the analysis, so the result reflects some imperfect use rather than performance under flawless compliance. That makes the measured effect useful for operations. Yet the record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis. Without a complete accounting of all 36 desks, staff cannot assess whether missing desk outcomes affected the comparison. Riverbend should resolve that question before treating the estimate as a settled citywide forecast.

The trial also leaves gaps outside the primary outcome. It measured corrected assignment errors, but the record provides no results for that measure. It did not measure overtime cost or driver satisfaction. The voluntary survey of 260 dispatch employees cannot fill those gaps or establish QuickTurn’s effects. Respondents could choose more than one answer; 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Because the report lacks respondent-level cross-tabulation, we do not know how many selected both. Because Riverbend did not link responses to desk assignment, we cannot compare staff using QuickTurn with staff using the free-form handoff. The survey describes selected impressions across two depots, not a treatment-control difference.

Mara Velez’s request for a citywide deployment by September 1 supplies a target date but no operational reason for it. Engineering expects twelve business days of configuration after leaders sign final requirements. Leaders have not signed them, and the record identifies no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, training estimate, or depot rollout estimate. Those omissions prevent dispatch and engineering staff from judging whether the date allows enough time for configuration and adoption. A citywide commitment now would outrun both the evidence and the available delivery plan.

Riverbend should keep QuickTurn at the original 18 desks for the proposed six-week extension and add the optional exception note. Before the extension begins, the trial team should document whether all desks from the first trial remained in the analysis and set out how it will handle every assigned desk during the extension. Riverbend has capacity to audit every QuickTurn handoff, so staff should use those audits to track completion of each required field and use of the exception note. The extension should measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload as planned. Staff should link workload responses to desk assignment so the results permit a comparison, while reporting response rates and overlapping selections.

At the end of six weeks, Riverbend can judge whether the late-start reduction persists, whether the exception note improves usable compliance, and whether overtime or dispatcher workload offsets the operational gain. If those results support expansion, leaders can sign final requirements and build a rollout schedule that includes engineering configuration, training, sequencing, and depot deployment time. Until Riverbend has those results and dates, QuickTurn should remain a controlled extension rather than a citywide requirement.
````

````text
Six More Weeks for QuickTurn at Riverbend

Riverbend tested QuickTurn for seven weeks at 36 dispatch desks in two depots. The five-field handoff applies when an incoming dispatcher takes responsibility for an unresolved route assignment. Eighteen desks used it, while 18 continued with the existing free-form handoff. The trial produced a large enough reduction in late starts to justify continued use at the test desks, but the record is too narrow to support a citywide deployment now. Riverbend should extend QuickTurn at those 18 desks for six weeks under the proposed extension plan.

The assignment method gives the trial result real weight. Before assignment, Riverbend grouped desks by depot and by their average late-start rate during the prior six weeks, then used a random draw within each group. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. Before the trial, desks later assigned to QuickTurn had a 13.2 percent late-start rate, nearly the same as the control desks’ 13.0 percent.

During the trial, 8.4 percent of scheduled routes at QuickTurn desks started more than 15 minutes late, compared with 12.8 percent at control desks. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 percentage points. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered comparison support that attribution for the desks, depots, and seven-week period studied. The confidence interval leaves uncertainty about the size of the reduction while favoring QuickTurn throughout its range.

Implementation was uneven. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, and their late-start outcomes remained in the analysis. Keeping those outcomes avoids presenting the result only from desks with better field completion. The record does not state whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis, so Riverbend cannot assume complete retention across all 36 desks.

The trial measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, although the record supplies no result for corrected assignment errors. It explicitly did not measure driver satisfaction or overtime cost. A citywide decision would therefore carry an unknown overtime effect, and this record offers no basis for a claim about how drivers experienced the handoff.

The voluntary post-trial survey adds context, rather than a treatment comparison. It received 260 responses from dispatch employees across the two depots. Respondents could select more than one answer; 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation.

The report has no respondent-level cross-tabulation, so the overlap between those counts is unknown. Responses were not linked to dispatch-desk assignment, leaving the survey unable to compare QuickTurn staff with control staff. The two selections may describe employee views, but they cannot be credited to QuickTurn from this survey design.

A limited extension fits the strength and boundaries of the evidence. QuickTurn would remain at the 18 assigned desks for six more weeks, keeping the scope to the tested desks and depots. The plan would add an optional exception note and measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload. Those measurements would broaden the operational record. The record does not describe a control comparison for the extension, so its stated design does not support promising a new causal estimate.

Riverbend also has capacity to audit every QuickTurn handoff during the extension. That audit can identify omitted required fields and show where the optional note is used. The capacity to inspect each handoff does not by itself prove that every handoff was recorded or that staff followed the form consistently. Audit findings should be reported with the extension outcomes, desk retention, and any changes in operating rules.

The requested September 1 citywide date is not a workable planning basis in the present record. Operations director Mara Velez gave no reason for that date. Engineering estimates twelve business days of configuration after final requirements are signed, but the requirements remain unsigned.

There is no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout. Riverbend cannot derive a credible citywide schedule from those missing inputs.

During the six-week extension, Riverbend should preserve the existing rules and desk assignments, audit the handoffs, document whether every assigned desk remains in the analysis, and collect each planned outcome without treating the survey as a control comparison. At the end of the extension, the deployment decision should use the late-start estimate alongside corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, dispatcher-reported workload, field completion, and a dated engineering, training, and depot rollout plan.
````

````text
# QuickTurn Shows Promise, but the Evidence Supports a Six-Week Extension—not Citywide Deployment

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn in a limited test for six more weeks at the 18 desks already assigned to use it. The seven-week trial provides credible evidence that the five-field handoff reduced late route starts under the conditions studied. That is a strong reason to continue using QuickTurn at those desks, but it is not yet a sufficient basis for citywide deployment. The record leaves important questions about implementation consistency, analysis completeness, costs, workload, and rollout timing. Stopping QuickTurn would discard a promising intervention before those questions are answered; deploying it everywhere now would move beyond what the current evidence can support.

The trial’s design makes its central result persuasive. Riverbend tested 36 dispatch desks in two depots, grouping them before assignment by depot and prior late-start rate. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. Those features reduce the likelihood that the measured difference arose from a known operating change or from systematically placing stronger desks in the QuickTurn group.

The groups were also closely matched before the trial. Desks later assigned to QuickTurn had a 13.2 percent late-start rate during the prior six weeks, compared with 13.0 percent among control desks. During the trial, the QuickTurn rate fell to 8.4 percent while the control rate was 12.8 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 percentage points. Because the full interval indicates a reduction, the result is not merely a favorable point estimate. Combined with random assignment, stable operating rules, and a pre-registered analysis, it supports attributing the improvement to QuickTurn for the desks, depots, and seven-week period studied.

That last qualification matters. The trial covered only two depots and 36 desks. The record does not establish that other depots have the same handoff patterns, operating pressures, or ability to complete the five fields consistently. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs. Their late-start outcomes remained in the analysis, which is useful because the reported effect was not produced by simply removing those relatively inconsistent users. However, the record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis. Without a complete accounting of all assigned desks, staff cannot fully assess whether missing desks or outcomes affected the estimate.

The outcome record is narrower than the decision Riverbend now faces. Late starts were the primary outcome, and the trial also measured corrected assignment errors, but no result for corrected errors is provided. We therefore cannot say whether QuickTurn improved, worsened, or had no effect on those errors. The trial did not measure driver satisfaction or overtime cost. It also offers no direct measure of dispatcher workload. A handoff can reduce late starts while creating costs or burdens elsewhere, and this record does not show whether that occurred.

The voluntary survey does not fill those gaps. Among 260 dispatch employees across the two depots, 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Respondents could choose more than one answer, but the report lacks respondent-level cross-tabulation, so the number selecting both is unknown. More importantly, responses were not linked to dispatch-desk assignment. The survey cannot compare QuickTurn staff with control staff, and its aggregate answers should not be treated as evidence that QuickTurn caused either perceived benefit. They are useful signals about what employees noticed, not a substitute for the randomized operational comparison.

A citywide launch by September 1 is also not supported by a documented implementation plan. Operations director Mara Velez requested that date, but the request gives no reason for it. Engineering needs twelve business days of configuration after final requirements are signed, yet the requirements remain unsigned. The record supplies no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout. Committing to citywide deployment against that date would therefore combine an evidence gap with an unresolved delivery schedule.

The limited extension directly addresses several of these weaknesses while preserving the demonstrated benefit at the original 18 desks. For six more weeks, Riverbend can add the optional exception note and measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload. Riverbend also has capacity to audit every QuickTurn handoff, allowing staff to document field completion rather than relying on partial observations. The exception note should be tracked separately so Riverbend can see how often it is used and interpret results from the modified handoff accurately.

The next action should be to authorize that six-week extension, finalize its requirements, and establish a complete desk-level accounting before it begins. Every assigned QuickTurn desk, every handoff audit, and every specified outcome should remain traceable through the extension, including desks with incomplete fields. At the end of six weeks, Riverbend can judge whether the late-start reduction persists, whether corrected errors improve, and whether overtime or reported workload introduces a material tradeoff. Only then should leaders set a citywide decision and a rollout date grounded in signed requirements, configuration timing, training, and depot sequencing.
````

````text
# QuickTurn has earned a longer test, not a citywide launch

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn for six weeks at the 18 desks already using it. The seven-week trial produced a clear and encouraging result, but it does not yet support a citywide deployment. Stopping QuickTurn would also be hard to justify. The best next step is to keep the current footprint, audit every handoff, and collect the operational and staff data that the first trial did not provide.

QuickTurn is a five-field handoff for unresolved route assignments when responsibility passes to an incoming dispatcher. Riverbend compared it with the existing free-form handoff at 36 desks in two depots. Before assignment, the desks were grouped by depot and by their average late-start rate over the previous six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the control process. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. That design gives the comparison more weight than a simple before-and-after review.

The primary outcome was the share of scheduled routes starting more than 15 minutes late. Before the trial, that rate was 13.2 percent at desks later assigned to QuickTurn and 13.0 percent at control desks. During the trial, the QuickTurn rate fell to 8.4 percent while the control rate was 12.8 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a reduction of 4.6 percentage points, with a 95 percent confidence interval of 1.9 to 7.2 points. In plain terms, the record supports a real reduction during the trial, although the exact size remains uncertain.

Random assignment matters here. So do the stable operating rules and the decision to specify the comparison in advance. Together, they support attributing the measured reduction to QuickTurn for the desks, depots, and seven-week period studied. We should not stretch that finding beyond its setting. Two depots may not represent conditions across Riverbend, and seven weeks may not reveal whether performance holds after the process becomes routine. The record also does not state whether every assigned desk, apart from the four desks discussed for incomplete fields, remained in the analysis. That missing information should be resolved before a broader decision.

Compliance was imperfect but visible. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, and their late-start outcomes remained in the analysis. Keeping those outcomes is useful because it avoids presenting QuickTurn only under ideal use. Still, Riverbend needs to know which fields were missed, why they were missed, and whether omissions affected results. The proposed extension gives us the capacity to audit every QuickTurn handoff, which should answer those questions. The optional exception note can also be tested to see whether it helps dispatchers handle cases that do not fit neatly into five fields.

The employee survey cannot settle the decision. It drew 260 voluntary responses across the two depots. In the aggregate, 118 respondents selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Respondents could choose more than one answer, and the report does not show how many chose both. More importantly, responses were not linked to desk assignment, so there is no QuickTurn-versus-control comparison. The survey tells us that employees reported these benefits somewhere within the two depots. It does not show that QuickTurn caused them or how common those views were among staff who used it.

The first trial also leaves important costs and working conditions unanswered. It measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but the record supplies no result for corrected errors. It did not measure overtime cost or driver satisfaction. A six-week extension would add overtime cost and dispatcher-reported workload while continuing to measure late starts and corrected assignment errors. Driver satisfaction would remain unmeasured, so any later citywide recommendation would still need to acknowledge that gap. Even so, the extension would give Riverbend a much better view of whether fewer late starts come with added staff burden or expense.

Mara Velez has requested citywide deployment by September 1, but the record gives no operational reason for that date. Engineering needs twelve business days after final requirements are signed, and those requirements are not signed. There is no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout. Riverbend should therefore hold the current 18-desk footprint for six more weeks, sign the extension requirements promptly, audit every handoff, and document whether all assigned desks remain in the analysis. At the end of that period, the deployment decision should rest on sustained late-start results, corrected-error results, overtime cost, reported workload, field completion, and a realistic rollout schedule.
````

</details>

````text
Riverbend Should Give QuickTurn Six More Weeks Before a Citywide Decision

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn at the 18 trial desks for six weeks before deciding on a citywide deployment. The seven-week trial gives dispatch staff a sound reason to keep testing the five-field handoff: it produced a sizable reduction in routes starting more than 15 minutes late under the conditions Riverbend studied. The same record leaves gaps about implementation, costs, staff workload, and the completeness of the analysis. A limited extension would preserve the gains under study while Riverbend collects the evidence needed for a broader decision.

QuickTurn applies when an incoming dispatcher assumes responsibility for an unresolved route assignment. Riverbend tested it at 36 dispatch desks in two depots, with 18 desks using QuickTurn and 18 retaining the free-form handoff. Before assignment, Riverbend grouped desks by depot and by their average late-start rate over the prior six weeks. Staff used a random draw within each group. That design reduced the chance that depot conditions or prior performance created the difference between the groups. Riverbend kept staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours the same for both groups during the trial.

The two groups started from close baselines. QuickTurn desks had a 13.2 percent late-start rate before the trial, compared with 13.0 percent at control desks. During the trial, the QuickTurn rate fell to 8.4 percent while the control rate measured 12.8 percent. Riverbend’s pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction from QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 points. The interval excludes zero and supports a real effect for the desks, depots, and seven-week period in the test. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and a pre-registered comparison strengthen that conclusion.

Those strengths do not settle a citywide decision. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Riverbend kept the late-start outcomes from those four desks in the analysis, which prevents their compliance problems from disappearing from the result. The record does not say whether each of the other assigned desks remained in the analysis. Dispatch staff cannot confirm complete retention across both groups. Riverbend should resolve that gap before relying on the estimate for a systemwide commitment.

The outcome record has more missing pieces. The trial measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but the record supplies no result for corrected assignment errors. It contains no measure of overtime cost or driver satisfaction. A voluntary survey drew responses from 260 dispatch employees in the two depots. Among them, 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Respondents could choose more than one answer, and the report lacks respondent-level cross-tabulation, so Riverbend cannot determine how many chose both. The survey had no link to desk assignment, which means it cannot compare the views of QuickTurn staff with those of control staff. These responses describe perceptions within the depots, not an estimated QuickTurn effect.

The September 1 citywide request raises a separate concern. Operations director Mara Velez gave no reason for that date. Engineering needs twelve business days of configuration after Riverbend signs final requirements, yet no one has signed them. The record gives no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, training estimate, or depot rollout estimate. Riverbend lacks a supported schedule for September 1. Committing to that date would treat engineering configuration as the whole deployment even though the record provides no basis for fitting training and depot rollout around it.

The proposed extension addresses several open questions. Riverbend can keep QuickTurn at the 18 assigned desks for six more weeks, add an optional exception note, and measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload. Dispatchers can use the optional note for cases that the five required fields do not cover. Riverbend has capacity to audit each QuickTurn handoff during the extension, so managers can inspect omitted fields and use of the exception note. The extension would leave driver satisfaction unmeasured.

Before the extension starts, Riverbend should confirm which desks and outcomes remain in the analysis and set the comparison method for all four measures. Staff should continue the assigned handoff at each desk, record field omissions through the audit, and keep operating rules stable. At the end of six weeks, Riverbend can judge whether the late-start reduction persists, whether corrected errors improve, and whether overtime cost or reported workload offsets the operational benefit. Riverbend should use those results to decide on citywide deployment and build a schedule after final requirements, training, and depot rollout have defined dates.
````

````text
# QuickTurn after seven weeks at two depots

Riverbend tested QuickTurn for seven weeks at 36 dispatch desks in two depots. The five-field handoff replaced the existing free-form handoff at 18 randomly assigned desks when an incoming dispatcher took responsibility for an unresolved route assignment. The other 18 desks continued with the existing handoff. Before assignment, desks were grouped by depot and by their average late-start rate during the prior six weeks, then assigned by random draw within each group. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups during the trial.

The primary outcome was the share of scheduled routes starting more than 15 minutes late. Before the trial, that rate was 13.2 percent at desks later assigned to QuickTurn and 13.0 percent at control desks. During the trial, the rates were 8.4 percent and 12.8 percent, respectively. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 percentage points. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and a comparison specified in advance support that attribution for the desks, depots, and seven-week period studied.

Riverbend should keep QuickTurn at the 18 assigned desks for the proposed six-week extension, then decide whether to expand it. The late-start result supports continued use where the handoff was tested. It does not support citywide deployment now because the trial covered two depots, lasted seven weeks, and leaves operating questions that a limited extension can address without expanding QuickTurn to more desks.

Handoff completion deserves close attention during that extension. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their late-start outcomes remained in the analysis, which avoids removing those desks from the reported effect because of field omissions. The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis, so the full retention status is unknown. That gap should be resolved before Riverbend treats the estimate as an account of all assigned desks.

The trial measured late starts and corrected assignment errors. It did not measure driver satisfaction or overtime cost. The proposed extension would add overtime cost and dispatcher-reported workload while continuing to measure late starts and corrected assignment errors. Those measures can describe the four outcomes during the extension, but the plan does not include driver satisfaction.

The voluntary post-trial survey does not fill those gaps or provide a treatment comparison. It drew 260 responses from dispatch employees across the two depots and allowed more than one answer. In aggregate, 118 respondents selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. The report has no respondent-level cross-tabulation, so the overlap between those selections is unknown. The survey was not linked to dispatch-desk assignment, which means it cannot tell us whether QuickTurn staff reported either answer more often than control staff. The responses describe what participating employees selected, but they cannot be credited to QuickTurn.

The extension offers a way to examine implementation. Riverbend can audit every QuickTurn handoff during the six weeks, giving operations a record of required-field completion in those handoffs and use of the optional exception note. The audit should report omissions by desk and field. Late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload should be reported for the extension period with the scope and comparison method stated. The extension plan states that QuickTurn can remain at the 18 assigned desks, but it gives no comparison design. Its results therefore should not be presented in advance as capable of proving that QuickTurn caused any change observed during those six weeks.

Mara Velez has requested citywide deployment by September 1, but the request gives no reason for that date. Engineering estimates 12 business days of configuration after final requirements are signed. Those requirements are not signed, and the record supplies no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout. Riverbend cannot assess from this record whether a September 1 deployment is feasible, and the date should not force a decision ahead of the missing work plan.

On approval, operations should retain the current 18 QuickTurn desks, add the optional exception note, and begin the handoff audit on the first day. Before the six weeks end, the trial team should document whether every originally assigned desk was retained in the first analysis and prepare the four extension measures with their stated comparison method. Engineering and operations should separately produce a signed-requirements date, configuration start, sequencing plan, training estimate, and depot rollout estimate. The citywide decision can then use the original causal result, the extension’s operating measures, the handoff audit, and an actual delivery schedule.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P7_R3_C0 -->
````text
# QuickTurn Has Earned a Controlled Extension, Not a Citywide Launch

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn for six weeks at the 18 desks already assigned to use it, rather than deploy it citywide now or stop using it. The seven-week trial provides credible evidence that the five-field handoff reduced late route starts under the conditions studied. That is a meaningful operational result, but the record leaves important questions about implementation quality, other costs and benefits, and readiness for a wider rollout. A limited extension would preserve the demonstrated practice while producing evidence needed for a more defensible citywide decision.

The trial design supports taking the late-start result seriously. Riverbend grouped 36 dispatch desks by depot and prior average late-start rate, then randomly assigned desks within those groups to QuickTurn or the existing free-form handoff. Eighteen desks entered each group. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same across the groups, reducing the likelihood that concurrent operating changes explain the difference. The groups were also closely matched before the trial: desks later assigned to QuickTurn had a 13.2 percent late-start rate, compared with 13.0 percent among control desks.

During the trial, the QuickTurn desks’ late-start rate fell to 8.4 percent, while the control rate was 12.8 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6-percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 percentage points. Because the interval remains above zero, the record supports a real reduction during the trial rather than merely a favorable raw difference. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered analysis together justify attributing the measured improvement to QuickTurn for the desks, depots, and seven-week period that were actually studied.

That qualification matters. The trial covered only two depots and 36 desks, so it does not establish that the same effect would appear at every Riverbend depot or under a citywide rollout. The record also says that four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs. Their late-start outcomes remained in the analysis, which is valuable because it prevents their imperfect use from being quietly excluded. However, the record does not state whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis. Without complete information on retention, Riverbend cannot fully assess whether the reported comparison preserves the original random assignment for all desks.

The trial’s outcome coverage is also narrower than the citywide decision requires. It measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but the record provides no corrected-error results. It did not measure overtime cost or driver satisfaction. The voluntary post-trial survey offers limited context, not comparative evidence. Among 260 dispatch employees across the two depots, 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Respondents could select more than one answer, and there is no respondent-level cross-tabulation showing how many selected both. More importantly, responses were not linked to desk assignment, so the survey cannot show whether QuickTurn staff viewed handoffs differently from control staff. The counts should not be treated as proof that QuickTurn caused either perceived benefit.

Stopping QuickTurn would therefore discard a practice that produced a statistically supported and operationally important reduction in late starts. Citywide deployment, however, would outrun both the evidence and the implementation record. Operations director Mara Velez has requested deployment by September 1, but the request gives no reason for that date. Engineering needs twelve business days of configuration after final requirements are signed, yet those requirements remain unsigned. There is no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout. The record consequently does not establish that September 1 is achievable, and a deadline without a stated operational basis should not force Riverbend past unresolved requirements and unmeasured rollout work.

The next action should be the available six-week extension at the same 18 QuickTurn desks. Riverbend should add the optional exception note and use its capacity to audit every QuickTurn handoff. Those audits can establish how consistently staff complete the five required fields and how the exception note is used, directly addressing the compliance concern raised by the first trial. The extension should measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload as planned. Keeping the extension limited to the existing desks avoids presenting it as evidence from new depots, but it allows Riverbend to test whether the late-start improvement persists beyond seven weeks and whether it is accompanied by acceptable error, cost, and workload results. Final citywide requirements, training needs, rollout sequencing, and any deployment date can then be set against a more complete operational record rather than the unsupported September 1 request.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P7_R3_C1 -->
````text
# QuickTurn has earned a longer test, not a citywide launch

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn at the 18 assigned desks for six more weeks, not deploy it citywide now or stop using it. The seven-week trial produced credible evidence that the handoff reduced late route starts in the two depots studied. That result is strong enough to justify continued use under controlled conditions. It is not enough to support a citywide rollout by September 1, especially when Riverbend still lacks cost and workload data, has an unresolved question about which desks remained in the analysis, and has no complete implementation schedule.

QuickTurn is a five-field handoff for unresolved route assignments when responsibility passes to an incoming dispatcher. Before the trial, Riverbend grouped the 36 participating desks by depot and prior late-start rate, then randomly assigned 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. That design matters. The QuickTurn and control desks entered the trial with nearly identical late-start rates, 13.2 percent and 13.0 percent, respectively. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. Those controls reduce the chance that an unrelated operating change caused the difference that followed.

During the trial, 8.4 percent of routes handled by QuickTurn desks started more than 15 minutes late, compared with 12.8 percent at control desks. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval ranging from 1.9 to 7.2 points. The interval does not include zero. Together with random assignment and stable operating rules, that supports a causal conclusion for the desks, depots, and seven-week period in the trial. In practical terms, the evidence says QuickTurn improved the outcome Riverbend chose in advance as its primary measure.

The record also contains an encouraging detail about imperfect use. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, yet their late-start outcomes stayed in the analysis. Excluding those desks after seeing their compliance could have made QuickTurn look better than it performed under normal working conditions. Keeping them provides a more realistic estimate. At the same time, the record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis. That missing information prevents a full assessment of whether the original random groups were preserved. Riverbend should resolve and document the desk count before treating the estimate as the basis for a larger commitment.

The trial also leaves important operational questions unanswered. It measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but the record gives no results for corrected errors. It did not measure overtime cost or driver satisfaction. Nor did it measure dispatcher workload. A process can reduce delays while shifting work into overtime or making handoffs harder during a busy shift. The current evidence cannot tell us whether that happened.

The employee survey does not fill those gaps. Of 260 voluntary respondents across the two depots, 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Respondents could choose more than one answer, but the report does not show how many chose both. More importantly, responses were not linked to desk assignment. The survey therefore describes what respondents selected in the aggregate; it does not compare the experience of QuickTurn staff with that of control staff. It should not be used as proof that QuickTurn caused either perceived benefit.

A September 1 citywide launch is also unsupported as an execution plan. Mara Velez requested that date but gave no reason for it. Engineering needs twelve business days after final requirements are signed, and those requirements remain unsigned. The record supplies no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, training estimate, or depot rollout estimate. Even if the trial evidence were broad enough, Riverbend could not judge from this record whether a responsible citywide launch by that date is feasible.

The six-week extension is the sound next action. Riverbend should keep QuickTurn at the original 18 desks, continue tracking the original control desks for comparison, and define the analysis and citywide decision criteria before the extension begins. The planned optional exception note should be documented as a modification because it may change how dispatchers use the handoff. The extension should measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload as proposed. Riverbend can audit every QuickTurn handoff during this period, which will show where fields are omitted, how often the exception note is used, and whether compliance changes over time. Before expanding further, the final review should account for every assigned desk and report both outcome and compliance results. That gives dispatch staff six more weeks of evidence tied to the work itself and gives Riverbend a defensible basis for the next deployment decision.
````

````text
# QuickTurn Has Earned a Longer Test, Not a Citywide Launch

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn for six weeks at the 18 desks that used it during the trial. QuickTurn gives an incoming dispatcher five fields for taking responsibility for an unresolved route assignment. The seven-week test produced credible evidence that this handoff reduced late starts in the two depots under study. The result warrants continued use at those desks and a tighter examination of costs, workload, and compliance. The current record does not support a citywide deployment by September 1. Riverbend lacks evidence on several effects that matter to dispatch operations, and the proposed date has no stated operational reason or complete delivery schedule.

The trial design gives the late-start result weight. Riverbend grouped 36 desks by depot and prior late-start rate, then used a random draw within each group to assign 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the free-form handoff. The groups began from close baselines: 13.2 percent at desks slated for QuickTurn and 13.0 percent at control desks. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. During the trial, the QuickTurn desks posted an 8.4 percent late-start rate, while control desks posted 12.8 percent. The control rate fell by 0.2 percentage points from its pretrial level; the QuickTurn rate fell by 4.8 points. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6-point reduction from QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 points. At the point estimate, that equals 46 fewer starts more than 15 minutes late per 1,000 scheduled routes under the trial conditions. The full confidence interval favors QuickTurn. Random assignment and stable operating rules support a causal judgment for the desks, depots, and seven weeks Riverbend studied.

Riverbend should keep that judgment within its proper boundary. The test covered 36 desks in two depots for seven weeks. The record offers no basis for assuming that other depots, operating conditions, or a longer period would produce the same result. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs. Evaluators kept outcomes from those four desks in the analysis, so the reported effect includes known lapses in use. Yet the record does not say whether evaluators retained each of the other assigned desks. Missing desks or outcomes could change confidence in the estimate, depending on their number and pattern. Riverbend should resolve that gap before treating the estimate as a complete account of the assigned groups.

The record leaves major operational questions unanswered. The trial measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but the record supplies no result for corrected errors. It did not measure overtime cost or driver satisfaction. A voluntary survey drew 260 responses across the two depots. Respondents could choose more than one answer; 118 chose faster assignment and 74 chose clearer escalation. Without respondent-level cross-tabulation, Riverbend cannot tell how many chose both. Because the survey report did not link respondents to desk assignment, Riverbend cannot compare QuickTurn and control staff. Those counts may describe what some respondents valued, but they cannot show that QuickTurn caused either perception. The survey cannot fill the missing evidence on driver satisfaction or overtime.

Mara Velez’s request for a September 1 citywide deployment adds urgency without evidence for the date. Engineering needs twelve business days of configuration after Riverbend signs final requirements. Riverbend has not signed them, and the record supplies no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, training estimate, or depot rollout estimate. Riverbend cannot judge whether September 1 is feasible. A commitment to that date would force a systemwide decision before the agency answers questions that the current record identifies and the extension can address. It could also leave less time than staff need for training and depot rollout, since no one has estimated either task.

Riverbend should authorize the limited extension, keep QuickTurn at the 18 assigned desks, and add the optional exception note. During those six weeks, staff should measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload as planned. Riverbend has capacity to audit each QuickTurn handoff, so auditors should record missing fields and use of the exception note. Before the extension begins, the evaluation team should confirm which desks and outcomes remained in the first analysis, define how it will handle missing records, and fix the comparison method in advance. The team should track whether staff use the exception note when they omit a required field, giving Riverbend a clearer view of whether the revised form fits difficult handoffs. Riverbend can reconsider citywide deployment after leaders review the extension’s outcome, cost, workload, and audit results. Until then, engineering should prepare requirements without treating September 1 as an approved launch date.
````

<details><summary>Stage 1 P7 R3, Anti-AI Writing Kit</summary>

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P7_R3_C3 -->
````text
Six More Weeks for QuickTurn Before a Citywide Decision

QuickTurn is a five-field handoff used when an incoming dispatcher takes responsibility for an unresolved route assignment. Riverbend should extend it at the 18 trial desks for six weeks before deciding whether to use it citywide. The seven-week test produced a meaningful reduction in late starts under credible trial conditions, so stopping now would discard a promising handoff. Citywide deployment by September 1 would go beyond what two depots can establish and would commit Riverbend to a schedule that the record does not support.

The trial was built to answer whether QuickTurn changed the share of scheduled routes starting more than 15 minutes late. Riverbend grouped 36 desks by depot and by their average late-start rate over the prior six weeks, then used a random draw within each group to assign 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. That design reduces the chance that depot differences, prior performance, or simultaneous operating changes explain the result.

Before the trial, the desks later assigned to QuickTurn had a 13.2 percent late-start rate, close to the control desks’ 13.0 percent. During the trial, the QuickTurn rate fell to 8.4 percent, while the control rate was 12.8 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 points. Because the interval remains above zero, the result is inconsistent with no reduction under the pre-registered analysis. The random assignment and stable rules support a causal conclusion for the desks, depots, and seven-week period studied.

The record does not carry that conclusion to the entire city. Only 36 desks in two depots participated, and operations elsewhere may differ. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs. Their late-start outcomes stayed in the analysis, which avoids presenting the effect only among those four desks with better field completion, but the record does not state whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis. That missing information prevents a full assessment of attrition and should be resolved before a broader decision.

The outcome record is also narrower than a citywide operating decision requires. The trial measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but the record provides no corrected-error result. It explicitly did not measure driver satisfaction or overtime cost. A voluntary survey of 260 dispatch employees at the two depots offers context, not a treatment comparison. Respondents could choose more than one answer: 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Without respondent-level cross-tabulation, Riverbend cannot tell how many selected both.

Because survey responses were not linked to desk assignment, the totals cannot establish whether QuickTurn staff viewed handoffs differently from control staff. Voluntary participation also means the responses describe respondents, not necessarily all dispatch employees at the depots.

Mara Velez’s request for citywide deployment by September 1 does not supply a basis for that date. Engineering needs twelve business days for configuration after final requirements are signed, but the requirements remain unsigned. The record gives no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, training estimate, or depot rollout estimate. The twelve-day figure therefore cannot be treated as a complete deployment schedule, and it cannot establish that a September 1 launch is feasible.

A limited extension uses the capacity Riverbend already has while addressing several decision gaps. QuickTurn would remain at the same 18 assigned desks for six more weeks, with an optional exception note added. Riverbend would measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload, and it has capacity to audit every QuickTurn handoff during that period. The audit can document field completion in the handoffs available for inspection and identify where dispatchers use the exception note. It should not be treated as proof that every handoff was recorded or that the form was followed consistently unless the audit evidence supports those claims.

Before the extension begins, the trial team should account for all 36 originally assigned desks and report the corrected-assignment-error results already measured. It should set in advance how each extension measure will be calculated, how dispatcher-reported workload will be collected, and how the handoff audits will be reported. The extension plan in the record does not describe a continuing control group or another randomized comparison, so its results can describe performance and operating burden during the added six weeks but cannot by themselves reproduce the original trial’s causal estimate. Riverbend should use the original randomized result as the evidence of effect and the extension to judge implementation quality, overtime cost, workload, and the practical use of the exception note.

Final requirements should remain unsigned until those six weeks are complete and Riverbend has reviewed the added measures alongside a realistic configuration, training, sequencing, and depot rollout plan. If late-start performance remains acceptable, field omissions can be managed, and the added cost and workload measures support expansion, Riverbend can then set a citywide date from an actual implementation schedule.
````

````text
QuickTurn Merits a Six-Week Extension Before Any Citywide Decision

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn in a limited six-week test at the 18 desks already assigned to use it, rather than deploy it citywide now or stop using it. The seven-week trial provides credible evidence that QuickTurn reduced late route starts under the conditions studied. However, the record leaves important questions about implementation consistency, other operational effects, and readiness for a wider rollout. A focused extension would preserve the demonstrated practice, add measurements needed for a sound citywide decision, and allow Riverbend to audit every QuickTurn handoff.

QuickTurn is a five-field handoff used when an incoming dispatcher assumes responsibility for an unresolved route assignment. Riverbend tested it at 36 dispatch desks in two depots. Before assignment, desks were grouped by depot and by their average late-start rate during the previous six weeks, after which a random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours remained unchanged for both groups. Those design features reduce the likelihood that differences in depot conditions, prior performance, staffing, or operating policy explain the trial result.

The primary outcome was the share of scheduled routes starting more than 15 minutes late. Before the trial, that rate was 13.2 percent at desks later assigned to QuickTurn and 13.0 percent at control desks, showing closely aligned starting points. During the trial, the rate fell to 8.4 percent for QuickTurn desks while remaining at 12.8 percent for control desks. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6-percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval ranging from 1.9 to 7.2 percentage points. Because the entire interval indicates a reduction, the result supports continuing QuickTurn rather than abandoning it.

The strength of that evidence should be stated narrowly. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and a pre-registered analysis support attributing the measured reduction to QuickTurn for the desks, depots, and seven-week period studied. They do not establish that the same effect would occur at every Riverbend desk, in other depots, or over a longer period. The trial covered only 36 desks in two depots, and the record provides no information about whether conditions elsewhere differ. A citywide decision would therefore extend beyond the population and operating period directly supported by the experiment.

The implementation record also warrants further examination. Four of the 18 QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their late-start outcomes remained in the analysis, which is useful because the estimated effect was not produced by excluding these imperfect adopters. At the same time, the record does not state whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis. That omission prevents a complete assessment of whether the analyzed groups fully preserved the original random assignment. Auditing every QuickTurn handoff during the extension would show where fields are omitted, whether the optional exception note affects completion, and whether performance persists under documented use.

The trial measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but the record supplies no corrected-error results. It did not measure overtime cost or driver satisfaction. The proposed extension would add overtime cost and dispatcher-reported workload while continuing to measure late starts and corrected assignment errors. These additions matter because a handoff can improve one operational outcome while affecting staff workload or cost in ways the current evidence cannot reveal. Driver satisfaction would remain unmeasured even after the extension, so any later deployment decision should acknowledge that continuing gap rather than treating the expanded evaluation as comprehensive.

The voluntary post-trial survey does not fill these gaps or provide a valid comparison between trial groups. Among 260 responding dispatch employees across the two depots, 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Respondents could select more than one answer, and the report lacks respondent-level cross-tabulation, so the number selecting both cannot be determined. More importantly, responses were not linked to dispatch-desk assignment. The survey therefore cannot show whether QuickTurn staff were more likely than control staff to perceive faster assignments or clearer escalation. Its aggregate results describe selected perceptions among voluntary respondents, not QuickTurn’s comparative effect.

A September 1 citywide deployment is not supported by the available readiness record. Engineering estimates 12 business days of configuration only after final requirements are signed, but those requirements remain unsigned. There is no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout, and the requested deadline has no stated rationale. Riverbend should begin the six-week extension at the existing 18 desks, add the optional exception note, retain all assigned desks in the analysis, audit every handoff, and predefine how late starts, corrected errors, overtime cost, workload, field completion, and desk retention will be reported. The citywide decision and any deployment date should follow that complete record.
````

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P7_R4_C1 -->
````text
QuickTurn has earned a longer test, not a citywide launch

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn for six weeks at the 18 desks already assigned to it, then decide whether a broader deployment is warranted. The seven-week trial produced a meaningful reduction in late starts under unusually clean operating conditions, so stopping now would discard a promising tool. But a September 1 citywide launch would ask the evidence to carry more weight than it can. The trial covered 36 desks in only two depots, left important operational and employee outcomes unanswered, and does not come with a credible citywide implementation schedule.

The strongest case for QuickTurn is the trial design. Before assignment, Riverbend grouped desks by depot and prior late-start rate, then used a random draw within each group to assign 18 desks to the five-field handoff and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same. Baseline performance was also nearly identical: 13.2 percent of routes started more than 15 minutes late at desks later assigned to QuickTurn, compared with 13.0 percent at control desks. Those details reduce the chance that the result came from easier routes, a more favorable depot mix, or a simultaneous policy change.

During the trial, the late-start rate fell to 8.4 percent at QuickTurn desks while the control rate was 12.8 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 points. Since the full interval is above zero, the result is not just a small numerical wobble in this sample. Random assignment, stable rules, and an analysis chosen in advance give Riverbend good reason to treat the reduction as caused by QuickTurn for the desks, depots, and seven weeks studied.

That last qualification matters. Two depots cannot tell us how the handoff will work at every depot, and seven weeks cannot show whether staff maintain the practice once it becomes routine. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their late-start outcomes remained in the analysis, which is reassuring because the reported effect did not depend on quietly dropping those weaker adopters. Still, the record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis. Riverbend should resolve that gap before treating the estimate as the final word.

The outcome picture is also incomplete. The trial measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but the record provides no result for corrected errors. It did not measure overtime cost or driver satisfaction. The voluntary survey of 260 dispatch employees offers some context, though not a treatment comparison. Respondents could choose more than one answer; 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Without respondent-level cross-tabulation, we do not know how many selected both. Because responses were not linked to desk assignment, we also cannot tell whether QuickTurn staff reported those benefits more often than control staff. The survey should not be used as proof that QuickTurn improved either experience.

The requested September 1 launch has practical problems apart from the evidence. No reason is given for that date. Engineering needs twelve business days after final requirements are signed, but the requirements remain unsigned. There is no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout. A citywide commitment made against that record would turn an unsupported deadline into the organizing fact of the project. Dispatch staff would then absorb the risk of rushed training or uneven adoption, precisely when the trial has already shown that required fields can be missed.

A limited extension makes better use of what Riverbend knows and what it can learn next. Keep QuickTurn at the same 18 desks for six more weeks, add the optional exception note, and measure late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload as planned. Riverbend can audit every QuickTurn handoff during that period, so the extension can show which fields are omitted, how often the exception note is used, and whether closer adherence is associated with steadier results. Because the optional note changes the handoff, results should distinguish handoffs that use it from those that do not.

Before the extension begins, Riverbend should document which desks and outcomes will remain in the analysis, how missing data will be handled, and whether the original control desks will continue to be tracked. The record does not specify that last point. Keeping the comparison, if operationally possible, would make the next result easier to interpret; if it is not possible, Riverbend should be candid that the extension will test durability and operations more than it tests causation. At the end of six weeks, leadership can judge expansion against observed late starts, errors, overtime, workload, and field compliance. For now, QuickTurn has shown enough to continue, but not enough to go citywide on an unexplained date.
````

````text
# Six More Weeks for QuickTurn Before a Citywide Decision

Riverbend should extend QuickTurn at the 18 trial desks for six weeks rather than deploy it citywide or stop using it. The seven-week trial found a substantial reduction in late route starts under conditions that support a causal conclusion. Yet the record leaves open questions about handoff compliance, missing desks, costs, workload, and performance outside two depots. A limited extension would preserve the gains at the desks where Riverbend tested the handoff while collecting the information needed for a sound citywide decision.

QuickTurn gives an incoming dispatcher five fields to complete when taking responsibility for an unresolved route assignment. Riverbend assigned desks through a random draw after grouping them by depot and prior late-start rate. Eighteen desks used QuickTurn, and 18 kept the free-form handoff. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. Before the trial, the two groups had close late-start rates: 13.2 percent at desks later assigned to QuickTurn and 13.0 percent at control desks. Those features reduce the chance that staffing, depot differences, or starting performance created the trial result.

During the trial, 8.4 percent of scheduled routes at QuickTurn desks started more than 15 minutes late, compared with 12.8 percent at control desks. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn. Its 95 percent confidence interval ran from 1.9 to 7.2 percentage points. Even the low end represents a useful reduction in late starts. Because Riverbend specified the comparison in advance, assigned desks at random within matched groups, and kept operating rules stable, dispatch staff can treat the result as credible for the desks, depots, and seven weeks studied.

That last boundary matters. Riverbend tested 36 desks in two depots, not the whole city. Other depots may handle different route mixes, staffing pressures, or handoff patterns. Seven weeks also cannot show whether dispatchers maintain field completion over a longer period. Four QuickTurn desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs. Riverbend kept their late-start outcomes in the analysis, which protects the estimate from excluding known cases of weak compliance. The record, however, does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis. Staff responsible for the extension should settle that question before treating the reported estimate as a complete account of the trial.

The employee survey cannot fill these gaps. Of 260 respondents across the two depots, 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Respondents could select more than one answer, and the report provides no respondent-level cross-tabulation, so Riverbend cannot tell how many employees chose both. Participation was voluntary, which permits response patterns that may differ from those of the full dispatch workforce. The survey also lacked links to desk assignment. It therefore offers no comparison between employees who used QuickTurn and employees who used the free-form handoff. The responses describe perceived benefits among respondents, but they do not measure QuickTurn’s effect on staff experience.

The trial also left two operational questions unanswered. Riverbend measured late starts and corrected assignment errors, but it did not measure driver satisfaction or overtime cost. A process that reduces late starts could still increase dispatcher workload or overtime. The present record gives no basis for deciding whether that occurred. The six-week extension would measure overtime cost and dispatcher-reported workload alongside late starts and corrected assignment errors. Those measures would let Riverbend judge whether the service benefit carries costs that a citywide rollout would multiply.

Mara Velez has requested deployment by September 1, but the record supplies no operational basis for that date. Engineering needs twelve business days of configuration after final requirements receive signatures. No one has signed those requirements, and Riverbend has no recorded signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, training estimate, or depot rollout estimate. A September 1 commitment would rest on missing schedule inputs. It could also force Riverbend to configure a citywide process before testing the proposed optional exception note or learning how the handoff affects overtime and workload.

Riverbend should begin the six-week extension at the 18 assigned QuickTurn desks, add the optional exception note, and use its available capacity to audit each QuickTurn handoff. The audit should record completion of all five required fields and use of the exception note, while the evaluation retains every assigned desk and reports any missing outcome data. Riverbend should track the four planned outcomes under the same definitions used in the trial and identify results by desk and depot where the record permits. During those six weeks, the responsible teams should sign final requirements and produce a dated configuration, training, and depot rollout plan. The citywide decision can then use audited compliance, service results, overtime cost, workload reports, and a schedule grounded in named tasks.
````

````text
Six More Weeks Before QuickTurn Goes Citywide

Riverbend should keep QuickTurn at the 18 desks assigned to it and run the proposed six-week extension before deciding on a citywide deployment. The seven-week trial produced a credible reduction in late starts, but it covered 36 dispatch desks in two depots and left operational costs, staff workload, and parts of implementation quality unresolved. Stopping now would discard a promising handoff method. Deploying citywide now would apply a local, short-term result across a larger operation before Riverbend has answered questions it already has the capacity to examine.

QuickTurn is a five-field handoff for the moment when an incoming dispatcher assumes responsibility for an unresolved route assignment. Before the trial, Riverbend grouped desks by depot and by their average late-start rate over the previous six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to QuickTurn and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Staffing rules, scheduling software, escalation policy, and shift hours stayed the same for both groups. Those choices reduced the chance that depot, prior performance, or a concurrent operating change accounted for the difference during the test.

The primary outcome was the share of scheduled routes starting more than 15 minutes late. Before assignment, that rate was 13.2 percent at desks later assigned to QuickTurn and 13.0 percent at control desks. During the trial, the respective rates were 8.4 percent and 12.8 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.6 percentage-point reduction attributable to QuickTurn, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.9 to 7.2 percentage points. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the planned analysis support that attribution for the desks, depots, and seven-week period studied.

The boundaries of that finding should govern the decision. Riverbend has evidence about late starts in two depots, not citywide performance. The trial also measured corrected assignment errors, but the record provides no result for that outcome. It explicitly did not measure driver satisfaction or overtime cost. A lower late-start rate could justify broader use, yet this record cannot say whether QuickTurn changed overtime expense or how drivers experienced the handoffs.

Completion of the five fields was uneven at four QuickTurn desks. Each of those desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs, and their late-start outcomes remained in the analysis. Keeping those outcomes avoids removing the known lower-adherence cases after assignment. The record does not state whether every other assigned desk remained in the analysis, so it does not establish complete retention across both groups. The reported estimate remains, while the missing retention information prevents a full assessment of how closely the final analysis preserved the original random assignment.

The voluntary post-trial survey adds staff impressions, though it cannot supply a treatment comparison. Among 260 dispatch employees who responded across the two depots, 118 selected faster assignment and 74 selected clearer escalation. Respondents could choose more than one answer, and the aggregate report has no respondent-level cross-tabulation. The overlap between those two counts is therefore unknown. Because responses were not linked to dispatch-desk assignment, Riverbend cannot use them to compare the views of QuickTurn staff with those of control staff.

The requested September 1 citywide date is not supported by an implementation schedule in the record. Operations director Mara Velez requested that date but gave no reason for it. Engineering estimates 12 business days of configuration after final requirements are signed. The requirements remain unsigned, and there is no signing date, work start date, sequencing plan, or estimate for training and depot rollout. The engineering estimate alone cannot establish that a September 1 launch is feasible, because it begins after an event that has not been scheduled and excludes other work for which no duration is given.

The limited extension can address several of the decision gaps without changing the desks already assigned to QuickTurn. Its plan adds an optional exception note and measures late starts, corrected assignment errors, overtime cost, and dispatcher-reported workload. These measures would add cost and workload evidence that the first trial did not provide, while continuing the two operational outcomes that trial measured. The extension record does not specify a new random assignment or a control comparison, so its results should not be treated as a fresh causal estimate unless its eventual design supplies that basis.

Riverbend can audit every QuickTurn handoff during the six weeks. The audit should record field omissions and use of the optional exception note alongside the planned outcome measures. That capacity permits review of each handoff presented for audit; by itself, it does not prove that every handoff was recorded or that every dispatcher followed the format. After the extension, Riverbend can weigh the late-start result against corrected errors, overtime cost, reported workload, and observed omissions, while treating the original causal estimate as specific to the original desks, depots, and trial period.
````
