````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care’s completed trial supports a causal conclusion within a defined setting: CallPath reduced the share of callbacks not completed within four hours at the scheduling desks, sites, and time period studied. The six-week trial ran from May 4 through June 14, 2026, at 36 desks in Toronto and Osaka.

Before assignment, desks were grouped by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the preceding six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Both groups retained the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours. This design reduced the risk that site differences, prior callback performance, or operating changes accounted for the result.

Before the trial, the late-callback rate was 13.7 percent among desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent among control desks. During the trial, it was 8.9 percent in the CallPath group and 13.1 percent in the control group. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that CallPath reduced callbacks not completed within four hours by 4.4 percentage points. Its 95 percent confidence interval ranged from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.

The random assignment, stable operating rules, and pre-registered analysis therefore support attributing the measured reduction to CallPath in this trial. CallPath’s five required fields—patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code—performed better on the primary callback outcome than the free-form handoff under the conditions tested.

## Claim Limits

The evidence does not yet justify a network-wide performance claim. The causal estimate applies to 36 desks in Toronto and Osaka over six weeks. It does not establish that the same effect would occur at other sites, under different shifts, or over a longer period.

Workflow adherence was also imperfect. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their callback outcomes remained in the analysis, so the reported estimate reflects assignment to CallPath under the implementation that actually occurred, rather than only fully compliant use. However, the record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. That uncertainty should be resolved in reporting the next test.

The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record supplies no routing-error results. No conclusion about CallPath’s effect on corrected routing errors is therefore supported here. Scheduler overtime minutes were explicitly not measured. The record also says nothing about whether scheduler-reported workload was measured. The completed trial cannot establish whether CallPath saves staff time, creates additional work, or changes perceived workload.

The voluntary questionnaire offers limited descriptive evidence. Of 52 CallPath respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six assigned staff did not respond. Because the questionnaire was voluntary, went only to CallPath staff, and had no control comparison, it cannot establish a causal effect on clarity or represent all assigned staff.

## Next Test

Meridian Care should proceed with the proposed six-week test at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. Grouping desks by site and baseline callback-completion rate, followed by random assignment within each group, will place 24 desks on CallPath and 24 on the existing workflow. Keeping staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours the same will preserve a credible comparison.

The primary outcome should remain the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is appropriately intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on callback completion during this new six-week period. Testing previously unexposed desks will provide evidence about whether the initial result extends beyond the original desks and sites represented.

Adding scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload as secondary outcomes directly addresses important gaps in the completed trial. Quality Operations can also audit every recorded CallPath status change. That capability may describe recorded activity, but it must not be presented as proof of complete workflow compliance: the record does not say the audit can identify missing status changes or establish compliance. The next test should separately document field omissions, participation in each analysis, and any missing outcome data.

## Decision

Meridian Care should continue CallPath only at the 18 trial desks while running the proposed next test. Stopping would discard a protocol that produced a credibly measured 4.4-percentage-point reduction in the primary outcome, with a 95 percent confidence interval excluding zero, under the tested conditions. Deploying across the network now would move beyond the evidence because performance at new desks, overtime effects, reported workload, and implementation consistency remain unresolved.

Limited continuation preserves the workflow where evidence currently supports it while the randomized test examines transferability and operating costs. After that test, Scheduling Operations can decide on broader deployment using callback results, secondary workforce outcomes, compliance information, and complete analysis accounting.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P8_R1_C0 -->

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P8_R1_C1 -->
````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka for six weeks, from May 4 through June 14, 2026. CallPath replaced the existing free-form handoff with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code.

Before assignment, Meridian grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the previous six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing workflow. Staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours stayed the same in both groups.

The primary outcome was the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. Before the trial, the rate was 13.7 percent at desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent at control desks. During the trial, those rates were 8.9 percent and 13.1 percent, respectively. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.4 percentage point reduction attributable to CallPath during the six-week trial. Its 95 percent confidence interval ran from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.

That is credible evidence of a causal effect within the tested setting. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered comparison support attributing the measured reduction to CallPath for these desks, these two sites, and this trial period.

## Claim Limits

The result does not establish that CallPath will produce the same reduction elsewhere in Meridian Care's network, under other operating conditions, or over a longer period. The estimate is 4.4 percentage points for the six weeks studied, with a confidence interval that leaves the size of the effect uncertain even within that setting. It should not be restated as a network-wide or permanent effect.

Implementation was also imperfect. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their callback outcomes remained in the analysis, which preserves those desks in the reported trial result. The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis, so Meridian should not claim complete retention without checking the underlying analysis.

The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record provides no result for corrected routing errors. It explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes, and the record does not say whether scheduler-reported workload was measured. The completed trial therefore cannot answer whether the protocol saves staff time, creates extra work, or shifts work beyond scheduled hours.

The voluntary questionnaire offers limited context, not a controlled comparison. It went only to staff assigned to CallPath. Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six assigned staff did not respond. Control staff received no questionnaire. Those responses describe the views of participating CallPath respondents; they do not show that CallPath improved perceived clarity relative to the existing workflow.

## Next Test

The proposed six-week test addresses the main gaps without outrunning the evidence. It will include 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. Meridian will group them by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then randomly assign 24 desks within those groups to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Both groups will keep the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

The primary outcome will again be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath's causal effect on that outcome during the six-week test. Adding scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload as secondary outcomes will show whether any callback benefit comes with an operational cost that the first trial did not measure.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change. That can verify what was recorded, but the record does not say the audit can detect missing status changes or establish compliance with the workflow. The test should report observed field completion separately and avoid treating the audit as proof of full compliance.

## Decision

Meridian Care should run the next test while continuing CallPath only at the 18 trial desks. The completed trial gives a sound reason to keep using the protocol where the measured benefit was established. It does not yet justify network-wide deployment, because the evidence covers only 36 desks in Toronto and Osaka over six weeks and leaves workload, overtime, broader transfer, and parts of implementation unresolved.

Stopping CallPath would also discard credible causal evidence of improved callback completion at the trial desks. Holding the current footprint while testing 48 new daytime desks is the proportionate choice. After six weeks, Scheduling Operations can judge whether the callback effect repeats outside the original desks and consider it alongside overtime, reported workload, and documented field completion before making a wider deployment decision.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P8_R1_C1 -->

````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka from May 4 through June 14, 2026. At 18 desks, CallPath replaced free-form handoffs with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. The other 18 desks kept the existing workflow.

Before assignment, Meridian Care grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the previous six weeks. A random draw within each group allocated desks to the two workflows. Both groups kept the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

Before the trial, callbacks missed the four-hour target at 13.7 percent of desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent of control desks. During the trial, the rates were 8.9 percent for CallPath and 13.1 percent for control. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that CallPath reduced the rate by 4.4 percentage points. The 95 percent confidence interval ran from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.

The completed trial supports a causal claim: at the participating Toronto and Osaka desks, under the operating rules used during those six weeks, CallPath reduced the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered comparison support attributing the measured reduction to CallPath.

## Claim Limits

The result does not establish CallPath’s effect across Meridian Care’s network, outside Toronto and Osaka, beyond six weeks, or under different operating conditions. The confidence interval also places the supported reduction between 1.7 and 7.1 percentage points. Meridian Care should not treat 4.4 points as a guaranteed result for each desk.

Compliance was imperfect. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their callback outcomes remained in the analysis, so the estimate includes their implementation lapses. The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. Scheduling Operations should treat that reporting gap as unresolved when judging the estimate’s robustness.

The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record supplies no routing-error result. It explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes. The record also says nothing about whether Meridian Care measured scheduler-reported workload. The callback result cannot establish labor cost or workload effects.

The voluntary questionnaire offers limited context. Of 52 CallPath respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer; six CallPath staff did not respond. Control staff received no questionnaire. These responses cannot support a comparison between workflows or represent all assigned staff.

## Next Test

Meridian Care should run the proposed six-week test at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. Meridian Care will group the desks by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then randomly assign 24 to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Both groups will keep the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

The primary outcome should remain the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison can estimate CallPath’s causal effect on callback completion during the new six-week period. Testing new desks will show whether the first result repeats beyond the original 36 desks, within the added test’s own site and daytime scope.

Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload should remain secondary outcomes. They address two gaps that matter before network deployment: whether the protocol changes paid time and how schedulers experience the workload. Reporting should identify assignments, missing observations, analysis coverage, and field completion by desk.

Quality Operations can audit each recorded CallPath status change. The audit can examine recorded activity, but the record does not establish that it can detect missing status changes or prove compliance with the workflow. The team should not use it as a complete compliance measure.

## Decision

Meridian Care should continue CallPath only at the 18 trial desks while running the next test. The completed trial gives Scheduling Operations a credible reason to preserve CallPath where Meridian Care observed the benefit. The evidence does not support network deployment because it covers 36 desks in two cities for six weeks and leaves overtime, reported workload, analysis coverage, and implementation questions unresolved.

Stopping CallPath would discard a measured callback improvement supported by random assignment. Network deployment would extend the protocol beyond the studied scope before Meridian Care answers the operational questions built into the next test. Continuing at the trial desks keeps their current practice in place while the 48-desk test checks replication and measures overtime and workload. Scheduling Operations can reconsider network deployment after reviewing callback effects, secondary outcomes, implementation records, and missing-data reporting.
````

````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka for six weeks, from May 4 through June 14, 2026. The protocol replaced free-form handoffs with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code.

Before assignment, Meridian Care grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the previous six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours stayed the same for both groups.

The primary outcome was the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. Before the trial, the rate was 13.7 percent at desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent at control desks. During the trial, the rates were 8.9 percent and 13.1 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.4 percentage-point reduction attributable to CallPath during the trial, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.

Random assignment within the groups, unchanged operating rules, and the pre-registered comparison support a causal finding for the desks, sites, and six-week period studied. The trial also measured corrected routing errors, but the record provides no result for that outcome.

## Claim Limits

The result does not establish how CallPath would perform across Meridian Care’s network. Only 36 desks in Toronto and Osaka participated, and the observed period ended after six weeks. The trial therefore supplies neither an effect estimate for other desks or sites nor evidence about performance over a longer period.

Execution was imperfect. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, and their callback outcomes remained in the analysis. The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. That gap prevents a claim of complete retention, although it does not erase the reported pre-registered estimate.

The trial explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes. The record says nothing about whether scheduler-reported workload was measured, so workload cannot be described as either measured or unmeasured. Meridian Care therefore lacks trial evidence on one stated labor measure and a documented measurement status for the other.

The staff questionnaire offers limited descriptive information. It went only to staff assigned to CallPath and was voluntary. Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer; six assigned staff did not respond. Control staff received no questionnaire. The responses report the views of participating CallPath staff, not a comparative effect on perceived clarity or workload.

## Next Test

The proposed test would add 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. Meridian Care would group them by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then randomly assign 24 within those groups to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Both groups would retain the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours for six weeks.

The primary outcome would again be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on that outcome during the test. Because the desks are new to the protocol, the estimate would extend the evidence beyond the original 18 CallPath desks, though only to the participating daytime desks, their sites, and that six-week period.

Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload would be secondary outcomes. Measuring them would address the explicit overtime omission and supply a stated workload measure for the new test. Results should be reported separately: neither secondary outcome should be treated as established by the callback result.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change during the test. That capability permits review of what the system records. It cannot, on the supplied record, identify missing status changes or establish compliance with the five-field workflow. The next analysis should also state which assigned desks and outcomes remain in each analysis, avoiding the retention uncertainty in the completed trial.

## Decision

Scheduling Operations should run the next test while keeping CallPath only at the 18 trial desks. The completed trial gives a reason not to stop: it found a 4.4 percentage-point reduction in callbacks not completed within four hours under the studied conditions, and the confidence interval excludes zero. Immediate network deployment would outrun evidence confined to two cities, 36 desks, and six weeks, while leaving labor effects and broader performance unresolved.

During the next six weeks, the trial desks can preserve the protocol already in use while the 48 new desks provide a separate randomized test. Afterward, Scheduling Operations should decide on wider deployment using the primary callback estimate, the two secondary labor outcomes, documented analysis retention, and the audit’s limited evidence about recorded status changes.
````

````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care’s completed trial supports a causal finding within a defined setting: CallPath reduced the share of callbacks not completed within four hours at the 36 Toronto and Osaka scheduling desks studied during the six weeks from May 4 through June 14, 2026.

Before assignment, desks were grouped by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the preceding six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Both groups retained the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours. These design features limited operational differences between the groups and strengthened the basis for attributing the measured change to CallPath.

Before the trial, the late-callback rate was 13.7 percent among desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent among control desks. During the trial, it was 8.9 percent in the CallPath group and 13.1 percent in the control group. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.4-percentage-point reduction attributable to CallPath, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points. The interval supports a reduction during the trial while also showing uncertainty about its precise size.

CallPath required five fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, and their callback outcomes remained in the analysis. That inclusion helps keep those desks’ actual implementation experience in the result.

## Claim Limits

The trial justifies a claim about callback completion at the participating desks, in the two participating sites, over the six-week trial period. It does not by itself establish that Meridian Care would obtain the same effect across its network, under different conditions, or over a longer period.

The record also does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. That omission prevents a stronger statement about complete retention of all assigned desks across all analyses. Although the trial measured corrected routing errors, no result for that measure is supplied, so no directional or causal claim about routing errors is justified.

The trial explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes. The record says nothing about whether scheduler-reported workload was measured. Consequently, the callback improvement cannot be treated as evidence that CallPath reduces overtime, leaves workload unchanged, or is operationally costless.

The staff questionnaire provides limited descriptive information, not a controlled comparison. It went only to staff assigned to CallPath. Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six assigned staff did not respond. Control staff received no questionnaire. The responses suggest that many participating respondents perceived clearer responsibility, but they cannot establish CallPath’s effect on staff perceptions relative to the existing workflow.

## Next Test

Meridian Care should run the proposed six-week test at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. Grouping desks by site and baseline callback-completion rate, followed by random assignment within each group, will place 24 desks on CallPath and 24 on the existing workflow. Keeping staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours the same across groups preserves the core comparison used successfully in the completed trial.

The primary outcome should remain the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on callback completion during the new six-week period. Testing nonparticipating desks will provide evidence beyond the original 36 desks, although the result will still apply directly only to the desks, operating conditions, and period studied.

Adding scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload as secondary outcomes addresses important gaps in the completed trial. These measures can show whether any callback benefit coincides with changes in staff time or perceived workload.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change. That capability can document what was recorded, but it cannot be assumed to identify missing status changes or establish overall workflow compliance. Audit findings should therefore be interpreted as evidence about recorded activity, not proof that every required action occurred.

## Decision

Meridian Care should continue CallPath only at the 18 trial desks while running the proposed next test. The completed randomized trial provides sufficient evidence to justify continued use where the measured benefit was demonstrated. It does not yet justify network-wide deployment because evidence is limited to Toronto and Osaka, six weeks, and the participating desks, while overtime and scheduler-reported workload remain unresolved.

Stopping CallPath would disregard a causally supported 4.4-percentage-point reduction in the primary outcome. Deploying it across the network now would extend the claim beyond the tested scope. Maintaining the current 18 desks and testing 48 new desks is the proportionate decision: it preserves the demonstrated use while generating evidence on new desks, callback completion, overtime, and reported workload before Meridian Care makes a broader deployment decision.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P8_R2_C0 -->

````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka from May 4 through June 14, 2026. CallPath replaced the free-form appointment handoff with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. Meridian grouped desks by site and prior average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing workflow.

Both groups followed the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours. Before the trial, callbacks not completed within four hours accounted for 13.7 percent at desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent at control desks. During the trial, the rates were 8.9 percent and 13.1 percent, respectively.

The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that CallPath reduced the rate by 4.4 percentage points during the six-week trial. The 95 percent confidence interval was 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the comparison specified in advance support attributing the reduction to CallPath for the desks, sites, and period studied.

Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their callback outcomes remained in the analysis. The estimate therefore reflects use at those desks despite those omissions.

## Claim Limits

The result does not justify network deployment by itself. The trial covered 36 desks in two cities and lasted six weeks. It does not establish the effect at other desks, other sites, or over a longer period. The record also does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. That gap warrants caution when reading the reported estimate, although the stated design supports attribution within the studied scope.

The primary result concerns callbacks not completed within four hours. The trial also measured corrected routing errors, but the record supplies no result for that measure. Meridian cannot claim that CallPath improved routing errors. The trial explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes, and the record says nothing about whether scheduler-reported workload was measured. It cannot support claims that CallPath reduced overtime or workload.

The staff questionnaire adds limited context, not a controlled comparison. It went only to staff assigned to CallPath. Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six assigned staff did not respond. Control staff received no questionnaire. Many respondents perceived clearer responsibility, but the responses do not estimate CallPath's effect on staff experience relative to the existing workflow.

The field omissions show that assignment did not guarantee complete handoffs. The next audit can examine every recorded CallPath status change, but the record does not say it can find missing status changes or establish workflow compliance. Audit coverage of recorded changes is not proof of compliance.

## Next Test

The proposed test will cover 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. Meridian will group them by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then randomly assign 24 to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. For six weeks, both groups will use the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

The primary outcome will again be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath's causal effect during the test. Repeating the result at new desks would provide stronger grounds for deciding whether the first finding applies beyond the original desks.

Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload will be secondary outcomes. These measures can show whether faster callback completion requires more staff time or feels harder to sustain. Quality Operations should use its audit to describe recorded status changes and how CallPath was used, not as a complete compliance measure.

## Decision

Meridian should run the next test and continue CallPath only at the 18 trial desks meanwhile. Stopping now would discard a causally supported 4.4 percentage point reduction at those desks, with a 95 percent confidence interval of 1.7 to 7.1 points. Network deployment now would exceed the evidence because performance elsewhere and over longer periods, overtime, and reported workload remain unresolved.

Keeping CallPath at the 18 desks preserves a use supported by the trial while the new randomized test examines new desks and staff effects. After six weeks, Scheduling Operations can weigh callback performance with overtime and reported workload, then decide whether broader deployment is justified.
````

````text
What CallPath Justifies Now

Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka from May 4 through June 14, 2026. At 18 desks, CallPath replaced the existing free-form handoff with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. The other 18 desks kept the existing workflow.

Before assignment, the trial team grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the previous six weeks. A random draw within each group determined which desks used CallPath. Both groups retained the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

Before the trial, callbacks remained incomplete after four hours at a rate of 13.7 percent among desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent among control desks. During the trial, the rates were 8.9 percent for CallPath and 13.1 percent for the control workflow. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.4 percentage-point reduction attributable to CallPath during the six-week trial. Its 95 percent confidence interval ran from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.

The trial team measured corrected routing errors, but the record supplies no result for that measure. Scheduling Operations therefore cannot claim that CallPath reduced those errors.

Claim Limits

The causal finding applies to the 36 desks, two sites, and six-week period studied. It does not establish that CallPath will produce the same reduction at other desks, across the network, during other hours, or over a longer period. Network deployment now would extend beyond the evidence.

Implementation varied. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their callback outcomes remained in the analysis, so the estimated effect did not discard those desks for poor field completion. The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. Scheduling Operations should not describe the result as a fully documented analysis of all assigned desks without resolving that gap.

The trial did not measure scheduler overtime minutes. The record says nothing about whether the team measured scheduler-reported workload. Any claim that CallPath saves labor, avoids overtime, or feels easier to use would exceed the record.

The voluntary questionnaire offers limited information about staff experience. Staff assigned to CallPath received it; control staff did not. Among 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six CallPath staff did not respond. That response supports the narrower statement that 37 respondents perceived clearer responsibility. Without control responses and full participation, it cannot establish a comparative effect on clarity, workload, or acceptance.

Next Test

Meridian Care should run the proposed six-week test at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not join the completed trial. The team will group desks by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then use a random draw within each group to assign 24 desks to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Both groups will follow the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

The primary outcome should remain the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison can estimate CallPath’s causal effect on callback completion during this six-week test. Testing new desks will show whether the completed trial’s result repeats beyond the original desks, though the daytime scope will still limit claims about other operating periods.

Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload should remain secondary outcomes. They address important questions the completed trial left unanswered without displacing the pre-specified callback outcome.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change. The team should use that audit to examine recorded activity, while stating its limit: it cannot identify status changes that staff never recorded or establish compliance with the full workflow. Field-level checks should track omissions across all five required fields, since four desks showed material omission rates in the first trial.

Decision

Scheduling Operations should continue CallPath only at the 18 trial desks while running the next test. The completed trial supports continued use there: random assignment, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered analysis attribute a measured 4.4 percentage-point reduction to CallPath within the studied scope. Stopping would discard a supported improvement at those desks.

Meridian Care should not deploy CallPath across the network now. One six-week trial at Toronto and Osaka does not answer whether the effect transfers to new desks, affects overtime, or changes reported workload. The proposed test addresses those gaps while preserving a concurrent control and stable operating conditions.

After the next test, Scheduling Operations should base any broader deployment decision on the callback estimate, its uncertainty, the secondary workload and overtime results, and documented field completion. Until then, limited continuation protects the demonstrated benefit without turning a bounded finding into a network-wide claim.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P8_R2_C3 -->
````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka for six weeks, from May 4 through June 14, 2026. CallPath replaced the existing free-form appointment handoff with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. Before assignment, the desks were grouped by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the previous six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing workflow.

Both groups kept the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours. The primary outcome was the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. Before the trial, the rate was 13.7 percent among desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent among control desks. During the trial, those rates were 8.9 percent and 13.1 percent, respectively. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that CallPath reduced the rate by 4.4 percentage points, with a 95 percent confidence interval of 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.

Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered comparison support attributing that measured reduction to CallPath for the desks, sites, and six-week period studied. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, and their callback outcomes remained in the analysis. The trial also measured corrected routing errors. A voluntary questionnaire went only to CallPath staff: 37 of 52 respondents said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six assigned staff did not respond.

## Claim Limits

The result applies to 18 assigned desks in Toronto and Osaka during the completed trial, under the operating rules held constant there. It does not establish what CallPath would do across Meridian Care’s network.

The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. Including callback outcomes from the four desks with higher field omission does not settle the participation status of all other desks.

The completed trial cannot support a claim about scheduler overtime because it explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes. The record says nothing about whether scheduler-reported workload was measured, so no conclusion is available on that outcome.

The questionnaire supplies a view from respondents assigned to CallPath, not a comparison with control staff. Its voluntary design, six nonrespondents, and absence of a control questionnaire prevent treating the 37 responses as evidence of a causal change in responsibility clarity across staff.

The field omissions also limit any claim that CallPath was followed consistently. The observed callback effect is an effect of assignment to CallPath under the trial conditions, including the recorded omissions. It is not an estimate of perfect execution, and the record does not establish compliance across all CallPath handoffs.

## Next Test

The proposed six-week test addresses the network-deployment question with a new sample of 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the first trial. The desks will be grouped by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then randomly assigned within each group: 24 to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours will remain the same in both groups. The primary outcome will again be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours, and the planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on that outcome during the test.

Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload will be secondary outcomes. These measures will add operating information that the completed record cannot supply, although the proposed design names callback completion as the primary outcome. Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change. That audit can examine recorded changes, but the record does not say it can find missing status changes or establish compliance with the workflow.

## Decision

Meridian Care should run the next test while continuing CallPath only at the 18 trial desks. The completed trial gives a causal estimate of a lower rate of callbacks not completed within four hours within its stated desk, site, and time boundaries. That supports retaining CallPath where it is already in use. Deployment across the network would extend the result beyond the studied setting before new daytime desks are tested and before overtime minutes and reported workload are measured.

Stopping CallPath would discard a protocol that reduced the primary outcome in the completed trial. Keeping use confined to the trial desks avoids a network-wide commitment while the new random assignment produces evidence from nonparticipating desks. After the six-week test, Scheduling Operations should assess the callback estimate together with the secondary overtime and workload results. Quality Operations should report what its audit finds in the recorded status changes without treating the audit as proof that no changes are missing or that staff complied with the workflow.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 1:P8_R3_C0 -->
````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care’s completed trial supports a causal claim about CallPath’s effect on callback completion within the studied setting. CallPath—the five-field handoff requiring patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code—was tested for six weeks, from May 4 through June 14, 2026, at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka. Before assignment, desks were grouped by site and their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the preceding six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing free-form handoff.

The groups operated under the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours. Before the trial, callbacks not completed within four hours represented 13.7 percent of callbacks at desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent at control desks. During the trial, the corresponding rates were 8.9 percent and 13.1 percent. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that CallPath reduced the rate by 4.4 percentage points, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.

Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered comparison support attributing that measured reduction to CallPath for the desks, sites, and six-week period studied. This is meaningful evidence that the structured handoff improved the primary callback outcome under those conditions.

## Claim Limits

The result does not justify treating CallPath as proven across Meridian Care’s network. The causal finding is bounded by the trial’s 36 desks, Toronto and Osaka sites, and May 4 through June 14 operating period. It does not establish the effect at other desks, sites, operating periods, or under different operating rules. The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record supplies no result for corrected routing errors. No claim about improvement in that outcome is therefore supported.

Implementation was also imperfect. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, and their callback outcomes remained in the analysis. That preserves evidence about the effect of assignment under actual trial implementation, but it does not show what the effect would be under complete compliance. The record also does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis, leaving an unresolved reporting limitation.

The completed trial cannot answer whether CallPath changes labor burden. Scheduler overtime minutes were explicitly not measured, and the record does not say whether scheduler-reported workload was measured. The voluntary questionnaire provides limited descriptive evidence only: 37 of 52 CallPath respondents said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six assigned staff did not respond. Because control staff received no questionnaire, the responses cannot support a comparative or causal claim about clarity or workload.

## Next Test

The proposed next test is appropriately designed to extend the evidence without assuming network-wide effectiveness. It will include 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. Desks will be grouped by site and baseline callback-completion rate before a random draw within each group assigns 24 to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Both groups will retain the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

For six weeks, the primary outcome will again be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on that outcome during the test. Adding scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload as secondary outcomes will address important questions left unanswered by the first trial.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change. That capability may help describe recorded activity, but it should not be presented as proof of workflow compliance: the record does not say the audit can identify missing status changes or establish compliance. Reporting should separately document field completion, participation in each analysis, callback results, overtime, and reported workload.

## Decision

Meridian Care should run the next test while continuing CallPath only at the 18 original trial desks. Stopping would disregard a randomized, pre-registered result showing a 4.4-percentage-point reduction in callbacks not completed within four hours during the completed trial. Deploying across the network now would move beyond the demonstrated scope before effects at new desks and effects on overtime and workload are known.

Keeping CallPath at the trial desks preserves the workflow where the evidence directly applies while the additional randomized test evaluates new daytime desks. After that test, Scheduling Operations should base any broader deployment decision on the callback estimate together with the secondary labor outcomes, documented analysis participation, and observed implementation quality.
````

````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care has credible evidence that CallPath improved one defined outcome under the conditions tested. The six-week trial ran from May 4 through June 14, 2026, at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka. Before assignment, Meridian grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the previous six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing free-form handoff.

CallPath replaced that handoff with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. Staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours stayed the same in both groups. This matters because the design held those operating conditions steady while changing the handoff protocol.

The primary outcome was the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. Before the trial, the rate was 13.7 percent at desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent at control desks. During the trial, it fell to 8.9 percent in the CallPath group and was 13.1 percent in the control group. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.4 percentage point reduction attributable to CallPath, with a 95 percent confidence interval of 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.

For these desks, these two sites, and this six-week period, the result supports a causal claim. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered comparison make CallPath, rather than a known difference in operating conditions, the supported explanation for the measured reduction.

## Claim Limits

That finding is narrower than a case for network deployment. It does not establish the same effect at other desks, sites, times, or operating settings. The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record supplies no result for corrected routing errors. It explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes. The record also does not say whether scheduler-reported workload was measured. We therefore cannot claim that CallPath reduced routing errors, saved overtime, or made work easier.

The staff questionnaire adds context, not a controlled comparison. It went only to staff assigned to CallPath. Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six assigned staff did not respond. Control staff received no questionnaire. Those answers show that many respondents perceived clearer responsibility, but they cannot establish CallPath's effect on staff experience relative to the existing workflow.

Compliance also needs a careful reading. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, and their callback outcomes remained in the analysis. That preserves those desks in the reported result and shows that the measured effect occurred despite documented omissions. Yet the record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. It also does not tell us how the protocol would perform under network-wide implementation.

## Next Test

The proposed test addresses part of that uncertainty without giving up a design capable of supporting causal inference. It will run for six weeks at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not join the completed trial. Meridian will group them by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then randomly assign 24 to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Both groups will keep the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

The primary outcome will again be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath's causal effect on that outcome during the test. Adding scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload as secondary outcomes will show whether the callback result comes with operational or staff costs that the first trial could not assess.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change. That can check what was recorded, but the record does not say the audit can find missing status changes or establish workflow compliance. The test should report field omissions, analysis inclusion, callback results, overtime, and workload without treating the audit as proof of full adherence.

## Decision

Meridian should run the next test while continuing CallPath only at the 18 trial desks. Stopping now would discard a protocol with credible, positive evidence at those desks. Deploying across the network now would reach beyond the measured sites, period, outcome, and staff evidence.

Keeping CallPath at the trial desks preserves the use already supported while the new randomized test checks whether the callback benefit appears at 48 previously untested daytime desks. It also gathers the overtime and workload evidence needed for a sounder operating decision. After that test, Meridian can reconsider broader deployment using results that cover both callback performance and the costs of achieving it.
````

````text
What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka for six weeks, from May 4 through June 14, 2026. CallPath replaced the existing free-form handoff at 18 desks with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. The other 18 desks kept the existing workflow.

The design gives Scheduling Operations a sound basis for judging the result within the trial. Before assignment, Meridian grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the prior six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned desks to the two workflows. Both groups kept the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours. Meridian pre-registered the comparison of changes.

Before the trial, callbacks not completed within four hours accounted for 13.7 percent at desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent at control desks. During the trial, the rates were 8.9 percent for CallPath and 13.1 percent for control. The pre-registered comparison estimated a 4.4 percentage-point reduction attributable to CallPath during the six-week trial. Its 95 percent confidence interval ran from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the planned analysis support a causal claim for the desks, sites, and period studied.

## Claim Limits

The completed trial supports a measured reduction in delayed callbacks under the conditions Meridian tested. It does not establish that CallPath will produce the same result at other desks, sites, shifts, or times. A network deployment would reach beyond the evidence.

Compliance needs closer examination. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their callback outcomes remained in the analysis, so the reported estimate does not exclude those desks. The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. Scheduling Operations should resolve that reporting gap before treating the estimate as a complete account of all assigned desks.

The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record supplies no routing-error result. Meridian did not measure scheduler overtime minutes, and the record says nothing about whether Meridian measured staff-reported workload. Meridian cannot claim that CallPath saves labor, avoids overtime, or leaves workload unchanged.

The staff questionnaire offers narrow context. It went only to staff assigned to CallPath, and 37 of 52 respondents said the named owner made responsibility clearer. Six assigned staff did not respond, and control staff received no questionnaire. Those answers describe respondents’ views; they do not estimate CallPath’s effect on staff experience against the existing workflow.

## Next Test

Meridian should run the proposed six-week test at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not join the first trial. Meridian will group desks by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then use a random draw within each group to assign 24 to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. This design extends the evidence while preserving a credible comparison. Both groups will keep the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

The primary outcome should remain the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison can estimate CallPath’s causal effect on that outcome during the new six-week test. Adding scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload as secondary outcomes will address two operational questions the completed trial cannot answer.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change. The team should describe that audit with care: the record does not show that it can find missing status changes or establish workflow compliance. Any report should separate what reviewers observed in recorded changes from claims about complete use of all five required fields.

## Decision

Scheduling Operations should continue CallPath only at the 18 trial desks while running the next test. Stopping would discard a protocol that produced a causal, measured reduction in delayed callbacks at those desks during the trial. Network deployment now would assume that the result transfers beyond Toronto and Osaka, beyond the tested desks and period, and without evidence on overtime or reported workload.

Keeping CallPath at the trial desks preserves the demonstrated local benefit while Meridian tests replication at 48 new daytime desks. After six weeks, Scheduling Operations can weigh callback completion alongside overtime, workload, analysis coverage, and the audit’s bounded findings. That evidence will support a better network decision than either immediate expansion or withdrawal.
````

````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka for six weeks, from May 4 through June 14, 2026. The protocol replaced free-form appointment handoffs with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code.

Before assignment, Meridian Care grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the preceding six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing free-form handoff. Staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours remained the same for both groups.

The primary outcome was the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. Before the trial, the rate was 13.7 percent at desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent at control desks. During the trial, the rates were 8.9 percent and 13.1 percent, respectively. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated that CallPath reduced the rate by 4.4 percentage points during the trial. Its 95 percent confidence interval was 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points.

Random assignment within the groups, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered comparison support attributing the measured reduction to CallPath for the desks, sites, and six-week period studied. Meridian Care can treat the reduction in late callbacks as evidence that CallPath improved the primary outcome under those trial conditions.

## Claim Limits

The completed trial does not establish what CallPath would do across Meridian Care’s network. It covered 36 desks in two cities for six weeks and supplies no effect estimate for other desks, sites, or periods.

Implementation was imperfect. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, and their callback outcomes remained in the analysis. The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. The reported estimate therefore includes those four desks, but it does not establish full workflow compliance or complete retention across all analyses.

The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record supplies no corrected-routing result. It explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes. Whether scheduler-reported workload was measured is unknown; the record cannot support a claim that workload was unmeasured or unchanged.

The voluntary questionnaire went only to CallPath staff. Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six CallPath staff did not respond. Control staff received no questionnaire. These responses cannot estimate CallPath’s comparative effect on whether the named owner made responsibility clearer.

## Next Test

The proposed test would run for six weeks at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. Meridian Care would group them by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then randomly assign 24 desks within those groups to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours would remain the same in both groups.

The primary outcome would again be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on callback completion during this six-week test. New participating desks would broaden the evidence while keeping any resulting claim tied to those desks, conditions, and period.

Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload would be secondary outcomes. The new measurements would address an explicit gap for overtime and the absence of information about whether workload was measured in the completed trial. They should be reported separately from the primary callback result.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change during the test. This permits review of recorded changes. It cannot identify missing status changes or establish workflow compliance on the supplied record. Any claim about field completion or compliance will need evidence beyond that audit.

## Decision

Scheduling Operations should run the proposed test and keep CallPath at the 18 original trial desks while it proceeds. Outside those desks, new CallPath use should occur only at the 24 desks assigned to it within the randomized test.

Stopping would set aside a protocol that produced a measured reduction in the primary outcome under credible trial conditions. Network deployment would exceed evidence limited to two sites, 36 desks, and six weeks, without overtime results or a known workload finding. The next test retains the existing use, examines 48 new daytime desks, and adds operational measures for a later network decision. After six weeks, Scheduling Operations should review the primary callback result, both secondary outcomes, and separately supported evidence on field completion before reconsidering broader deployment.
````

````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care’s completed trial supports a causal claim that CallPath reduced the share of callbacks not completed within four hours at the scheduling desks, sites, and time studied. The six-week trial ran from May 4 through June 14, 2026, at 36 desks in Toronto and Osaka. Before assignment, desks were grouped by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the preceding six weeks. Within each group, a random draw assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing free-form handoff.

CallPath required five fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. Staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours remained the same in both groups. Those stable conditions, together with random assignment and the pre-registered comparison, reduce the likelihood that operating differences explain the result.

Before the trial, callbacks not completed within four hours represented 13.7 percent of callbacks at desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent at control desks. During the trial, the rate was 8.9 percent in the CallPath group and 13.1 percent in the control group. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.4-percentage-point reduction attributable to CallPath during the trial. Its 95 percent confidence interval was 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points. The interval does not include zero and indicates that the measured effect could plausibly be smaller or larger than the point estimate.

Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their callback outcomes remained in the analysis, so the reported estimate did not exclude those known cases of incomplete field use.

## Claim Limits

The finding should remain bounded to callback performance during this six-week trial at the participating Toronto and Osaka desks. It does not establish that CallPath will produce the same result across Meridian Care’s network, at untested sites, or under different operating conditions. The record also does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis, leaving an unresolved reporting question beyond the four desks explicitly retained.

The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record provides no routing-error result. It therefore supports no claim that CallPath improved that outcome. Scheduler overtime minutes were explicitly not measured. The record is silent on whether scheduler-reported workload was measured. Consequently, the callback improvement cannot be treated as evidence that CallPath reduced overtime, lowered workload, or avoided increasing either one.

The staff questionnaire offers limited descriptive context, not a controlled comparison. Among 52 CallPath respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six assigned staff did not respond. Because participation was voluntary and control staff received no questionnaire, these responses cannot establish CallPath’s causal effect on perceived clarity or compare staff experience between workflows.

## Next Test

Meridian Care should run the proposed six-week test at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. Grouping desks by site and baseline callback-completion rate, followed by random assignment within each group, will place 24 desks on CallPath and 24 on the existing workflow. Keeping staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours the same across groups will preserve a clear comparison.

The primary outcome should remain the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is designed to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on that outcome during the new six-week period. Testing previously unexposed desks will also show whether the completed trial’s callback result repeats in a broader set of daytime operations.

Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload should be retained as secondary outcomes. They address important uncertainties left by the first trial and can reveal whether improved callback completion is accompanied by operational or staff costs.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change. That capability can assess the recorded changes, but it should not be described as proof of full workflow compliance: the record does not say the audit can identify missing status changes or establish compliance. Reporting should separately disclose field omissions, participation in each analysis, and any departures from assigned workflows.

## Decision

Meridian Care should continue CallPath only at the 18 trial desks while running the next test. Stopping would disregard a causally supported reduction in late callbacks in the completed trial. Network-wide deployment, however, would extend the evidence beyond the desks, sites, period, and measured outcomes that support it.

Maintaining CallPath at the trial desks preserves the protocol where a benefit was demonstrated, while the new randomized test evaluates replication at previously untested desks and adds overtime and workload evidence. After that test, Scheduling Operations can reconsider broader deployment using callback results, secondary outcomes, compliance information, and complete analysis-participation reporting.
````

````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care has good evidence that CallPath improved four-hour callback completion at the trial desks. The six-week test ran from May 4 through June 14, 2026, at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka. Before assignment, Meridian Care grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours over the prior six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned 18 desks to CallPath and 18 to the existing free-form handoff.

CallPath replaced that free-form handoff with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. Staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours stayed the same in both groups.

Before the trial, callbacks not completed within four hours accounted for 13.7 percent at desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent at control desks. During the trial, the rates were 8.9 percent and 13.1 percent, respectively. The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.4 percentage-point reduction attributable to CallPath. Its 95 percent confidence interval ran from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points. Because assignment was random, operating rules were stable, and the comparison was specified in advance, the result supports a causal claim for the participating desks, the two sites, and the six-week trial period.

The analysis also retained callback outcomes from four CallPath desks that omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of handoffs. The measured result therefore does not depend on removing those four desks for poor adherence.

## Claim Limits

The trial does not justify a network-wide effectiveness claim. It covered 36 desks in Toronto and Osaka for six weeks, not the rest of Meridian Care's sites, shifts, or operating conditions. The estimate should remain attached to that population and period until a new test supplies evidence elsewhere.

There is also an analysis-reporting gap. The record confirms that the four higher-omission CallPath desks stayed in the callback analysis, but it does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. That omission does not erase the reported result, though it prevents a broader claim about complete retention across all analyses.

The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record supplies an estimate only for callbacks not completed within four hours. It gives no corrected-routing-error result, so no direction or size of effect can be claimed for that measure. Scheduler overtime minutes were explicitly not measured. The record does not say whether scheduler-reported workload was measured. Claims that CallPath saves labor, reduces overtime, or makes the job easier would go beyond the evidence.

The questionnaire is narrower still. Of 52 CallPath respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer, while six assigned staff did not respond. Control staff received no questionnaire. This is useful feedback about the respondents' experience, but it is neither a comparison with the existing workflow nor proof of an effect among all assigned staff.

## Next Test

Meridian Care should run the proposed six-week test at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not join the first trial. Grouping desks by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then randomly assigning 24 to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow, carries the original design into a new setting. Keeping staffing rules, the scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours equal between groups will again help isolate CallPath's effect.

The primary outcome should remain the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is suited to estimating CallPath's causal effect on that outcome during the new six-week period. Adding scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload as secondary outcomes addresses two practical questions the completed trial could not answer.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change. That can document what appears in the system, but it cannot be described as a full compliance check. The record does not establish that the audit can detect status changes that were never recorded or prove that staff followed the workflow. Results should therefore report observed field completion and audit findings without treating either as complete evidence of adherence.

## Decision

Continue CallPath only at the 18 trial desks while running the next test. Stopping would discard a credible, measured improvement where CallPath has already been studied. A network-wide rollout would outrun the evidence, which is limited to two sites, 36 desks, and six weeks and says nothing conclusive about overtime or reported workload.

Holding use at the trial desks preserves the current, evidence-backed scope while the 48-desk test examines whether the callback benefit carries to new daytime desks and what it costs staff. Meridian Care should reconsider broader deployment after reviewing the new primary result, the two secondary outcomes, retention in each analysis, and the documented limits of the status-change audit.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 1:P8_R4_C1 -->

````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka from May 4 through June 14, 2026. The five-field protocol required each appointment handoff to name the patient request, owner, next action, deadline, and status code. Before assignment, the trial team grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the preceding six weeks. A random draw within each group put 18 desks on CallPath and left 18 with the existing free-form handoff.

The two groups followed the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours. Their baseline rates sat close together: 13.7 percent for desks assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent for control desks. During the trial, the CallPath rate fell to 8.9 percent, while the control rate reached 13.1 percent.

The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.4 percentage-point reduction in callbacks not completed within four hours. Its 95 percent confidence interval ran from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points. Random assignment within the site and baseline groups, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered analysis support attributing that measured six-week reduction to CallPath at the participating Toronto and Osaka desks.

## Claim Limits

Meridian Care should keep the claim tied to the outcome, desks, sites, and dates in the trial. The study supports a causal claim about the share of callbacks not completed within four hours during those six weeks. It does not establish the same effect at other desks, in other sites, outside the trial period, or under different operating conditions. The record gives no basis for translating 4.4 percentage points into a permanent network-wide effect.

Compliance varied. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs. Their callback outcomes remained in the analysis, so the reported estimate did not discard those four desks for that reason. The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis. Scheduling Operations should not describe the result as an analysis of all 36 assigned desks without resolving that gap.

The trial measured callback completion and corrected routing errors, but the record supplies no routing-error result. It measured no scheduler overtime minutes. The record gives no answer on whether the trial measured scheduler-reported workload. Those omissions prevent a claim that CallPath saves labor, avoids overtime, or leaves workload unchanged.

The staff questionnaire offers narrow context. Among 52 CallPath respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer; six assigned staff did not respond. Control staff received no questionnaire. The responses describe the views of respondents exposed to CallPath. They do not measure a comparative effect on clarity, workload, or staff acceptance.

## Next Test

Meridian Care should run the proposed six-week test at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not join the completed trial. The team will group desks by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then use a random draw within each group to assign 24 desks to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Both groups will retain the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

The primary outcome will remain the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. That continuity lets the team test whether CallPath produces a causal effect on callback completion in a new set of desks during another six-week period. Adding scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload as secondary outcomes will address two operational questions the first trial left unanswered.

Quality Operations can audit each recorded CallPath status change. The team should use that audit to examine the records it can see while avoiding a broader compliance claim. The record does not say the audit can detect missing status changes or prove adherence to the workflow. The next test should document whether every assigned desk enters every analysis and report omissions by field and desk.

## Decision

Scheduling Operations should continue CallPath at the 18 trial desks while running the next test. The completed trial supplies credible evidence of a reduction in late callbacks at those desks, and Meridian Care has kept the protocol in place there. Stopping now would abandon a protocol that improved the primary outcome under the studied conditions. Network-wide deployment would outrun the evidence because the completed trial covered two sites, 36 desks, and six weeks, with unresolved questions about workload, overtime, compliance, analysis coverage, and performance at new desks.

This choice keeps use inside the population with direct evidence while Meridian Care tests transfer to 48 nonparticipating daytime desks. After the next six weeks, Scheduling Operations can weigh the new callback estimate alongside overtime, reported workload, analysis coverage, and the limits of the status-change audit. Until those results arrive, the record justifies continued use at the 18 trial desks and no broader rollout.
````
