````text
# What CallPath Justifies Now

## Completed Trial

Meridian Care tested CallPath at 36 scheduling desks in Toronto and Osaka for six weeks, from May 4 through June 14, 2026. CallPath replaced the existing free-form handoff at 18 desks with five required fields: patient request, named owner, next action, deadline, and status code. The other 18 desks retained the existing workflow. Both groups kept the same staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours.

Before assignment, Meridian Care grouped desks by site and by their average rate of callbacks not completed within four hours during the previous six weeks. A random draw within each group assigned desks to CallPath or control. The primary outcome was the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. Before the trial, the rate was 13.7 percent among desks later assigned to CallPath and 13.5 percent among control desks. During the trial, the rates were 8.9 percent and 13.1 percent, respectively.

The pre-registered comparison of changes estimated a 4.4 percentage-point reduction attributable to CallPath during the trial. Its 95 percent confidence interval ran from 1.7 to 7.1 percentage points. Random assignment, stable operating rules, and the pre-registered comparison support a causal attribution for the desks, sites, and six-week period studied.

The trial also measured corrected routing errors, but the record provides no result for that outcome. Four CallPath desks omitted at least one required field in more than 12 percent of their handoffs, and their callback outcomes remained in the analysis. The record does not say whether every other assigned desk remained in every analysis.

## Claim Limits

The completed trial supports continued use at the 18 CallPath desks while Meridian Care gathers evidence from new desks. It does not supply a measured effect outside Toronto and Osaka, outside the participating desks, or beyond the six-week period. Those boundaries rule out a network-wide claim based on this trial alone.

The trial explicitly did not measure scheduler overtime minutes. The record says nothing about whether scheduler-reported workload was measured, so workload cannot be classified as either measured or unmeasured in the completed trial.

A voluntary questionnaire went only to staff assigned to CallPath. Of 52 respondents, 37 said the named owner made responsibility clearer; six CallPath staff did not respond. Because control staff received no questionnaire and participation was incomplete, these responses describe respondents’ views rather than a comparative effect on staff experience.

The four desks with field omissions remained in the callback analysis, so their observed outcomes contributed to the reported estimate. Retention of every other assigned desk across every analysis is unspecified. The assignment result does not establish that every handoff contained all five fields.

## Next Test

The proposed test should proceed at 48 daytime scheduling desks that did not participate in the completed trial. It will run for six weeks. Desks will first be grouped by site and baseline callback-completion rate, then randomly assigned within each group: 24 to CallPath and 24 to the existing workflow. Staffing rules, scheduling system, escalation policy, and operating hours will remain the same across both groups.

The primary outcome will again be the share of callbacks not completed within four hours. The planned comparison is intended to estimate CallPath’s causal effect on callback completion during this new six-week test. Scheduler overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload will be secondary outcomes. These measures add operating information that the current decision lacks, while the new set of desks tests whether the callback result appears under the stated conditions beyond the original participants.

Quality Operations can audit every recorded CallPath status change during the next test. That audit can examine recorded changes, but the record does not say it can find missing status changes or establish compliance with the workflow. The test should therefore report field omissions separately and avoid treating the audit as a complete compliance check.

## Decision

Meridian Care should run the next test while continuing CallPath only at the 18 trial desks. Stopping would discard a protocol that produced a pre-registered causal estimate of a 4.4 percentage-point reduction at those desks during the trial. Network-wide deployment would extend that finding beyond the sites, desks, and period studied before Meridian Care has comparative evidence on overtime or scheduler-reported workload.

During the next six weeks, the trial desks should keep CallPath under their current operating rules. The 48 new desks should follow the proposed random assignment, with callback completion as the primary outcome and overtime minutes and scheduler-reported workload as secondary outcomes. After that test, Scheduling Operations can base the next deployment decision on the new callback estimate, the two staff outcomes, and reported field omissions within the new test’s scope.
````

````text
After three customers separately asked whether we could lower the music while they browsed, I decided to test a weekly quiet hour at our Bristol bookshop. For eight Wednesdays in May and June, we switched off the music from 5 p.m. to 6 p.m., while staying open as usual: staff answered questions and used the till.

We recorded 143 purchases during those hours, compared with 129 in the same hour on the previous eight Wednesdays. That doesn’t show the quiet hour caused an increase: the periods had different book releases and weather, and we didn’t count visitors who left without buying.

Of 14 written comments, 11 wanted the quiet hour to stay, two preferred music, and one suggested a quiet morning. One colleague found it restful; another found closing tasks awkward in silence.

I’ll keep the Wednesday quiet hour through September, then ask customers and staff again before deciding whether it continues.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P1_R1_C0 -->

````text
After three customers separately asked if we could lower the music while they browsed, I decided to try a weekly quiet hour. For eight Wednesdays in May and June, we switched the music off from 5 to 6 p.m. The shop stayed open as normal: staff answered questions and used the till.

We recorded 143 purchases during those eight hours, compared with 129 in the same hour on the previous eight Wednesdays. I’m wary of reading too much into that. The weather and book releases differed, and we didn’t count visitors who left without buying.

Fourteen people left notes. Eleven wanted the quiet hour to stay, two preferred music, and one suggested a quiet morning. One colleague found it restful; another said the silence made closing jobs feel awkward.

For now, I’m keeping the quiet hour on Wednesdays through September. Then I’ll ask customers and staff again before deciding whether it stays.
````

````text
In May, after three customers each asked on a different visit if we could lower the music while they browsed, I decided to try a quiet hour. For eight Wednesdays in May and June, we switched the music off from 5 to 6 p.m. We stayed open, answered questions and used the till as usual.

During those eight hours, we recorded 143 purchases, compared with 129 in the same hour on the previous eight Wednesdays. I can’t attribute the difference to quiet: the weather and book releases differed, and we didn’t count visitors who left without buying.

Fourteen customers left written comments. Eleven wanted us to continue, two preferred music, and one suggested a quiet morning. One member of staff found the hour restful; another felt the silence made closing tasks awkward.

I’ve decided to keep our Wednesday quiet hour through September. Before I decide what happens after that, I’ll ask customers and staff again.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P2_R1_C0 -->
````text
Subject: Freezer B Temperature Incident and Required Actions

Dear Freezer B Researchers,

At 6:42 a.m. on Monday, July 13, Freezer B triggered a temperature alarm after its temperature rose from −80°C to −61°C. The backup generator was operating. At 7:25 a.m., Facilities identified a failed door seal.

Between 7:40 a.m. and 8:18 a.m., staff moved all 86 labeled sample boxes from Freezer B to Freezers D and E. No box was discarded. The shared inventory has been updated to show the destination freezer and shelf for every moved box.

Please check the shared inventory by noon on Tuesday, July 14. Confirm that your boxes are listed and correctly placed in the specified destination freezer and shelf. Report any missing or incorrectly placed box directly to me by that deadline.

Temperature probes show that the air inside Freezer B remained above −70°C for 54 minutes. However, the probes do not provide the temperature inside individual sample tubes. Under the lab’s existing protocol, each principal investigator must determine whether this exposure affects the planned use of their samples. Principal investigators must record their sample-use decision in the incident sheet by 5:00 p.m. on Thursday, July 16.

Facilities expects the replacement door seal to arrive Wednesday morning, but the delivery time has not been confirmed. Freezer B must remain empty until the repair is complete and the unit has maintained −80°C for 12 continuous hours.

Do not return any boxes or other materials to Freezer B until further notice. I will send another email when the freezer has met the required temperature period and is cleared for use.

Regards,

Priya Shah  
Laboratory Manager  
Westbridge Materials Lab
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P2_R1_C0 -->

</details>

````text
Subject: Freezer B temperature incident and required actions

Dear Freezer B researchers,

At 6:42 a.m. on Monday, July 13, Freezer B triggered a temperature alarm after its temperature rose from minus 80°C to minus 61°C. The backup generator was working. Facilities inspected the freezer and found a failed door seal at 7:25 a.m.

Staff moved all 86 labeled sample boxes from Freezer B to Freezers D and E between 7:40 a.m. and 8:18 a.m. The shared inventory now lists the destination freezer and shelf for every moved box. No box was discarded.

Temperature probes show that the air inside Freezer B remained above minus 70°C for 54 minutes. The probes do not measure the temperature inside individual sample tubes, so those tube temperatures remain unknown. Under our existing protocol, each principal investigator must decide whether this exposure affects the planned use of their samples.

Please complete the following actions:

1. Check the shared inventory by noon on Tuesday, July 14. Confirm that each of your boxes is listed in the correct freezer and shelf location. Report any missing or incorrectly placed box directly to me.
2. Principal investigators must record their sample-use decision in the incident sheet by 5 p.m. on Thursday, July 16.

Facilities expects the replacement seal to arrive Wednesday morning, but the delivery time has not been confirmed. Freezer B must remain empty after the repair until it has held minus 80°C for 12 continuous hours. Do not return any boxes or other materials to it before clearance.

I will send another email when Freezer B is cleared for use.

Priya Shah  
Laboratory Manager  
Westbridge Materials Lab
````

````text
Subject: Required actions following Freezer B temperature alarm

Dear Freezer B researchers,

At 6:42 a.m. on Monday, July 13, Freezer B triggered a temperature alarm after its temperature rose from −80°C to −61°C. The backup generator was working. Facilities inspected the unit and found a failed door seal at 7:25 a.m.

Between 7:40 a.m. and 8:18 a.m., laboratory staff moved all 86 labeled sample boxes from Freezer B to Freezers D and E. Staff discarded no boxes. The shared inventory lists the destination freezer and shelf for each box.

Please check the shared inventory by noon on Tuesday, July 14. Confirm that the location recorded for each of your boxes is correct, then check the listed freezer and shelf. Report any missing or incorrectly placed box directly to me by that deadline.

Under the laboratory’s existing protocol, each principal investigator must make that determination for their samples. Principal investigators must record their sample-use decision in the incident sheet by 5 p.m. on Thursday, July 16.

Facilities expects the replacement door seal to arrive Wednesday morning, but the delivery time remains unconfirmed. Freezer B must remain empty until Facilities completes the repair and the unit holds −80°C for 12 continuous hours.

I will send another email when Freezer B has met that requirement and is cleared for use.
````

````text
At 6:42 a.m. on Monday, July 13, Freezer B triggered an alarm after its temperature rose from minus 80°C to minus 61°C. The backup generator was working. Facilities inspected the freezer and found a failed door seal at 7:25 a.m.

Between 7:40 a.m. and 8:18 a.m., laboratory staff moved all 86 labeled sample boxes from Freezer B to Freezers D and E. The shared inventory lists the destination freezer and shelf for each box. No box was discarded.

Temperature probes show that the air inside Freezer B remained above minus 70°C for 54 minutes. The probes do not measure the temperature inside individual sample tubes, so the extent of exposure within those tubes remains uncertain. Under the lab's existing protocol, each principal investigator must decide whether this exposure affects the planned use of their samples.

Please complete the following actions:

1. Check the shared inventory by noon on Tuesday, July 14. Confirm that your boxes are listed in the correct freezer and shelf location. Report any missing or incorrectly placed box to me by that deadline.

2. Principal investigators must record their sample-use decision in the incident sheet by 5 p.m. on Thursday, July 16.

Facilities expects the replacement seal to arrive Wednesday morning, but the delivery time has not been confirmed. Freezer B must remain empty until the repair is complete and the freezer has held minus 80°C for 12 continuous hours.

I will send another email when Freezer B is cleared for use.

Regards,

Priya Shah  
Laboratory Manager  
Westbridge Materials Lab
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P2_R3_C1 -->

````text
Subject: Action Required: Freezer B Temperature Incident

Dear Freezer B researchers,

At 6:42 a.m. on Monday, July 13, Freezer B triggered a temperature alarm after its temperature rose from minus 80°C to minus 61°C. The backup generator continued working. Facilities identified a failed door seal at 7:25 a.m.

Lab staff moved 86 labeled sample boxes to Freezers D and E between 7:40 a.m. and 8:18 a.m. The shared inventory records the destination freezer and shelf for each box. Staff discarded no boxes.

Temperature probes recorded freezer air above minus 70°C for 54 minutes. These probes measure the air inside the freezer; they do not measure the temperature inside individual sample tubes. We therefore do not know the temperature each tube reached during the incident. Under the lab’s existing protocol, each principal investigator must decide whether this exposure affects the planned use of their samples.

Please complete these actions:

1. Check the shared inventory by noon on Tuesday, July 14. Confirm that each of your boxes appears in the listed freezer and shelf location. Report any missing or incorrectly placed box to me by that deadline.
2. Principal investigators must record their sample-use decisions in the incident sheet by 5 p.m. on Thursday, July 16.

Facilities expects the replacement seal to arrive Wednesday morning, but the supplier has not confirmed that delivery time. Freezer B must remain empty until Facilities completes the repair and the freezer holds minus 80°C for 12 continuous hours.
````

````text
Subject: Freezer B temperature incident and required sample review

Dear Freezer B researchers,

At 6:42 AM on Monday, July 13, Freezer B triggered a temperature alarm after its temperature rose from minus 80°C to minus 61°C. The backup generator was working, and at 7:25 AM Facilities found a failed door seal in Freezer B.

Between 7:40 AM and 8:18 AM, staff moved 86 labeled sample boxes to Freezers D and E, and no box was discarded. For every moved box, the shared inventory lists its destination freezer and shelf.

Please check the shared inventory by noon on Tuesday, July 14. Confirm that your boxes are listed and in the recorded locations. Report any missing or incorrectly placed box directly to me by that deadline.

Under the lab’s existing protocol, each principal investigator must decide whether this exposure affects the planned use of their samples. Principal investigators must record that decision in the incident sheet by 5 PM on Thursday, July 16.

Facilities expects the replacement door seal to arrive Wednesday morning, but the delivery time has not been confirmed. Freezer B must remain empty until the repair is complete and the freezer has held minus 80°C for 12 continuous hours.
````

````text
# Moving crates for a simpler local move

Rent sturdy, lidded plastic crates for your house move in Manchester or Salford—without buying cardboard boxes.

**Book your Stackbox pack**

## What you receive

A standard home pack includes 25 reusable plastic crates, 25 labels and one wheeled dolly. Each crate measures 60 × 40 × 32 centimetres and holds up to 25 kilograms.

The crates arrive ready for you to pack. The fitted lids close securely, while the dolly helps you move packed crates around your home. Your standard rental period is 14 days, giving you time to pack before moving day and unpack afterwards.

## Delivery and collection

Stackbox delivers the empty crates to your old address and collects them from your new address. When booking, you choose a two-hour window for delivery and another two-hour window for collection.

Delivery is available Tuesday through Saturday. Same-day delivery is not available, so please book ahead when planning your move.

Plans can change. You may amend your booking without charge until 4 p.m. two days before your scheduled delivery.

## Prices

The standard home pack costs **£89** for 14 days. This price includes delivery and collection within Manchester or Salford.

Need longer to unpack? Each additional week costs **£24**.

You can add extra crates for **£2.50 each** for the original rental period. Missing crates, or crates returned with structural damage, are charged at **£18 each**. You will not be charged for ordinary marks from normal use.

## What Stackbox does—and doesn’t—provide

Stackbox is a crate rental and delivery service. We do not provide packing, removal, storage or cleaning services. You will need to pack, transport and unpack the crates yourself or arrange those services separately.

Crates must remain within mainland Great Britain and be ready for collection at your new address during your chosen window.

**Book crates for your move**
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P3_R1_C0 -->

</details>

````text
# Move house without the cardboard pile

Rent lidded plastic crates for your local move in Manchester or Salford. We bring them to your old address and pick them up from your new one.

Book your Stackbox

## What you receive

A standard home pack gives you 25 reusable crates, 25 labels and one wheeled dolly. Use the labels to keep track of each crate, then load the dolly when you need to move several at once.

Each crate measures 60 by 40 by 32 centimetres and can hold up to 25 kilograms. The crates have lids, so there is no cardboard to buy or tape together. Your standard rental lasts 14 days.

## Delivery and collection

Choose a two-hour window for delivery to your old address and another two-hour window for collection from your new address. Delivery is available Tuesday through Saturday. We do not offer same-day delivery, so please book ahead.

Plans can shift during a house move. You can change your booking without charge until 4 p.m. two days before your delivery date.

## Prices

The standard home pack costs £89 for 14 days. That price includes delivery and collection within Manchester or Salford.

Need the crates for longer? Each additional week costs £24. You can add extra crates for £2.50 each for the original rental period.

## What Stackbox does not cover

Stackbox is a crate rental service. We do not pack your belongings, move them, store them or clean the crates during your rental. Crates must stay within mainland Great Britain.

Ordinary marks from normal use are not charged. If a crate is missing when we collect your pack, or comes back with structural damage, the charge is £18 per crate.

Choose your delivery window
````

````text
# Reusable crates for your Manchester or Salford move

Rent lidded plastic crates for your home move. We deliver them to your old address, then collect them from your new one.

**Book your Stackbox pack**

## What you receive

A standard home pack gives you 25 plastic crates, 25 labels and one wheeled dolly. Each crate measures 60 by 40 by 32 centimetres and holds up to 25 kilograms.

You rent the crates instead of buying cardboard boxes. Label each crate, load it, and use the dolly to move crates around your home. Your standard rental lasts for 14 days.

## Delivery and collection

Choose a two-hour window for delivery to your old address and another two-hour window for collection from your new address. Stackbox delivers from Tuesday to Saturday. We do not offer same-day delivery, so book before the day you need your crates.

Plans can shift during a house move. You can change your booking without charge until 4 p.m. two days before your delivery. After you unpack at your new address, have all the crates and the dolly ready for collection during your chosen window.

## Prices

The standard home pack costs £89. The price covers 25 crates, 25 labels, one wheeled dolly, a 14-day rental, delivery and collection within Manchester or Salford.

Need more time? Each additional week costs £24. You can add extra crates for £2.50 each for the original rental period.

We charge £18 for each crate you do not return or return with structural damage. We do not charge for ordinary marks from use.

## Service limits

Stackbox rents, delivers and collects crates. We do not pack your belongings, move them between addresses, store them or clean for you. You must keep the crates within mainland Great Britain throughout the rental.

**Choose your crates and dates**
````

````text
# Move home with reusable crates

Rent sturdy, lidded moving crates for a local move in Manchester or Salford. We deliver them to your old address and collect them from your new one.

**Book a home pack**

## What you receive

Instead of buying cardboard boxes for your move, rent the crates and return them after unpacking. A standard home pack contains 25 plastic crates, 25 labels and one wheeled dolly.

Each crate measures 60 by 40 by 32 centimetres and can hold up to 25 kilograms. Use the labels to mark the contents or destination room, and keep each packed crate within the weight limit. The dolly is provided for moving packed crates around the property.

Your standard rental lasts 14 days. Choose your dates and two-hour windows when you book.

## Delivery and collection

Choose a two-hour window for delivery to your old address and another two-hour window for collection from your new address. Delivery is available Tuesday through Saturday, with no same-day delivery.

## Prices

The standard home pack costs £89, including the 14-day rental, delivery and collection within Manchester or Salford.

## Service limits

Stackbox supplies, delivers and collects the crates. We do not pack your belongings or provide removal, storage or cleaning services. You will need to arrange those parts of the move separately. Crates must stay within mainland Great Britain.

Ordinary marks from normal use are not charged. If a crate is missing or comes back with structural damage, the charge is £18 per crate.

**Book your Stackbox pack**
````

````text
# Move house without buying cardboard boxes

Rent lidded plastic crates for a local move in Manchester or Salford. We deliver them to your old address and collect them from your new one.
````

````text
# Move with crates, not cardboard

Rent lidded plastic crates for your local move in Manchester or Salford. We deliver them empty and collect them after you unpack.

**Book your Stackbox pack**

## What you receive

The standard home pack includes 25 reusable crates, 25 labels and one wheeled dolly. Each crate measures 60 by 40 by 32 centimetres and can hold up to 25 kilograms. You can pack without buying or assembling cardboard boxes.

Your standard rental lasts 14 days, with time to pack before the move and unpack at your new home.

## Delivery and collection

Stackbox delivers the empty crates to your old address, then collects them from your new address. You choose a two-hour window for each visit.

Delivery is available Tuesday to Saturday. We do not offer same-day delivery. If your plans change, you can amend your booking free of charge until 4 p.m. two days before delivery.

## Prices

A standard home pack costs £89. This includes the 14-day rental, delivery and collection within Manchester or Salford.

Need longer? Each additional week costs £24. Extra crates are £2.50 each for the original rental period.

We charge £18 for each crate that is missing or returned with structural damage. Ordinary marks from normal use are fine and do not incur a charge.

## What Stackbox does and does not cover

Stackbox is a crate rental service for local house moves. We deliver and collect the equipment, but we do not provide packing, removal, storage or cleaning services.

Crates must stay within mainland Great Britain. If your move falls outside our Manchester and Salford delivery area, or you need a service we do not provide, Stackbox will not be suitable for that move.

**Book your crates**
````

````text
# Move house with reusable crates

Rent lidded plastic moving crates for your local move in Manchester or Salford. Skip the trip for cardboard boxes: Stackbox brings your pack to your old address and picks it up from your new one.

**Book your home pack**

## Your standard home pack

You receive 25 lidded plastic crates, 25 labels and one wheeled dolly. Each crate measures 60 by 40 by 32 centimetres and holds up to 25 kilograms.

Use the labels to keep track of each crate, and use the dolly to move stacked crates around your home. Your standard rental lasts 14 days.

The crates replace cardboard moving boxes for the rental period. Pack them at your old home, unpack them at the new one, then have the full set ready for collection.

## Delivery and collection

Choose a two-hour window for delivery to your old address and another two-hour window for collection from your new address. Stackbox delivers Tuesday through Saturday. We do not offer same-day delivery.

Plans can change during a move. You can amend your booking at no charge until 4 p.m. two days before delivery.

## Prices

A standard home pack costs £89 for 14 days. The price includes delivery and collection within Manchester or Salford.

Need more time or more crates? Each additional week costs £24. Extra crates cost £2.50 each for your original rental period.

## Service limits

Stackbox rents crates and equipment. Our team does not pack your belongings, move them between addresses, store them, or clean for you.

You must keep the crates within mainland Great Britain. We charge £18 for each crate that you do not return or return with structural damage. We do not charge for ordinary marks from use.

**Book your Stackbox pack**
````

````text
# Move with reusable crates

## What Stackbox does not provide

Stackbox is a crate rental service. We do not provide packing, removal, storage or cleaning services, so you will need to arrange those separately if your move requires them.
````

````text
# Plastic crates for your Manchester or Salford move

Rent lidded plastic crates instead of buying cardboard boxes. Stackbox delivers them to your old address and collects them from your new one.

**Book a home pack**

## What you receive

A standard home pack contains 25 plastic crates, 25 labels and one wheeled dolly. Use the labels to identify each crate, then keep the whole pack for the standard 14-day rental period.

Each crate measures 60 by 40 by 32 centimetres and holds up to 25 kilograms. The crates have fitted lids, so your packed belongings stay contained while you prepare for your local move.

## Delivery and collection

Choose a two-hour window for delivery to your old address and a separate two-hour window for collection from your new address. Delivery is available Tuesday through Saturday. Same-day delivery is not available, so choose your delivery date in advance.

You can change your booking without charge until 4 p.m. two days before delivery. Changes made after that point are not covered by the free-change policy.

## Prices

A standard home pack costs £89 for 14 days, including delivery and collection within Manchester or Salford.

If you need the crates for longer, each additional week costs £24. If 25 crates are not enough for your move, extra crates cost £2.50 each for the original rental period.

## Service limits

Stackbox rents, delivers and collects the crates. Packing, removal, storage and cleaning services are not included. The service ends with crate collection at your new address. The crates cannot be taken outside mainland Great Britain.

A missing crate, or one returned with structural damage, is charged at £18 per crate. Ordinary marks are not charged.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P4_R1_C0 -->
````text
Bellhaven Evening Childcare Pilot Draws Demand and Prompts Expansion

Bellhaven Community College will expand an evening childcare pilot at its East Campus after the ten-week trial attracted 87 student users, reached capacity nine times and received broadly positive responses from those who completed a voluntary survey.

The pilot ran for ten teaching weeks in spring 2026, offering childcare from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., Monday through Thursday. It was available to children aged three through ten whose parent or guardian was attending an evening class on campus. The room had 24 places each evening, and families paid £3 per child per visit.

College records show that 87 students registered at least one child during the trial. Sixty-two of those students used the service on four or more evenings, indicating that much of the demand came from families who returned repeatedly rather than using the room only once.

In total, the college recorded 1,146 child-visits. That number counts each evening attendance separately and does not show how many individual children used the service. A child attending on five evenings, for example, would account for five visits in the total.

The room reached its 24-place limit on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one registered family was unable to book a place. However, the college did not record how many other families saw that the room was full and decided not to make a request. Its records therefore capture some, but not necessarily all, unmet demand.

“Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was,” early-years coordinator Darren Cole said.

For parent Sofia Mendes, the service offered an alternative to less certain arrangements while she attended class. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

A voluntary survey completed by 49 participating students also suggested that the service helped many users manage evening study. Forty-one respondents said it made attending class easier. Because participation in the survey was voluntary, the answers do not necessarily represent all 87 registered users. Respondents could also select more than one answer, meaning response totals across the survey cannot be treated as separate groups of students.

Course records provide another point of comparison, although college data cannot show that childcare caused any difference in academic outcomes. Among students registered for the childcare pilot, 74 percent completed all of their spring evening courses. Across all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, the completion rate was 68 percent.

The six-percentage-point difference does not establish that the pilot improved course completion. Students chose whether to register for and use the childcare room, rather than being assigned to it. Users and other evening students may differ in employment, family support, course choices or other ways that the college’s records did not measure. The figures show an association between being a registered user and a higher completion rate, but they cannot prove cause and effect.

The college spent £42,300 on the ten-week pilot. Families paid a combined £3,438 in fees, leaving £38,862 to be covered through the student-services budget. The figures show that parent fees met only a small share of the total cost, with the college subsidising the rest.

Safety records state that no injury requiring outside medical care was recorded during the pilot. That measure is limited to injuries serious enough to require such care and does not, by itself, describe every minor incident that may have occurred.

Bellhaven has now approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. The childcare room will increase from 24 to 32 places per evening while retaining the same Monday-to-Thursday hours and the £3-per-child fee.

The next trial will also introduce a waiting list and record requests received after an evening has filled. Those changes should give the college a clearer measure of unmet demand, including families who seek places after capacity has been reached, while providing an ordered process for filling any cancellations.

The second pilot remains limited to East Campus. Bellhaven has not approved evening childcare at either of its other two campuses, so students taking evening classes at those locations will not yet have access to the college-run service. The expanded trial will allow the college to gather another ten weeks of attendance, waiting-list and course data before making any further decisions.
````

````text
Bellhaven expands evening childcare after East Campus pilot reaches capacity

Bellhaven Community College will expand its evening childcare pilot after a ten-week trial at East Campus drew 87 student parents and guardians, with the room reaching its capacity on nine evenings.

The pilot ran during the spring 2026 teaching term. It offered childcare from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., Monday through Thursday, for children aged three to ten. To qualify, a child’s parent or guardian had to be attending an evening class on the campus.

The room had 24 places each evening, and families paid £3 per child per visit. Eighty-seven students registered at least one child during the trial. Of those students, 62 used the service on four or more evenings.

Attendance records show 1,146 child-visits across the ten weeks. That total counts each evening visit, rather than the number of individual children who attended. A child attending on five evenings, for example, would account for five visits.

The room filled all 24 places on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one registered family was unable to book. The records do not show the full extent of unmet demand because the college did not count families that saw an evening was full and decided not to request a place.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

For parent Sofia Mendes, the service provided an alternative to less certain arrangements while she attended college. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

A voluntary survey also found that most respondents believed the service helped with attendance. Forty-nine participating students completed the survey, and 41 said the childcare made it easier to attend class. Students could choose more than one answer, so that figure should not be read as a breakdown of mutually exclusive responses. Thirty-eight registered users did not complete the survey.

Course records show that 74 percent of students registered for the childcare pilot completed all of their spring evening courses. Across all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, the completion rate was 68 percent.

The six percentage point difference does not establish that childcare caused students to complete their courses. Students decided for themselves whether to register for or use the service, and those who did may differ from other evening students in ways the college’s records do not capture. The figures show an association between registration and completion, but the records cannot determine why the rates differed.

The pilot cost £42,300. Families paid a total of £3,438 in fees, leaving £38,862 to be covered by the college’s student-services budget. The fee income therefore met a little more than 8 percent of the total cost.

College records show no injury during the pilot that required outside medical care. That measure does not cover minor incidents handled in the childcare room, but it records that none required treatment elsewhere.

Bellhaven has now approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. It will increase capacity from 24 to 32 children each evening while keeping the same Monday-to-Thursday hours and the £3 fee.

The next trial will also introduce a waiting list. Staff will record requests made after all places have been booked, addressing a gap in the first pilot’s records. Those figures should give the college a clearer count of families seeking care on full evenings, including those who cannot secure a place.

The second pilot remains limited to East Campus. Bellhaven has two other campuses, but the college has not approved evening childcare at either of them. Any decision about continuing or widening the service will therefore follow another ten weeks of testing at the original site.
````

````text
Bellhaven Plans Larger Evening Childcare Trial as First Pilot’s Figures Raise Capacity Question

Bellhaven Community College will expand its evening childcare pilot after families filled all 24 places on nine nights during a ten-week trial at East Campus this spring. Students made repeat use of the room and survey respondents reported help with class attendance, but the college’s attendance total conflicts with the stated schedule and evening limit.

The college ran the childcare room Monday through Thursday from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. during ten teaching weeks in spring 2026. It accepted children aged three through ten whose parent or guardian attended an evening class on campus. Families paid £3 per child for each evening.

Eighty-seven students registered at least one child, and 62 used the room on four or more evenings. Repeat users made up about 71 percent of registered students, though the college gave no breakdown of how many visits each family made. The college recorded 1,146 child-visits across the pilot. That total counts each evening visit, so it cannot show how many individual children attended or how often each one used the room.

The reported numbers do not fit within the stated capacity. Ten weeks of service from Monday through Thursday would provide no more than 40 evenings. At 24 places per evening, the room could accommodate no more than 960 child-visits under the college’s definition, 186 fewer than the reported 1,146. Filling every place on every scheduled night would still leave the total below 1,146. Repeat attendance cannot bridge the difference because the college counts each child once for each evening visit under its stated counting method.

The pilot record offers no explanation for the discrepancy. The £3,438 collected in fees equals £3 for each of 1,146 visits, so the fee total matches the reported attendance total but does not resolve the conflict.

Demand reached the stated 24-place limit on nine evenings. On seven of those nights, at least one registered family tried to book but could not secure a place. College staff did not count families who saw that a night had filled and decided against making a request. The records leave part of the unmet demand unknown. They also do not say how many families missed out on each of the seven nights.

Parent Sofia Mendes said, “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

Forty-nine participating students completed a voluntary survey, a little over half of the 87 registered users. Forty-one respondents, about 84 percent of those who answered, said the service made it easier to attend class. Those 41 students represent about 47 percent of all registered users. Respondents could select more than one answer, and the college cannot infer the views of the 38 registered users who did not submit a survey.

Course records provide another comparison. Among students who registered to use childcare, 74 percent completed all their spring evening courses. Across all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, 68 percent completed all their spring evening courses.

The six-percentage-point gap does not show that childcare caused the higher completion rate. Students chose whether to use the service, and users may differ from the wider evening student population in ways the college did not measure. The college cannot determine how those students would have fared without the room. Its comparison also places the registered users inside the larger group of 1,920 evening students instead of comparing two separate groups.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

Bellhaven spent £42,300 on the ten-week pilot. Family fees brought in £3,438, leaving £38,862 for the student-services budget to cover. Fees accounted for about 8 percent of the total cost. The college will keep the £3 fee during the next trial.

College staff recorded no injury that required outside medical care. The record does not say whether children had minor injuries or incidents that required no outside treatment.

The college has approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. Staff will increase capacity from 24 to 32 children each evening, a rise of one-third, while keeping the same Monday-to-Thursday hours and fee. Over 40 evenings, the new stated limit would provide up to 1,280 places. Staff will add a waiting list and count requests made after an evening fills, addressing a gap in the first pilot’s demand records.

Bellhaven has not approved the service at either of its other two campuses. The college has announced no decision beyond the second East Campus trial.
````

````text
Bellhaven Adds Eight Childcare Places After East Campus Evening Trial

Bellhaven Community College will add eight places to its evening childcare room at East Campus after a 10-week trial reached capacity nine times this spring.

The college has approved a second 10-week pilot with 32 places available each evening, up from 24. The next trial will also count families who try to book after the room is full, filling one gap in the records from the first run.

Bellhaven opened the room Monday through Thursday from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. during 10 teaching weeks in spring 2026. It accepted children aged three through 10, provided their parent or guardian was attending an evening class at East Campus. Families paid £3 for each child on each evening.

The eligibility rules limited the service to families connected to evening classes at that campus and to children within the stated age range. Students taking classes at Bellhaven’s other campuses were outside the East Campus pilot.

Eighty-seven students registered at least one child. Sixty-two used the room on four or more evenings, nearly three-quarters of all registered students. The available figures do not give a count of unique children or state how often the other 25 registered students used the service.

Attendance records instead count child-visits, with a new visit recorded each time a child attended for an evening. They show 1,146 child-visits over the pilot. A child who attended on several evenings therefore appears several times in that total.

Registration did not guarantee that a family could book on a particular night. The room reached its 24-place limit on nine evenings. On seven of those dates, at least one registered family could not obtain a place. At 24 visits apiece, the nine full evenings account for 216 child-visits, almost 19 per cent of the attendance total.

The college did not record how many other families decided not to make a request after seeing that the room was full. The records consequently show some unsuccessful bookings, but do not provide a complete count of unmet demand.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

Parent Sofia Mendes described the arrangement she sometimes used before the pilot. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

Forty-nine participating students completed a voluntary survey, and 41 said the service made it easier to attend class. The survey response rate among registered students was about 56 per cent. Those 41 answers cover only the survey respondents; 38 registered students did not complete it.

Survey participants could choose more than one answer. Counts for the available answers may therefore overlap and should not be added together as though each respondent belonged to only one category.

Course records offer a separate comparison. Among registered childcare users, 74 per cent completed all their spring evening courses. Across all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, the completion rate was 68 per cent.

The records do not establish that childcare caused the six-percentage-point difference. Students chose whether to use the room, and the two groups may differ in circumstances the records do not measure.

The 68 per cent figure covers the full evening student body, including registered childcare users. It does not come from a separately assigned comparison group.

The first pilot cost £42,300. Fees paid by families totalled £3,438, exactly £3 for each of the 1,146 recorded child-visits. The student-services budget paid the remaining £38,862. Fees covered just over 8 per cent of the total cost. That is an average budget contribution of £33.91 for each recorded child-visit.

No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded during the 10 weeks. That safety figure is limited to injuries meeting the stated threshold for outside care.

For the second pilot, the room will again open Monday through Thursday from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., and the £3 fee will remain unchanged. Capacity will rise to 32 places per evening, allowing eight more bookings than the first pilot’s nightly limit. That is a one-third increase in nightly capacity.

Staff will add a waiting list. Requests submitted after an evening fills will be recorded, while families who see that no place is available and decide not to request one will remain outside the count.

Bellhaven has not approved evening childcare at either of its other two campuses. Its current approval applies only to the next 10-week pilot at East Campus.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P4_R2_C0 -->
````text
Bellhaven College Expands Evening Childcare Pilot After Capacity Reached

Bellhaven Community College will expand its evening childcare pilot at East Campus after the service reached capacity on nine evenings during a ten-week trial this spring. A second pilot will offer eight additional places each evening while collecting more information about families unable to book.

The childcare room operated Monday through Thursday from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. during ten teaching weeks in spring 2026. It accepted children aged three through ten whose parent or guardian was attending an evening class on the campus.

The room had 24 places each evening, and families paid £3 per child per evening. Eighty-seven students registered at least one child during the trial. Of those students, 62 used the service on four or more evenings, indicating that most registered users returned repeatedly rather than using it only once or twice.

Attendance records show 1,146 child-visits across the pilot. That figure counts each evening visit, not individual children, so it does not reveal how many unique children attended. A child using the room on several evenings would be counted once for each visit.

The room reached its 24-place limit on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one registered family was unable to book a place. However, the college did not record how many other families saw that an evening was full and decided not to make a request. The existing figures therefore show some unmet demand but do not measure its full extent.

Parent Sofia Mendes described the service in relation to the arrangements she had previously used while attending campus. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

A voluntary survey completed by 49 participating students provided another indication of how users viewed the trial. Forty-one respondents said the childcare service made it easier for them to attend class. Students could select more than one answer in the survey, and fewer than all 87 registered users responded, so the results reflect the views of those who chose to complete it rather than every participating family.

Course records show that 74 percent of registered childcare users completed all their spring evening courses. Across all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, the corresponding completion rate was 68 percent.

The six-percentage-point difference does not establish that childcare caused users to complete their courses. Students decided for themselves whether to register for and use the service, and childcare users may differ from other evening students in ways the college’s records do not measure. The figures are an observed comparison, not the result of a study in which otherwise similar students were assigned different childcare options.

The pilot cost £42,300. Fees paid by families totaled £3,438, leaving the student-services budget to cover the remaining cost. The fee income represented only a small portion of the expense, meaning the service depended primarily on college funding during the trial.

No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded during the ten weeks. That record describes the threshold the college tracked; it should not be read as evidence that no minor incident or routine first-aid need occurred, because the information provided does not address those events.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said the capacity figures demonstrated both demand for the room and the restriction created by its size. “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

The college has now approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. Capacity will rise from 24 to 32 places per evening, while the operating hours will remain 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., Monday through Thursday. The £3 fee per child per evening will also remain unchanged.

The next pilot will add a waiting list and record requests received after an evening is full. That change should give the college information the first trial did not capture, including a clearer count of registered families seeking places after capacity has been reached. It will also provide a record of unmet requests rather than relying solely on the number of full evenings.

For now, the expansion applies only to East Campus. Bellhaven has not approved evening childcare at either of its other two campuses. The second trial will therefore test a larger version of the same East Campus service, rather than mark a college-wide rollout.
````

````text
Bellhaven Community College will expand its evening childcare pilot after a ten-week trial at East Campus drew 87 student parents and reached capacity on nine nights.

The college ran the service for ten teaching weeks in spring 2026. Its childcare room opened from 5.30pm to 9.30pm, Monday to Thursday, for children aged three to ten. To qualify, a child’s parent or guardian had to be attending an evening class on the campus.

The room had 24 places each evening, and families paid £3 per child per night. Sixty-two of the 87 students who registered a child used the service on at least four evenings.

Parent Sofia Mendes said the arrangement helped with a problem she had previously managed through a mixture of family support and improvisation.

“Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

Attendance records show 1,146 child-visits during the trial. That figure is a count of evening visits, not of individual children. A child attending on five nights, for example, would account for five visits.

Demand did not remain within the available space on every night. The room filled all 24 places on nine evenings. On seven of those nights, at least one registered family tried to book but could not get a place.

The records leave part of the unmet demand unknown. Bellhaven did not count families who saw that a night was full and decided not to make a request. The figures can therefore show confirmed unsuccessful bookings, but not everyone who may have wanted the service.

In a voluntary survey, 49 participating students responded and 41 said the childcare made it easier for them to attend class. Students could choose more than one answer, so that result should not be treated as the only reason respondents used the room or as a measure of all 87 registered users.

The ten-week pilot cost £42,300. Families paid £3,438 in fees, leaving £38,862 to be covered by the student-services budget. Fees therefore met a little more than 8 per cent of the total cost.

No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded during the pilot. That finding describes the threshold used in the college’s records and does not, by itself, provide a wider assessment of the service.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said the capacity figures gave the college both evidence of use and a reason to change the next trial.

“Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

Bellhaven has now approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. The room will have 32 places per evening, eight more than in the first trial. The opening hours will remain 5.30pm to 9.30pm from Monday through Thursday, and the fee will stay at £3 per child per evening.

The next pilot will also introduce a waiting list. Staff will record requests made after an evening has filled, giving the college a clearer account of demand that could not be met. That change will address one gap in the spring records, although it will still depend on families making a request.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P4_R2_C3 -->
````text
Bellhaven expands evening childcare after East Campus pilot

Bellhaven Community College will increase the number of evening childcare places at its East Campus from 24 to 32 after a ten-week pilot that was full on nine nights. The next trial will retain the same evening hours and £3 fee while collecting more information about families unable to book.

During ten teaching weeks in spring 2026, the childcare room opened from 5.30pm to 9.30pm, Monday through Thursday, for children aged three through ten. To use it, a child’s parent or guardian had to be attending an evening class on the campus. The room was available for four hours each night it opened, and families paid £3 per child for each evening.

Eighty-seven students registered at least one child, and 62 used the room on four or more evenings. That means about 71 percent of registered students returned at least four times. The remaining 25 registered students used the room on fewer than four evenings, if they used it at all. These figures count students. The attendance total counts a child again on every evening that child attends.

Attendance records list 1,146 child-visits over the ten weeks, an average of 114.6 per teaching week. Each visit in that total represents one child attending on one evening. A child who attended several times appears several times, and the records do not establish how many individual children used the service.

Parent Sofia Mendes described the family arrangements she had sometimes made before the East Campus service became available. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

The room had 24 places each evening. It reached that limit on nine evenings, and on seven of those nights at least one registered family was unable to book. Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

The booking records establish that registered families were turned away on seven full evenings. The college did not record how many families decided not to request a place after seeing that an evening was full.

A voluntary survey offers a view from some of the students who took part. Forty-nine participating students completed it, and 41 said the childcare made it easier to attend class. That is about 84 percent of respondents, who made up about 56 percent of registered students. They were allowed to select more than one answer, and the 41 respondents could also have selected other choices.

The survey describes the students who chose to respond. It does not establish that the same share of all 87 registered students would have given that answer, because participation in the survey was voluntary.

Course records provide a separate comparison. Among students registered for the childcare pilot, 74 percent completed all their spring evening courses. Across all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, the completion rate was 68 percent. For registered users, completion meant finishing all of their spring evening courses. The second rate covers the college’s full evening-course enrollment. The comparison places a subgroup against the full evening enrollment, which contains that subgroup alongside other students.

Registered users had a completion rate six percentage points higher than the rate for all evening students. Students chose whether to use childcare, however, and the groups may differ in ways the records do not measure. The figures do not establish that the pilot caused the difference in course completion.

The ten-week service cost £42,300. The £3 payments associated with 1,146 recorded child-visits produced £3,438 in fees, about 8 percent of the total cost. Dividing the total cost by the recorded visits gives about £36.91 per child-visit. The student-services budget paid the remaining £38,862. No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded. That safety entry is limited to injuries meeting that threshold.

The college has approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. The room will keep its Monday-to-Thursday schedule, its 5.30pm to 9.30pm hours and the £3 fee, while raising capacity to 32 children each evening. At full capacity, the room will be able to accept eight more children per evening, a one-third increase.

The next pilot will add a waiting list and record requests made after an evening is full. Those records will give the college a count it did not collect in the spring: the number of requests made after all available places had been booked.

Bellhaven has not approved the service at its other two campuses. The second pilot, including the larger room and waiting-list records, will remain at East Campus.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P4_R3_C0 -->
````text
Bellhaven Evening Childcare Pilot Draws Demand, but Impact Remains Unclear

Bellhaven Community College will expand its evening childcare pilot at East Campus after a ten-week trial attracted 87 student parents and guardians, reached capacity nine times and received broadly positive responses from survey participants.

The pilot ran for ten teaching weeks in spring 2026. Its childcare room opened Monday through Thursday from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., serving children aged three through ten whose parent or guardian was attending an evening class on campus. The room offered 24 places each evening, with families paying £3 per child per visit.

Eighty-seven students registered at least one child during the pilot, and 62 used the service on four or more evenings. Attendance records show 1,146 child-visits over the ten weeks. That total represents evening visits rather than individual children, meaning a child attending several times was counted on each occasion.

The room filled all 24 places on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one registered family tried to book but could not secure a place. However, the college did not record how many other families saw that the room was full and decided not to make a request. The figures therefore document some unmet demand but do not show its full extent.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said the capacity problems would help shape the next trial. “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

Participating parent Sofia Mendes described the practical difficulty she had faced before the pilot. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

A voluntary survey completed by 49 participating students also indicated that many users found the service helpful. Of those respondents, 41 said it made attending class easier. Because fewer than all 87 registered users completed the survey, the results cannot be assumed to represent every participant. Respondents were also allowed to select more than one answer, an important qualification when interpreting the survey’s findings.

Course records show that 74 percent of registered childcare users completed all their spring evening courses. Among all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, the corresponding completion rate was 68 percent.

The six-percentage-point difference does not establish that childcare caused participating students to complete their courses. Students chose whether to register for and use the service, rather than being assigned to it. Users and non-users may have differed in motivation, schedules, family support, course selection or other ways that the college’s records did not measure. The records show an association between childcare registration and higher completion, but they cannot explain why that association occurred.

The trial cost £42,300. Families paid a combined £3,438 in fees, leaving £38,862 to be covered by the college’s student-services budget. The fee revenue amounted to slightly more than 8 percent of the pilot’s total cost. The college has not said that the service was intended to pay for itself through family charges.

Safety records show that no injury requiring outside medical care was recorded during the ten-week period. That measure does not account for minor incidents that may not have required such treatment, but it establishes that none crossed the college’s stated threshold for outside medical attention.

College officials have now approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. It will increase the room’s nightly capacity from 24 to 32 places while retaining the same Monday-to-Thursday schedule, opening hours and £3 fee.

The next pilot will also introduce a waiting list and record requests received after an evening is full. Those changes should give the college a clearer count of families unable to obtain places, including demand that the first pilot’s booking records did not capture.

For now, the expansion remains limited to East Campus. Bellhaven has not approved evening childcare at either of its other two campuses. The second pilot will therefore test a larger version of the service at the original location, while leaving open the questions of whether demand can be met consistently and whether the program should eventually be offered more widely.
````

````text
Bellhaven childcare pilot fills up as college approves larger second trial

Bellhaven Community College will expand its evening childcare pilot after the service filled all 24 places on nine nights during a ten-week trial at East Campus this spring.

The college has approved a second ten-week pilot with 32 places each evening. It will keep the original opening hours and £3 fee, while adding a waiting list and recording requests made after the room is full. The service has not been approved for Bellhaven's other two campuses.

The first pilot ran for ten teaching weeks in spring 2026. The childcare room opened from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., Monday through Thursday, for children aged three to ten. To qualify, a child's parent or guardian had to be attending an evening class on the campus. Each session had 24 places.

Eighty-seven students registered at least one child during the pilot, and 62 used the service on four or more evenings. The registration figure counts students, not children, while the usage figure identifies those who returned regularly. The records supplied by the college do not give the number of unique children who attended.

The reported attendance total also does not fit the stated schedule and capacity. Ten weeks of four evenings provide 40 sessions, and 24 places at each would allow no more than 960 child-visits. The £3,438 collected in fees equals £3 for each of the reported 1,146 visits, but the record does not explain how those visits could exceed the room's stated maximum. The figures therefore require clarification before they can provide a reliable measure of occupancy across the pilot.

The room reached its 24-place limit on nine evenings. On seven of those nights, at least one registered family was unable to book. Even that understates the possible unmet demand. The college did not count families that saw an evening was full and decided not to request a place, so its records cannot show how many families wanted care on those nights.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

Parent Sofia Mendes described how she had managed childcare before the pilot. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

That result describes the people who returned the survey, not every user. It amounts to 84 percent of respondents and 47 percent of all registered users, but the college did not collect answers from the other 38. The survey cannot show whether nonrespondents had similar experiences.

Course records show a higher completion rate among registered childcare users than among evening students overall. Seventy-four percent of registered users completed all their spring evening courses. The rate was 68 percent among all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course. The college's measure concerns completion of a student's full set of spring evening courses, not attendance on individual nights.

That comparison does not establish that childcare caused the difference. Students chose whether to use the service, rather than being assigned to it, and users may differ from other evening students in ways the records do not measure. The overall evening-student figure also includes the childcare users, so it is not a separate comparison group. The figures cannot show what either group's results would have been under the same circumstances.

The pilot cost £42,300. Fees covered £3,438, with the student-services budget paying the remaining £38,862. On the reported totals, family payments met a little more than 8 percent of the cost. College records show no injury that required outside medical care, although that measure does not address minor injuries below that threshold.

The next trial will add eight places per evening. Its waiting list and records of requests after sessions fill should give the college information the first pilot did not capture. For now, the plan remains limited to another ten weeks at East Campus, with the same four evening sessions and fee. Bellhaven has made no commitment to extend the service to its other sites.
````

````text
Bellhaven Expands Evening Childcare Pilot After Demand Tests 24-Place Limit

Bellhaven Community College will expand its evening childcare pilot at East Campus after families filled all 24 places on nine nights during a ten-week trial this spring. The next trial will add eight places and collect information that the first booking system missed.

The college opened the childcare room from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., Monday through Thursday, during ten teaching weeks in spring 2026. It accepted children aged three to ten whose parent or guardian attended an evening class on the campus. Families paid £3 for each child on each evening they used the room. The eligibility rule tied each booking to a parent or guardian studying at East Campus that night. The schedule provided four hours of care on four evenings each week.

Eighty-seven students registered at least one child, and 62 students used the service on four or more evenings. That leaves 25 registered students who used it fewer than four times, though the figures supplied by the college do not divide that group between those who attended once, twice or three times and those who registered without attending.

Attendance records counted 1,146 child-visits across the pilot. College staff counted each visit on a separate evening. A child who attended on five evenings accounted for five visits, so the total does not show how many individual children attended. It also cannot show how often each family used the room.

The room reached its 24-place limit on nine evenings. On seven of those nights, at least one registered family tried to book and could not get a place. The college did not record how many other families saw that the room was full and chose not to make a request, leaving staff without a full count of unmet demand.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

Parent Sofia Mendes described the arrangements she had made before the college offered the room. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

A voluntary survey gathered responses from 49 participating students, a little more than half of the 87 who registered. Forty-one respondents said the service made it easier to attend class. Respondents could select more than one answer, so that total should not be treated as one part of a set of exclusive choices.

The survey offers accounts from participants, but 38 registered users did not complete it. The college’s results describe the 49 respondents and cannot establish whether the students who skipped the survey had the same experience.

Course records provide another measure, though they cannot show the pilot’s effect on academic progress. Among registered childcare users, 74 percent completed all their spring evening courses. Across the 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, 68 percent completed all their spring evening courses.

The broader figure covers the full evening-student population, rather than a separate group of students who did not use childcare. Registered users chose whether to use the room, and they may differ from other evening students in ways the records did not measure. The six-percentage-point gap does not establish that childcare caused the higher completion rate.

Bellhaven spent £42,300 on the ten-week pilot. Families paid £3,438 in fees, and the college paid the remaining £38,862 from its student-services budget. Fees covered about 8 percent of the cost. The fee total matches 1,146 child-visits at £3 each.

Staff recorded no injury requiring outside medical care during the trial. The information supplied by the college does not provide a count of minor injuries or other incidents.

The college has approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. Staff will increase capacity from 24 to 32 children each evening, a rise of one-third, while keeping the Monday-to-Thursday schedule, 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. hours and £3 fee.

For the second trial, staff will introduce a waiting list and record requests that families make after an evening fills. Those changes will give the college a count of families who ask for care beyond the room’s capacity, closing one of the gaps in the first pilot’s records.

Bellhaven has not approved evening childcare at its other two campuses. The larger room and revised record-keeping will apply to East Campus during another ten-week trial.
````

````text
Bellhaven expands evening childcare after East Campus pilot

Bellhaven Community College will increase the capacity of its evening childcare room after a spring pilot filled up on nine nights and left some registered families unable to book.

The college ran the pilot for 10 teaching weeks in spring 2026 at its East Campus. The room was open from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., Monday through Thursday. It accepted children aged three through 10 when their parent or guardian was attending an evening class on the campus.

Families paid £3 per child per evening. The room had 24 places each night, so availability depended on a family securing a booking for the evening when the parent or guardian had class.

Eighty-seven students registered at least one child. Sixty-two used the service on four or more evenings, meaning more than seven in 10 registered students returned at least three times after their first use. The other 25 registered students used the room on fewer than four evenings or did not use it.

Parent Sofia Mendes said, “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

Attendance records show 1,146 child-visits during the pilot. Each child’s visit on one evening counts once in that total. A child who attended on five evenings, for example, would account for five child-visits rather than one unique child.

If two children from one family attended on the same night, each visit would count. The figure measures use of the room across evenings, not the number of individual children served.

The 24 places were all booked on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one registered family could not get a place. The records do not establish how many different families were affected across the seven nights.

The unmet demand could have been higher. The college did not record how many families decided against requesting a place after seeing that an evening was already full. Its figures therefore cannot include demand from families that checked availability but did not ask for a booking.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

Forty-nine participating students completed a voluntary survey. Of those respondents, 41, or about 84 percent, said the service made it easier to attend class. Because participation in the survey was voluntary, the result describes the students who answered rather than all 87 students who registered a child.

Respondents could select more than one answer. The 41 responses about class attendance can be reported as a response to that question, but totals from different answer choices cannot be combined as though each came from a different student.

Course records show that 74 percent of registered childcare users completed all their spring evening courses. Among all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, 68 percent completed all their spring evening courses. Registered users were six percentage points above the rate for the full evening-course population.

The comparison does not establish that the childcare pilot caused the difference. Students decided for themselves whether to use the room, and users may differ from other evening students in ways the records do not measure. The comparison also uses the full evening-student population, which includes the registered users, rather than two separately assigned groups.

The pilot cost £42,300. Fees collected from families totalled £3,438, equal to £3 for each of the 1,146 recorded child-visits. That left £38,862 to be paid from the student-services budget. Fees covered just over 8 percent of the cost.

No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded during the 10 weeks. The record is limited to injuries that reached that threshold.

The college has approved a second 10-week pilot at East Campus. Capacity will rise from 24 to 32 places per evening, adding room for eight more children, a one-third increase. The hours, Monday-to-Thursday schedule and £3 fee will remain unchanged during the second pilot.

A waiting list will be added, and staff will record requests made after an evening is full. The new record will count requests made after all 32 bookings have been taken. It will not count families that see no availability and decide not to ask for a place.

The college has not approved the service at its other two campuses. The next 10-week trial will remain at East Campus and continue to serve children aged three through 10 whose parent or guardian is attending an evening class there.
````
