````text
Bellhaven Expands Evening Childcare Pilot After Demand Tests 24-Place Limit

Bellhaven Community College will expand its evening childcare pilot after a ten-week trial helped dozens of students attend classes at East Campus, although college records cannot establish whether the service improved course completion.

The spring 2026 pilot offered childcare Monday through Thursday, from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., during teaching weeks. It was available to children aged three through ten whose parent or guardian was attending an evening class on campus. The room had 24 places each evening, and families paid £3 per child per visit.

Eighty-seven students registered at least one child during the pilot. Sixty-two of those students used the service on four or more evenings, suggesting that most registered users returned repeatedly rather than treating it as a one-off option.

Attendance records show 1,146 child-visits over the ten weeks. That figure counts each evening visit, not individual children, so it does not reveal how many unique children attended. A child who attended on several evenings would appear several times in the total.

The service reached its 24-place limit on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one registered family tried to book but could not obtain a place. The college did not record how many other families saw that the room was full and decided not to make a request, meaning the records may not capture all unmet demand.

Parent Sofia Mendes described the practical difficulties she faced before the pilot. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

A voluntary survey provided further evidence about how participating students viewed the service. Forty-nine students completed the survey, and 41 said the childcare made it easier for them to attend class. Respondents could choose more than one answer, and the survey did not include every student who registered, so its findings reflect the views of those who chose to respond rather than all users.

Course records show that 74 percent of registered childcare users completed all of their spring evening courses. Among all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, the comparable completion rate was 68 percent.

The six-percentage-point difference does not demonstrate that childcare caused students to complete their courses. Students decided for themselves whether to use the service, and users may have differed from the wider evening-student population in ways that college records did not measure. The figures therefore show an association between registration for the pilot and a higher completion rate, but they cannot explain the reason for it.

The pilot cost £42,300. Families paid a combined £3,438 in fees, leaving £38,862 to be covered by the college’s student-services budget. The £3 charge accounted for only a small share of the total cost, so continuing or expanding the service would require further college funding unless its financing model changes.

No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded during the ten-week trial. That record describes the outcome during this pilot but does not, by itself, provide a broader assessment of safety or predict what would happen during a longer programme.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said the capacity figures carried two messages for the college. “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

Bellhaven has now approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. The childcare room will retain the same Monday-to-Thursday hours and the £3-per-child evening fee, but capacity will increase from 24 to 32 places each night.

The second trial will also introduce a waiting list. Staff will record requests received after an evening has filled, addressing a gap in the first pilot’s data and giving the college a clearer measure of demand beyond the available places.

The next pilot remains limited to East Campus. Bellhaven has not approved evening childcare at either of its other two campuses. Results from the expanded trial, including the new waiting-list records, will give college officials more information as they consider what should follow the second ten-week period.
````

````text
Bellhaven adds childcare places as pilot records leave attendance discrepancy

Bellhaven Community College will increase the capacity of its evening childcare service after a spring pilot at East Campus recorded 1,146 child-visits over ten teaching weeks. The college’s figures point to regular use and several full evenings, but they also contain an unresolved discrepancy over the total attendance.

The pilot was available to students with children aged three to ten. To use it, a parent or guardian had to be attending an evening class on the campus. Each booking was tied to an evening when the adult was in class. The record does not say whether any registered family used more than one place on the same night. The childcare room opened from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., Monday through Thursday, and had space for 24 children each evening. Families paid £3 per child per evening.

College records show that 87 students registered at least one child during the pilot. Of those, 62 used the service on four or more evenings. Registration did not necessarily mean regular attendance, while the records supplied do not give a breakdown of how often the other 25 students booked places.

The 1,146 attendance figure is a count of visits, not children, so a child attending on five evenings appears five times. It does not show how many individual children used the room. More importantly, that total cannot be reconciled with the stated schedule and capacity. Ten Monday-to-Thursday teaching weeks provide at most 40 evenings. At 24 places an evening, the room could accommodate no more than 960 child-visits. The reported total is 186 above that maximum. The fee total of £3,438, meanwhile, equals £3 multiplied by 1,146. The records provided do not explain the conflict, so they cannot establish an accurate total within the stated 24-place limit.

Demand sometimes exceeded the space available. The room reached its limit on nine evenings. On seven of those nights, at least one family that had already registered was unable to book. That is a confirmed minimum, not a complete count of unmet demand. The college did not record how many families saw that a session was full and decided not to make a request.

For Sofia Mendes, the room offered an alternative to arrangements that depended on relatives being available. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

A voluntary survey provides another view of the pilot, though only 49 participating students completed it. Forty-one said the service made it easier to attend class. Respondents could choose more than one answer, so that response was not necessarily their only reason or view. The survey records what those 49 students reported. It does not establish what all 87 registered users thought, and participation was voluntary.

Course records show a difference in completion rates. Among students registered for childcare, 74 percent completed all their spring evening courses. Across all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, the comparable figure was 68 percent. The first group sits within the second, rather than forming a separate comparison sample.

Those numbers cannot show that childcare produced the six percentage point difference. Students decided for themselves whether to register and use the room. They may differ from the wider evening student population in ways the college’s records do not measure. The records show an association between registration and course completion, not cause and effect. Nor do they say whether more frequent users had different results from students who used the room only once or twice.

Running the pilot cost £42,300. The college collected £3,438 in family fees, leaving £38,862 to be covered by the student services budget. Fee income amounted to a little over 8 percent of the total cost. Because the attendance count conflicts with the stated capacity, it cannot reliably support a per-visit cost calculation without further explanation. No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

The college has approved a second ten-week pilot at East Campus. Capacity will rise from 24 to 32 places each evening, while the Monday-to-Thursday hours and the £3 fee will stay the same. The next trial will introduce a waiting list and record requests made after an evening has filled. That should give the college a fuller count of families unable to secure a place, though it will also need to resolve how total visits are counted.

For now, the service remains limited to East Campus. Bellhaven has not approved evening childcare at either of its other two campuses.
````

````text
# Bellhaven Expands Evening Childcare Pilot as Records Show Demand and a Counting Discrepancy

Bellhaven Community College will add eight places to its evening childcare service after a ten-week East Campus pilot filled its room on nine nights and left some registered families unable to book.

The college registered 87 students for the spring 2026 pilot. Sixty-two used the service on four or more evenings, indicating that most registered users returned several times. Participants had to attend an evening class at East Campus while their children, ages three through ten, stayed in the childcare room.

Bellhaven opened the room Monday through Thursday from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. Families paid £3 per child per evening, and the college offered 24 places each night.

Parent Sofia Mendes described how she had managed the hours before the college offered the service. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

A voluntary survey gives one account of how participants viewed the pilot. Of the 49 participating students who responded, 41 said the service made it easier to attend class. Respondents could choose more than one answer.

The survey did not include 38 of the 87 registered students, so its findings do not represent a response from every user. Nor do the records say what the nonrespondents thought. They establish that 41 students reported an attendance benefit, rather than showing that all participants had the same experience.

The registration, repeat-use and survey counts answer different questions. Registration shows how many students signed up at least once. It does not show how many individual children attended. The repeat-use figure identifies 62 students who used the room on four or more nights, but the records do not divide all visits by family. The survey count covers only those participants who chose to respond.

Booking records point to demand beyond the room’s capacity on some nights. Families booked all 24 places on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one registered family could not get a place.

The college did not count families who chose not to request a place after seeing that an evening had filled. Its records do not capture that possible source of demand. They establish that some registered families missed out on seven nights but cannot show the total number of families who wanted care after bookings closed.

Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said the full evenings showed both the service’s value and its constraint. “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

The attendance and fee totals raise a separate question that the supplied figures do not resolve. The college reported 1,146 child-visits, counting each child’s evening visit rather than unique children. Ten teaching weeks from Monday through Thursday provide 40 evenings. At 24 places per evening, the stated maximum would be 960 child-visits, 186 fewer than the reported total.

The £3,438 collected from families equals 1,146 payments of £3, so the fee total matches the reported child-visit count. Neither total explains how attendance could exceed the capacity implied by the schedule. The records also cannot show how many individual children attended because they count repeat visits again on each evening.

Course figures offer a comparison, with limits. Among registered childcare users, 74 percent completed all their spring evening courses. Among all 1,920 students taking at least one evening course, 68 percent completed all their spring evening courses.

Students chose whether to register for childcare, and users may differ from the broader evening student population in ways the records did not measure. The six-percentage-point difference does not establish that childcare caused users to complete courses at a higher rate.

Bellhaven spent £42,300 on the pilot and collected £3,438 in family fees. The student-services budget covered the remaining £38,862. The college recorded no injury requiring outside medical care.

The college has approved another ten-week pilot at East Campus with 32 places per evening. It will keep the same hours and £3 fee. A new waiting list will record requests made after an evening fills, allowing the college to measure demand that the first pilot missed.

The next pilot will remain at East Campus. Bellhaven has not approved the service at its other two campuses. The second trial will give East Campus families more places, while the waiting-list records should provide a fuller count of families who seek care when all 32 places have gone.
````

````text
Bellhaven expands East Campus evening childcare to 32 places

Bellhaven Community College has approved a second ten-week childcare pilot at East Campus, raising the number of places available each evening from 24 to 32. The college's first spring trial reached its 24-place limit nine times. The larger room will operate on the same schedule and charge families the same fee. The increase of eight places amounts to a one-third rise in nightly capacity.

The first pilot ran for ten teaching weeks in spring 2026. Its childcare room opened from 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m., Monday through Thursday, for children aged three through ten. A parent or guardian had to be attending an evening class at East Campus. Families paid £3 for each child on each evening they used the room, and bookings were limited to 24 places a night. The service was limited to families connected to evening classes at that campus.

Eighty-seven students registered at least one child during the pilot. Sixty-two used the room on four or more evenings, about 71 per cent of the registered students. Attendance records list 1,146 child-visits across the ten weeks. That total is a count of evening visits, so a child who attended on several nights appears several times. It does not give the number of individual children who used the room. Registration counted students, while attendance counted child-visits, so the totals describe different units.

The attendance total is inconsistent with the stated schedule and capacity. Ten Monday-to-Thursday teaching weeks would provide 40 evenings and no more than 960 places at 24 a night, yet the recorded total is 186 visits higher. The £3,438 fee total equals £3 multiplied by 1,146 visits, so it repeats the same visit figure without explaining the excess over the stated capacity.

Demand exceeded the available bookings on some nights. The room filled all 24 places on nine evenings. On seven of those evenings, at least one family that had registered for the pilot could not get a place. The seven evenings with an unsuccessful booking were a subset of the nine that reached capacity.

This establishes a minimum of seven unsuccessful family bookings, but the records do not say whether the same families were affected on several nights or how many different families missed out. The college also did not record how many families saw that a session was full and decided not to make a request. Early-years coordinator Darren Cole said, “Nine full evenings told us the room was useful, but they also showed where the limit was.”

For parent Sofia Mendes, the room offered another way to manage an evening class and her son's care. “Before this, I sometimes brought my son to the library and hoped my sister could collect him after work.”

Other users reported a practical effect on class attendance. Forty-nine participating students completed a voluntary survey, and 41 said the service made it easier to attend class. That was about 84 per cent of respondents.

Respondents could select more than one answer, so their choices were not mutually exclusive. The survey covered 49 of the 87 students who registered a child rather than every registered user.

Course records provide a separate comparison. Among students registered for the childcare pilot, 74 per cent completed all their spring evening courses. Across all 1,920 students enrolled in at least one evening course, the completion rate was 68 per cent. The six-percentage-point difference does not establish that the pilot caused higher completion. Students chose whether to use childcare, and the two groups may differ in ways the records do not measure.

The ten-week pilot cost £42,300. Families paid a total of £3,438 in fees. Fee income covered about 8 per cent of the cost. The remaining £38,862, or about 92 per cent, came from the student-services budget. The second pilot will keep the £3 charge.

No injury requiring outside medical care was recorded during the pilot. The safety record supplied for the trial is limited to injuries meeting that threshold.

The second East Campus pilot will run for another ten weeks with the same Monday-to-Thursday hours. Alongside the eight extra places each evening, the college will introduce a waiting list. Staff will record requests made after a session is full, adding information that was missing from the first pilot's booking records. The new count will cover families that make a request after the room fills; it will not capture those who see that it is full and do not ask. For now, the expansion remains confined to East Campus. Bellhaven has not approved the service at its other two campuses.
````

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P5_R1_C0 -->
````text
Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition rather than replacing both staffed audio-guide desks or abandoning the lockers. During August, we should keep one staffed desk open, operate the lockers with an employee continuously assigned to the locker area, and defer any decision on full replacement until the system has been tested under peak demand and its accessibility limitations have been addressed.

The June pilot shows that lockers can process transactions faster: the median was 42 seconds, compared with 96 seconds at the staffed desk. However, the issue totals—984 successful locker issues and 1,106 staffed-desk issues—do not establish which service model attracts or handles more demand. They came from separate service points, and visitors were not assigned between them. A full move would also remove both staffed desks, yet the record contains no approved staffing plan for that option. Abandoning the lockers would likewise be premature because the pilot demonstrated faster transactions and allowed one employee to spend four hours each day on the gallery floor.

Pilot Record

The 21-day June pilot covered 6,420 admissions. One of the two entrance-hall desks remained staffed; the other was replaced with 30 lockers. Visitors obtained a device by scanning the barcode on their admission ticket. Transaction-time findings are based on timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each service point.

Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. The recorded primary reasons were 48 unreadable ticket screens, 39 lockers that did not open on the first attempt, and 30 requests for help changing the device language. Because each intervention received one primary reason, the figures do not establish whether any transaction involved additional difficulties.

Eleven devices were returned to the wrong locker, but staff found all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing: one returned the next morning, while one was not recovered. In the preceding 21 days, when both desks were staffed, one device was not recovered among 5,870 admissions. These periods differed in visitor volume and service arrangements, so the raw figures should be retained without treating them as proof that either system changes loss risk.

Operational Risks

The strongest immediate concern is accessibility. Accessibility staff observed three visitors with low vision who could not use the locker screen independently. The supplier’s planned audio-navigation update may address this limitation, but it is scheduled only generally for September; there is no release date or test version. We should not base an August operating model on an unavailable update.

Capacity and support requirements are also unresolved. August 2025 daily admissions averaged 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average, and the lockers have never been tested at that volume. The 117 interventions show that self-service still required staff assistance with tickets, locker opening, and language changes. Removing both desks before establishing how that assistance would be staffed would create an unsupported operating arrangement.

The employee transferred to the gallery provided four additional hours of floor coverage each pilot day. However, the museum did not measure whether that coverage affected visitor questions, rule compliance, or incidents. It should therefore not be presented as a demonstrated operational benefit beyond the added staff presence itself.

August Plan

Visitor Operations should retain one staffed desk and assign one employee to the locker area throughout August, as current staffing permits. The staffed desk will preserve a direct service route, including for visitors unable to operate the screen independently, while the locker-area employee can record and respond to interventions.

We should accept the supplier’s offer to add ten lockers by July 27 for £2,800, expanding the bank from 30 to 40 before the busiest month. This is a measured capacity addition, not evidence that lockers can yet replace all staffed service.

Throughout August, we should record admissions, successful issues at each point, transaction times, interventions by one primary reason, wrong-locker returns, devices missing at closing, and final recovery status. Accessibility staff should continue documenting cases requiring assistance. After August, Visitor Operations can assess performance at peak volume and review any firm supplier information on audio navigation before recommending full replacement, continued mixed service, or withdrawal of the lockers.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P5_R1_C0 -->

````text
To: Museum Director  
From: Rowan Blake, Visitor Operations Manager

## Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition: keep one staffed audio-guide desk open, continue using the 30 self-service lockers, and place one employee in the locker area throughout August. We should not replace both staffed desks or abandon the lockers on the June evidence.

The lockers completed transactions faster in the sampled records, but the pilot did not test an unattended service. Staff intervened 117 times, and three visitors with low vision could not use the screen independently. The September audio-navigation update is still only a supplier plan, with no release date or test version. A staffed option is therefore necessary in August.

I would not approve the additional ten lockers for £2,800 by July 27. The issue totals do not show that 30 lockers lacked capacity, and the system has not operated at August volume. We should base any expansion on August operating records, not on June admissions alone.

## Pilot Record

The 21-day June pilot covered 6,420 admissions. One of the entrance hall's two staffed desks remained open, while 30 self-service lockers replaced the other. Visitors scanned the barcode on their admission ticket to unlock a device.

The staffed desk issued 1,106 guides, and the lockers recorded 984 successful issues. These figures describe separate service points. Because visitors were not assigned between them, they do not provide a controlled comparison of demand or visitor preference.

Timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point produced median times of 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the staffed desk. The lockers were faster within those samples. That finding does not tell us how an unstaffed locker area would perform.

Staff helped with 117 locker transactions. The recorded primary reasons were 48 unreadable ticket screens, 39 lockers that did not open on the first attempt, and 30 questions about changing the device language. Each intervention received one primary reason, so the categories should not be read as a record of every problem within a transaction.

## Operational Risks

Eleven devices went into the wrong locker. Staff found all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing: one returned the next morning, and one was never recovered. In the 21 days before the pilot, the staffed desks also had one unrecovered device, during 5,870 admissions. We do not have the earlier audio-guide issue total, so admissions alone cannot support a comparison of device loss rates.

The June schedule moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours each day. The museum did not measure whether that coverage changed visitor questions, rule compliance, or incident rates. We should not count an operational benefit that was not measured.

August presents a separate capacity risk. Average daily admissions in August 2025 were 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average, and the lockers have not been tested at that level. A full move would also remove both desks, but there is no approved staffing plan for that option.

## August Plan

Run the limited arrangement for the full month. Keep one staffed desk open and assign one employee to the lockers throughout August. Visitors who need help with ticket scanning, locker opening, language changes, or screen access can then receive it at the point of service, while the staffed desk remains an independent route to a guide.

Record successful issues, transaction times, interventions by one primary reason, wrong-locker returns, devices missing at closing, and final recovery status. Record these measures separately for the desk and lockers. Also note periods of queuing and any point at which all lockers are occupied, so we can judge whether capacity, rather than admissions alone, supports buying ten more units.

Accessibility staff should observe the locker service again in August. Unless an audio-navigation test version becomes available, we should treat independent use by visitors with low vision as unresolved. At month's end, the director can review the August record before deciding whether to expand, redesign, or end the locker service.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P5_R1_C1 -->

</details>

````text
## Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition through August. Kestrel Museum should keep one staffed audio-guide desk open, operate the self-service lockers, and place one employee in the locker area throughout the month. We should defer a decision on replacing both desks or abandoning the lockers until we have evidence from peak-volume operation, an accessible interface, and an approved staffing plan for a full move.

The June pilot supports continued testing. Locker transactions took less than half the median time of staffed-desk transactions in the samples. Yet staff intervened 117 times, three visitors with low vision could not use the screen without help, and the museum has no record of locker performance at August volume. A staffed desk preserves another service point while we test those conditions.

## Pilot Record

For 21 days in June, the museum replaced one of its two entrance-hall desks with 30 lockers and kept the other desk staffed. Visitors scanned their admission-ticket barcode to unlock a device. The pilot covered 6,420 admissions.

The staffed desk issued 1,106 audio guides, while the lockers logged 984 successful issues. Visitors chose between separate service points; the museum did not assign them. Those totals measure use at each point, not a controlled comparison of demand.

Timestamped samples covered 300 transactions at each point. The lockers had a median transaction time of 42 seconds, compared with 96 seconds at the staffed desk. Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. They assigned one primary reason to each: 48 unreadable ticket screens, 39 lockers that did not open on the first attempt, and 30 requests for help changing the device language.

Visitors returned 11 devices to the wrong locker. Staff found all 11 before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing. A visitor returned one the next morning; staff did not recover the other. During the preceding 21 days, staff did not recover one device that they had issued from the desks. Admissions totaled 5,870. The record omits the earlier issue total, so it cannot support a comparison of device-loss rates.

The pilot moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours per day. The museum did not measure changes in visitor questions, rule compliance, or incidents, so we cannot credit the schedule change with any gallery outcome.

## Operational Risks

Average daily admissions in August 2025 exceeded the June 2026 pilot average by 41 percent. The museum has not operated the lockers at that volume. June transaction speed does not establish how the locker bank, surrounding area, or support workload will perform in August.

Museum accessibility staff observed three visitors with low vision who could not use the locker screen without assistance. The supplier plans an audio-navigation update for September, but it has supplied neither a release date nor a test version. We cannot base August service on that update.

The June interventions identify support needs for ticket-screen scanning, locker opening, and language selection. Return errors and missing devices also require reconciliation at closing. The pilot and prior period each had one unrecovered device, but the records cover different admission volumes and omit the earlier issue total. They do not establish equal loss risk.

A full move would remove both staffed desks. Museum managers have approved no staffing plan for that option, and the June pilot tested a mixed arrangement.

## August Plan

Authorize the supplier to add ten lockers by July 27 for £2,800. Keep one staffed desk open and assign one employee to the locker area throughout August. Give that employee a response protocol for the three intervention categories, accessibility assistance, wrong-locker returns, and missing devices.

Visitor Operations should record admissions, successful issues at each point, transaction-time samples, interventions by one primary reason, requests for accessibility help, wrong-locker returns, and devices missing at closing. Staff should reconcile each locker at closing and record next-day returns without changing the closing count.

We should review the August record after the month ends. Before full replacement, the director should approve a staffing plan, and accessibility staff should test the supplier’s update. Until those conditions exist, the mixed arrangement should remain the museum’s operating model.
````

````text
Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition through August: keep one staffed audio-guide desk open, operate the self-service lockers, and place one employee in the locker area. We should neither replace both desks now nor abandon the lockers. The June pilot found a shorter median transaction time at the lockers, but it did not test the system at August volume or allow a controlled comparison of demand. It also recorded three visitors with low vision who could not use the locker screen independently. A full move would remove both staffed desks, and there is no approved staffing plan for that option.

Pilot Record

The museum currently issues audio guides from two staffed desks in the entrance hall. For the 21-day June pilot, one desk remained staffed while 30 self-service lockers replaced the other. Visitors scanned the barcode on their admission ticket to release a device.

The pilot covered 6,420 admissions. Staffed-desk records show 1,106 audio-guide issues, while locker logs show 984 successful issues. These totals came from separate service points, and visitors were not assigned between them. They therefore do not provide a controlled comparison of demand.

Timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point put the median transaction time at 42 seconds for lockers and 96 seconds for the staffed desk. Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. The primary recorded reason was an unreadable ticket screen in 48 cases, a locker failing to open on the first attempt in 39, and a request for help changing the device language in 30. Each intervention received one primary reason.

Eleven devices were returned to the wrong locker; staff found all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing during the pilot. One came back the next morning, and one was not recovered. In the 21 days before the pilot, one device issued from the staffed desks was not recovered, during a period with 5,870 admissions.

The pilot schedule moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours each day. The museum did not measure whether that coverage changed visitor questions, rule compliance, or incident rates.

Operational Risks

The supplier plans an audio-navigation update for September but has supplied neither a release date nor a test version. August operations cannot depend on that update to address the access problem observed by accessibility staff.

Capacity is also untested. August is the museum's busiest month, and average daily admissions in August 2025 were 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average. The lockers have not operated at that volume. The June record also shows that locker transactions sometimes needed staff help and that wrong-locker returns required same-day recovery work. It does not establish how those demands will change in August.

August Plan

Visitor Operations should use August as a limited, staffed transition. We can keep one staffed desk open and assign one employee to the locker area throughout the month. The desk will preserve a staffed route, including for visitors who cannot independently use the screen, while the locker employee handles ticket-screen problems, first-attempt opening failures, language questions, and return checks.

I recommend authorizing the supplier to add ten lockers by July 27 for £2,800, raising the total from 30 to 40. The addition increases capacity for August without committing the museum to removing the remaining desk; the record does not establish that 40 lockers will be sufficient.

For August, Visitor Operations should retain separate counts for staffed-desk issues and successful locker issues, sample transaction times at both points, and record locker interventions by one primary reason. We should also record wrong-locker returns, devices missing at closing, later returns, and unrecovered devices. Accessibility staff should observe independent use by visitors with low vision. At month's end, we can assess the operating record before deciding whether to expand, retain, or end locker service.
````

````text
To: Museum Director  
From: Rowan Blake, Visitor Operations Manager

# Recommendation

Kestrel Museum should run a limited transition rather than replace both staffed audio-guide desks or abandon the lockers. Throughout August, we should operate the 30 self-service lockers alongside one staffed desk and station one employee in the locker area. This arrangement preserves a staffed route for visitors who need or prefer assistance while allowing us to test self-service under the museum’s busiest conditions.

The June pilot shows that lockers can issue devices more quickly than the staffed desk: median transaction time was 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the desk. It does not, however, establish that lockers can replace both desks. Visitors were not assigned between the two service points, so the separate issue totals are not a controlled comparison of demand. The system has also not been tested at August volume, and there is no approved staffing plan for a full move to lockers.

We should not abandon the lockers because they completed 984 issues during the pilot and supported faster sampled transactions. We should also defer the proposed £2,800 addition of ten lockers. The current record does not show that 30-locker capacity constrained service, and adding units before the August test would not address the recorded intervention, accessibility, or staffing questions.

# Pilot Record

During the 21-day June pilot, one of the entrance hall’s two staffed desks remained open while the other was replaced with 30 lockers. Visitors unlocked devices by scanning the barcode on their admission tickets. Across 6,420 admissions, the staffed desk issued 1,106 guides and the lockers completed 984 successful issues.

Timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point produced the median times of 96 seconds at the desk and 42 seconds at the lockers. These figures support a comparison of transaction speed within the samples. The issue totals do not demonstrate relative visitor preference or capacity because the museum did not allocate visitors between the service points.

Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. The recorded primary reasons were 48 unreadable ticket screens, 39 lockers that did not open on the first attempt, and 30 requests for help changing device language. Because only one primary reason was recorded for each intervention, the figures do not establish whether any transaction involved additional difficulties.

Eleven devices were returned to the wrong locker; staff found all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing. One returned the next morning, while one was not recovered. In the preceding 21-day period, when 5,870 visitors used the museum and guides were issued from staffed desks, one device was not recovered. The differing admission totals and service arrangements limit what can be concluded from that comparison.

# Operational Risks

August demand is the principal operational uncertainty. Daily admissions in August 2025 averaged 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average. Neither the locker system nor an unstaffed locker-only arrangement has been tested at that volume.

The intervention record also shows a continuing need for nearby assistance with ticket scanning, locker opening, and language selection. Accessibility staff reported that three visitors with low vision could not use the locker screen independently. The supplier’s planned audio-navigation update may address relevant functionality, but no release date or test version has been supplied. We therefore cannot base August operations on its availability or effectiveness.

The pilot moved one employee to the gallery floor for four hours daily, but the museum did not measure changes in visitor questions, rule compliance, or incident rates. No operational benefit should be attributed to that reassignment without evidence. Likewise, removing both desks would require an approved staffing plan that the current record does not contain.

# August Plan

We should keep one staffed desk open, operate the existing 30 lockers, and assign one employee continuously to the locker area throughout August. Visitors unable to use the locker independently, including those encountering accessibility barriers, should be served at the staffed desk or assisted by the assigned employee.

Visitor Operations should record daily admissions, successful locker issues, staffed-desk issues, sampled transaction times, interventions by the same primary-reason categories, wrong-locker returns, and devices missing at closing or unrecovered. We should also document instances in which visitors cannot use the screen independently.

At the end of August, we can assess performance at peak volume and decide whether expansion, redesign, abandonment, or a further limited transition is supported. Any later proposal for full replacement should include tested accessibility arrangements, evidence from August operations, and an approved staffing plan.
````

````text
To: Museum Director  
From: Rowan Blake, Visitor Operations Manager

## Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition, not a full replacement of the two staffed audio-guide desks and not abandonment of the lockers. The June pilot shows that lockers can issue devices faster than the staffed desk, but it does not establish that they can carry all demand or operate without nearby staff. August is the wrong month to make that leap. It is our busiest month, the system has not been tested at that volume, and we have no approved staffing plan for removing both desks. We should keep one staffed desk, run the lockers with dedicated support, and use August to collect the evidence needed for a later decision.

## Pilot Record

The museum currently issues audio guides from two staffed desks in the entrance hall. For 21 days in June, we kept one desk staffed and replaced the other with 30 self-service lockers. Visitors opened a locker by scanning the barcode on their admission ticket.

The pilot covered 6,420 admissions. The staffed desk issued 1,106 guides, while the lockers recorded 984 successful issues. These were separate service points, and visitors were not assigned between them. The totals therefore do not provide a controlled comparison of demand.

The stronger comparison is transaction time. In timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point, the median was 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the desk. Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions: 48 for unreadable ticket screens, 39 when a locker did not open on the first attempt, and 30 when visitors asked how to change the device language. Staff recorded one primary reason for each intervention.

Eleven devices were returned to the wrong locker. Staff found all 11 before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing; one came back the next morning and one was not recovered. During the preceding 21 days, one device issued from the staffed desks was not recovered, from 5,870 admissions.

## Operational Risks

Accessibility is the clearest reason not to remove staffed service. Accessibility staff observed three visitors with low vision who could not use the locker screen independently. The supplier plans an audio-navigation update for September, but has supplied neither a release date nor a test version. We cannot treat that update as available for August planning.

Volume is the second concern. Average daily admissions in August 2025 were 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average. The lockers have not been tested under that demand. The June intervention record also shows that self-service still required staff assistance, although it does not tell us what intervention rate to expect in August.

The loss record does not justify a conclusion that either service point is more secure. Each 21-day period ended with one unrecovered device, but admissions differed, and the June pilot also included a device returned the following morning. Likewise, the 11 wrong-locker returns were resolved the same day, but they show a task staff had to manage.

The pilot moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours daily. We did not measure changes in visitor questions, rule compliance, or incident rates, so no operational benefit from that coverage can be claimed.

## August Plan

Keep one staffed desk open and assign one employee to the locker area throughout August, as Visitor Operations can support. Do not remove the second staffed service point permanently. This arrangement preserves an assisted route, gives immediate help with scanning, opening lockers, and language changes, and lets us observe the system at peak volume.

I recommend adding the ten lockers available by July 27 for £2,800. That would take the bank to 40 units before August while retaining the staffed desk and dedicated locker support. During August, record admissions, issues at each point, transaction times, interventions by one primary reason, wrong-locker returns, devices missing at closing, later returns, and unrecovered devices. Also record when visitors cannot use the lockers independently, including low-vision access. We should review the August record, and any testable accessibility update, before deciding whether to expand, retain, or abandon the locker model.
````

````text
## Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition through August: keep one staffed audio-guide desk open, retain the 30 lockers, and assign one Visitor Operations employee to the locker area. We should not replace both desks or abandon the lockers on the June record.

The lockers handled 984 issues and cut the median transaction time from 96 seconds at the staffed desk to 42 seconds. Those results justify more use. They do not justify a full conversion before our busiest month. A full conversion would also remove both staffed desks, yet we have no approved staffing plan for that model.

I do not recommend buying ten more lockers for £2,800 by July 27. The pilot did not establish that the 30-locker bank lacked capacity. We should use August to test the mixed service model, document demand and service failures, and decide on any expansion from that evidence.

## Pilot Record

For 21 days in June, we kept one of the two entrance-hall desks staffed and replaced the other with 30 lockers. Visitors scanned the barcode on an admission ticket to unlock a device. Across 6,420 admissions, the staffed desk issued 1,106 guides and the lockers issued 984.

We did not assign visitors to either point, so these totals do not provide a controlled comparison of demand. Transaction samples offer a narrower comparison: timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point produced medians of 42 seconds for lockers and 96 seconds for the desk.

Staff recorded one primary reason for each of 117 interventions. Unreadable ticket screens accounted for 48, lockers that did not open on the first attempt for 39, and language-change questions for 30. Eleven visitors returned devices to the wrong locker. Staff found all eleven before closing that day.

Two devices remained missing at closing during the pilot. One came back the next morning; staff did not recover the other. In the 21 days before the pilot, staff failed to recover one device issued from the desks. That period had 5,870 admissions, and the record gives no audio-guide issue total for it, so we cannot compare loss rates.

## Operational Risks

August 2025 daily admissions averaged 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average. The lockers have not faced that volume. June transaction times suggest a speed advantage, but they do not show how queues, interventions, or returns will behave in August.

The accessibility gap requires staffed support. Three visitors with low vision could not operate the locker screen independently. The supplier plans an audio-navigation update for September but has supplied neither a release date nor a test version. We should not base August service on that update.

The pilot moved one employee from the replaced desk to the galleries for four hours each day. We did not measure any change in visitor questions, rule compliance, or incidents. Assigning an employee to the locker area in August means we cannot count those four gallery hours as a demonstrated benefit of the mixed model.

## August Plan

Visitor Operations will run the 30 lockers beside one staffed desk and place one employee at the lockers throughout August. The employee will help with ticket scans, failed openings, language changes, accessibility needs, and incorrect returns.

We will record admissions, issues at each service point, transaction times, interventions by one primary reason, queue observations, wrong-locker returns, and devices missing at closing. We will also record whether and when missing devices return. Accessibility staff will document cases in which visitors cannot use the lockers independently.

After August, I will compare the mixed model with the June pilot on transaction time and intervention patterns, while keeping the uncontrolled service-point demand totals separate. I will bring you a recommendation on locker expansion, desk staffing, and any broader transition after we have August-volume results and firm information on the supplier’s accessibility update.
````

````text
# Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition through August: retain one staffed audio-guide desk, continue operating the 30 self-service lockers, and station one employee in the locker area. We should neither replace both staffed desks nor abandon the lockers on the present evidence.

The lockers completed transactions faster in the June samples, but they still required frequent staff intervention and were not independently usable by three visitors with low vision. Conversely, abandoning the lockers would discard a service point that successfully issued 984 guides and had a substantially shorter sampled median transaction time.

I do not recommend purchasing the additional ten lockers for £2,800 by July 27. The record does not establish that 30 lockers are insufficient, and adding capacity would not address the documented screen-accessibility and assistance requirements. August should be used to test the mixed model under peak demand and produce the evidence needed for a longer-term decision.

# Pilot Record

During the 21-day June pilot, one of the museum’s two staffed entrance-hall desks remained open while the other was replaced by 30 lockers. Visitors accessed a device by scanning the barcode on their admission ticket. Across 6,420 admissions, the staffed desk issued 1,106 guides and the lockers recorded 984 successful issues.

These totals should not be treated as a controlled comparison of visitor demand or preference. They came from separate service points, and visitors were not assigned between them. The valid transaction-time evidence is narrower: in timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point, the median was 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the staffed desk.

Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. The recorded primary reasons were 48 unreadable ticket screens, 39 lockers that did not open on the first attempt, and 30 requests for help changing the device language. Because only one primary reason was recorded for each intervention, these counts do not show whether individual transactions involved additional problems.

Eleven devices were returned to the wrong locker; staff found all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing. One returned the next morning, while one was not recovered. In the preceding 21 days, when the staffed desks served 5,870 admissions, one device was not recovered. The earlier record does not provide the number of guides issued, so it cannot support a like-for-like loss-rate comparison.

The pilot also moved one employee to the gallery floor for four hours daily. No measurements were taken of changes in visitor questions, rule compliance, or incidents, so no operational benefit can be attributed to that additional coverage.

# Operational Risks

August demand is the principal uncertainty. Average daily admissions in August 2025 were 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average, and the lockers have not operated at that volume. The June timing result shows faster individual locker transactions in the sampled records, but it does not establish performance, waiting times, or capacity during August conditions.

Accessibility remains unresolved. Accessibility staff observed that three visitors with low vision could not use the locker screen independently. The supplier’s planned audio-navigation update has no release date or test version, so it should not be assumed available or effective for August.

The intervention record also shows that self-service still requires nearby human support. A full move would eliminate both staffed desks, yet no approved staffing plan exists for that arrangement. Device reconciliation requires attention as well, given the wrong-locker returns and missing devices, although the available figures do not establish that lockers have a higher loss rate than staffed service.

# August Plan

Visitor Operations should operate the existing 30 lockers alongside one staffed desk throughout August, with one employee assigned continuously to the locker area. Visitors who cannot use the lockers independently must be able to obtain a guide through the staffed desk.

For the full month, we should separately record issues at each point; timestamp transactions; count interventions using the existing primary-reason categories; and document wrong-locker returns, devices missing at closing, and final recovery status. We should also record periods when lockers are unavailable or all devices are in use, so the need for additional capacity can be assessed directly.

Accessibility staff should review locker use during August, and no full transition should be approved on the assumption that the September update will arrive. After August, we can decide whether to expand, modify, or discontinue the locker model using peak-volume evidence and an approved staffing arrangement.
````

````text
## Pilot Record

For 21 days in June, one of our two entrance-hall desks remained staffed while 30 self-service lockers replaced the other. Visitors opened a locker by scanning the barcode on an admission ticket. Across 6,420 admissions, the staffed desk issued 1,106 guides and the lockers recorded 984 successful issues.

Those totals describe activity at separate service points. We did not assign visitors to one point or the other, so they are not a controlled comparison of demand. The sound comparison is transaction time: timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point produced medians of 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the desk.

Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. The primary recorded reason was an unreadable ticket screen in 48 cases, a locker failing to open on the first attempt in 39, and a request for help changing the device language in 30. Since staff recorded one primary reason per intervention, the figures do not show whether any transaction involved more than one problem.

Eleven devices went into the wrong lockers; staff found all of them before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing. One came back the next morning, while the other was not recovered. In the preceding 21 days, when 5,870 visitors used the museum and both desks were staffed, one device was not recovered.

## Operational Risks

August 2025 admissions averaged 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot's daily average. The lockers have not been tested at that level. Faster median transactions are encouraging, but they do not establish how the system will perform under the museum's busiest conditions.

The pilot also depended on staff support. The 117 interventions and eleven wrong-locker returns required attention, even though the record does not establish how much staff time they took. Removing both desks without an approved staffing plan would therefore go beyond what we tested.

Accessibility remains a specific concern. Accessibility staff observed three visitors with low vision who could not use the locker screen independently. The supplier plans an audio-navigation update for September but has supplied neither a release date nor a test version. We cannot treat that update as available for August.

The pilot moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours daily. We did not measure any change in visitor questions, rule compliance, or incidents, so that reassignment cannot yet be counted as a demonstrated benefit.

## August Plan

Visitor Operations should open one staffed desk and place one employee at the lockers throughout August. The staffed option must remain available, including for visitors who cannot use the screen independently. Locker staff should record interventions under the same primary-reason categories, while noting any additional problem separately.

We should continue timestamped samples at both points and record daily issues, queue conditions, wrong-locker returns, devices missing at closing, and later recoveries. We should also record when all lockers are occupied or otherwise unavailable. Gallery coverage, visitor questions, rule compliance, and incidents should be tracked if an employee is reassigned there.

At the end of August, we can review performance at peak volume and decide whether more lockers are warranted. No full replacement should proceed until that review, an approved staffing plan, and independent accessibility testing of any supplier update.
````

````text
To: Museum Director  
From: Rowan Blake, Visitor Operations Manager  
Subject: Audio-guide service in August

Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition through August: keep one staffed audio-guide desk open, operate the 30 self-service lockers, and place one employee in the locker area throughout the day.

The pilot supports continued testing under supervision. Locker transactions had a median time of 42 seconds, compared with 96 seconds at the staffed desk, in timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point. The pilot schedule moved one employee to the gallery floor for four hours each day. We did not measure whether that coverage changed visitor questions, rule compliance, or incident rates, so I assign it no operational benefit.

The record does not support full replacement. Staff helped with 117 locker transactions, three visitors with low vision could not use the screen without help, and the supplier has provided no release date or test version for its planned September audio-navigation update. We also lack an approved staffing plan for removing both desks. Abandoning the lockers would end a faster service option before a peak-volume test.

Pilot Record

During the 21-day June pilot, the museum replaced one of its two entrance-hall desks with 30 lockers and kept the other desk staffed. Visitors scanned their admission-ticket barcode to unlock a device. The museum admitted 6,420 visitors. Staff issued 1,106 guides at the desk, while locker logs recorded 984 successful issues.

Those totals describe separate service points, not a controlled comparison. We did not assign visitors to either point, so the counts cannot establish relative demand or preference.

Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. The primary recorded reason was an unreadable ticket screen in 48 cases, a locker failing to open on the first attempt in 39, and a request for help changing the device language in 30. Staff recorded one primary reason per intervention.

Visitors returned eleven devices to the wrong locker. Staff found them before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing; one came back the next morning, and staff did not recover the other. In the 21 days before the pilot, the staffed desks lost one device during a period with 5,870 admissions. The periods differed in volume and service arrangement, so the losses do not establish that either method has a lower loss rate.

Operational Risks

August will put the lockers under untested conditions. Average daily admissions in August 2025 were 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average. The transaction samples establish median service time, but they do not show performance at August volume.

The intervention record requires on-site support. Ticket-screen failures, first-attempt opening failures, and language questions accounted for the 117 primary reasons. A full move would remove the staffed alternative while leaving these needs unresolved.

Accessibility staff observed three visitors with low vision who could not operate the screen without assistance. The supplier’s statement about a planned update does not resolve that barrier. Staff need a version to test and a confirmed release date before we treat audio navigation as available.

August Plan

Visitor Operations should run the mixed service for the full month. We can keep one staffed desk open and assign one employee to the locker area, as current staffing permits. That employee should help visitors use the screen, address failed opening attempts, explain language changes, and check wrong-locker returns before closing.

We should record daily admissions, successful locker issues, staffed-desk issues, interventions by one primary reason, wrong-locker returns, and devices missing at closing and at opening the next day. We should keep the service-point counts separate and avoid presenting them as a demand comparison without a controlled assignment.

I do not recommend approving ten extra lockers for £2,800 by July 27. The record identifies no June capacity shortfall and gives no basis for judging whether ten units would meet August demand. After August, we can assess service times, interventions, access failures, and device recovery under peak conditions. Any proposal to remove the final staffed desk should include an approved staffing plan and accessibility test results for the supplier’s update.
````

````text
Recommendation

Director, I recommend a limited transition: retain one staffed audio-guide desk and operate the self-service lockers alongside it through August. We should not replace both desks now. The June pilot established that locker transactions can be shorter, but it did not provide a controlled comparison of demand, test the system at August volume, or resolve the access problem reported by visitors with low vision. Abandoning the lockers would also discard the chance to test a service point with a 42-second median transaction time. I recommend approving £2,800 for ten more lockers by July 27, bringing the August bank to 40, while keeping staff immediately available at both service points.

Pilot Record

The museum currently issues audio guides from two staffed desks in the entrance hall. For the 21-day June pilot, one desk remained staffed and 30 self-service lockers replaced the other. A visitor opened a locker by scanning the barcode on an admission ticket.

The pilot covered 6,420 admissions. The staffed desk issued 1,106 guides, while locker logs recorded 984 successful issues. These totals came from separate service points, and visitors were not assigned between them. They therefore cannot support a controlled comparison of demand. In timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point, median transaction time was 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the staffed desk.

Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. The primary reason recorded for each intervention was an unreadable ticket screen in 48 cases, a locker failing to open on the first attempt in 39, and a request for help changing the device language in 30. Eleven devices were returned to the wrong locker; staff located all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing. One came back the next morning, and one was not recovered.

The 21 days before the pilot had 5,870 admissions, and one device issued from the staffed desks was not recovered. The differing periods and visitor volumes do not establish that either service point has a lower loss risk.

The pilot schedule moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours a day. The museum did not measure whether that coverage changed visitor questions, rule compliance, or incident rates, so those hours should not be counted as a demonstrated benefit of the lockers.

Operational Risks

Accessibility staff observed three visitors with low vision who could not use the locker screen independently. The supplier plans an audio-navigation update for September but has supplied neither a release date nor a test version. A full move before that update is available and tested would remove the staffed alternative despite the observed access barrier.

August is the museum’s busiest month. Average daily admissions in August 2025 were 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average, and the lockers have not been tested at that volume. The June interventions, wrong-locker returns, and missing devices also required staff attention. Removing both desks would leave those demands to a full-locker operation for which the record contains no approved staffing plan.

August Plan

Visitor Operations should keep one staffed desk open and assign one employee to the locker area throughout August, as current capacity allows. The added ten lockers should be installed by July 27 and checked before August service begins. The staffed desk should remain available to any visitor who needs or prefers assistance, including visitors unable to use the screen independently.

During August, Visitor Operations should record issues at each service point, transaction times, staff interventions using the June primary-reason categories, wrong-locker returns, devices missing at closing, and final recoveries. Records should keep the two service points separate and note daily admissions. We should also record whether visitors with low vision can complete a locker transaction independently; the planned supplier update should not be treated as available until a release date and test version are provided. At the end of August, these records can support the next decision on the size and staffing of the locker service, without assuming that separate service-point totals measure comparative demand.
````

````text
# Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition through August: retain one staffed audio-guide desk, operate the existing 30 self-service lockers, and assign one employee continuously to the locker area. We should not replace both staffed desks yet, because the system has not been tested at August volume, some visitors required accessibility assistance, and there is no approved staffing plan for a full move. We should not abandon the lockers either. Their median transaction time was substantially shorter than at the staffed desk, and the pilot provides a workable basis for further testing under controlled operating conditions.

I do not recommend purchasing the ten additional lockers by July 27. The £2,800 addition would expand capacity before we have established how the current system performs during peak demand. August should be used to assess that question and to define the support and accessibility requirements for any later expansion.

# Pilot Record

The museum currently issues audio guides from two staffed desks in the entrance hall. During the 21-day June pilot, one desk remained staffed while the other was replaced by 30 lockers. Visitors unlocked devices by scanning the barcode on their admission tickets.

The pilot covered 6,420 admissions. The staffed desk issued 1,106 guides, and the lockers recorded 984 successful issues. These figures represent separate service points, and visitors were not assigned between them. They therefore show activity at each point but do not provide a controlled comparison of demand.

Timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point produced median transaction times of 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the staffed desk. Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions: 48 for unreadable ticket screens, 39 because a locker did not open on the first attempt, and 30 for questions about changing device language. Each intervention was recorded under one primary reason.

Eleven devices were returned to the wrong locker; staff found all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing. One returned the next morning, while the other was not recovered. In the preceding 21 days, when 5,870 visitors were admitted, one device issued from the staffed desks was not recovered.

The pilot also moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours daily. We did not measure whether that coverage changed visitor questions, rule compliance, or incident rates.

# Operational Risks

August demand is the central operational risk. Average daily admissions in August 2025 were 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot average, and the lockers have not been tested at that volume. The June record does not establish whether 30 lockers can meet August demand or how often assistance will be required when the entrance hall is busier.

Accessibility remains unresolved. Accessibility staff observed three visitors with low vision who could not use the locker screen independently. The supplier plans an audio-navigation update for September but has supplied neither a release date nor a test version. We should not treat that update as available for August planning.

The intervention record also shows a continuing requirement for nearby staff support. Because only one primary reason was recorded for each intervention, the figures should not be interpreted as a complete account of every difficulty within those transactions. Device control requires attention as well, although the record does not establish that lockers have a higher loss rate than staffed issue.

# August Plan

Visitor Operations should keep one staffed desk open, operate the 30 lockers, and station one employee in the locker area throughout August. Staff should provide an accessible staffed route for any visitor unable or unwilling to use the screens and assist with ticket scanning, locker opening, and language changes.

We should record daily admissions, successful locker and desk issues, transaction samples, interventions by primary reason, misplaced returns, and devices missing at closing or subsequently recovered. We should also record periods when lockers are unavailable or capacity constrains service.

At the end of August, I will assess performance at peak volume and present a recommendation on expansion, abandonment, or a broader transition. Any proposal to remove the remaining staffed desk should include an approved staffing plan and should await evidence on accessibility and supplier delivery.

Rowan Blake  
Visitor Operations Manager
````

````text
Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition through August: keep the 30 self-service lockers, keep one audio-guide desk staffed, and place one employee in the locker area. I also recommend adding the ten lockers available by 27 July for £2,800. August admissions in 2025 averaged 41 percent above the June pilot's daily average, and the system has not operated at that volume. The added units allow a stronger August test, but they are not evidence that a full conversion will work.

We should neither replace both desks now nor abandon the lockers. The faster sampled transactions justify further use. The accessibility failure, the intervention record, and the absence of an approved staffing plan rule out removing both staffed desks. The August arrangement keeps an assisted route available while we collect evidence under the museum's busiest conditions.

Pilot Record

For 21 days in June, one of the two entrance-hall desks remained staffed and 30 lockers replaced the other. Visitors opened a locker by scanning the barcode on an admission ticket. Across 6,420 admissions, the staffed desk issued 1,106 guides and the lockers completed 984 issues. These totals came from separate service points, and visitors were not assigned between them. They therefore do not provide a controlled comparison of demand.

Timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point produced a median of 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the staffed desk. Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions: 48 for unreadable ticket screens, 39 when a locker did not open on the first attempt, and 30 when visitors asked how to change the device language. Staff recorded one primary reason for each intervention.

Eleven devices went back to the wrong locker. Staff found all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing; one returned the next morning and one was not recovered. In the 21 days before the pilot, when admissions totalled 5,870, one device issued from the staffed desks was not recovered.

The pilot moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours a day. We did not measure any change in visitor questions, rule compliance, or incidents, so the record does not establish a benefit from that move.

Operational Risks

The most immediate concern is independent access. Accessibility staff observed that three visitors with low vision could not use the locker screen independently. The supplier's planned audio-navigation update may address the interface, but there is no release date or test version. We cannot base an August operating model on it.

Higher visitor volume is the second concern. The June trial does not tell us how the lockers, entrance hall, or intervention workload will perform at August levels. The 117 interventions show that self-service still required regular staff help. Wrong-locker returns and devices missing at closing also require daily control, although the record does not establish that lockers create a higher loss rate than staffed issue.

A full move would remove both staffed desks, yet no approved staffing plan exists. It would also remove the current alternative for visitors who cannot operate the screen independently.

August Plan

By 27 July, add ten lockers and confirm that all 40 can issue and accept returns. Throughout August, operate one staffed desk and assign one employee to the locker area. Direct any visitor who cannot use the locker independently to the staffed desk. Do not count this model as a staffing reduction or assume it will provide the four hours of added gallery coverage used in June.

Record daily issues separately for the desk and lockers, while making clear that visitors remain free to choose either point. Continue logging interventions by one primary reason, wrong-locker returns, devices missing at closing, and later returns. Take timestamped transaction samples at both points during August and record each occasion when a visitor cannot use the screen independently.

At the end of August, review performance before deciding on any further replacement of staffed service. Test the supplier's audio-navigation update only when a version is provided. No full conversion should be proposed until it has been tested, August operating data have been reviewed, and a staffing plan has been approved.
````

````text
To: Museum Director  
From: Rowan Blake, Visitor Operations Manager

## Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition through August: keep one staffed audio-guide desk open, operate the 30 self-service lockers, and assign one employee to the locker area. Do not add ten lockers before we have tested the current system at peak demand. Do not remove the second staffed desk as a permanent measure yet.

The June pilot established that lockers can issue guides faster than the staffed desk, but it did not test full replacement. Visitors chose between two separate service points, staff supported 117 locker transactions, and the system has not faced August volume. We also lack an approved staffing plan for a full move. Abandoning the lockers would discard a service option that completed 984 issues with a lower median transaction time.

## Pilot Record

For 21 days in June, the museum kept one entrance-hall desk staffed and replaced the other with 30 lockers. Visitors scanned the barcode on an admission ticket to unlock a device. Across 6,420 admissions, the staffed desk issued 1,106 guides and the lockers completed 984 issues.

Those totals do not provide a controlled comparison of demand. The museum did not assign visitors to either point, so we cannot use the totals to conclude which service visitors preferred or how many issues each format would handle under equal conditions.

Timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point produced a median time of 42 seconds for lockers and 96 seconds for the desk. Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions: 48 for unreadable ticket screens, 39 when a locker did not open on the first attempt, and 30 when visitors asked how to change the device language. Staff recorded one primary reason for each intervention.

Eleven devices went back to the wrong lockers. Staff found all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing; one came back the next morning and one remained missing. In the preceding 21 days, the staffed desks also lost one device, during 5,870 admissions.

The pilot moved one employee to the gallery floor for four hours per day. The museum collected no evidence on whether that coverage changed visitor questions, rule compliance, or incidents.

## Operational Risks

Accessibility presents the clearest barrier to full replacement. Accessibility staff observed three visitors with low vision who could not use the locker screen without help. The supplier plans an audio-navigation update for September but has supplied neither a release date nor a test version. We should not base an August operating model on that update.

August demand creates a second risk. Average daily admissions in August 2025 exceeded the June 2026 pilot average by 41 percent, and the lockers have not operated at that volume. The pilot also depended on staff intervention. Removing both desks without an approved staffing plan would leave unanswered questions about assistance, accessibility, and coverage.

The loss record does not establish that either service model protects devices better. Each 21-day period ended with one unrecovered device, but admissions differed and the pilot produced eleven wrong-locker returns that staff corrected the same day.

## August Plan

Visitor Operations should run the existing 30 lockers beside one staffed desk for all of August. One employee should remain in the locker area to help with ticket screens, failed openings, language changes, returns, and access needs. The desk should serve visitors who cannot or do not use the lockers.

We should defer the £2,800 addition of ten lockers. The supplier can install them by July 27, but the June record does not show that 30 lockers lack capacity. August will give us the first evidence at the museum’s busiest-month volume.

During August, Visitor Operations should record admissions, issues by service point, transaction times, interventions by one primary reason, wrong-locker returns, devices missing at closing, and final losses. Staff should also record when a visitor cannot use the lockers without help. At month end, we can decide whether to expand, revise, or abandon the transition using peak-volume results and the status of the accessibility update.
````
