````text
## Recommendation

I recommend a limited transition through August: retain one staffed audio-guide desk, keep the 30 self-service lockers in service, and station one employee at the locker area. This keeps an assisted route available while the system is tested during the museum’s busiest month. I do not recommend replacing both desks now. There is no approved staffing plan for a full move, and the June pilot did not test the locker system at August volume.

I also recommend that we do not commit £2,800 for ten more lockers by July 27. The available issue totals do not establish demand at either service point, so they do not provide a sound basis for deciding that 40 lockers are needed. We should use the August operating record to decide whether further locker capacity is warranted.

## Pilot Record

The museum currently issues audio guides from two staffed desks in the entrance hall. For the 21-day June pilot, one desk remained staffed and 30 self-service lockers replaced the other. Visitors scanned the barcode on their admission ticket to unlock a device.

The pilot covered 6,420 admissions. The staffed desk recorded 1,106 audio-guide issues, while locker logs recorded 984 successful issues. These totals came from separate service points, and visitors were not assigned between them. They therefore cannot be treated as a controlled comparison of demand.

In timestamped samples of 300 transactions at each point, median transaction time was 42 seconds at the lockers and 96 seconds at the staffed desk. The result establishes the difference in those samples, but not how either point would perform under August demand.

The pilot schedule moved one employee from the replaced desk to the gallery floor for four hours each day. The museum did not measure whether that coverage changed visitor questions, rule compliance, or incident rates.

## Operational Risks

Staff intervened in 117 locker transactions. Each intervention was recorded under one primary reason: an unreadable ticket screen in 48 cases, a locker that did not open on the first attempt in 39, or a visitor asking how to change the device language in 30.

Eleven devices were returned to the wrong locker. Staff found all eleven before closing that day. Two devices were missing at closing during the pilot; one came back the next morning and one was not recovered.

In the 21 days before the pilot, admissions totaled 5,870 and one device issued from the staffed desks was not recovered. The periods differ in visitor volume and service arrangement, so these records do not establish that either issue method has a lower loss risk.

Accessibility staff observed that three visitors with low vision could not use the locker screen independently. The supplier plans an audio-navigation update for September but has supplied neither a release date nor a test version. August operations must therefore proceed without relying on that update.

August 2025 daily admissions averaged 41 percent above the June 2026 pilot daily average. The system has not operated at that volume. Removing both desks would also leave operations without an approved staffing plan.

## August Plan

Visitor Operations can keep one staffed desk open and assign one employee to the locker area throughout August. The staffed desk will provide an issue route for visitors who cannot use the screen independently and for those who choose staffed service. The locker employee will assist with ticket screens, opening failures, and language changes, using the same primary-reason categories applied during the pilot.

During August, we should record issues at each service point, transaction-time samples, locker interventions by primary reason, devices returned to the wrong locker, and devices missing at closing or not recovered. We should also separately record visitor questions, rule compliance, and incidents during the four hours of added gallery coverage if that schedule continues. At month end, Visitor Operations can report these results alongside daily admissions and make a separate proposal on locker numbers, staffing, and whether either staffed desk should be removed.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening electricity rate from October 1

# Understanding your new Home Flex rates

From October 1, 2026, Alder Energy is introducing a new evening electricity rate for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter. This newsletter explains when each price applies, what to expect on your first bill, and the options available if you would prefer to change plans.

## When the rates apply

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). Electricity used at all other times will cost 21 pence per kWh.

At weekends and on public holidays, the 21-pence rate applies throughout the day. Your standing charge will not change.

The applicable rate is determined by when electricity is used. It is not based on when an appliance is started, when it finishes, or when your bill is issued. For example, if an appliance runs across the start or end of the weekday evening period, the electricity it uses within each time band will be charged at the rate for that band.

To illustrate the difference, a household using 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity:

6 kWh × 34p = £2.04

The same 6 kWh used outside that weekday period—or at any time during a weekend or public holiday—would cost £1.26:

6 kWh × 21p = £1.26

These examples cover electricity usage only and exclude the standing charge. Alder Energy has not estimated how much any individual household will save or spend under the new rate. The effect on your bill will depend on how much electricity your household uses in each time band.

## How your first bill will work

Your first bill after October 1 may include electricity used both before and after the new rate begins. Any usage before October 1 will be charged under your previous tariff. Eligible usage from October 1 onward will be divided between the 34-pence weekday evening rate and the 21-pence rate according to when the electricity was used.

You can review your half-hour electricity usage in the Alder Energy app. This lets you see which time band applies to each period. You can also download your usage information as a CSV file if you would like to review it separately.

Usage data normally appears in the app by noon on the following day. If smart-meter data is delayed, it may take up to 72 hours to appear. A delay in the app does not change the time band used to calculate the charge.

Customers without a working smart meter will remain on their existing tariff until a meter is installed or repaired. The new evening rate cannot be applied without the required smart-meter usage data.

## Your options

You can remain on Home Flex and pay the new rates from October 1 if you have a working smart meter. Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances; decisions about when and how they operate remain entirely with your household.

You may also leave Home Flex without paying an exit fee. If you request a switch to another plan before September 20, the new plan will be applied by October 1. Requests made later may take up to ten working days to complete, so the Home Flex rates could apply until your switch is processed.

Before choosing, you can use the app or your downloaded CSV data to understand how much electricity your household has used during the weekday 4 p.m. to 8 p.m. period. Your available choices are to continue with Home Flex or request a move to another Alder Energy plan.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P6_R1_C0 -->

</details>

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex electricity rates are changing on October 1

# Your new Home Flex evening rate, explained

Hello,

From October 1, 2026, Alder Energy is changing the electricity rates for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter.

This email explains when each rate applies, what you may see on your first bill, and what you can do before the change. Your standing charge will stay the same.

## When you will pay each rate

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). At all other times, the rate will be 21 pence per kWh.

The 21-pence rate applies all day on Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays.

The rate is based on when you use the electricity. It does not depend on when you start an appliance or when we issue your bill. For example, if an appliance runs across the start or end of the weekday evening period, the electricity used during each part of its cycle will be charged at the rate that applies at that time.

To show the difference, a household using 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity:

6 kWh × 34p = £2.04

The same 6 kWh used outside the weekday evening period would cost £1.26:

6 kWh × 21p = £1.26

These examples do not include the standing charge. Alder Energy has not estimated whether an individual household will spend more or less under the new rates. The effect will depend on how much electricity the household uses and when it uses it.

You can check your half-hour electricity usage in the Alder Energy app. You can also download the information as a CSV file if you prefer to review it elsewhere. Usage data normally appears in the app by noon the following day. If meter data is delayed, it may take up to 72 hours to appear.

## What to expect on your first bill

Your first bill after October 1 may cover electricity used both before and after the new rates begin.

Any electricity used before October 1 will be charged under your previous tariff. Electricity used from October 1 onward will be charged at the new Home Flex rates according to the day and time it was used. The standing charge will not change.

Your smart meter readings allow us to separate usage across the change date and apply the appropriate rate. Customers who do not have a working smart meter will remain on their existing tariff until their meter is installed or repaired. The new Home Flex evening rate will not apply to them in the meantime.

## Your choices

You can remain on Home Flex and review your half-hour usage in the app once the new rates take effect. Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances. You remain in control of when and how you use them.

You may also leave Home Flex without paying an exit fee. If you ask to switch plans before September 20, we will apply your new plan by October 1. Requests made after September 20 may take up to ten working days to complete, so the Home Flex rates could apply for part of that period.

Before choosing another plan, check its electricity rates, standing charge, and terms so you can compare them with Home Flex. We cannot predict which option will cost less for your household.

Thank you,

The Alder Energy team
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex electricity rates change on 1 October

# Your new evening electricity rate

From 1 October 2026, Alder Energy will introduce new electricity prices for residential customers who have a working smart meter and use our Home Flex plan. Your standing charge will stay the same.

This newsletter explains the times and prices, how we will calculate your first bill after the change, and the options you have before the new rate starts.

## When the prices apply

On weekdays, electricity you use from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). Electricity you use at all other times will cost 21 pence per kWh.

At weekends and on public holidays, you will pay 21 pence per kWh all day. The 34-pence price applies only during the weekday evening period.

We base the price on the time you use each unit of electricity. The time you start an appliance does not set the price for its full cycle. If an appliance uses electricity both inside and outside the 4 p.m. to 8 p.m. period, we will charge each portion at the price that applies when you use it. The date when we issue your bill also has no effect on the rate.

For example, a household that uses 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity:

6 kWh × 34p = £2.04

The same 6 kWh used outside that period would cost £1.26:

6 kWh × 21p = £1.26

Both examples exclude the standing charge. Alder Energy has not estimated how much your household will spend or save under the new prices. Your cost will depend on how much electricity you use and when you use it.

Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances. You remain in control of when and how you use them.

## How your first bill will work

Your first bill after 1 October may cover electricity used on both sides of the change date. We will charge usage before 1 October under your previous tariff. We will charge usage from 1 October at the new Home Flex prices, according to the day and time you used the electricity. Your standing charge will not change.

You can check the calculation against your smart-meter records. The Alder Energy app shows your electricity usage in half-hour periods, and you can download the same data as a CSV file. App data usually appears by noon on the following day. If we receive delayed meter data, it can take up to 72 hours to appear.

Customers without a working smart meter will stay on their existing tariff. The new Home Flex rate will apply after Alder Energy installs or repairs the meter, subject to the plan terms in effect at that time.

## Your choices

You can remain on Home Flex and pay the new prices from 1 October if you have a working smart meter. You can use the app or CSV download to review the half-hour usage that Alder Energy uses to calculate your charges.

You can also choose another Alder Energy plan. If you ask us to switch your plan before 20 September, we will apply the new plan by 1 October. Requests made on or after 20 September may take up to ten working days to complete. During that processing period, the tariff active on each date will determine your charges.

You may leave Home Flex without paying an exit fee. Before choosing a plan, compare its unit rates, standing charge, eligibility rules and any other terms with the way your household uses electricity.

If your smart meter is not working, contact Alder Energy so we can arrange a repair or replacement. Until then, you will remain on your existing tariff.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening electricity rate from 1 October

# Your new Home Flex rates, times and choices

From 1 October 2026, Alder Energy’s new evening electricity rate will apply to residential customers who are on the Home Flex plan and have a working smart meter. Your standing charge will not change.

The new rates affect what you pay for each kilowatt-hour (kWh) of electricity. We have not estimated whether an individual household will save or spend more under the new rate. The result will depend on how much electricity that household uses in each time band.

## When each rate applies

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kWh, while electricity used at all other times will cost 21 pence per kWh. At weekends and on public holidays, the 21-pence rate applies all day.

The price is based on when the electricity is used. It is not based on when an appliance was started or when your bill is issued. For example, if an appliance runs across the start of the weekday evening period, electricity used before 4 p.m. is charged at 21 pence per kWh, while electricity used from 4 p.m. is charged at 34 pence per kWh. If it continues running after 8 p.m., electricity used after that time returns to 21 pence per kWh.

A household using 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity. The same 6 kWh used outside that period would cost £1.26. Both examples exclude the standing charge.

## What to expect on your first bill

Your first bill issued after 1 October may include electricity used both before and after the rate change. Any usage before 1 October will be charged under your previous tariff. Eligible usage from 1 October onward will be divided between the 34-pence weekday evening rate and the 21-pence rate according to the time it was used. Your bill may therefore show your previous tariff as well as the two new Home Flex rates. Electricity used on 30 September and 1 October could appear on the same bill at different rates.

You can check how the calculation relates to your household’s usage in the Alder Energy app. The app shows usage in half-hour periods, and you can also download the same usage data as a CSV file. Data normally appears by noon on the following day. If meter data is delayed, it can take up to 72 hours to appear.

Customers who do not have a working smart meter will stay on their existing tariff until a smart meter is installed or repaired. The new time bands will not be applied to those customers while the meter is not working.

## Your plan choices

You can remain on Home Flex and pay the new rates from 1 October if you have a working smart meter. Alder Energy will use your smart meter readings to assign electricity usage to the correct time band. We will not remotely control your appliances.

You can also leave Home Flex without paying an exit fee. If you request a switch to another plan before 20 September, the new plan will be applied by 1 October. Requests made later may take up to ten working days, so a later switch might take effect after the new Home Flex rate begins.

Before choosing whether to stay or switch, you can review your half-hour usage in the app or download the CSV file. This lets you see how much electricity your household has used during the weekday 4 p.m. to 8 p.m. period and at other times.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening electricity rate starts October 1

# Your new Home Flex rates, clearly explained

From October 1, 2026, Alder Energy is introducing a new evening electricity rate for residential customers on our Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter. This newsletter explains when each rate applies, what to expect on your first bill, and the choices available to you.

Customers without a working smart meter will remain on their existing tariff until their meter is installed or repaired. Standing charges are not changing.

## When the rates apply

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). Electricity used at all other times will cost 21 pence per kWh.

Weekends and public holidays are charged at 21 pence per kWh throughout the day. This means that the 34-pence rate applies only during the weekday evening period.

The applicable rate is based on when the electricity is used. It is not based on when an appliance is first started or when your bill is issued. For example, if an appliance runs across the start or end of the evening period, the electricity used during each part of its operation will be charged at the rate applying at that time.

To illustrate the difference, a household using 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity:

6 kWh × 34p = £2.04

The same 6 kWh used outside that weekday evening period, or at any time on a weekend or public holiday, would cost £1.26:

6 kWh × 21p = £1.26

These examples cover electricity usage only and exclude the standing charge.

Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances. You remain in control of when and how you use electricity. We have not estimated whether an individual household will save or spend more under the new rate, as the result will depend on that household’s own usage at the different times.

## Your first bill and usage information

Your first bill issued after October 1 may include electricity used both before and after the new rate begins. Any usage before October 1 will be charged according to your previous tariff. Eligible usage from October 1 onward will be divided between the 34-pence and 21-pence rates according to when the electricity was used.

The standing charge will continue to appear separately and will not change as part of this update.

You can review your half-hour electricity usage in the Alder Energy app. This can help you see which usage falls within the weekday 4 p.m. to 8 p.m. period and which usage falls outside it. You can also download your half-hour usage information as a CSV file.

Usage data normally appears in the app by noon on the following day. If meter data is delayed, it may take up to 72 hours to appear. A delay in the app does not change the rate that applies: billing will still be based on the time the electricity was used.

## Your available choices

You can remain on Home Flex and have the new rates applied automatically from October 1, provided your smart meter is working.

You may also choose to leave Home Flex, and there is no exit fee for doing so. If you request a switch to another plan before September 20, Alder Energy will apply the new plan by October 1. Requests made later may take up to ten working days to complete. During that processing period, the tariff active on each date will apply.

Before choosing, you can review the plan information alongside your own half-hour usage in the app or downloaded CSV file. This allows you to compare the rate periods with your household’s recorded electricity use. Whatever you decide, Alder Energy will not remotely operate or schedule any of your appliances.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex electricity rate is changing on 1 October

# Your new evening electricity rate, explained

Dear customer,

From 1 October 2026, Alder Energy is introducing a new electricity rate for residential customers on our Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter. The price you pay for each kilowatt-hour (kWh) will depend on the time and day you use the electricity.

This email explains the two prices, what you will see on your first bill after the change, and the options open to you. Your standing charge will stay the same.

## When the two prices apply

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kWh. Electricity used at all other times will cost 21 pence per kWh. At weekends and on public holidays, the 21 pence rate will apply all day.

The price is based on when the electricity is used. It is not based on when you start an appliance, and it has nothing to do with the date your bill is issued. For example, if an appliance runs across 4 p.m. on a weekday, electricity used before 4 p.m. will be charged at 21 pence per kWh, while electricity used from 4 p.m. will be charged at 34 pence per kWh.

Here is a simple cost comparison. A household using 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity. The same 6 kWh used outside that period would cost £1.26. Both figures exclude the standing charge.

## Your first bill and your usage data

Your first bill after 1 October may cover electricity used both before and after the new rate begins. We will charge any usage before 1 October under your previous tariff. Usage from 1 October onward will be charged at the Home Flex rate that applies at the time it was used. Your unchanged standing charge will also appear on the bill as usual.

You can review your half-hour electricity usage in the Alder Energy app. This can help you match the time shown for each reading with the rate charged. If you prefer to work with the figures yourself, you can download the same usage data as a CSV file.

App data normally appears by noon on the following day. If meter data is delayed, it may take up to 72 hours to appear. A delay in the app does not change the time at which the electricity was used or the rate that applies to it.

If your smart meter is not working, you will stay on your existing tariff until the meter has been installed or repaired. The new Home Flex rate will not apply until we can receive the meter data needed to charge by time of use.

## Your choices before October

You can stay on Home Flex, or you can ask to move to another available plan. There is no exit fee for leaving Home Flex. If you request a plan switch before 20 September, we will apply your chosen plan by 1 October. A request made later may take up to ten working days to complete.

Before deciding, you can use the app or your downloaded CSV file to see when your household has used electricity in the past. Alder Energy has not estimated whether an individual household will spend more or less under the new rate. The result will depend on that household's metered usage at each price.

The choice remains yours. Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances, whether you stay on Home Flex or switch to another plan.

Thank you,

Alder Energy
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening electricity rate from 1 October

# Home Flex rates are changing on 1 October

Dear customer,

From 1 October 2026, Alder Energy will introduce a new evening electricity rate for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter. Your standing charge will stay the same.

If your home does not have a working smart meter, you will remain on your existing tariff until Alder Energy installs or repairs one. We need smart-meter readings to place your electricity use in the correct time band.

## When the rates apply

On weekdays, Alder Energy will charge 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh) for electricity you use from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. We will charge 21 pence per kWh at all other times.

On Saturdays, Sundays and public holidays, the 21-pence rate applies all day. The calendar day and time when you use electricity determine the rate. The time when you start an appliance does not determine the rate, and neither does the date when we issue your bill.

If an appliance runs across 4 p.m., for example, we will charge the electricity used before 4 p.m. at 21 pence per kWh and the electricity used from 4 p.m. at 34 pence per kWh.

If your household uses 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday, that electricity will cost £2.04. The same 6 kWh outside that period will cost £1.26. These examples exclude the standing charge.

## Your first bill after the change

Your first bill after 1 October may cover electricity used on both sides of the change date. Alder Energy will charge any electricity used before 1 October under your previous tariff. For electricity used from 1 October, we will use the new Home Flex time bands.

Your bill may show separate calculations for the period before 1 October and the period from 1 October. Check the dates beside each section when reviewing the rates. The date when we issue the bill does not decide which tariff covers your electricity.

You can check how much electricity your household used in each half-hour period in the Alder Energy app. You can also download the data as a CSV file. In most cases, the app will show each day’s data by noon on the following day. If we receive delayed meter data, your readings can take up to 72 hours to appear.

These readings can help you check which rate covered each half-hour. They do not provide an estimate of your total bill. Alder Energy has not estimated how much your household will spend or save under the new rate because the result depends on your recorded use in each time band.

## Your choices

You can stay on Home Flex, or you can ask us to move you to another available plan. You may leave Home Flex without paying an exit fee.

If you request a plan change before 20 September, we will apply your new plan by 1 October. Requests made on or after 20 September may take up to ten working days.

You remain in control of when and how you use your appliances. Alder Energy will not remotely control them under Home Flex. You can review the app’s half-hour data to understand how the two rates apply to your household, then decide whether to remain on the plan or request a change.

You do not need to contact us if you want to stay on Home Flex. If you want another plan in place by 1 October, please submit your request before 20 September.

Alder Energy
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening electricity rate from 1 October

# What changes on 1 October

Dear customer,

From 1 October 2026, Alder Energy is introducing a new evening electricity rate for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter. It changes the price of each kilowatt-hour (kWh) according to the time and day when the electricity is used. Your standing charge will stay the same.

Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances under Home Flex. The rate changes what electricity costs during the stated hours; it does not give Alder Energy control over when or how an appliance runs.

## When each rate applies

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kWh, while electricity used at every other time will cost 21 pence per kWh. At weekends and on public holidays, the 21-pence rate applies all day, including the hours from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m.

The applicable price is based on when the electricity is used, not when an appliance was started or when Alder Energy issues your bill. If an appliance runs across the start or end of the weekday evening period, the electricity used within the period is charged at 34 pence per kWh, while electricity used outside it is charged at 21 pence per kWh.

For example, 6 kWh used between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday will cost £2.04. The calculation is 6 multiplied by 34 pence, which equals 204 pence, or £2.04. The same 6 kWh used outside that period will cost £1.26: 6 multiplied by 21 pence equals 126 pence, or £1.26. Both examples cover electricity use only and exclude the standing charge, which is not changing.

## How your first bill will be worked out

Your first bill issued after 1 October may include electricity used both before and after the new rate begins. Any electricity used before 1 October will be charged under your previous tariff. Eligible electricity used from 1 October onward will be charged at the Home Flex rate for the time and day when it was used.

This means the bill may show charges calculated under two sets of prices. The date of the bill does not move earlier usage onto the new rate: electricity used on 30 September, for example, remains subject to your previous tariff even if it appears on a bill issued in October.

Alder Energy has not estimated how much an individual household will save or spend under the new rate. The amount charged will depend on how many kilowatt-hours that household uses in each price period, together with the unchanged standing charge.

## Check your usage or choose another plan

You can view your electricity use in half-hour periods in the Alder Energy app. You can also download the same usage data as a CSV file if you want to review it outside the app. Data normally appears by noon on the day after the electricity was used. When meter data is delayed, it may take up to 72 hours to appear. The app and CSV provide two ways to check which half-hour periods contain your recorded usage.

You may stay on Home Flex or leave the plan without paying an exit fee. If you ask to switch plans before 20 September, your new plan will be applied by 1 October. Requests made later may take up to ten working days, so the Home Flex rate may begin before a later switch is completed.

If your smart meter is not working, you will remain on your existing tariff until the meter has been installed or repaired.

Alder Energy
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening electricity rate starts October 1

# Your new Home Flex rates, explained

From October 1, 2026, Alder Energy is introducing a new evening electricity rate for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter. This newsletter explains when each rate applies, what to expect on your first bill, and the options available to you.

## When the rates apply

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). At all other times, it will cost 21 pence per kWh.

Weekends and public holidays are charged at 21 pence per kWh throughout the day. Your standing charge will not change.

The applicable rate is determined by when electricity is used. It is not based on when you start an appliance or when your bill is issued. For example, if an appliance runs across the start or end of the weekday evening period, electricity used during each part of its cycle will be charged at the rate applying at that time.

To illustrate the difference, a household using 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity:

6 kWh × 34p = 204p, or £2.04

The same 6 kWh used outside that weekday period—or at any time on a weekend or public holiday—would cost £1.26:

6 kWh × 21p = 126p, or £1.26

These examples exclude the standing charge. They illustrate how usage is priced and are not estimates of what your household will save or spend. Alder Energy has not estimated the individual bill impact for any household.

## Your first bill and usage data

Your first bill issued after October 1 may include electricity used both before and after the new rate takes effect. Any usage before October 1 will be charged under your previous tariff. Eligible usage from October 1 onward will be divided between the 34-pence weekday evening rate and the 21-pence rate according to when it was recorded.

You can review your half-hour electricity usage in the Alder Energy app. This can help you see which usage periods were charged at each rate. You can also download your half-hour data as a CSV file if you would prefer to examine it separately.

Usage data normally appears in the app by noon on the following day. If meter data is delayed, it may take up to 72 hours to appear. A delay in the app does not change the rate associated with the time when the electricity was used.

Customers without a working smart meter will remain on their existing tariff until a smart meter is installed or repaired. The new evening rate will not be applied while the required meter data is unavailable.

## Your choices

You can remain on Home Flex and pay the new rates from October 1 if you have a working smart meter. Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances; decisions about when and how they operate remain with you.

You may also leave Home Flex without paying an exit fee. If you request a plan switch before September 20, your new plan will be applied by October 1. Requests made later may take up to ten working days to complete, so Home Flex rates could apply until the switch has been processed.

Before choosing, you can review your half-hour usage in the app or CSV download and compare when your household uses electricity with the new rate periods. This information shows your recorded consumption, but it does not predict your future costs.

Whatever you decide, your standing charge remains unchanged. If your smart meter is not working, you do not need to choose immediately: your existing tariff will continue until the meter is installed or repaired.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex electricity rates are changing on October 1

# Your new evening electricity rate, explained

From October 1, 2026, Alder Energy is changing the electricity rates for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter.

Under the new rates, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. on weekdays will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). Electricity used at all other times will cost 21 pence per kWh. On weekends and public holidays, the 21 pence rate will apply all day.

Your standing charge will not change. Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances, and we have not estimated how much your household will save or spend under the new rate. The effect on your bill will depend on how much electricity you use and when you use it.

## When each rate applies

The rate is based on the time electricity is used. It is not based on when you start an appliance or when we issue your bill.

For example, if an appliance runs across the start or end of the weekday evening period, the electricity it uses during each part of the day will be charged at the rate applying at that time. Usage before 4 p.m. will be charged at 21 pence per kWh, while usage from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will be charged at 34 pence per kWh. The 21 pence rate will apply again outside that period.

A household using 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity:

6 kWh × 34p = 204p, or £2.04

The same 6 kWh used outside the weekday evening period would cost £1.26:

6 kWh × 21p = 126p, or £1.26

These examples cover electricity usage only and exclude the standing charge.

You can check your half-hour usage in the Alder Energy app. You can also download the data as a CSV file if you would prefer to review it elsewhere. Usage data normally appears in the app by noon the following day. If meter data is delayed, it may take up to 72 hours to appear.

## What to expect on your first bill

Your first bill after October 1 may include electricity used both before and after the new rates begin.

Any electricity used before October 1 will be charged under your previous tariff. Electricity used from October 1 onward will be charged at the new Home Flex rates, according to the day and time it was used. This means one bill may show charges calculated under two sets of rates.

Your standing charge will continue as usual and is not affected by this change.

If you do not have a working smart meter, the new rate will not apply yet. You will remain on your existing tariff until a smart meter is installed or your current meter is repaired.

## Your choices

You can remain on Home Flex, or you can choose another available Alder Energy plan. There is no exit fee for leaving Home Flex.

If you ask to switch plans before September 20, we will apply your new plan by October 1. Requests made later may take up to ten working days to complete. During that processing period, the tariff currently active on your account will continue to determine your charges.

Before deciding, you may want to review your half-hour usage in the app or CSV download to see how much electricity your household has recently used during the weekday evening period. This data shows your recorded usage, but it is not a forecast of what you will pay.

The new Home Flex rates will take effect automatically on October 1 if you are an eligible residential customer with a working smart meter and have not changed plans.
````

````text
Subject: Your new Home Flex evening rate from October 1

# Home Flex rates are changing on October 1

From October 1, 2026, Alder Energy will introduce a new evening electricity rate for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter.

The price you pay will depend on the day and time you use electricity. Your standing charge will stay the same.

We have not estimated whether an individual household will spend more or less under the new rates. Your costs will depend on how much electricity you use in each time band.

## When each rate applies

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). Electricity used at all other times will cost 21 pence per kWh.

At weekends and on public holidays, the 21-pence rate will apply all day.

We base the rate on when your household uses the electricity. The time you start an appliance does not determine the rate for its full cycle. If an appliance runs across the 4 p.m. boundary on a weekday, we will charge electricity used before 4 p.m. at 21 pence per kWh and electricity used from 4 p.m. at 34 pence per kWh. The date when we issue your bill does not affect the rate.

For example, 6 kWh of electricity used between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would cost £2.04:

6 kWh × 34p = 204p, or £2.04.

The same 6 kWh used outside that period would cost £1.26:

6 kWh × 21p = 126p, or £1.26.

Both examples exclude the standing charge.

Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances. You decide when and how you use them.

## Your first bill after the change

Your first bill after October 1 may cover electricity used on both sides of the change date. We will charge electricity used before October 1 under your previous tariff.

For electricity used from October 1, we will apply the new Home Flex rates according to the day and time recorded by your smart meter. Your bill may therefore include charges from your previous tariff as well as charges at 21 pence and 34 pence per kWh. The standing charge will not change.

You can review your half-hour electricity usage in the Alder Energy app. You can also download the data as a CSV file if you want to examine it in a spreadsheet.

Meter data reaches the app by noon on the following day in most cases. If we receive the data late, it may take up to 72 hours to appear.

Customers without a working smart meter will remain on their existing tariff until Alder Energy installs or repairs their meter. The new time-based rates will not apply to those customers in the meantime.

## Your choices

You can remain on Home Flex and use the app or CSV download to see how much electricity your household uses in each rate period.

You can also leave Home Flex without paying an exit fee. If you ask us to switch your plan before September 20, we will apply your new plan by October 1. Requests made on or after September 20 may take up to ten working days to complete.

Before choosing another plan, check its unit rates, standing charge and terms. Alder Energy cannot predict whether Home Flex or another plan will cost less for your household because the result depends on your usage.

If you stay on Home Flex, you do not need to take any action before October 1.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening rate starts on 1 October

# Your new Home Flex electricity rate

From 1 October 2026, Alder Energy is introducing a new evening electricity rate for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter. This email explains which hours have the higher rate, how we will handle your first bill after the change, and the options open to you.

## When each rate applies

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). At all other times, the price will be 21 pence per kWh. On weekends and public holidays, the 21-pence rate applies all day. Your standing charge will not change.

The price is based on the time you use the electricity. It is not based on when you start an appliance or when we issue your bill. For example, if an appliance runs across the start of the evening period on a weekday, electricity used before 4 p.m. is charged at 21 pence per kWh, while electricity used from 4 p.m. is charged at 34 pence per kWh. If it continues past 8 p.m., electricity used after the evening period is charged at 21 pence per kWh.

For a simple cost comparison, 6 kWh used between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would cost £2.04. The same 6 kWh used outside that period would cost £1.26. Both examples exclude the standing charge.

## What to expect on your first bill

Your first bill after 1 October may cover electricity used on both sides of the change date. Any electricity you used before 1 October will be charged under your previous tariff. Eligible electricity used from 1 October onward will be charged at the new Home Flex rates according to the day and time of use. The date we issue the bill does not decide which rate applies.

This means one bill may show charges calculated under your previous tariff as well as charges calculated at 21 pence or 34 pence per kWh under the new rate. The unchanged standing charge will also appear in the usual way.

You can check your half-hour electricity use in the Alder Energy app. This lets you see which half-hour periods fall within the weekday evening rate and which are charged at 21 pence per kWh. You can also download your half-hour usage as a CSV file. App data normally appears by noon on the following day, although it can take up to 72 hours if meter data is delayed.

## Your choices

You can remain on Home Flex or move to another available plan. Alder Energy has not estimated how much your individual household will save or spend under the new rate. Your result will depend on how much electricity is recorded in each rate period, together with your standing charge.

If you choose to leave Home Flex, there is no exit fee. Submit a plan switch by 20 September and your new plan will be applied by 1 October. Requests made after 20 September may take up to ten working days, so the Home Flex evening rate may apply before a later switch is completed.

Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances. The rate changes the price charged for electricity at the stated times; decisions about whether and when to use an appliance remain with your household.

The new rate applies only when your smart meter is working. If you do not have a working smart meter, you will stay on your existing tariff until a meter is installed or repaired. Once meter data is available, you can use the app or CSV download to review the half-hour periods used to calculate your charges.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening electricity rate starts October 1

# Understanding Your New Home Flex Rate

From October 1, 2026, Alder Energy is introducing a new evening electricity rate for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter. This newsletter explains when each rate applies, how your first bill will be calculated, and the options available to you.

## When the rates apply

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). Electricity used at all other times will cost 21 pence per kWh.

On weekends and public holidays, the 21-pence rate applies all day. Your standing charge will not change.

The rate is determined by when electricity is used. It is not based on when you start an appliance or when Alder Energy issues your bill. If an appliance runs across the start or end of the weekday evening period, the electricity recorded during each time band will be charged at the rate for that band.

For example, a household using 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity:

6 kWh × 34p = 204p, or £2.04.

The same 6 kWh used outside that weekday period, or at any time on a weekend or public holiday, would cost £1.26:

6 kWh × 21p = 126p, or £1.26.

These examples cover electricity usage only and exclude the standing charge. Alder Energy has not estimated how much any individual household will save or spend under the new rate. Your actual bill will depend on how much electricity you use and when you use it.

## What to expect on your first bill

Your first bill after October 1 may include electricity used both before and after the new rate begins. Any usage before October 1 will be charged under your previous tariff. Eligible usage from October 1 onward will be divided between the 34-pence weekday evening rate and the 21-pence rate according to when the electricity was used.

You can review your half-hour electricity usage in the Alder Energy app. You can also download the information as a CSV file if you would like to examine the readings separately.

Usage data normally appears in the app by noon on the following day. If meter information is delayed, it may take up to 72 hours to appear. A delay in the app does not change which rate applies; the charge is still based on the time the electricity was used.

Customers without a working smart meter will remain on their existing tariff until a smart meter is installed or repaired. The new evening rate will not apply to those customers while their meter is not working.

## Your choices

You can remain on Home Flex and pay the new rates from October 1 if you are eligible. Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances; decisions about when and how they operate remain with you.

You may also leave Home Flex without paying an exit fee. If you ask to switch plans before September 20, your new plan will be applied by October 1. Requests made later may take up to ten working days to complete, so the Home Flex rates could apply until your switch takes effect.

Before choosing another plan, you can use the half-hour data in the app or your downloaded CSV file to understand when your household has used electricity. This information shows past usage but does not guarantee what you will pay under Home Flex or another plan.

Alder Energy will confirm any requested plan change and the date on which it becomes effective.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening electricity rate from October 1

# A guide to your new Home Flex rates

From October 1, 2026, Alder Energy is changing the electricity rates for residential customers on the Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter.

This email explains when each rate applies, how we will calculate your first bill after the change, and the options you have. Your standing charge will stay the same.

## When each rate applies

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). At all other times, the price will be 21 pence per kWh.

The 21-pence rate applies all day on Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays. This means the 34-pence rate only applies during the weekday evening period.

The price is based on when you use the electricity. It does not depend on when you start an appliance or when we issue your bill. For example, if an appliance continues running into or beyond the 4 p.m. to 8 p.m. period, the electricity recorded in each time band will be charged at the rate for that band.

To put the difference in pounds and pence, a household using 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would pay £2.04 for that electricity:

6 kWh × 34p = £2.04

The same 6 kWh used outside that period would cost £1.26:

6 kWh × 21p = £1.26

These examples cover electricity use only and do not include the standing charge.

The timing of your electricity use remains your choice. Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances.

## How your first bill will work

Your first bill after October 1 may cover electricity used both before and after the new rates begin. We will charge any electricity used before October 1 under your previous tariff. Electricity used from October 1 onward will be charged at the new Home Flex rates according to the day and time of use.

Your smart meter records usage in half-hour periods, allowing us to apply the appropriate rate to each period. The date when we issue the bill does not determine which rate you pay.

We have not estimated whether an individual household will spend more or less under the new rates. The result will depend on how much electricity that household uses in each time band.

If you do not have a working smart meter, you will remain on your existing tariff. The Home Flex rates described in this email will apply after your meter has been installed or repaired and is working.

## Checking your usage and considering your options

You can view your half-hour electricity usage in the Alder Energy app. You can also download the data as a CSV file if you would prefer to review it in a spreadsheet or keep your own copy.

Usage data normally appears in the app by noon on the following day. If there is a delay in receiving information from your meter, the data may take up to 72 hours to appear. A delay in the app does not change the rate that applies to the electricity you used.

You may stay on Home Flex or move to another available plan. There is no exit fee for leaving Home Flex.

If you ask to switch plans before September 20, we will apply the new plan by October 1. Requests made after September 20 may take up to ten working days to complete. Until a switch takes effect, your current plan and its applicable rates will continue.

Please review your plan options and half-hour usage in the Alder Energy app before deciding what works for your household.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex electricity rates change on 1 October

# Your guide to the new evening rate

From 1 October 2026, Alder Energy will introduce new electricity rates for residential customers on our Home Flex plan who have a working smart meter. Your rate will depend on the day and time when you use electricity.

We have set out the times, prices and billing details below so you can check how the change applies to your home. Alder Energy has not estimated whether your household will spend more or less under these rates. Your result will depend on how many kilowatt-hours you use in each time band.

## Rates by day and time

On weekdays, electricity you use from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour (kWh). At all other weekday times, the price will be 21 pence per kWh. On weekends and public holidays, you will pay 21 pence per kWh all day.

The time when you use each unit of electricity determines its price. The time when you start an appliance does not set the price for its whole cycle. For example, if a dishwasher runs across 4 p.m. on a weekday, we will charge for electricity used before 4 p.m. at 21 pence per kWh and electricity used from 4 p.m. at 34 pence per kWh. The date when we issue your bill does not affect the rate.

If your household uses 6 kWh between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday, that electricity will cost £2.04. The same 6 kWh used outside that period will cost £1.26. Both examples exclude the standing charge. Your standing charge will stay the same.

You can review your half-hour electricity use in the Alder Energy app. You can also download the data as a CSV file if you want to examine it in a spreadsheet. In most cases, the app will show each day’s data by noon on the following day. If we receive delayed meter data, the update can take up to 72 hours.

## Your first bill after 1 October

Your first bill after the change may cover electricity you used both before and after 1 October. We will charge electricity used before 1 October under your previous tariff. For electricity used from 1 October onward, we will use the new Home Flex rates and assign each unit to the relevant time band.

Your bill will continue to show your electricity charges and standing charge. The billing period itself does not decide which unit rate applies. We use the date, day and half-hour in which your smart meter records the electricity use.

If you do not have a working smart meter, you will remain on your existing tariff. The Home Flex evening rate will apply only after Alder Energy installs or repairs your smart meter.

## Your choices

You can stay on Home Flex and use the app or CSV download to see how much electricity your household uses in each rate period. Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances. You decide when and how you use them.

You may also choose another Alder Energy plan or leave Home Flex without paying an exit fee. If you request a plan switch by 20 September, we will apply your new plan by 1 October. If you make the request after 20 September, the switch may take up to ten working days. Until we complete the switch, the tariff in effect for your account will determine your charges.

Before choosing, compare the prices and terms of the plans available to you. Your own half-hour usage data can show how much electricity you have used during the new evening period, but it cannot guarantee what you will spend in the future.
````

````text
Subject: Your Home Flex evening electricity rate starts 1 October

# Your new Home Flex electricity rate

From 1 October 2026, Alder Energy is changing the electricity rates for residential customers who have a working smart meter and are on the Home Flex plan. The new rates depend on the day and time when electricity is used.

Customers without a working smart meter will stay on their existing tariff until their meter is installed or repaired. If your smart meter is working and you remain on Home Flex, the rates below will apply automatically from 1 October.

## When each rate applies

On weekdays, electricity used from 4 p.m. through 8 p.m. will cost 34 pence per kilowatt-hour. Electricity used at all other times will cost 21 pence per kilowatt-hour. On weekends and public holidays, the 21-pence rate applies all day.

The rate is based on when the electricity is used. It is not based on when an appliance is started or when your bill is issued. If an appliance runs across the start or end of the weekday evening period, the electricity used within each time band is charged at that band’s rate.

For example, 6 kilowatt-hours used between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. on a weekday would cost £2.04. The same 6 kilowatt-hours used outside that period would cost £1.26. Both examples cover the electricity used and exclude the standing charge.

Alder Energy has not estimated whether an individual household will save or spend more under the new rate. The amount charged will depend on how many kilowatt-hours that household uses in each time band.

## How your first bill will be calculated

Your first bill issued after 1 October may include electricity used both before and after the change. Electricity used before 1 October will be charged under your previous tariff. Eligible Home Flex usage from 1 October onward will be charged at 34 pence or 21 pence per kilowatt-hour according to the day and time of use.

This means the date when the bill is issued does not set the rate for all usage on that bill. Alder Energy will separate the usage by date, then apply the tariff that was in effect when the electricity was used. For usage from 1 October, the weekday evening band and the other-time rate will be applied as described above. Your existing standing charge will continue without a rate change.

You can review the usage behind your bill in the Alder Energy app. The app shows half-hour usage, which lets you see when your recorded electricity use falls within the weekday evening period. You can also download that data as a CSV file.

Usage data normally appears in the app by noon on the following day. When meter data is delayed, it can take up to 72 hours to appear. A delay in the app does not change which rate applies: the charge is still based on when the electricity was used.

## Your Home Flex choices

You may stay on Home Flex, or you may leave the plan without paying an exit fee. If you ask to switch plans before 20 September, Alder Energy will apply the new plan by 1 October. Requests made after 20 September may take up to ten working days, so the Home Flex rates may apply until a later switch is completed.

If you are deciding whether to remain on Home Flex, you can use the app’s half-hour view or CSV download to review your own recorded usage by day and time.

The choice of plan does not give Alder Energy control over your household equipment. Alder Energy will not remotely control your appliances.
````

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P7_R1_C0 -->
````text
From January to April 2026, I ran our “make a mug in one afternoon” class on 14 Saturdays. Each class had eight places, every place was booked, and 112 people attended in total. On the face of it, that sounds like a class I should have continued. But I stopped offering it because the promise and the process didn’t line up.

Participants did shape a mug in one afternoon. They had two hours to make it, and then they left it with us. After that, studio staff handled the drying, the bisque firing, the glazing, the final firing, and getting the finished mug ready for collection.

The advertised collection time was three weeks after the class. Out of 112 mugs, 58 were ready within those three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer. So just over half were ready within the time we had advertised.

There were also seven mugs that cracked during drying or firing. We offered each of those participants either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class. The booking page did say that cracking was possible. But the three-week collection time appeared in the headline, and that gave it much more emphasis.

That was my responsibility. I set a clear expectation before we had a process that could meet it consistently. Clay takes the time it takes through drying and firing, but that doesn’t change the fact that I chose the wording and put three weeks at the top of the page.

The amount of work after each class was also greater than I had allowed for. Staff spent an average of 46 minutes per mug finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing it after the participant had gone home. When I set the class price at £48, I had estimated 25 minutes of staff time per mug. That difference matters when there are eight mugs from every class and 112 across the whole run.

We received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those emails came from 18 participants. We didn’t track whether the delays affected later class bookings or shop sales, so I can’t say that they did. What I can say is that people had been given a collection date and then had to ask us for updates.

Beginning in June, we’re replacing the one-afternoon class with a two-session mug class priced at £72. The two sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mugs. In the second, they’ll glaze them themselves. Collection will then be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

This format gives participants a role in both making and glazing the mug, and the timetable on the booking page will reflect both sessions and the later collection date.

I also want to be clear that the new format hasn’t been tested yet. I’m going to run four classes before deciding whether to keep it. After those four, I’ll have actual results to look at rather than assuming the schedule works because it looks reasonable on paper.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P7_R1_C0 -->

````text
I want to explain why I have stopped offering the "make a mug in one afternoon" class, and what I am going to try instead.

We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April this year. There were eight places each time, every place was booked, and 112 people attended altogether.

During the class, people spent the first two hours shaping their mug. Once they left, the studio team took over. We handled the drying, the bisque firing, the glazing and the final firing, then tracked and packed each finished mug for collection.

The collection time in the headline was three weeks after the class. That was the promise people saw when they booked, and it was my responsibility to make sure the studio could meet it. The booking page did say that cracking was possible, but that warning does not change the fact that three weeks appeared right up front.

Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within those three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four. The remaining 13 took five weeks or longer. Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered those participants either a replacement made by me or a place in another class.

We also received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those came from 18 participants. I do not want to make those numbers say more than they do. We did not track whether the delays affected later class bookings or sales in the shop, so I cannot tell you that they did.

What I can tell you is that I underestimated the work left for the studio after each Saturday. When I set the class price at £48, I allowed for 25 minutes of staff time per mug after the class. The actual average was 46 minutes for finishing, glazing, tracking and packing each one. That is nearly twice the time I had put into the price, and all of it happened after the participant had gone home.

So I have stopped offering that version of the class. From June, I will try a two-session mug class instead. It will cost £72, and the sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mug. In the second, they will glaze it themselves. Collection will be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

This gives participants a part in the glazing rather than handing every remaining step to the studio team. It also means the collection date is tied to the second session, instead of asking us to complete all of the drying, firing and glazing within three weeks of the first afternoon.

I should be clear that the new format has not been tested yet. I do not know whether it will solve every problem, and I do not want to make another promise before I have the results. I will run four classes, look at how they go, and then decide whether to keep the format.

The original class asked the studio to do more work, in less time, than I had allowed for. That was my mistake. The new class is my attempt to set a timetable and a price that match the work more closely, and I will judge it on what happens in those first four classes.
````

````text
I’ve stopped offering our “make a mug in one afternoon” class, and I want to explain why.

We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April this year. Each class had eight places, every place was booked, and 112 people took part. In the first two hours, each person shaped a mug. After they left, the studio staff took care of drying it, bisque firing it, glazing it, putting it through the final firing, and getting it ready for collection.

We charged £48, and the headline on the booking page said people could collect their mug three weeks after the class. The page also said that cracking was possible, but the three-week collection time sat in the headline. I set that expectation, and I got it wrong.

Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four. The remaining 13 took five weeks or longer. Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered those seven participants either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class.

The workload after each Saturday was much larger than I had allowed for. When I set the price, I estimated that we would spend 25 minutes per mug after class. In practice, staff spent an average of 46 minutes finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each one. That is 21 minutes more per mug than I had built into the class.

When mugs took longer, some participants wrote to ask about them. I received 23 emails about collection times from 18 participants.

I don’t want to stretch those numbers beyond what we know. We did not track whether the delays affected later class bookings or sales in the shop. I can’t tell you that they did, and I can’t tell you that they didn’t. I can say that 54 of the 112 mugs were not ready within the advertised three weeks.

Beginning in June, we’re replacing the one-afternoon class with a two-session mug class. It will cost £72, and the sessions will take place two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mugs. In the second, they will glaze them. We will schedule collection for two weeks after that second session.

You’ll take part in the glazing instead of leaving that stage to the studio staff. The scheduled collection date will also come two weeks after glazing, rather than three weeks after the day you shape the mug.

I need to be clear about one thing: we haven’t tested this format yet. I’m going to run four classes before I decide whether to keep it. I’ll look at how the sessions work in the studio and whether we can meet the collection schedule we give people.

All 112 places in the original classes sold, but the class combined a three-week headline promise with a workload estimate that was far too low. That was my responsibility.

The new class gives participants a second session in the studio and puts glazing into the part of the process they experience themselves. After four classes, I’ll decide whether the format works well enough to continue.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P7_R1_C2 -->

````text
Between January and April this year, we ran our “make a mug in one afternoon” class on 14 Saturdays. There were eight places each time, and every place was booked, so 112 people came through the studio.

In the class, you shaped your mug during the first two hours. Once you left, the rest passed to us. Studio staff handled the drying, the bisque firing, the glazing and the final firing. We advertised collection three weeks after the class.

From the participant’s point of view, the class ended after those two hours. In the studio, the mug was still at the start of the drying and firing process. It needed drying, two firings, and glazing between them, then tracking and packing before collection. The one-afternoon format put all of those steps in staff hands, out of sight, after the class.

That three-week collection time was the part we did not deliver consistently. Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within three weeks. Forty-one were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer. The possibility of cracking was mentioned on the booking page, but the three-week collection time was in the headline. I put it there, and I take responsibility for making that promise more prominent than the warning and for offering a timetable we did not consistently meet.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered each of those participants either a replacement made by me or a place in another class. Cracking can happen in ceramics, and we had said that it could. Even so, knowing that something is possible does not make a cracked mug any less disappointing when you have spent an afternoon making it.

We also got the work estimate wrong. When I set the class price at £48, I allowed for 25 minutes of staff time after class for each mug. The actual average was 46 minutes for finishing, glazing, tracking and packing each one. Across a fully booked class of eight, that is 168 more minutes of staff work than I had allowed for.

There was also the uncertainty while people waited. We received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready, from 18 participants. We did not track whether the delays affected later class bookings or shop sales, so I cannot claim that they did.

I have stopped offering the one-afternoon version because too much of the making happened after the person who had shaped the mug went home, and because the collection promise did not match what we delivered for 54 of the 112 mugs.

From June, we will try a two-session mug class at £72. The sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mugs. In the second, they will glaze them. Collection will be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

This changes who does the glazing and gives the process a different timetable. The collection date will be a scheduled date two weeks after glazing, rather than a headline suggesting the whole process fits into one afternoon plus a three-week wait.

We have not tested this new format yet. I am going to run four classes, then decide whether to keep it.
````

````text
I want to explain why I’ve stopped offering our “make a mug in one afternoon” class, and what we’re going to try instead.

The class ran on 14 Saturdays between January and April 2026. There were eight places each Saturday, every place was booked, and 112 people attended in total.

During the class, participants spent the first two hours shaping their mugs. After everyone left, the studio team took care of the rest: drying the mugs, bisque firing them, glazing them, putting them through the final firing, tracking them, and packing them for collection.

We advertised collection three weeks after the class. Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within those three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer.

So, although most mugs were ready within four weeks, the headline promise was three weeks, and for nearly half of the people who came, we didn’t meet it. The booking page did explain that cracking was possible, but the three-week collection time appeared in the headline. That was the clearest part of the offer, and I’m responsible for putting it there.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered each of those participants either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class. Cracking can happen in ceramics, but mentioning that possibility doesn’t cancel out the expectation created by a prominent collection date.

We also underestimated how much work remained after each class. When I set the price at £48, I estimated that staff would spend about 25 minutes on each mug after the participant had left. The actual average was 46 minutes for finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each one. Across 112 mugs, that difference was significant.

We received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those emails came from 18 participants. I don’t want to make that number mean more than it does, and we didn’t track whether delays affected later class bookings or shop sales. So I can’t claim that they did. What I can say is that the timetable we advertised wasn’t reliable enough, and the class involved substantially more staff time than I had allowed for in the price.

That’s why I’ve stopped offering the one-afternoon version.

Beginning in June, we’ll try a two-session mug class instead. It will cost £72, and the sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mugs. In the second, they’ll glaze them themselves. Collection will then be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

This format hasn’t been tested yet, so I’m not going to promise that it solves everything. We’ll run four classes and then decide whether to keep it.

The aim is to make the timing clearer and to include glazing as part of the class rather than leaving all of that work to happen after participants go home. But it’s still a trial. After those first four classes, I’ll look at what actually happened before deciding what comes next.
````

````text
Hi, it's Nico. I want to explain why I've stopped offering our "make a mug in one afternoon" class, and what we're trying instead.

We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April this year. There were eight places each time, every place was booked, and 112 people attended in total.

In the class, participants spent the first two hours shaping a mug. Once they left, the studio team took over. We handled the drying, bisque firing, glazing, final firing, tracking, and packing. The booking page said collection would be three weeks after the class.

That timing was the problem. Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer. So nearly half weren't ready by the time we'd advertised.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We had said on the booking page that cracking was possible, and those seven participants were offered either a replacement made by me or a place in another class. But the three-week collection time appeared in the headline. That's what people saw first, and I put it there. The fact that pottery can be unpredictable doesn't make that promise any less mine.

We also received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those came from 18 participants. I don't want to inflate what that tells us. We didn't track whether delays affected later class bookings or sales in the shop, so I can't claim that they did. What I can say is that people had been given a date and then had to chase us for an update.

I also got the work involved wrong when I set the price. The class cost £48, and I'd estimated that staff would spend about 25 minutes on each mug after the participant left. The actual average was 46 minutes for finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each one. That gap matters in a small studio, and it showed me that the format and price were based on an estimate that didn't hold up.

So I don't think we should keep running the same class with the same promise.

Beginning in June, we'll try a two-session mug class for £72. The sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mugs. In the second, they'll glaze them themselves. Collection will be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

I want to be careful about how I describe this. The new format hasn't been tested yet. It gives participants a hand in the glazing and gives the studio a different timetable, but I don't yet know whether it will work well in practice. I'll run four classes before deciding whether to keep it.

I liked seeing all 112 people come in and make something of their own. Stopping the afternoon class isn't a judgment on their work or on the idea of a mug class. It's a response to a promise I made too confidently and a workload I underestimated. The next four classes are our chance to see whether this new version is workable, and I'll judge it from what actually happens.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P7_R2_C1 -->
