````text
I want to explain why I’ve stopped running our “make a mug in one afternoon” class, and what we’re going to try instead.

We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April this year. Each class had eight places, and people booked every place, so 112 people came through the studio.

During the afternoon, you had two hours to shape your mug. Once you left, the studio team took care of the rest: drying it, bisque firing it, glazing it, putting it through the final firing, tracking it, and packing it for collection.

We advertised the class with a three-week collection time. Out of 112 mugs, 58 were ready within those three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer.

I didn’t meet the collection promise in the headline for many of those mugs. I’m responsible for that. The booking page did say that cracking was possible, but I put the three-week collection time in the headline.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered each of those participants either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class. Cracking can happen in ceramics, but those seven people didn’t receive the mug they had made.

We also received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those emails came from 18 participants. We didn’t track whether the delays affected later class bookings or shop sales, so I can’t say whether they did.

I also got the workload estimate wrong when I set the price. I estimated that the team would spend 25 minutes on each mug after the class. Staff spent an average of 46 minutes finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each one. I used the 25-minute estimate when I set the class price at £48.

I don’t want to keep offering the same format with the same promise. Beginning in June, we’ll try a two-session mug class at £72. The sessions will take place two weeks apart. You’ll shape your mug in the first session, then come back and glaze it in the second. We’ll schedule collection for two weeks after that second session.

In the old class, staff glazed your mug after you left. In the new class, you’ll do that part yourself. The change means coming to the studio twice, and the price is higher.

The new format hasn’t been tested. We’ll run four classes before deciding whether to keep it. Until we’ve done those four, I won’t know how well the format works.

The original class asked people to complete the making part in one afternoon, while the team completed every later stage. I underestimated the time that work would take, then put a three-week collection time at the top of the booking page. Some mugs took four weeks, some took five weeks or longer, and seven cracked.

If you booked the original class and waited longer than three weeks, I’m sorry. I made a promise based on an estimate that turned out to be wrong. The two-session class is our next format, and the first four classes will tell us whether we should continue with it.
````

````text
Between January and April this year, we ran our “make a mug in one afternoon” class on 14 Saturdays. Each class had eight places, and every place was booked, so 112 people came through the studio. I’m grateful that people wanted to spend a Saturday making pottery with us. But I’ve decided to stop offering that class in its original format.

The afternoon part worked as advertised: participants shaped a mug in the first two hours. After everyone went home, though, there was still a lot to do. Our studio staff handled the drying, bisque firing, glazing, final firing, tracking, and packing.

We told people they could collect their mug three weeks after the class. That collection time appeared in the headline on the booking page. The page also said that cracking was possible, but the three-week promise was much more prominent. That was my decision, and I take responsibility for it.

We did not meet that promise consistently. Fifty-eight mugs were ready within three weeks. Forty-one were ready in week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer. Seven mugs cracked while drying or firing. In those cases, we offered the participant either a replacement made by me or a place in another class.

People quite reasonably asked where their mugs were. I received 23 emails about collection times from 18 participants. We did not track whether those delays affected later class bookings or shop sales, so I can’t make a claim about that either way.

The amount of work after each class was also much higher than I had allowed for. When I set the price at £48, I estimated that finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each mug would take about 25 minutes. The actual average was 46 minutes per mug. Across a full class of eight people, the estimate allowed for three hours and 20 minutes. At the actual average, that work took just over six hours.

I had treated the work after class as a short finishing job. It wasn’t. On average, staff had a longer job for each participant once the teaching session had ended. The bad estimate was mine to fix, just as the collection promise was.

People did shape a mug in one afternoon. I stopped this version of the class because I sold it with a collection date that our process did not reliably meet, and I priced it around an estimate that was too low.

Beginning in June, we’re replacing it with a two-session mug class priced at £72. In the first session, participants will shape their mug. They’ll come back two weeks later for the second session and glaze it themselves. Collection will then be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

This gives participants a hand in another part of the process, and it changes how the studio’s work is divided. I want to be careful about what I promise, though. We haven’t tested this format yet, so I’m not claiming that it has solved the timing or workload issues.

We’ll run four of the new classes, and only after those four will I decide whether to keep the format.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P7_R2_C3 -->

</details>

````text
I want to explain why I’ve stopped offering our “make a mug in one afternoon” class, and what we’re going to try instead.

We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April 2026. There were eight places in each class, and every place was booked, so 112 people attended in total. During the first two hours, each person shaped their own mug. After everyone left, the studio team took care of drying the mugs, bisque firing them, glazing them, putting them through the final firing, and packing them for collection.

We advertised collection three weeks after the class. Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within that time. Another 41 were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer. So although just over half were ready when promised, a significant number were not.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. That is a possibility with ceramics, and we did mention it on the booking page. For those seven participants, we offered either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class. But while the possibility of cracking appeared in the booking information, the three-week collection time was in the headline. That gave it much more prominence, and I’m responsible for making that promise.

We also underestimated how much work remained after each class. When I set the price at £48, I estimated that our team would spend about 25 minutes per mug on finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing. The real average was 46 minutes per mug. That’s 21 minutes more than I allowed for every participant, before taking the drying and firing timetable into account.

We received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those emails came from 18 participants. I don’t want to exaggerate what that tells us: most participants did not email, and we didn’t track whether delays affected later class bookings or sales in the shop. So I can’t claim that the delays had an effect in either direction. What I can say is that our advertised timetable wasn’t met for 54 mugs, and some people had to chase us for an update.

That’s why I’ve stopped offering the one-afternoon version. Beginning in June, we’ll try a two-session mug class instead. It will cost £72, and the sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mugs, just as they did before. In the second session, they’ll come back and glaze their own work. Collection will then be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

This changes two important parts of the original setup. Glazing becomes part of the class rather than work completed entirely by studio staff, and the collection schedule starts after that glazing session rather than from the day the mug is shaped.

I also want to be clear that this new format has not yet been tested. I’m not presenting it as a proven solution. We’ll run four classes, look at what actually happens, and then decide whether to keep the format.

The original class asked people to trust a timeline that we didn’t consistently deliver. Stopping it is my response to the numbers, not an attempt to make the situation sound bigger than it was. Now we’ll test the two-session version and judge it on the results.
````

````text
I want to explain why I've stopped offering our "make a mug in one afternoon" class, and what I'm going to try instead from June.

We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April 2026. There were eight places each Saturday, every place was booked, and 112 people attended in total. During the first two hours, each person shaped a mug. After everyone went home, the studio team took care of drying the mugs, bisque firing them, glazing them, putting them through the final firing, and getting them ready for collection.

The collection time we advertised was three weeks after the class. We did say on the booking page that pottery can crack, but the three-week collection time was in the headline. That made it sound much firmer than it turned out to be, and that was my responsibility.

Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer. Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered each of those participants either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class.

I also received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those came from 18 participants. I understand why people asked. They had been given a collection time, and in many cases we had not met it.

I got the price wrong as well. The class cost £48, and when I set that price, I estimated that the work after each class would take the team 25 minutes per mug. It actually took an average of 46 minutes to finish, glaze, track, and pack each one. That's a big difference when there are eight mugs from every Saturday.

I can't tell you whether the delays affected later class bookings or sales in the shop, because we didn't track that. I don't want to guess or pretend the numbers tell us something they don't.

So, beginning in June, we'll offer a two-session mug class for £72. The sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mug. In the second, they'll glaze it themselves. Collection will be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

In the old class, participants left after shaping the mug, and the studio team did everything that followed. With the new format, they'll come back and take part in the glazing. The collection schedule will also start from that second session, rather than from the afternoon when the mug was first shaped.

I need to be clear that we haven't tested this format yet. I'm going to run four classes and then decide whether to keep it. I'm not presenting it as a perfect fix. We need to see how it works in practice.

Stopping the one-afternoon class is my response to a promise I made too confidently. The booking page mentioned the possibility of cracking, but I put the three-week collection time in the headline, and our results didn't support that promise. The next four classes will tell us whether this new format works better.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P7_R3_C1 -->

````text
I’ve stopped offering our “make a mug in one afternoon” class. I want to explain why, because the class filled every time we ran it, and plenty of people enjoyed making their mugs. The problem came after they left the studio.

We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April this year. Each class had eight places, and all eight were booked. That gave us 112 participants in total. During the first two hours, each person shaped a mug. Then our studio team took over.

We handled the drying, bisque firing, glazing, final firing, tracking, and packing. We told participants they could collect their mugs three weeks after the class. Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within those three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four. The remaining 13 took five weeks or longer.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered each of those participants either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class. Our booking page did say that cracking was possible. The three-week collection time, however, appeared in the headline. I put the clearest promise in the most prominent place, and we failed to meet it for 54 people.

I also underestimated how much work each mug would need after the class. When I set the price at £48, I allowed 25 minutes of staff time per mug. In practice, finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each one took an average of 46 minutes. Across 112 mugs, that difference added up to more work than I had built into the class.

We received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those emails came from 18 participants. Some people contacted us more than once because we still couldn’t give them a collection date. We didn’t track whether those delays affected later class bookings or sales from the shop, so I can’t tell you that they did.

I can say that the class depended on our staff completing most of the process after participants had gone home. We charged for one afternoon, while the studio carried the mug through several more stages. That setup made the three-week promise hard to control, especially when drying and firing times varied.

From June, we’ll replace the one-afternoon class with a two-session mug class priced at £72. The sessions will take place two weeks apart. You’ll shape your mug in the first session, then return to glaze it in the second. We’ll schedule collection for two weeks after that second session.

The new format gives participants a hand in another part of the process and gives us a timetable based on two studio visits. It also costs more, so I want to be clear that we haven’t tested it yet. I don’t know whether the timing will work as planned or whether people will prefer coming back for a second session.

We’ll run four of these classes before deciding whether to keep the format. During those four classes, we’ll see how the workload and collection schedule hold up. For now, retiring the one-afternoon version is my way of taking responsibility for a promise the studio couldn’t meet often enough.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P7_R3_C2 -->

</details>

````text
I’ve decided to stop offering our “make a mug in one afternoon” class. We ran it on 14 Saturdays between January and April this year. There were eight places each Saturday, every place was booked, and 112 people took part. So there was clearly interest in the class. The problem was the promise I made about what happened afterwards.

In the class, participants shaped a mug during the first two hours. Once they left, the studio staff took over the drying, bisque firing, glazing and final firing. We advertised collection three weeks after the class. Cracking was mentioned on the booking page, but the three-week collection time was in the headline. I put it there, and I should have allowed for the amount of work and the uncertainty in the process before making that promise.

The actual collection times were mixed. Fifty-eight mugs were ready within three weeks. Forty-one were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer. That means the headline time was not a reliable guide to what people would experience, even though some mugs were ready when advertised.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered each of those participants either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class. We also received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those emails came from 18 participants. I don’t want to make those numbers mean more than they do. We didn’t track whether delays affected later class bookings or sales from the shop, so I can’t say that they did.

There was also much more work after each class than I had budgeted for. Staff spent an average of 46 minutes finishing, glazing, tracking and packing each participant’s mug after they had gone home. When I set the class price at £48, I had estimated 25 minutes of staff time per mug. I got that estimate wrong. The difference was 21 minutes for every mug, on average, across 112 bookings.

Taken together, the timing and workload made the original class format one I’m no longer willing to offer with that promise and that price. People booked an afternoon class, but a large part of making their mug still sat with the studio, out of sight, after they left. I was responsible for setting up the class that way and for putting three weeks in the headline.

From June, we’re going to try a two-session mug class for £72. The sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mug. In the second, they’ll glaze it themselves. Collection will be scheduled for two weeks after that second session. The main change is that glazing moves into the class, instead of being one of the jobs staff complete after participants leave.

This new format hasn’t been tested yet, so I’m not going to claim that it has solved the problems. I’ll run four classes in the new format before deciding whether to keep it. That gives us four classes to see how the schedule works in practice, with participants doing the glazing in the second session.
````

````text
I want to explain why I’ve stopped offering our “make a mug in one afternoon” class, and what we’re going to try instead.

We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April 2026. Each class had eight places, every place was booked, and 112 people attended in total. In the first two hours, each participant shaped a mug. After everyone left, the studio team handled the drying, bisque firing, glazing, final firing, tracking, and packing.

The booking page advertised collection three weeks after the class. Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within those three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer. So, for almost half the participants, we didn’t meet the collection time we had advertised.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered each of those participants either a replacement made by me or a place in another class. The booking page did say that cracking was possible, but the three-week collection time appeared in the headline. That matters. I made the three-week promise prominent, and it was my responsibility to make sure our process could reliably meet it. It couldn’t.

There was also much more work after each class than I had allowed for when I set the price. Staff spent an average of 46 minutes finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each participant’s mug. When I priced the class at £48, I had estimated 25 minutes of staff time per mug after the session. That estimate was wrong.

We received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those emails came from 18 participants. I don’t want to make that figure mean more than it does, but it shows that some people had to chase us for information after the collection date they had been given.

There are also things we don’t know. We didn’t track whether the delays affected later class bookings or shop sales, so I can’t claim that they did. What I can say is that the original format combined a clear three-week headline promise with a process that often took longer, while leaving a large part of the work to staff after participants had gone home.

Beginning in June, we’ll offer a two-session mug class for £72. The sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape their mugs. In the second, they’ll glaze them. Collection will then be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

This new format hasn’t been tested yet, so I’m not presenting it as a proven solution. We’ll run four classes before deciding whether to keep it. That will give us a chance to see how the schedule works in practice before making a longer-term decision.

The aim is straightforward: make the timing we advertise match the class we actually run, and give participants a more direct role in making their finished mug. The one-afternoon version didn’t do that consistently, and that’s why I’ve stopped offering it.
````

````text
I want to explain why I'm stopping the "make a mug in one afternoon" class, and what I'm going to try instead.

We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April 2026. There were eight places each time, every place was booked, and 112 people took part altogether. So, on the face of it, the class was working. People wanted it, and they did make a mug during the afternoon. But the name and the timetable gave a misleading idea of how much work was still to come.

Participants shaped their mugs in the first two hours. Once they left, the studio staff took over. We handled the drying, bisque firing, glazing, final firing, tracking, and packing. I had estimated that this would take about 25 minutes of staff time for each mug when I set the class price at £48. In practice, it took an average of 46 minutes per mug.

The bigger problem was the collection promise. We advertised collection three weeks after the class. Of the 112 mugs, 58 were ready within those three weeks. Another 41 were ready during week four, and 13 took five weeks or longer.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered those participants either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class. Cracking is a known possibility with ceramics, and the booking page did say that it could happen. But the three-week collection time was in the headline. That is what people saw first, and it was my responsibility to make sure that promise was realistic. It wasn't.

We received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready, from 18 participants. That doesn't tell me everything about how people felt, but it does show that some people needed updates. We also didn't track whether the delays affected later class bookings or shop sales, so I can't claim that they did or didn't.

I could simply change the headline and give the same one-afternoon class a longer collection window. But that would leave the participants doing only the first part of making their mug, while the studio completed the rest. I want to try a format where they can do the glazing too.

Beginning in June, we'll offer a two-session mug class for £72. The sessions will be two weeks apart. In the first session, participants will shape the mug. In the second, they'll glaze it. Collection will be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

This new format hasn't been tested yet, and I don't want to present it as a solved problem. We'll run four classes, look at how they go, and then decide whether to keep the format. The extra session changes the price and asks people to come back two weeks later, so it may not suit everyone who liked the idea of a single afternoon.

I still like the basic idea of teaching people to make a mug. What I'm changing is the promise around it. The old class suggested that the work happened in one afternoon and that collection in three weeks was dependable. Our own figures show that wasn't true often enough. So we're retiring that version and testing one that gives participants more of the process and gives the studio a timetable we can judge honestly.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P7_R4_C1 -->

</details>

````text
I’ve stopped offering the “make a mug in one afternoon” class, and I want to explain the decision and the format that will take its place.

I ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April 2026. Each Saturday had eight places. People booked all eight places each time, so 112 people attended.

During the class, each person spent the first two hours shaping a mug. After everyone left, my staff and I took care of the rest: drying the mugs, bisque firing them, glazing them, then putting them through the final firing. We also tracked each mug and packed it for collection. Participants left after shaping, while our work on each mug continued in the studio.

I advertised collection three weeks after the class. We had 58 mugs ready within those three weeks. We finished 41 during week four, and 13 took five weeks or more.

I take responsibility for that gap. I put the three-week collection time in the headline on the booking page. On the same page, we also told people that mugs could crack, but the headline gave people a clear date to expect their work. We did not meet that date for 54 mugs.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered each of those participants either a replacement mug made by me or a place in another class. Cracking can happen in ceramics, and we had said so on the booking page. I still needed to set a collection time that allowed for the amount of work and the risks involved.

I also underestimated how much staff time each mug would need after the class. I based the £48 price on an estimate of 25 minutes of work per mug once the participant had left. We measured an average of 46 minutes for finishing, glazing, tracking, and packing each mug. That is 21 minutes more than I allowed for when I set the price.

We received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. Those emails came from 18 participants. We did not track whether the delays affected later bookings or shop sales. I have no figures that answer that question.

From June, I’ll offer a two-session mug class for £72. The sessions will take place two weeks apart. You’ll shape your mug in the first session, and you’ll glaze it in the second. We’ll schedule collection for two weeks after that second session.

This gives participants a hand in both shaping and glazing, and it puts the collection date after the glazing session instead of three weeks after shaping. It also means the class asks for two visits, and the price will be higher.

I haven’t tested this format yet, so I don’t know whether the timing or structure will work as intended. I’ll run four classes and then decide whether to keep it.

If you booked the one-afternoon class, thank you for trusting us with your mug after you left. I set the original collection promise. After I saw how often my staff and I missed it, I decided to stop running that class.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P7_R4_C2 -->

</details>

````text
I’ve stopped offering our “make a mug in one afternoon” class, and I want to explain why. We ran the class on 14 Saturdays between January and April 2026. There were eight places each Saturday, every place was booked, and 112 people attended in total.

During the class, participants spent the first two hours shaping a mug. Once they left, studio staff took over. We handled the drying, bisque firing, glazing and final firing, then tracked and packed each finished mug. The advertised collection time was three weeks after the class.

For 58 mugs, we met that three-week collection time. Forty-one were ready during week four. Thirteen took five weeks or longer. So, although some people collected on time, 54 of the 112 mugs were not ready within the three weeks we had advertised.

Seven mugs cracked during drying or firing. We offered those participants a choice between a replacement made by me and a place in another class. The booking page did say that cracking was possible. The problem is that the three-week collection time appeared in the headline. Putting the collection time in the headline gave it more prominence than the warning about cracks. That headline was my responsibility, and so was the promise it made.

I also got the workload estimate wrong. When I set the price at £48, I allowed for 25 minutes of staff time per mug after the class. In practice, staff spent an average of 46 minutes finishing, glazing, tracking and packing each participant’s mug. That work was happening after the two hours participants spent shaping their pieces.

We received 23 emails asking when a mug would be ready. They came from 18 participants. I don’t want to attach effects to those delays that we didn’t measure: the studio did not track whether they affected later bookings or shop sales.

I’m ending the one-afternoon format because the promise and the process didn’t match. Participants finished their part after shaping, while the studio still had several stages to complete. We gave a three-week collection time even though only 58 mugs were ready by then. The amount of staff work after each class was also much higher than the estimate used to set the price.

Beginning in June, we’ll offer a two-session mug class for £72. The sessions will be two weeks apart. Participants will shape their mugs in the first session and glaze them in the second. Collection will be scheduled for two weeks after that second session.

The second session means participants will handle one of the stages that staff previously completed after they left. It also means the collection date will be counted from the glazing session, rather than from the day the mug is shaped.

I need to be clear about one more thing: this new format has not been tested yet. I can explain what we’re changing, but I can’t claim that the new schedule works before we’ve run it. There isn’t a result to report yet, and I don’t want the new timetable to become another promise made too early. I’ll run four of the new classes and then decide whether to keep the format.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P7_R4_C3 -->

</details>

````text
Clarity at the Point of Decision: Why Every Assignment Needs an AI Statement

Calder University should require an AI-use statement on every assessed assignment. Its institution-wide policy establishes an important framework, but it cannot by itself answer the question students face when beginning particular work: What may I do here? Because the policy allows each instructor to permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI, it delegates the decisive rule to the instructor. That flexibility is defensible, but delegation without a consistent written decision leaves students to interpret silence, recollect verbal guidance, or infer rules from other assignments. The university should preserve instructors’ discretion while requiring them to exercise it visibly.

The review of assignment briefs shows why the current arrangement is inadequate. Of 240 briefs, only 94 explicitly stated whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven referred students to the university policy, while 89 did not mention AI. These figures do not prove that 146 assignments lacked guidance: some students may have received verbal instructions, and a policy reference may have been sufficient in context. Nevertheless, written briefs are the durable record that students consult while working and that staff later examine when questions arise. A general policy saying that instructors may choose among several approaches does not necessarily reveal which approach governs a specific assignment. When the operative rule can vary, the place where the task is defined is also where the rule should appear.

Student responses reinforce the case, although they must be interpreted cautiously. Among the 623 respondents to the student union’s survey, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment. The survey had a response rate of roughly one third, did not identify which briefs respondents had seen, and cannot connect uncertainty to briefs that were explicit, policy-only, or silent. It therefore cannot demonstrate that missing statements caused confusion. Yet it would be equally mistaken to dismiss the result. A substantial number of students chose to report uncertainty under a system intended to guide them, and 207 said rules differed between assignments in the same module. Those differences may have been intentional. If so, that strengthens rather than weakens the case for assignment-level statements: legitimate variation needs to be communicated precisely where it occurs.

The Academic Integrity Office’s record gives the issue institutional as well as pedagogical significance. Of 73 AI-related referrals, 21 were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. A dismissal does not prove that a student used AI, nor does it show that better wording would have prevented the referral. Still, these cases expose a basic procedural weakness. Universities should not initiate allegations that their written instructions cannot sustain. Ambiguous or unavailable rules consume staff time, place students under avoidable suspicion, and make enforcement appear arbitrary. Clear assignment-level statements would not eliminate disputed facts or poor judgment, but they would establish a more reliable basis for deciding whether a referral is warranted.

The strongest practical objection is that a compulsory statement could become a bureaucratic checkbox that oversimplifies complex teaching decisions. The history chair is right that some assignments require a paragraph of explanation, while others may need nothing beyond the institution-wide policy. A rigid menu could also encourage formulaic wording that is technically present but educationally useless. Engineering’s experience illustrates both the promise and the limitation: after its school required an “AI use for this assignment” box, 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained a completed box, but the audit did not assess clarity or student compliance. High completion demonstrates feasibility, not effectiveness.

That objection argues for careful design, not for abandoning the requirement. The teaching committee’s proposal already allows instructors to add explanation after the statement. A concise opening declaration can orient students, while a paragraph can distinguish, for example, brainstorming from drafting, or coding assistance from producing an answer. If an instructor believes the university policy alone is sufficient, the assignment can say explicitly that the policy applies without additional restrictions. Requiring a statement need not require identical rules, identical length, or the student union’s exact three-option scheme. It requires only that students not have to guess whether silence is permissive, prohibitive, or accidental.

Implementation should therefore combine a mandatory field with limited, sensible support. The university can offer standard options—prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure—without forcing every assignment into them. Instructors should be able to supplement or replace standard wording when disciplinary or task-specific distinctions demand it. Module leaders can also check consistency where assignments intentionally differ and explain those differences. This creates a modest administrative obligation, but one proportionate to the authority instructors already possess and to the consequences attached to misunderstanding their choices.

An assignment brief is not merely a description of academic work; it is the immediate contract by which that work is judged. Calder’s institution-wide policy properly defines the boundaries of instructor discretion and requires disclosure when AI use is permitted. It does not, and cannot, communicate every assignment’s chosen boundary. The available evidence does not prove that a mandatory statement will reduce uncertainty or referrals by a particular amount. It does show that explicit written guidance is uneven, reported uncertainty is substantial, and some allegations have lacked adequate instructional support. Requiring a clear AI-use statement on every assessed assignment is therefore not an attempt to centralize pedagogy. It is the necessary counterpart to decentralizing the rules.
````

````text
# Put the AI rule where students need it

Calder University should require an AI-use statement on every assessed assignment. An institution-wide policy remains necessary, but it cannot answer the question a student faces while working: What may I do on this particular task? Calder's policy gives instructors discretion to permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI. Once the university allows that variation, it also assumes a duty to communicate each decision at the point of use. A rule that exists somewhere in university policy is not enough when its application can change from one assignment to the next.

The review of assignment briefs shows how unreliable the current arrangement is. Of 240 briefs examined in February 2026, only 94 explicitly said whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven referred students to the university policy, which explains the range of choices available to instructors but may not reveal which choice applies. Another 89 did not mention AI at all. Some instructors may have supplemented those briefs verbally, and the review cannot tell us otherwise. Yet assessed work should not depend on a student's memory of something said in class, especially when classmates may have been absent or understood the instruction differently. Written briefs are the durable record shared by students, markers, and integrity panels. They should contain the operative rule.

The student union's survey adds reason for concern, though it does not prove that omissions in briefs caused confusion. Among 623 respondents, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment. The survey cannot link those students to any category in the brief review, and its respondents were only part of the 1,860 students invited. Those limits matter. Even so, uncertainty reported by well over half of the respondents is too substantial to dismiss as a few careless students failing to read policy. The 207 respondents who said rules differed between two assignments in the same module point to another communication problem. The differences may have been intentional. If so, that is a stronger argument for assignment-level statements, not a weaker one. Students need to know when the rule has changed and why.

The integrity figures make the case more urgent. From September through March, the Academic Integrity Office received 73 AI-related referrals. Twenty-one were dismissed before a formal hearing because the assignment instructions available did not support the alleged violation. We should be precise about what that means. A dismissal does not show that a student used AI, nor does it prove that clearer wording would have prevented the referral. Still, an allegation that cannot be supported by the written instructions is an avoidable institutional failure. Referrals consume staff time and place students under suspicion. The later outcomes, including 18 violations, 14 findings of no violation, and 20 cases still open on April 1, also show that AI cases are not simple. Clear instructions will not settle every factual dispute, but they can establish what rule the evidence is supposed to test.

A required statement need not force every assignment into the same pedagogical mold. The student union's three options offer a workable base: AI is prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure. The teaching committee's proposal sensibly allows instructors to add explanation. That final provision answers the history chair's fair concern that some assignments require a paragraph rather than a box. A source analysis might permit AI for brainstorming search terms but prohibit generated prose. A programming task might allow help with debugging while requiring students to explain any generated code. A short statement can name the category; a paragraph can handle the nuance.

The strongest practical objection is that a universal requirement creates repetitive administration and may produce bad boilerplate. Instructors already manage crowded templates, and requiring another field on every brief could encourage hurried wording that students skim. Some assignments may genuinely need nothing beyond the institution-wide policy. The engineering school's experience does not dispose of this objection. Its required box appeared in 46 of 50 sampled briefs, but the audit checked completion, not clarity or student compliance. A box can be filled and still fail.

That objection calls for a modest design, not abandonment of the requirement. Calder should provide standard options, permit additional explanation, and make instructors choose the applicable one when preparing each brief. For an assignment governed entirely by the default policy, the statement can say exactly that. Repetition is useful here. Students should not have to infer that silence means the default, wonder whether an old verbal comment still applies, or search a general policy for a local decision. The extra work is one deliberate choice and, where needed, a few sentences.

Assignment-level statements will not eliminate uncertainty, misconduct, or disputed referrals. The evidence does not justify that promise. They will do something more basic: give every assessed task a written rule that students can find and instructors can defend. Calder has already chosen flexibility over a single university-wide answer. It should now require the clarity that flexibility demands.
````

````text
# Each Assignment Needs Its Own AI Rule

Calder University should require an AI-use statement on each assessed assignment. Its institution-wide policy gives instructors discretion to permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI and asks students to disclose permitted use. That framework sets authority and responsibility, but it does not tell a student which choice an instructor has made for a given task. Students need the rule where they encounter the work: on the assignment brief. They should not have to cross-reference a general policy and guess which local rule governs before they begin. A short statement would turn institutional discretion into usable instruction while preserving an instructor’s control over assessment design.

The university’s review found a large gap between policy and written practice. Of 240 briefs, 94 stated whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven pointed to the university policy, which lists the instructor’s options without identifying the option for that assignment. Eighty-nine said nothing about AI. A silent brief might have come with verbal guidance, so the review cannot prove that students received no direction. Yet assessed work should not depend on a student’s memory of a classroom remark, attendance at one session, or access to an informal clarification. Written briefs provide the common record for students, instructors, and case reviewers.

The student survey adds cause for action, though it cannot settle cause. Among 623 respondents, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment. The survey cannot link those students to silent briefs, policy-only briefs, or explicit briefs. It also drew 623 responses from 1,860 students, so administrators should not treat its percentages as a precise measure of the whole student body. Even with those limits, 381 reports warrant attention. The university can address one plausible source of uncertainty at low cost by requiring instructors to record their decision on each task.

Differences within modules make assignment-level statements more useful. In the survey, 207 respondents said two assignments in the same module had different rules. The survey did not ask whether instructors intended those differences. Some differences may reflect sound design: an instructor might permit brainstorming for one task and prohibit AI drafting for another. An institution-wide policy can accommodate such choices, but only a task-level statement can communicate them at the point of submission. Requiring a statement would also help instructors distinguish a planned variation from an accidental omission.

Academic integrity cases expose another weakness in the current arrangement. From September through March, the Academic Integrity Office received 73 AI-related referrals. The office dismissed 21 before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. Those dismissals do not prove that students used AI, and they do not show that better wording would have stopped a referral. They show something narrower and useful: universities cannot enforce a task rule that the written instructions fail to establish. Clear assignment records protect students from unsupported allegations and give instructors a firmer basis for raising warranted concerns.

The strongest practical objection comes from the history department. Its chair argues that some tasks need a paragraph while others need no AI-specific instruction beyond university policy. A required box could invite stock wording, add template clutter, and flatten distinctions among disciplines. The engineering audit cannot answer that concern. After the school added a required box, 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained one, but auditors checked completion rather than clarity or student compliance. High completion shows that a mandate can change documentation; it does not show that a box produces good guidance.

Calder can answer that objection through the design of the requirement. The teaching committee’s proposal lets instructors add explanation after the statement. It should keep that flexibility and require a clear baseline choice, not a fixed explanation. The student union’s three options offer a workable starting point: prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure. An instructor who needs nuance could name allowed uses, define limits, or add a paragraph. An instructor who believes the university policy supplies all needed guidance could say that the policy governs the task, but should still state the permitted category so students need not infer it.

A statement mandate will not cure vague prose, careless referrals, or student misconduct. Calder should review sample wording and allow departments to adapt templates to their forms of assessment. It should also treat the first cycle as a chance to improve language, since the engineering audit measured presence alone. These steps impose some work on instructors during the transition. Yet instructors already must decide whether AI fits each assessment. Recording that decision asks them to make an existing judgment visible, not undertake a new branch of course design.

University policy belongs in the background as a source of shared principles, disclosure duties, and instructor authority. Assignment briefs must carry the operational rule. Calder’s record cannot prove that a statement mandate will remove uncertainty or reduce referrals, and the university should not promise either result. It provides enough evidence of inconsistent written guidance, reported confusion, and enforcement problems to justify a modest requirement. The committee should vote in May for an AI-use statement on each assessed assignment, with room for the explanation that sound teaching may demand.
````

````text
Put the AI Rule Where the Student Needs It

Calder University should require an AI-use statement on every assessed assignment. Its institution-wide policy, adopted in August 2025, gives instructors the right degree of academic discretion: they may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI, and students must disclose permitted use. What it does not give students is a reliable answer at the point of decision. A rule that can change from one assignment to the next belongs on each assignment.

The university’s own review makes the practical gap hard to dismiss. Of 240 written briefs across 60 undergraduate modules, only 94 explicitly said whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven referred students only to the institution-wide policy, which cannot tell them what choice a particular instructor has made. Eighty-nine did not mention AI at all. Some instructors may have supplemented those briefs verbally; the February 2026 review examined written documents alone. Yet assessed work should not depend on a student remembering an oral qualification or finding the right general policy and guessing how it applies. Written assignment instructions are the shared record available to student and instructor.

Student responses point in the same direction without proving a causal link. The student union received 623 responses from 1,860 students surveyed in March 2026, and 381 respondents reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment that academic year. The survey did not identify which briefs they had seen, so those responses cannot be mapped to the review’s three categories. Nor can the survey tell us whether assignment-level statements would have resolved each doubt. Even with those limits, uncertainty reported by well over half of respondents is a service problem worth fixing when the remedy is a short, local instruction.

The 207 respondents who said rules differed between two assignments in the same module expose a related issue. The survey did not ask whether the differences were intentional. They may have been entirely sound: an instructor might allow AI-assisted brainstorming for one task and prohibit generated prose for another. That possibility strengthens the case for assignment-level statements. Variation is legitimate only when students can see it. A module-level understanding, like an institution-wide policy, cannot carry instructions that deliberately change with the assessed task.

The integrity process gives the written rule a second audience. Between September 2025 and March 2026, the Academic Integrity Office recorded 73 AI-related referrals. Twenty-one were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. A dismissal does not establish that a student used AI, and clearer wording cannot be credited with preventing a referral that never happened. Still, these dismissals identify a concrete administrative failure: the university lacked written instructions adequate to support the allegation it had received. The other referrals resulted in 18 findings of a violation, 14 findings of no violation, and 20 cases still open on April 1. Whatever the eventual balance, instructors, students, and case reviewers all need the same assignment-specific text.

The strongest objection is that a mandatory statement could flatten legitimate differences among disciplines and tasks. The history department’s chair made the point plainly: some assignments need a paragraph of explanation, while others need nothing beyond the university policy. A rigid three-choice box could also invite false confidence. “Permitted for named tasks,” for example, still requires someone to name the tasks with enough precision to guide research, drafting, editing, and disclosure. Requiring words in a template is not the same as requiring useful words.

That objection should shape the rule, not defeat it. The teaching committee’s May proposal already allows instructors to add an explanation after the required statement. It should preserve that freedom and require a minimum declaration, rather than impose the student union’s three options as exhaustive language. An instructor could state “prohibited,” “unrestricted with disclosure,” or “permitted only for the following tasks,” then add as much detail as the assignment needs. Where the institution-wide policy truly supplies the whole answer, the brief can say so explicitly. That sentence still performs a useful function: it confirms that the omission was not accidental and directs the student to the controlling rule.

Engineering’s experience suggests that this requirement is workable, though it says less about quality. After the school added a required “AI use for this assignment” box to its brief template in January 2026, 46 of 50 briefs sampled in February contained a completed box. The audit checked completion only. It did not assess clarity or student conduct.

If the committee adopts the requirement in May, its next review should read the statements rather than count completed boxes. Reviewers should also check whether assignment language matches the institution-wide disclosure duty, since a local permission that omits disclosure could leave students with only half the applicable instruction. It should assess whether each brief identifies the permitted tasks, any prohibition, and the disclosure expected under the central policy. A later student survey could ask about named assignments and the written statements attached to them. That design would describe where uncertainty remains without claiming that a statement caused or cured it. Calder would then be judging the instruction students received, not the presence of a label.
````

````text
# Every Assignment Should Answer the AI Question

Calder University should require every assessed assignment to state what uses of generative AI are permitted. Its institution-wide policy establishes an essential framework, but it cannot perform the more specific work students and instructors need at the point of assessment. Because the policy expressly allows instructors to permit, limit, or prohibit AI, it creates legitimate variation across assignments. That flexibility is valuable only when students can see how it applies to the work in front of them. A short assignment-level statement would preserve instructor judgment while making the terms of assessment explicit.

The university’s review of written briefs shows why the general policy is insufficient on its own. Of 240 briefs, only 94 explicitly stated whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven referred students to the institution-wide policy, while 89 did not mention AI at all. These figures do not prove that students received no guidance: some instructors may have explained their expectations verbally. Yet assessed work should not depend on whether a student attended to, accurately remembered, or later reconstructed a spoken instruction. Written briefs are the durable record available to students while they work and to staff if a dispute arises. If an AI rule matters enough to affect assessment, it belongs in that record.

The student union survey strengthens the case, although it must be interpreted carefully. Among 623 respondents, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment. The survey cannot connect those students to particular categories of briefs, and its 623 responses do not automatically represent all 1,860 students surveyed. It therefore cannot establish that missing statements caused the uncertainty. Still, the result identifies a substantial practical problem: many responding students did not feel able to determine the rules reliably. An assignment-level requirement directly addresses that problem by putting an answer where students are most likely to look.

The survey’s finding that 207 respondents encountered different rules on two assignments in the same module might initially appear to support a uniform institutional rule instead. It does not. The survey did not ask whether the differences were intentional, and there are good reasons, visible within Calder’s own policy, for requirements to vary. One task may assess unaided composition; another may invite students to critique generated material. The important objective is not identical rules but intelligible rules. Indeed, variation within a module makes assignment-level statements more necessary, because a module-level assumption may be correct for one task and wrong for the next.

The Academic Integrity Office’s records reveal a second reason for requiring written statements: clarity protects the integrity process as well as students. Of 73 AI-related referrals, 21 were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. Those dismissals do not prove that students used AI, nor that better wording would have prevented every referral. They do show, however, that in nearly three out of ten recorded referrals, the written instructional basis was inadequate to proceed. That is costly in attention, anxiety, and institutional credibility even when no conclusion about student conduct can be drawn. Clear assignment terms would give instructors, students, and reviewers a common starting point.

The strongest practical objection comes from the history department. Its chair argues that some assignments require a paragraph of explanation, whereas others need nothing beyond the university policy. A mandatory box could encourage formulaic wording, oversimplify complicated judgments, and add repetitive administrative work. These concerns deserve more than dismissal. A poorly designed requirement might produce technically completed statements that remain ambiguous, as the engineering evidence itself warns: 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained a completed box, but the audit did not examine clarity or student compliance.

That objection argues for a flexible requirement, not for leaving statements optional. The teaching committee’s proposal already permits instructors to add explanation after the statement. Calder should use a concise declaration as the entry point, with additional wording whenever the assessment demands it. The student union’s three options—prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure—offer a useful baseline, though the middle option will often need elaboration. For an assignment governed entirely by the institutional default, an instructor could simply state that the university policy applies and that there are no additional assignment-specific conditions. Repeating that fact imposes a very small burden while removing the need for students to infer whether silence is deliberate.

Implementation should therefore emphasize meaningful completion rather than mere box-ticking. The template should prompt instructors to name permitted tasks where relevant and to align any disclosure requirement with the university policy. Departments should retain freedom to supply a paragraph, examples, or discipline-specific qualifications. The institution need not dictate one detailed formulation for every kind of work; it need only require that each brief contain a findable, written answer. The engineering school’s experience suggests that template compliance is operationally feasible, even though it does not yet demonstrate that the resulting language is effective.

Calder’s current policy sensibly leaves academic decisions with instructors. Requiring an assignment-level AI statement would not reverse that settlement; it would make those decisions visible. The available evidence cannot prove that the requirement will eliminate uncertainty, prevent referrals, or improve compliance. No wording rule can do so. But the record shows inconsistent written communication, reported student uncertainty, and integrity cases weakened by inadequate instructions. Against those concrete problems, the principal cost is a brief declaration supplemented when necessary. The teaching committee should vote yes: flexibility belongs in the substance of AI rules, not in whether students are told what those rules are.
````

````text
Clarity at the Point of Use: Why Every Assignment Needs an AI Statement

Calder University’s institution-wide AI policy gives instructors sensible freedom. They may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI in assessed work, and students must disclose any permitted use. What the policy does not reliably give students is an answer at the moment they need one: when they sit down to complete a particular assignment. The university should therefore require every assessed assignment to state what AI use is permitted. An assignment-level statement would not replace the institution-wide policy or restrict academic judgment. It would make that judgment visible.

The written record shows a serious communication gap. In February 2026, Calder reviewed 240 assignment briefs across 60 undergraduate modules. Only 94 briefs explicitly explained whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven referred students to the university policy, while 89 did not mention AI at all. Some instructors may have provided verbal directions, so the review cannot tell us that students received no guidance in every silent case. Even so, assessed work should not depend on a student remembering a remark made in class or finding the relevant passage in a general policy. Written briefs are the durable instructions against which students plan their work and, if necessary, defend it.

The student union’s survey adds reason for concern, though not proof of a direct causal link. Of 623 respondents, 381 said they had been unsure about permitted AI use on at least one assignment that academic year. The survey had a modest response rate from the 1,860 students contacted, and it did not identify which briefs respondents had seen. We cannot claim that silent briefs caused the reported uncertainty. Nor can we dismiss the result. When a clear majority of respondents report confusion about an issue that instructors are free to decide assignment by assignment, the burden should fall on the university to make those decisions easier to find.

Variation itself is not the problem. Different assignments may call for different rules. An instructor might permit AI-assisted brainstorming for one task, prohibit it in a test of independent analysis, and allow unrestricted use with disclosure in another. Such distinctions can be pedagogically sound. Yet 207 survey respondents said rules differed between two assignments in the same module. Because the survey did not ask whether those differences were intentional, that figure does not establish inconsistency or poor design. It does show why relying on a module handbook, an early lecture, or general institutional language is inadequate. If rules may change within a module, each brief must tell students which rule applies.

The Academic Integrity Office’s figures make the cost of ambiguity harder to ignore. Between September 2025 and March 2026, it received 73 AI-related referrals. Twenty-one were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. A dismissal does not prove that a student used AI, and clearer language might not have prevented every referral. Still, those cases reveal a basic administrative failure: students were referred under instructions that could not sustain the allegation. Such referrals consume staff time and place students under avoidable suspicion. The other outcomes, including 18 findings of a violation, 14 findings of no violation, and 20 cases still open on April 1, also caution against easy conclusions. The figures do not measure how much clarity would change conduct. They do show that enforceable expectations need a clear written basis.

The engineering school offers a useful, limited example. After it added a required “AI use for this assignment” box to its template in January 2026, 46 of 50 briefs sampled in February contained a completed box. The audit did not judge the wording or examine student compliance, so it cannot establish that the box improved understanding. It does establish something more modest and immediately relevant: a template requirement can achieve high written coverage quickly. Calder is not considering an administrative fantasy. One part of the university has already shown that instructors can routinely complete such a field.

The strongest objection comes from the history department. Its chair argued that some assignments need a paragraph of explanation, while others need nothing beyond the university policy. That concern deserves more than a standardized checkbox that flattens genuine differences among disciplines and tasks. The student union’s proposed three options could become too blunt if treated as complete instructions. “Permitted for named tasks,” for example, is useful only if those tasks are actually named with enough precision.

But this is an argument for flexible statements, not for silence. The teaching committee’s proposal already allows instructors to add an explanation. A brief could state that AI is prohibited, identify permitted uses, allow unrestricted use with disclosure, or say that the institution-wide policy applies without further restriction. A historian who needs a paragraph can write one. An instructor whose assignment requires no special rule can use a single sentence. The requirement concerns disclosure of the rule, not uniformity of the rule itself.

Calder should adopt the proposal in May, while resisting the temptation to treat completion as success. A box can contain vague language just as easily as a paragraph can. The university should review wording over time and revise its template when recurring confusion appears. It should also preserve instructors’ authority to match rules to learning aims.

An institution-wide policy is necessary, but it sits too far from the work students actually submit. The assignment is where general policy becomes a specific academic expectation. Requiring a statement there would give students fair notice, give instructors a firmer basis for enforcement, and give administrators a written record when disputes arise. Calder’s evidence cannot promise that the requirement will eliminate uncertainty or misconduct. It supports the more practical conclusion that no assessed assignment should leave its AI rule to inference.
````

````text
# Calder Needs an AI Statement on Every Assignment

Calder University should require an AI-use statement on each assessed assignment. Its institution-wide policy gives instructors sound discretion: they may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI, and students must disclose any use that an instructor permits. Yet discretion works if students can find the instructor’s decision at the point where they need it. A university policy can set the governing framework. It cannot tell a student whether using a chatbot to outline a lab report, revise a paragraph, or generate code fits the rules for the task in front of them. The assignment brief should do that job.

Calder’s review shows a large gap between policy and written instructions. Of 240 briefs from 60 undergraduate modules, 94 stated whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven referred readers to the university policy, which leaves the key choice to the instructor. Eighty-nine said nothing about AI. In other words, 146 briefs gave students no written assignment-specific decision. Some instructors may have supplied verbal directions, so the review cannot prove that all 146 left students uninformed. Yet verbal guidance creates its own weakness. Students may miss a class or remember a qualification in different terms. Instructors and integrity panels then lack a shared text to consult.

The student union survey adds a reason to act, though it does not establish cause. Among 623 respondents, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment. The survey cannot link those students to any category in the brief review, and its 623 responses do not speak for all 1,860 students whom the union surveyed. Nor can we infer that assignment-level statements would erase uncertainty. Still, a university should not dismiss uncertainty reported by more than half of the respondents when its own document review found assignment-specific guidance in fewer than two briefs out of five. The two findings describe the same governance weakness from different angles: Calder has delegated the choice to instructors without requiring them to record the choice where students encounter assessed work.

Case referrals show the cost of that weakness for staff and students. From September through March, the Academic Integrity Office received 73 AI-related referrals. The office dismissed 21 before a formal hearing because the assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. Those dismissals do not show whether students used AI, and no one can claim that better wording would have prevented each referral. They do establish a narrower point. In almost three referrals out of ten, written instructions could not sustain the allegation. An instructor who wants to prohibit or limit AI needs to communicate that rule before assessment, rather than reconstruct it after suspicion arises. A required statement would give the instructor, student, and case reviewer the same starting document.

Critics have a serious practical objection. A required box can turn an academic judgment into a compliance ritual. The history chair argues that some tasks need a paragraph while others need no instruction beyond university policy. The first claim has force. A generic label may hide questions about brainstorming or editing. Requiring lengthy prose for a task with a straightforward rule can add clutter to a brief and work to an instructor’s load. The second claim is harder to defend because Calder’s policy does not decide what students may do. It delegates that decision. A reference to the policy sends students back to a document that tells them to consult their instructor.

Calder should answer the valid part of the objection through design. The proposal before the teaching committee lets instructors add explanation after the statement. It should require a short declaration, then leave its length and detail to the person who designed the assessment. An instructor could prohibit AI, permit it for named tasks, or allow unrestricted use with disclosure, using the student union’s three options as a starting structure. Instructors who need nuance could add a paragraph. Those with a simple rule could complete one line. The requirement concerns recording the decision while preserving varied teaching practice.

Engineering’s experience suggests that such a requirement can work. After the school added an “AI use for this assignment” box to its template in January, a February audit found completed boxes in 46 of 50 sampled briefs. The audit did not test clarity or student compliance, and one month in one school offers no basis for broad claims about success. It does show that instructors can incorporate the field into routine brief preparation at a high rate. Calder should pair the requirement with examples suited to different kinds of assessment and ask departments to review ambiguous wording. The box supplies a prompt; instructors must supply the judgment.

The report that 207 survey respondents encountered different rules on two assignments in the same module may reflect sound assessment design. A module might permit AI during one exercise and prohibit it during another because the tasks assess different capabilities. The survey did not ask whether instructors intended those differences. Assignment-level statements would make such variation easier to defend. Students could see that the change belongs to the design, while instructors could explain its purpose in the space after the declaration. Calder can place each rule in a consistent location while allowing assignments to use different rules.

Calder adopted its university policy in August 2025, but the record through spring 2026 shows that a framework alone leaves too much inference to students, instructors, and integrity staff. The evidence cannot promise fewer violations or referrals, and it does not prove that silence in a brief caused the uncertainty reported in the survey. Universities need not wait for proof of causation before fixing an evident communication gap. The teaching committee should require an AI-use statement on each assessed assignment, preserve instructors’ control over the substance, and allow as much explanation as the task demands. A rule that changes from assignment to assignment belongs on each assignment.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P8_R2_C2 -->

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P8_R2_C3 -->
````text
Put the AI Rule Where the Student Needs It

Calder University’s institution-wide AI policy delegates the decisive question to instructors: they may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI in assessed work. That discretion is appropriate because assignments have different purposes. It also means the general policy cannot tell a student what is allowed on a particular task. Its disclosure requirement applies only after AI use has been permitted, so disclosure cannot answer the prior question of permission. Every assessed assignment should therefore carry a written statement of permitted AI use. The statement need not be long or standardized beyond making the decision visible; it should give local effect to the discretion that the university has already granted.

The review conducted in February 2026 found that only 94 of 240 assignment briefs explicitly stated whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven referred only to the institution-wide policy, which leaves unanswered the choice that the policy assigns to the instructor. Eighty-nine did not mention AI at all. Some of those briefs may have been accompanied by spoken directions, since the review examined written briefs only. Verbal guidance is a poor substitute for a rule that students may need to consult while working and that staff may later need when handling a referral. The review identifies a large gap in the written record without establishing that this gap caused confusion.

The student union survey gives that gap practical weight without proving its cause. Of 623 respondents from a survey of 1,860 students, 381 said they had been unsure about permitted AI use on at least one assignment during the academic year. The survey did not identify which briefs respondents had seen, so those answers cannot be tied to briefs that were explicit, policy-only, or silent. Nor can the response group be treated as representative of every Calder student. Still, uncertainty reported by 381 respondents is an administrative problem worth addressing. A statement on each brief targets the moment at which the missing decision becomes relevant.

The same logic applies when rules vary. In the survey, 207 respondents said that two assignments in the same module had different rules. The survey did not ask whether the differences were intentional. They may have reflected sound assessment design: an instructor might allow AI for preliminary idea generation in one task and prohibit it in another. Assignment-level statements would preserve that flexibility while making the change deliberate and inspectable. Consistency should not mean identical rules. It should mean that students do not have to infer whether a difference is planned, accidental, or merely omitted from the brief.

The Academic Integrity Office’s cases expose the cost of leaving that inference unresolved. It recorded 73 AI-related referrals between September 2025 and March 2026. Twenty-one were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. A dismissal establishes neither that a student used AI nor that clearer wording would have prevented the referral. The narrower finding is consequential: in 21 cases, the instructions available to the university could not sustain the allegation. Referrals consume staff attention and place students under suspicion. A university should state the applicable rule before asking either side to defend conduct against it. The remaining referrals produced 18 findings of a violation and 14 findings of no violation, while 20 cases were still open on April 1.

The strongest objection is that a mandatory statement could become a bureaucratic box that flattens real differences among disciplines and assignments. The history department’s chair put the problem well: some tasks need a paragraph of explanation, while others need nothing beyond the university policy. A rigid three-choice form, such as the student union’s options of prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure, may not capture every teaching decision. The proposal before the teaching committee does not confine instructors to those labels. It allows explanation after the statement. An instructor who needs a paragraph can write one; an instructor who intends the general policy to govern can say so. The common element is the presence of a decision, while its length and reasoning remain matters for the instructor. Requiring an answer is different from prescribing the answer’s substance.

Engineering’s experience offers limited evidence about the administrative burden and none about the quality of the statements. After the school added an “AI use for this assignment” box in January 2026, a February audit found a completed box in 46 of 50 sampled briefs. The audit checked whether the box was completed; it did not assess whether the wording was clear or students followed it.

Calder should pair the requirement with review aimed at meaning rather than mere completion. The first audit should examine whether each statement identifies prohibited uses, permitted tasks, and disclosure expectations where those details apply. Later evaluation should keep separate questions separate: whether students understand the wording, whether instructors apply it as written, and whether unsupported referrals decline. None of those outcomes is guaranteed by adding a box. The institution-wide policy has already made assignment-specific judgment unavoidable. The written brief is where that judgment should become accessible, stable, and fair enough to administer. If the teaching committee adopts the requirement in May, its next task should be to assess the words inside the box.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P8_R2_C3 -->

</details>

````text
disclosure—offer
````
