Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P8_R3_C0 -->
````text
Clarity at the Point of Use: Why Every Assignment Needs an AI Statement

Calder University should require an AI-use statement on every assessed assignment. Its institution-wide policy establishes an essential common framework, but it cannot tell students what is permitted in a particular piece of work when instructors remain free to permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI. That discretion is academically valuable, yet it creates a corresponding duty to communicate each decision where students actually need it: on the assignment brief. A short, assignment-level statement would not replace the university policy or constrain legitimate teaching choices. It would translate a general policy into usable instructions.

The February 2026 review shows why the general policy is not sufficient by itself. Of 240 assignment briefs, only 94 explicitly stated whether and how generative AI could be used. Fifty-seven referred students only to the institution-wide policy, although that policy does not itself settle what is allowed on any given task. Eighty-nine did not mention AI at all. Some of those briefs may have been supplemented by verbal instructions, so the review cannot prove that students received no guidance. Even so, assessed work should not depend on whether a student remembers an announcement, attended a particular class, or interpreted a general policy in the same way as an instructor. Written instructions provide a common, reviewable record for students, markers, and integrity panels.

Student responses reinforce the case, though they must be interpreted cautiously. Of the 623 students who answered the union’s survey, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment. Because only 623 of 1,860 surveyed students responded, the result cannot be treated as a precise measure of uncertainty across the university. Nor can the survey connect respondents’ experiences to briefs that contained explicit guidance, merely cited university policy, or said nothing. Nevertheless, 381 reports of uncertainty are not trivial. They demonstrate that the existing arrangements have left many respondents unsure at least once about conduct that can affect both their grades and their academic standing.

The integrity data make clear that ambiguity also imposes institutional costs. Between September 2025 and March 2026, the Academic Integrity Office received 73 AI-related referrals. Twenty-one were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. Those dismissals do not prove that students used AI, and they do not prove that clearer wording would have prevented every referral. They do show, however, that enforceable expectations require assignment-specific evidence. When instructions cannot support an allegation, students endure avoidable anxiety and staff spend time preparing referrals that cannot proceed. Clear statements would not eliminate disputed cases, but they would give everyone a firmer basis for deciding whether a referral is warranted.

Assignment-level variation is not itself a problem. Indeed, it may be pedagogically necessary. An instructor might prohibit AI in an exercise designed to test independent analysis, permit it for brainstorming in another task, and allow broad use with disclosure in a project about evaluating AI output. The 207 survey respondents who said rules differed between two assignments in the same module may have encountered inconsistency, but the survey did not ask whether the differences were intentional. The right response is therefore not to force uniform permissions across a module. It is to make intentional differences visible. The more legitimate variation the university allows, the less defensible it becomes to leave students to infer the rule.

The strongest practical objection is that a mandatory statement could become a bureaucratic box-ticking exercise. The history department reasonably observes that some assignments require a paragraph of explanation while others need nothing beyond the university policy. Complex disciplines and assessments cannot always be governed by three neat labels. A poorly designed template might encourage instructors to select “prohibited” or “permitted” without addressing editing tools, idea generation, translation, coding, feedback, or disclosure. The engineering school’s experience illustrates both the promise and the limit of templates: after it introduced a required box, 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained a completed one, but the audit did not test clarity or student compliance. Completion is not comprehension.

That objection argues for a flexible requirement, not for silence. The teaching committee’s proposal already allows instructors to add explanation after the statement. Calder should require every brief to identify the governing position and then permit as much elaboration as the task demands. An instructor who believes the university policy fully resolves the matter could state that explicitly and identify the applicable permission. Another could add a detailed paragraph distinguishing acceptable assistance from work students must perform independently. The student union’s three options—prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure—offer a useful starting structure, provided “permitted for named tasks” is accompanied by those names and instructors may explain important boundaries.

A requirement would also improve accountability without pretending that wording alone can govern behavior. Students would still need to read instructions, disclose permitted use, and seek clarification when unusual circumstances arise. Instructors would still need to design meaningful rules, and the university would need to review whether statements are understandable rather than merely present. The current evidence cannot establish that assignment-level statements will reduce violations, referrals, or uncertainty by a particular amount. It is strong enough, however, to identify a preventable weakness: the university permits local decisions while often failing to record those decisions locally.

Calder should adopt the requirement in May. A university-wide policy should supply shared principles and definitions; each assignment should state how those principles apply. This modest division of labor respects instructor judgment, accommodates disciplinary complexity, and gives students fair notice at the moment it matters. In an area where rules can legitimately change from one task to the next, clarity is not administrative decoration. It is part of sound assessment design.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P8_R3_C0 -->

</details>

- P013, Manufactured quotable, slogan, or aphorism: This restates the assignment-level-policy argument as a slogan-like travel metaphor primarily shaped for a memorable, quotable ending.

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P8_R3_C1 -->
````text
# Put the rule where the work is

Calder University should require an AI-use statement on every assessed assignment. Its institution-wide policy settles who has authority: instructors may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI, and students must disclose any permitted use. What it does not settle is what a student may do on Tuesday’s lab report, next week’s source analysis, or the final project. That decision belongs to the assignment, so the answer should appear on the assignment brief.

The university’s review shows how often students must look elsewhere. Of 240 briefs examined in February 2026, only 94 explicitly stated whether and how generative AI could be used. Fifty-seven referred students to the university policy, which merely sends them back to a document that says instructors can set different rules. Another 89 did not mention AI at all. Some instructors may have supplied verbal guidance, and the review cannot tell us how often that happened. But assessed work should not depend on a student remembering a comment made in class or finding an announcement buried in a module site. Written briefs exist to preserve instructions at the point where students need them.

The student union survey points in the same direction, though it cannot prove the cause. Among 623 respondents, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment during the academic year. The survey did not identify which briefs they had seen, so no one can claim that missing statements produced that uncertainty. Even so, the scale of uncertainty matters. A university does not need causal proof before fixing an obvious weakness in its instructions. When more than half the respondents say they were unsure about a rule that can affect their academic record, directing them to a general policy is not enough.

Assignment-level statements also respect the educational reason Calder gave instructors discretion in the first place. Appropriate AI use can vary between tasks, even within one module. A lecturer might prohibit generated prose in a close-reading exercise, permit help with debugging in a programming task, and allow unrestricted use with disclosure in a project about evaluating AI output. The 207 survey respondents who reported different rules for two assignments in the same module may have encountered inconsistency, but the differences may also have been intentional. The survey did not ask. Requiring a statement would not eliminate variation. It would make the reasoned exercise of academic judgment visible.

Clearer written rules would also improve the handling of suspected misconduct. From September 2025 through March 2026, the Academic Integrity Office received 73 AI-related referrals. Twenty-one were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. Those dismissals do not prove that students used AI, nor do they show that better wording would have stopped the referrals. They do show something narrower and still troubling: in nearly three out of ten referrals, the university could not proceed on the instructions it had. That is costly for staff and frightening for students. An allegation should not travel further than the written rule beneath it.

The strongest practical objection comes from the history department. Its chair argues that some assignments need a paragraph of explanation, while others need nothing beyond the university policy. That concern deserves more than a tidy checkbox imposed for administrative convenience. A rigid menu can flatten genuine differences between disciplines and tempt instructors to select an option without thinking through what it means. It may also create repetitive work, particularly when several assignments share the same rule.

But the proposal before the teaching committee is not rigid in that way. It requires a statement and allows instructors to add an explanation. The student union’s three suggested options, prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure, can provide a starting point rather than a complete vocabulary. If a history essay needs a paragraph distinguishing brainstorming from drafting, the instructor can write one. If the university policy is fully sufficient for a task, the brief can say so explicitly and explain that the default applies. Repetition is a small price for removing doubt, and templates can keep that burden to one sentence when the answer is simple.

Engineering’s experience suggests that such a requirement is workable. After the school added an "AI use for this assignment" box to its template in January 2026, 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained a completed box. The audit did not judge clarity or student compliance, so it would be foolish to present those figures as proof that the policy succeeded. They establish only that instructors usually completed the field. Still, feasibility is exactly what the practical objection puts in question, and on that limited point the engineering audit is useful. A required field can become part of ordinary assignment preparation without demanding a university-wide script.

Calder should therefore adopt the May proposal, with two safeguards. The template should permit explanation rather than treating the three options as self-explanatory, and departments should review wording for clarity rather than merely checking that a box contains text. The university should also make clear that verbal guidance cannot quietly replace the written statement. Students may still misunderstand rules, and misconduct cases will not disappear. No policy can promise that. But every assessed assignment should answer the question students are expected to get right. If AI rules can change with the work, the rules must travel with the work.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P8_R3_C1 -->

</details>

````text
# Put the AI Rule on the Assignment

Calder University has given instructors discretion over generative AI. It should require them to exercise that discretion in writing on each assessed assignment. The proposed assignment-level statement would preserve the choices in the August 2025 policy: an instructor could prohibit AI, permit it for named tasks, or allow unrestricted use with disclosure. An explanation could follow. Such a requirement would preserve differences among disciplines and tasks. It would ask a modest question: can a student find the rule that governs the work in front of them?

The institution-wide policy cannot answer that question. It says instructors may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI, and it requires students to disclose any permitted use. A student reading it learns the range of possible rules but not which rule applies to a lab report, source analysis, design exercise, or essay. The instructor must make that choice somewhere. If the assignment brief omits it, the student must search course materials, recall a verbal instruction, or ask. A university should place conditions for assessed work beside the task and its other requirements.

Calder’s review shows a written system riddled with gaps. Reviewers examined 240 briefs from 60 undergraduate modules. Ninety-four stated whether and how students could use AI. Fifty-seven sent students back to the university policy, which provides no assignment-specific answer. Eighty-nine said nothing about AI. Reviewers did not examine verbal instructions, so those 89 briefs do not prove that instructors gave no guidance. They do show that a student who returned to the written brief could find no AI rule there. For conditions that can affect an academic-integrity allegation, spoken guidance offers a poor common record for student and instructor.

The student survey adds evidence of uncertainty without locating its cause. Of 623 respondents, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment. The survey drew those responses from 1,860 students and did not identify which briefs the respondents had seen. No one can use it to claim that silent briefs caused 381 students to feel unsure. Yet the result identifies a substantial group with a problem the current system has failed to contain. An assignment-level statement addresses the point where that uncertainty must end: before the student begins assessed work.

The Academic Integrity Office’s figures give the issue an administrative cost. From September through March, the office received 73 AI-related referrals. It dismissed 21 before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. Those dismissals do not show that the students used AI, and clearer language might not have prevented each referral. They establish a narrower point with force: in more than one-quarter of referrals, the written or available instructions could not sustain the allegation. Instructors, students, and integrity staff all need a rule they can quote. Eighteen findings of a violation, 14 findings of no violation, and 20 open cases also counsel against treating referral counts as proof of misconduct.

Variation within modules strengthens the case. In the survey, 207 respondents said rules differed between two assignments in the same module. The survey did not ask whether instructors intended those differences. Many may have. A tutor might allow AI-assisted brainstorming for one task and prohibit generated prose for another. Assignment-level statements would make such design choices legible. A module-level notice may mislead when instructors change the purpose, process, or evidence of learning from one assessment to the next.

The strongest practical objection comes from the history chair. Some assignments need a paragraph; others need no AI-specific instruction beyond university policy. A required box can invite boilerplate, flatten disciplinary judgment, and add another template chore. The engineering audit cannot settle that concern. After the school introduced its box, 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained a completed one, but auditors assessed completion rather than clarity or student compliance. High uptake proves that staff can use the field. It does not prove that the words help.

Calder can meet that objection through the design before the teaching committee. Require a statement, then let instructors add an explanation. The three options supported by the student union offer a starting vocabulary, while added prose carries the nuance. An instructor who believes the university policy supplies enough guidance can state which permitted category applies and refer students to its disclosure rules. That takes a line. An instructor whose assignment requires distinctions among brainstorming, editing, coding, translation, or generated text can use the paragraph the history chair says some tasks need.

The mandate should govern presence and leave length to the instructor. Departments can provide examples, check statements during routine brief review, and examine clarity after adoption instead of counting completed boxes alone. Completing the statement also requires an instructor to decide before submissions arrive whether AI serves or defeats the learning task.

Requiring a statement will not eliminate uncertainty, misconduct, or disputed referrals. Calder’s records support no such promise. The requirement would do something basic and within the university’s control: each instructor would state the applicable rule at the place where a student receives the assessed task. The university has chosen instructor discretion. It owes students a dependable way to see how each instructor used it.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P8_R3_C2 -->

````text
Put the AI Rule Where Students Need It

Calder University’s teaching committee should vote in May to require an AI-use statement on every assessed assignment. The institution-wide policy adopted in August 2025 is a sound framework: instructors may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI, and students must disclose any permitted use. Yet a framework that deliberately leaves the decision to each instructor cannot, by itself, tell a student what applies to a particular piece of work. That decision belongs on the assignment.

The university’s February 2026 review makes the written-information gap plain. Of 240 briefs from 60 undergraduate modules, 94 explicitly said whether and how generative AI could be used. Fifty-seven referred students only to the institution-wide policy, which leaves assignment wording to instructors. Eighty-nine were silent about AI. Some briefs in that last group may have been accompanied by verbal instructions; written briefs were the sole scope of the review. Even so, 146 written briefs lacked an assignment-specific answer. Reconstructing an assessed-work rule from a general policy, a classroom remark, or a guess about an instructor’s intent is an unreasonable demand on students.

The student union’s March 2026 survey strengthens the case for a written statement without proving which kind of brief produced uncertainty. It received 623 responses from 1,860 students surveyed, and 381 respondents reported being unsure about permitted AI use on at least one assignment during the academic year. Because the survey neither identified the briefs each respondent had seen nor connected uncertainty to the review’s three categories, those 381 reports cannot be attributed to a category. They still identify a problem that an assignment-level statement is designed to address at the point where a student needs an answer.

Variation within modules gives the requirement a second purpose. In the same survey, 207 respondents said rules differed between two assignments in one module. The survey did not ask whether the differences were intentional. They may well have been: an instructor could reasonably permit AI for one task and prohibit it for another. That possibility supports assignment-level statements. When the rule can properly change from one assessment to the next, a module-level assumption is unsafe. A statement records the instructor’s choice where the choice applies.

The Academic Integrity Office’s cases show what ambiguity can cost after submission. From September 2025 through March 2026, it recorded 73 AI-related referrals. Twenty-one were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. A dismissal does not establish that a student used AI, and clearer wording cannot be assumed to have prevented the referral. Still, those 21 cases expose an avoidable procedural weakness: the university was asked to evaluate alleged misconduct against instructions that could not sustain the allegation. Of the other referrals, 18 ended in findings of a violation, 14 in findings of no violation, and 20 remained open on April 1. Clear assignment terms would give students, instructors, and reviewers a shared written basis for judgment while leaving each case to be decided on its merits.

The strongest practical objection is that a required box may flatten legitimate differences among disciplines and assignments. The history department voted against one for just that reason. Its chair argued that some tasks require a paragraph of explanation, while others need nothing beyond the university policy. The proposal can accommodate that concern because it permits instructors to add an explanation after the statement. A short declaration can direct students to the institution-wide rule when that rule is sufficient, while a tailored paragraph can name permitted tasks, limits, or conditions when it is not. The extra task is finite: select a declaration and, when needed, write the explanation already required to communicate the intended rule.

The student union’s three options offer a workable starting grammar: prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure. They should function as declarations, not as substitutes for academic judgment. “Permitted for named tasks,” in particular, invites the instructor to specify what those tasks are. The institution-wide disclosure duty remains in force. The assignment statement simply resolves the prior question of what use is permitted.

Engineering’s experience suggests that the administrative burden is manageable. Its school added a required “AI use for this assignment” box to the brief template in January 2026. A February audit found a completed box in 46 of 50 sampled briefs. The audit checked completion only; it did not assess clarity or whether students followed the wording. It therefore supports a modest conclusion: instructors can complete such a field at a high rate when it is built into the template. It does not establish that the field alone changes student conduct.

Under the proposed requirement, every assessed brief could contain one of the three declarations, with only as much explanation as the task demands. Departments would retain disciplinary discretion over the substance, while the university would set the minimum location and form. The institution-wide policy would continue to supply the common framework; assignment briefs would supply the local decision that the framework leaves to instructors. After adoption, the university’s first review should examine the clarity of the wording students receive, not merely whether a box is filled.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P8_R3_C3 -->

</details>

three options—prohibited, permitted for named tasks

unrestricted with disclosure—while allowing whatever elaboration

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P8_R4_C0 -->
````text
Clarity at the Point of Use: Why Every Assignment Needs an AI Statement

Calder University should require every assessed assignment to state what use of generative AI is permitted. Its institution-wide policy remains necessary, but it is not sufficient. The policy establishes instructors’ authority to permit, limit, or prohibit AI and requires students to disclose permitted use. Yet because the acceptable practice can vary by instructor and assignment, students need to know which rule applies when they begin the particular work being assessed. A brief assignment-level statement would connect the university’s general framework to the decision directly facing the student.

The university’s own review shows that this connection is often missing from written instructions. Of 240 assignment briefs examined in February 2026, only 94 explicitly stated whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven referred students only to the institution-wide policy, while 89 did not mention AI at all. These figures do not prove that 146 assignments lacked guidance: some instructors may have given verbal directions, and a general policy may have been adequate for some tasks. Nevertheless, assessed expectations should not depend on students remembering a classroom comment or inferring an instructor’s choice from a document that merely says choices are possible.

The student survey reinforces the case for action, although it cannot establish a direct causal link. Among 623 respondents, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment during the academic year. The response group was self-selected from 1,860 surveyed students, and the survey did not match respondents with specific briefs. It therefore cannot show that silence or policy-only references caused their uncertainty. Still, the result identifies a substantial practical problem: many responding students did not feel sure of the applicable rules. A university need not wait for a controlled demonstration of causation before improving a plainly inconsistent communication system.

That inconsistency matters because the institution-wide policy deliberately leaves the central decision to instructors. Flexibility is defensible: brainstorming with AI may support the aims of one task while defeating the aims of another. But decentralized discretion requires localized communication. Otherwise, the policy tells students that the answer varies without reliably telling them what the answer is. The 207 survey respondents who said rules differed between two assignments in the same module may have encountered intentional and pedagogically sound distinctions; the survey did not ask. Precisely for that reason, each assignment should state its own rule. Intentional differences become educative only when instructors explain them clearly.

Academic integrity referrals make the stakes more concrete. Between September 2025 and March 2026, 21 of 73 AI-related referrals were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. These dismissals do not show that the students used AI, nor do they prove that clearer instructions would have prevented every referral. They do show, however, that in almost three out of ten referrals the written or otherwise available instructions could not sustain the accusation. Ambiguity at that stage consumes staff time, subjects students to avoidable anxiety, and weakens confidence in enforcement. Clear assignment-level statements would create a more reliable basis for both compliance and judgment.

The strongest practical objection is that a required statement may become an administrative ritual: another box to complete, another line of boilerplate, and perhaps an oversimplification of assignments needing nuanced guidance. The history chair is right that some tasks require a paragraph while others may require nothing beyond the university policy. A rigid three-word label could obscure distinctions among idea generation, language correction, coding assistance, source discovery, and production of submitted prose. It could also encourage instructors to select a default option without considering how AI relates to learning outcomes.

That objection argues for a well-designed requirement, not for silence. The teaching committee’s proposal permits instructors to add an explanation after the statement. Calder should adopt a concise required declaration modeled on the student union’s three options—prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure—while allowing whatever elaboration the assignment needs. An instructor who believes the university policy supplies all necessary detail can say so explicitly by selecting the corresponding option and referring students to it. An instructor facing a complex task can add a paragraph. The requirement standardizes the location and existence of the answer, not the pedagogical judgment behind it.

Engineering’s experience suggests that such a system is operationally feasible. After the school added an “AI use for this assignment” box to its template, 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained a completed box. The audit did not assess clarity or student compliance, so it cannot demonstrate educational success. It does, however, show that a template requirement can achieve high uptake within a short period. Calder should treat completion as the minimum and support quality through examples, review, and permission for fuller explanations. The four incomplete boxes also warn against assuming that a template enforces itself.

An assignment-level requirement would not eliminate uncertainty, misconduct, or contested cases. Language can still be vague, tools change, and students may disregard explicit rules. Nor should every instructor be forced into an identical AI policy. The sound principle is narrower: whenever the university assesses work under rules that may vary by task, it owes students a written statement of the applicable rule at the point of use. Calder’s present record cannot prove how much improvement the requirement will produce, but it shows enough uncertainty, inconsistency, and unenforceable allegation to justify a modest response. The committee should vote yes in May and make clarity part of every assignment, not an optional supplement to institutional policy.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P8_R4_C0 -->

</details>

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P8_R4_C1 -->
````text
# Put the AI rule where students need it

Calder University should require an AI use statement on every assessed assignment. Its institution-wide policy answers the general question of who decides: instructors may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI. It does not reliably answer the question facing a student about to begin a particular piece of work: what may I do here? That answer belongs on the assignment brief, where students can find it when they need it and where instructors can refer to it if a dispute arises.

The distinction matters because a university policy and an assignment instruction do different jobs. The policy establishes authority and common obligations, including disclosure of permitted AI use. The brief translates those rules into the terms of an actual task. An instructor might allow AI to suggest topics but not draft prose, permit it for code debugging, or ban it from a reflective exercise. None of those choices conflicts with the university policy. Yet a student cannot infer which choice applies simply by reading that policy.

Calder's review of 240 briefs shows how often students must make that inference. Only 94 briefs explicitly stated whether and how AI could be used. Fifty-seven referred students to the institution-wide policy, which tells them that instructors have discretion without necessarily telling them how that discretion was exercised. Another 89 did not mention AI at all. Some instructors may have given verbal guidance, so the review does not prove that those students received no instructions. Still, assessed work should not depend on a remembered comment in class, especially when the written brief is meant to define the task.

The student survey adds a reason for concern, though not a clean causal explanation. Of 623 respondents, 381 reported uncertainty about permitted AI use on at least one assignment. The survey cannot connect those students to any category of brief, and its respondents may not represent all 1,860 students who were invited. We should not pretend the figures prove that missing statements caused the uncertainty. They do show that uncertainty was common enough to deserve a direct response. Requiring one written statement on each assignment is a modest response tailored to the problem students reported.

The case referral figures make the need more concrete. From September 2025 through March 2026, the Academic Integrity Office received 73 AI-related referrals. Twenty-one were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. A dismissal does not prove that a student used AI, nor does it tell us whether clearer wording would have prevented the referral. It does establish something narrower but important: in nearly three out of ten referrals, the written instructions could not sustain the allegation. Ambiguous rules waste staff time and place students under suspicion without an adequate documentary basis.

A required statement would also protect instructors. When expectations are written down, staff can distinguish a genuine breach from conduct that merely feels inconsistent with the assignment's purpose. The requirement need not make every assignment permissive, and it need not force one rule across a module. In the survey, 207 respondents said rules differed between two assignments in the same module. Those differences may have been intentional. A source analysis, a coding exercise, and a personal reflection can reasonably demand different approaches to AI. Assignment-level statements preserve that discretion while making each decision visible.

The strongest practical objection comes from the history department. Its chair argues that some tasks need a paragraph of explanation, while others need nothing beyond the university policy. That concern is sensible. A rigid checkbox could flatten genuine distinctions, invite careless boilerplate, and turn academic judgment into template compliance. Calder's engineering school illustrates both the attraction and the limit of such a system. After it added an "AI use for this assignment" box, 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained a completed box. That is strong evidence that a template can change documentation habits. But the audit did not test whether the wording was clear or whether students followed it. A filled box is not the same as a useful instruction.

The answer is to require a statement without pretending that the statement must always be short. The teaching committee's proposal already allows instructors to add explanation. It should adopt a simple minimum based on the student union's options: prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure. An instructor who needs nuance can add a paragraph. An instructor who believes the university policy is sufficient can say so explicitly, provided the brief also makes clear which permission level applies. Even "No assignment-specific restrictions beyond the university policy" tells a student more than silence does.

This requirement will not eliminate uncertainty, misconduct, or disputed referrals. The available evidence cannot support that promise. It can establish a fairer starting point: before students are assessed, they should be able to read the rules governing the work in front of them. Calder has already left decisions about AI use with instructors. Requiring those decisions to appear on each assignment does not weaken that freedom. It makes the freedom accountable, usable, and fit for assessment.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P8_R4_C1 -->

</details>

Requiring an assignment-level statement preserves academic discretion while making that discretion usable, reviewable, and fair.

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P8_R4_C2 -->
````text
# Put the Rule Where the Student Needs It

Calder University should require an AI-use statement on each assessed assignment. The institution-wide policy gives instructors sound discretion: they may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI. Yet discretion works when students can find the choice that governs the work in front of them. A rule on an assignment brief connects an instructor’s judgment to a named task, at the moment when a student must act. The university policy should supply the framework; each brief should supply the answer.

Calder’s review shows a communication gap large enough to warrant action. Among 240 briefs, 94 stated whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven sent students to the institution-wide policy, which cannot tell them which of its permitted choices their instructor made. Eighty-nine said nothing about AI. In sum, fewer than two briefs in five gave a written assignment-level answer. Some instructors may have given verbal directions, since the review examined written briefs alone. That possibility does not make the written gap harmless. Students need a durable instruction they can consult while working, and instructors need a record of the rule they set.

The student survey adds cause for concern without proving cause and effect. Of 623 respondents, 381 had felt unsure about permitted AI use on at least one assignment during the academic year. The survey cannot link those students to any category of brief, and its response pool represents 623 of the 1,860 students invited. We should not claim that silent briefs produced all, or any fixed share, of the uncertainty. A university need not wait for a controlled experiment before correcting an evident design flaw. Calder offers instructors three possible stances while many briefs leave students unable to identify the stance that applies.

Variation within modules makes assignment-level wording more valuable. Two hundred seven survey respondents reported different rules across two assignments in the same module. Instructors may have intended those differences. A literature review, a coding exercise, and an oral reflection can call for different limits because each assesses different work. Such variation supports task-specific statements. A module handbook or university policy cannot express a decision that changes from one assessment to the next. If the differences arose by accident, a required field would prompt the instructor to notice and resolve them.

Calder has an integrity problem that involves instruction as well as conduct. The Academic Integrity Office received 73 AI-related referrals between September and March. Staff dismissed 21 before a hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. Those dismissals do not prove AI use, and they do not show that better wording would have prevented any referral. They establish a narrower point: in close to three cases out of ten, the university could not sustain the allegation from the instructions available. A required statement would give students, instructors, and case reviewers a common written reference. It would protect students from charges unsupported by the assigned rule and help staff pursue cases where the rule and evidence support action.

The strongest practical objection comes from the history department. Its chair argues that some assignments need a paragraph while others need no AI instruction beyond the university policy. A required box can tempt instructors to select stock language without thinking through the task. The engineering audit illustrates that limit. After the school added a box to its template, 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained a completed box, but auditors did not judge clarity or student compliance. Completion measures presence, not quality.

That objection should shape the mandate rather than defeat it. The teaching committee’s proposal lets instructors add an explanation after the statement, so a historian can give a paragraph and an engineer can name permitted coding tasks. Calder should adopt the student union’s three starting options: prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure. The middle option requires instructors to name the tasks. For an assignment that needs no rule beyond the university policy, the instructor can say which policy option applies and refer students to its disclosure terms. Repeating one line costs little; asking each student to infer an unstated choice costs much more.

Implementation will require care. Calder should place the statement beside submission and assessment information, preserve room for explanation, and ask departments to review ambiguous wording. The university should make instructors update reused briefs when they redesign a task. A required field cannot ensure good judgment, and the engineering figures do not show that it changes student behavior. It can ensure that omission becomes visible. The 46 completed engineering boxes suggest that a template requirement can secure high uptake within a school, though Calder should not assume the same rate across all departments.

The institution-wide policy settled who decides. Calder needs to ensure that instructors communicate each decision where students encounter it. The current record cannot assign a precise number of confused students or dismissed referrals to missing statements. It gives the committee enough evidence to act: written guidance appears on a minority of briefs, reported uncertainty is common among survey respondents, and case reviewers have faced allegations that the available instructions could not support. Requiring an assignment-level statement preserves academic discretion while making that discretion usable, reviewable, and fair.
````
<!-- OUTPUT-END 2:P8_R4_C2 -->

</details>

Complete output:

<!-- OUTPUT-START 2:P8_R4_C3 -->
````text
Put the AI Rule Where Students Need It

Calder University’s teaching committee should require an AI-use statement on every assessed assignment. The institution-wide policy adopted in August 2025 is a necessary source of authority, but it cannot do the work of an assignment brief. It gives instructors three broad powers: they may permit, limit, or prohibit generative AI. It also requires students to disclose permitted use but supplies no wording for individual assignments. A disclosure duty cannot tell a student whether the proposed use is permitted in the first place. That answer belongs beside the task, criteria, and submission instructions.

The university’s review of written briefs gives the committee a practical reason to act. Of 240 briefs from 60 undergraduate modules, 94 explicitly said whether and how students could use generative AI. Fifty-seven referred only to the university policy, which does not itself settle what is permitted on a particular task. Eighty-nine did not mention AI. Some of those briefs may have been accompanied by verbal directions, since the review examined only the written documents. Even so, verbal advice is a poor substitute for a rule that students may need to consult while working or when their conduct is later questioned.

The student survey adds evidence of uncertainty without identifying its source. Among 623 respondents from a survey of 1,860 students, 381 said they had been unsure about permitted AI use on at least one assignment during the academic year. The survey cannot connect those students to any of the three brief categories, so it does not prove that missing statements caused their uncertainty. It does establish that uncertainty was common among the students who responded. A requirement aimed directly at making the rule visible on each task is a proportionate response, even though the survey cannot predict how much uncertainty it would remove.

Variation within modules makes assignment-level wording more useful, not less. In the same survey, 207 respondents said rules differed between two assignments in one module. The survey did not ask whether those differences were intentional. They may well have reflected sound teaching choices: an instructor might prohibit AI in an exercise assessing unaided analysis, then permit it for named tasks in a later project. An institution-wide policy is designed to accommodate such choices. Assignment-level statements would communicate them at the point where they change.

The Academic Integrity Office’s record raises a separate concern about enforceability. It received 73 AI-related referrals between September 2025 and March 2026. Twenty-one were dismissed before a formal hearing because the available assignment instructions did not support the alleged violation. Those dismissals establish neither that the students used AI nor that better wording would have prevented the referrals. A written statement would give students, instructors, and case reviewers one document against which to assess conduct on that assignment.

The other outcomes counsel against exaggeration. Eighteen referrals ended in findings of a violation, 14 in findings of no violation, and 20 remained open on April 1. The record does not make unclear briefs the general explanation for AI cases. The case figures support a narrower point. When a university permits assignment-by-assignment variation, the assignment record must be strong enough to identify the applicable rule.

The strongest practical objection comes from the history department. Its chair argued that some assignments need a paragraph of explanation, while others need no AI-specific instruction beyond university policy. A required box can invite boilerplate, and a fixed menu may compress distinctions that matter in different disciplines or assessments. Requiring instructors to revisit settled language on every brief also adds work, especially where the intended rule remains unchanged across a module. That cost recurs when instructors create a new assignment or revise an existing one.

That objection should shape the requirement rather than defeat it. The committee’s proposal allows instructors to add explanation after the statement, so a complex task can have the paragraph that history wants. A simple task can use one sentence. Instructors would keep the discretion granted by the university policy; the new requirement would make the result of that discretion visible. The student union’s proposed options of prohibited, permitted for named tasks, or unrestricted with disclosure offer a useful starting structure, but “permitted for named tasks” must leave room to name those tasks and any conditions. Departments can provide approved language that instructors reuse when the rule stays constant, reducing repeated drafting without leaving a brief silent. The committee should require a definite status and allow as much explanation as the assignment needs.

Engineering’s experience suggests that adding a field to the template is administratively possible, at least in one school. After the engineering school introduced a required “AI use for this assignment” box in January 2026, 46 of 50 sampled briefs contained a completed box in February. Because the audit assessed completion rather than clarity or student compliance, the result is evidence of uptake, not proof that a box solves the underlying problem. Four sampled briefs still lacked a completed box. If adopted, the university should audit wording rather than mere box completion and revise templates where students cannot identify permitted tasks, restrictions, and disclosure duties.
````
